blocks:
Ayah

سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَسْرَىٰ بِعَبْدِهِۦ لَيْلًا مِّنَ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ إِلَى ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْأَقْصَا ٱلَّذِى بَـٰرَكْنَا حَوْلَهُۥ لِنُرِيَهُۥ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَآ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ (١)
Immaculate is He who carried His servant on a journey by night from the Sacred Mosque to the Farthest Mosque whose environs We have blessed, that We might show him some of Our signs. Indeed, He is the All-hearing, the All-seeing. (1)

1- The Names of This Surah and Its Place of Revelation

The most famous name of this Surah is "Bani Isra'il," and it also has other names such as "Isra'" and "Subhan." [^1] It is clear that each of these names is proportionate to the subjects mentioned within the Surah. If it is named "Bani Isra'il," it is because a significant portion at the beginning and end of this Surah concerns the Children of Israel.

If it is called "Isra'," it is because of its first verse, which speaks about the Isra' (Night Journey/Ascension) of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. "Subhan" is derived from the very first word of the Surah.

However, in the narrations describing the virtue of this Surah, we primarily encounter the name "Bani Isra'il," and for this reason, most commentators have chosen this name for the Surah.

In any case, it is well-known and widely accepted that all one hundred and eleven verses of this Surah were revealed in Mecca, and its concepts and content are entirely consistent with Meccan Surahs.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Alusi, Vol. 15, p. 2.

Although some commentators believe that a few of its verses were revealed in Medina, the famous view seems more correct.

2- The Virtue of This Surah

Great rewards have been narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and also from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام for anyone who recites this Surah. Among them:

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever recites Surah Bani Isra'il every Friday night will not die until he reaches the Qa'im (the Living Imam) and becomes one of his companions."

We have repeatedly stated that the rewards and virtues mentioned for the Surahs of the Quran are never for mere recitation alone; rather, it is a recitation accompanied by reflection and thought, followed by drawing inspiration for action.

Specifically, in one of these narrations regarding the virtue of this Surah, we read: "His heart softens at the mention of parents." This means that for one who recites this Surah, when he reaches Allah's recommendations regarding parents, his emotions are stirred, and he feels greater affection toward them—such a person earns that reward.

Therefore, while the words of the Quran are undoubtedly respected and highly valuable, these words are a prelude to the meanings, and the meanings are a prelude to action.

3- The Content of This Surah at a Glance

As mentioned, this Surah is famously among those revealed in Mecca, and naturally, it possesses the characteristics of Meccan Surahs, including an effective call to Monotheism (Tawhid), drawing attention to the Hereafter, beneficial advice, and a struggle against all forms of polytheism, injustice, and deviation.

Generally, it can be said that the verses of this Surah revolve around the following matters:

  1. Proofs of Prophethood, especially the Quran and the Mi'raj (Ascension).
  2. Discussions related to the Hereafter, the issue of punishment and reward, the Record of Deeds, and its consequences.
  3. A portion of the eventful history of the Children of Israel, which appears at the beginning and end of the Surah.
  4. The issue of free will and choice, and that the result of every good or bad deed returns to the human being himself.
  5. The issue of accountability in this life, which serves as a sample for the next world.
  6. Gratitude and recognizing rights at all levels, especially regarding relatives, and particularly parents.
  7. The prohibition of "extravagance and wastefulness," "miserliness," "killing children," "adultery," "consuming the property of orphans," "short-changing in trade," "arrogance," and "bloodshed."
  8. Discussions in the field of Monotheism and the knowledge of God.
  9. Combating all forms of stubbornness against the truth and how sins cast a veil between man and the observation of the face of Truth.
  10. The personality of man and his virtue and superiority over other creatures.
  11. The effect of the Quran in healing all moral and social diseases.
  12. The miracle of the Quran and the inability to challenge it.
  13. The whisperings of Satan and a warning to all believers regarding the ways Satan influences man.
  14. A section on various moral teachings.
  15. And finally, passages from the history of the Prophets as lessons for all humans and as evidence for the above matters.

In any case, the collection of ideological, moral, and social discussions in this Surah constitutes a complete prescription for the elevation and perfection of humanity in various fields. Interestingly, this Surah begins with the "Glorification of Allah" (Tasbih) and ends with "His Praise and Greatness" (Hamd and Takbir). Tasbih is a sign of purification and stripping away every defect, Hamd is a sign of adorning with virtuous attributes, and Takbir is a symbol of progress and greatness.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ سُبْحانَ الَّذِي أَسْرى‌ بِعَبْدِهِ لَيْلاً مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى الَّذِي بارَكْنا حَوْلَهُ لِنُرِيَهُ مِنْ آياتِنا إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ (1)

Commentary

The Place of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله Ascension

The first verse of this Surah speaks of the issue of "Isra'," meaning the night journey of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله from the Sacred Mosque (al-Masjid al-Haram) to the Farthest Mosque (al-Masjid al-Aqsa in Jerusalem), which was a prelude to the Mi'raj (Ascension to the Heavens). This journey, which took place in one night and in a short duration, was by no means possible through ordinary means, at least under the conditions of that time, and it possessed a miraculous and completely extraordinary nature.

First, He says: "Glory be to Him Who took His servant by night from the Sacred Mosque to the Farthest Mosque" (سُبْحانَ الَّذِي أَسْرى‌ بِعَبْدِهِ لَيْلًا مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى‌).

This extraordinary night journey was: "in order that We might show him some of Our signs" (لِنُرِيَهُ مِنْ آياتِنا).

And at the end, He adds: "Indeed, He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing" (إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ).

This is an indication that if Allah chose His Prophet for this honor, it was not without reason, for he possessed such pure and worthy speech and conduct that this mantle fit his stature perfectly. Allah heard the speech of His Prophet, saw his deeds, and accepted his merit for this station.

Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that the above phrase is a threat to those who deny this miracle—that Allah hears their words, sees their actions, and is aware of their plots.

This verse, while concise, expresses most of the characteristics of this miraculous night journey:

  1. The phrase "Asra" shows that this journey took place at night, because Isra' in the Arabic language means a night journey, whereas the word Sayr is used for traveling during the day.
  2. The word "Laylan" (by night), while being an emphasis for what is understood from the word Asra, also expresses the fact that this journey took place entirely in a single night. The significance lies in the fact that the distance between the Sacred Mosque and Jerusalem, which is more than a hundred leagues and would have taken days or weeks under the conditions of that time, occurred in only one night.
  3. The word "Abd" (servant) shows that this honor and dignity were due to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله station of servitude and worship. The highest station for a human is to be a true servant of Allah, to prostrate before none but Him, and to submit to no command but His; to do everything for Allah and to seek His pleasure in every step.
  4. Furthermore, the expression "Abd" shows that this journey took place while awake and that it was a physical journey, not merely spiritual. A spiritual journey has no rational meaning other than a dream or a state similar to a dream, but the word "Abd" shows that the body and soul of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله participated in this journey. However, those who could not properly digest this miracle in their minds mentioned the possibility of it being spiritual as a justification for the verse, whereas we know that if someone says to another, "I took such-and-such a person to such-and-such a place," it does not mean it was in the world of dreams or imagination, or that his thoughts and mind undertook such a journey.
  5. The beginning of this journey (which was a prelude to the issue of the Ascension to the heavens, the proofs of which will be mentioned later) was "al-Masjid al-Haram" in Mecca, and its end was "al-Masjid al-Aqsa" in Jerusalem. There is discussion among commentators as to whether the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله began this journey from beside the Kaaba or from the house of one of his relatives (and since the entire city of Mecca is sometimes called al-Masjid al-Haram out of respect, this expression is used in the verse). However, without a doubt, the apparent meaning of the verse is that the starting point of his journey was the Sacred Mosque.
  6. The goal of this journey was to observe the signs of Divine greatness, just as the continuation of this journey in the heavens was carried out for the same purpose—so that the great soul of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله might attain even greater majesty in the light of observing those clear signs and find increased readiness for guiding humanity. It was not as the narrow-minded imagine—that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله went on the Ascension to see Allah! Thinking that Allah has a place in the heavens!! In any case, although the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had known the greatness of Allah and was aware of the greatness of His creation, "hearing is never like seeing!" In the verses of Surah an-Najm, which refer to the continuation of this journey—the Ascension in the heavens—we also read: لَقَدْ رَأى‌ مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرى‌: "He certainly saw of the greatest signs of his Lord."
  7. The phrase "barakna hawlahu" (We blessed its surroundings) expresses the fact that al-Masjid al-Aqsa, in addition to being a holy land itself, is surrounded by a blessed and fruitful land. This may refer to its physical blessings, as we know it is located in a lush, green area full of trees, flowing waters, and settlements. It may also refer to its spiritual blessings, as this holy land has throughout history been the center for the great Prophets of Allah and the birthplace of the light of Monotheism and God-worship.
  8. The phrase "innahu huwa al-sami'u al-basir" (Indeed, He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing), as we said, indicates that the granting of this gift to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was not arbitrary but was due to the merits he acquired through his speech and deeds, of which Allah was well aware.
  9. Additionally, the word "Subhan" is proof that this program of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is itself a sign of Allah being Holy and Pure from any defect or deficiency.
  10. The word "min" (from/some) in "min ayatina" (of Our signs) shows that the signs of Allah's greatness are so numerous that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, despite all his greatness, observed only a portion of them during this magnificent journey.

The Issue of the Mi'raj (Ascension)

It is well-known and famous among Muslim scholars that while the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was in Mecca, he traveled in one night from the Sacred Mosque to the Farthest Mosque in Jerusalem by the power of the Lord, and from there he ascended to the heavens, observing the signs of Allah's greatness in the expanse of the sky, and returned to Mecca that same night.

It is also well-known and famous that he performed this earthly and heavenly journey with both body and soul together.

However, since this is an extraordinarily wondrous subject, a group has attempted to justify it by interpreting it as a spiritual ascension, which would be something like a dream or a spiritual vision. But as we said, this subject is completely contrary to the apparent meaning of the verses, for the appearance of the verses testifies to the physical nature of the event.

In any case, there are many questions surrounding this discussion, including:

  1. The nature of the Mi'raj according to the Quran, Hadith, and history.
  2. The belief of Islamic scholars, both Shia and Sunni, in this regard.
  3. The purpose of the Mi'raj.
  4. The possibility of the Mi'raj from the perspective of modern science.

Although a lengthy discussion on these matters is beyond the scope of a single tafsir commentary, we will attempt to provide a summary of these issues for the dear readers below.

1- The Mi'raj from the Perspective of Quran and Hadith

This issue is mentioned in two Surahs of the Quran:

First is this very Surah al-Isra', which describes only the first part of this journey (i.e., the journey from Mecca and the Sacred Mosque to the Farthest Mosque and Jerusalem).

However, in Surah an-Najm, through six verses from verse 13 to 18, the second part of the Mi'raj—the heavenly journey—is mentioned. There He says: وَ لَقَدْ رَآهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرى‌. عِنْدَ سِدْرَةِ الْمُنْتَهى‌. عِنْدَها جَنَّةُ الْمَأْوى‌. إِذْ يَغْشَى السِّدْرَةَ ما يَغْشى‌. ما زاغَ الْبَصَرُ وَ ما طَغى‌. لَقَدْ رَأى‌ مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرى‌: The summary of the content of these six verses is that "the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saw and met the angel of revelation, Gabriel, in his original form for the second time (the first time was at the beginning of the revelation at Mount Hira).

This meeting took place near the Eternal Paradise. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not fall into error or mistake in observing this scene. He observed great signs and tokens of Allah's majesty."

These verses, which according to most commentators speak of the Mi'raj, also show that this event happened while awake. Specifically, the phrase ما زاغَ الْبَصَرُ وَ ما طَغى‌ (The sight did not swerve, nor did it transgress) is another witness to this subject. From the perspective of Hadith, a very large number of narrations regarding the issue of the Mi'raj have been recorded in famous Islamic books, such that many Islamic scholars have confirmed their tawatur (successive transmission) or fame. For example:

The great jurist and commentator Sheikh Tusi, in the tafsir al-Tibyan, under the verse in question, says: "The Shia scholars believe that on the same night Allah took His Prophet from Mecca to Jerusalem, He ascended him toward the heavens and showed him the signs of His greatness in the heavens, and this was while awake, not in a dream."

The eminent commentator, the late Tabarsi, in his tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses of Surah an-Najm, says: "What is famous in our reports is that Allah took the Prophet with this very body while awake and alive to the heavens, and most commentators hold this same belief."

The renowned traditionist Allamah Majlisi says in Bihar al-Anwar: "The journey of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله from the Sacred Mosque to Jerusalem and from there to the heavens is among the matters that the verses and mutawatir (successively transmitted) hadiths of both Shia and Sunni indicate. Denying such matters or interpreting them as a spiritual ascension or a dream of the Prophet arises from a lack of knowledge of the reports of the Imams of Guidance or a weakness of certainty."

He then adds: "If we were to collect the reports that have arrived regarding this, it would become a large book." [^1] Among Sunni scholars, Mansur Ali Nasif, a contemporary scholar from al-Azhar and author of the famous book al-Taj, has collected the hadiths of the Mi'raj in his book.

Fakhr Razi, the famous commentator, under the verse in question, after mentioning a series of rational arguments for the possibility of the Mi'raj, says: "From the perspective of Hadith, the hadiths of the Mi'raj are among the famous narrations recorded in the Sahih books of the Sunnis, and their content is the journey of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله from Mecca to Jerusalem and from there to the heavens."

Sheikh Abdul Aziz bin Abdullah bin Baz, the head of the departments of "Scientific Research, Ifta, Da'wah, and Guidance," who is a contemporary conservative Wahhabi scholar, says in the book al-Tahdhir min al-Bida': "There is no doubt that the Isra' and Mi'raj are among the great signs that indicate the truthfulness of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the loftiness of his station and rank," until he says: "Mutawatir reports have been narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that Allah took him to the heavens and opened the gates of heaven for him." [^1]

It is absolutely necessary to mention that among the narrations of the Mi'raj, fabricated or weak hadiths are seen that are by no means acceptable. Therefore, the great commentator, the late Tabarsi, under this very verse, divided the hadiths of the Mi'raj into four groups:

  1. Narrations that are certain by the rule of tawatur, such as the core subject of the Mi'raj.
  2. Hadiths whose acceptance has no rational obstacle and are explicitly stated in the reports, such as observing many signs of Allah's greatness in the expanse of the sky.
  3. Narrations that conflict with the standards and principles we hold but can be interpreted, such as hadiths saying the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saw a group in Paradise and a group in Hell in the heavens; it must be said that the meaning was observing the qualities of the people of Paradise and Hell (or the Paradise and Hell of the Barzakh).
  4. Narrations that contain irrational and false matters, and their wording is a clear witness to their fabrication, such as narrations saying the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saw Allah openly, spoke with Him, and sat with Him—which is consistent with no logic and is contrary to rational and textual evidence. Without a doubt, these types of narrations are fabricated.

Regarding the date of the Mi'raj, there is a difference of opinion among Islamic historians. Some consider it to be in the tenth year of the Prophetic mission on the night of the 27th of Rajab; some consider it in the twelfth year on the night of the 17th of Ramadan; and some have mentioned it in the early years of the mission. However, the difference in the date of its occurrence does not prevent agreement on the fact of its occurrence.

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 6, old edition, p. 368. [^1]: al-Tahdhir, p. 7.

It is also necessary to note that it is not only Muslims who believe in an ascension; this belief exists more or less among followers of other religions. For instance, it is seen in a more heavy-handed form regarding Prophet Jesus عليه السلام. As we read in the Gospel of Mark Chapter 16, the Gospel of Luke Chapter 24, and the Gospel of John Chapter 21, that Jesus, after being hung, killed, and buried, rose from the dead and lived among the people for forty days, then ascended to the heavens (and went on a permanent ascension).

Additionally, it is understood from some Islamic narrations that some previous Prophets also had an ascension.

Was the Mi'raj Physical or Spiritual?

In addition to the fact that it is famous among Islamic scholars (both Shia and Sunni) that this event took place while awake, the appearance of the Quranic verses themselves at the beginning of Surah al-Isra' and also Surah an-Najm (as explained above) also testifies to the occurrence of this event while awake.

Islamic histories are also a truthful witness to this subject, for we read in history that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله raised the issue of the Mi'raj, the polytheists vehemently denied it and considered it an excuse to attack the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. This clearly testifies that the Prophet never claimed a dream or a spiritual vision; otherwise, it would not have caused such an uproar. And if we read in a narration from Hasan al-Basri that "it was a vision he saw in a dream," or the report narrated from Aisha: "By Allah, the body of the Messenger of Allah was not missing, but he was ascended in his soul," these apparently had a political aspect and were intended to quiet the controversy that had arisen among some regarding the issue of the Mi'raj.

The Purpose of the Mi'raj

Given the previous discussions, it is clear to us that the purpose of the Mi'raj was not for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to rush to meet Allah in the heavens! As the simple-minded have imagined—and unfortunately, some Western scholars, due to ignorance or to distort the face of Islam in the eyes of others, have quoted it. For instance, "Gheorghiu" in the book Muhammad: The Prophet to be Re-evaluated says: "Muhammad reached a place during the journey of the Mi'raj where he heard the sound of Allah's pen and understood that Allah was busy keeping the accounts of individuals! But although he heard the sound of Allah's pen, he did not see Him! For no one can see Allah, even if he be a Prophet" (Muhammad: The Prophet to be Re-evaluated, p. 125).

This shows that they think the pen is specifically of the wooden type! Which trembles and makes a sound when moving on writing paper!! And similar superstitions and nonsense.

No, the goal was for the great soul of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to observe the secrets of Allah's greatness throughout the universe, especially the upper world, which is a collection of the signs of His majesty, and to find a new insight and vision for guiding and leading humanity.

This goal is explicitly stated in verse 1 of Surah al-Isra' and verse 18 of Surah an-Najm.

An interesting narration is also quoted in this regard from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام in response to a question about the reason for the Mi'raj, where he said:

ان اللَّه لا يوصف بمكان، و لا يجرى عليه زمان، و لكنه عز و جل اراد ان يشرف به ملائكته و سكان سماواته، و يكرمهم بمشاهدته، و يريه من عجائب عظمته ما يخبر به بعد هبوطه:

"Indeed, Allah is never described by a place, and time does not flow over Him. But He, the Mighty and Majestic, intended to honor His angels and the inhabitants of His heavens with him (the Prophet), and to grace them by his presence, and to show him the wonders of His greatness so that he might inform the people after his descent." [^1]

The Mi'raj and Modern Science

In the past, some philosophers who believed in the nine Ptolemaic "onion-skin" spheres considered the major scientific obstacle to the Mi'raj to be the existence of these spheres and the necessity of "tearing and mending" (kharq wa iltiyam) within them. [^2] However, with the collapse of the foundations of the Ptolemaic system, the issue of tearing and mending was forgotten. But with the progress made in modern astronomy, new issues have been raised regarding the Mi'raj, and questions such as:

  1. To undertake a space journey, the first obstacle is the force of gravity, which must be overcome with extraordinary means, for to escape the "Earth's gravitational field," a speed of at least forty thousand kilometers per hour is required!
  2. Another obstacle is the lack of air in the space outside the Earth's atmosphere, without which a human cannot live.
  3. The third obstacle is the scorching heat of the sun and the killing cold that exists in the part where the sun shines directly and the part where it does not.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 2, p. 400. [^2]: Kharq means tearing and iltiyam means mending. Some ancient philosophers believed this was impossible in the celestial spheres.

  1. The fourth obstacle is the dangerous radiations that exist beyond the atmosphere, such as cosmic rays, ultraviolet rays, and X-rays. These rays, if they hit the human body in small amounts, do no harm to its organism, but outside the Earth's atmosphere, these rays are extremely intense, lethal, and deadly. However, for us inhabitants of Earth, the existence of the atmospheric air layer prevents their radiation.
  2. The problem of weightlessness. Although a human can gradually get used to weightlessness, for us inhabitants of the Earth, if we were transferred outside the atmosphere without preparation and the state of weightlessness occurred, enduring it would be very difficult or impossible.
  3. And finally, the problem of time is the sixth problem and one of the most important obstacles, for modern science says there is no speed higher than the speed of light, and if someone wants to travel throughout the heavens, he must have a speed greater than the speed of light.

In response to these questions, several points are necessary

  1. We know that despite all the problems in space travel, man has finally been able to overcome them with the power of science, and except for the problem of time, all problems have been solved, and the problem of time relates to traveling to distant regions.
  2. Without a doubt, the issue of the Mi'raj was not an ordinary matter but was carried out using the infinite power and strength of Allah, and all the miracles of the Prophets are the same. To put it clearly, a miracle must not be rationally impossible, and as long as it is rationally possible, the rest is solvable with help from the power of Allah. When mankind, with the progress of science, finds the ability to build fast vehicles—so fast that they leave the Earth's gravitational field—builds spaceships that solve the problem of deadly rays outside the atmosphere, wears clothes that protect him against extreme cold and heat, and gets used to weightlessness through practice—in short, where man can traverse this path using his limited power, is it not solvable with help from the unlimited Divine power?! We are certain that Allah placed a high-speed vessel at the disposal of His Prophet that was suitable for this space journey and took him under His protection regarding the dangers that existed in this journey. What was this vessel like and what was its name? Buraq? Rafraf? Or another vessel? In any case, it is a mysterious and unknown vessel to us. Beyond all this, the hypothesis of maximum speed mentioned above has become shaky among scientists today, even though "Einstein" firmly believed in it in his famous hypothesis. Scientists today say that gravitational waves are transmitted from one side of the universe to the other and take effect instantly without the need for time. There is even the possibility that in movements related to the expansion of the universe (we know the universe is expanding and stars and galaxies are moving away from each other rapidly), there are systems that move away from the center of the universe at a speed greater than the speed of light (pay attention). In short, none of the problems mentioned constitute a rational obstacle in this path—an obstacle that would make the Mi'raj a rational impossibility. Rather, they are difficulties that are solvable using the necessary means and power. In any case, the issue of the Mi'raj is neither impossible from the perspective of rational arguments nor from the perspective of the standards of modern science, and everyone accepts its extraordinary nature. Therefore, whenever it is proven by decisive textual evidence, it must be accepted. [^1] Regarding the discussions of the Mi'raj, there are other matters that, God willing, will come under Surah an-Najm.

[^1]: For more explanation, please refer to the book Everyone Wants to Know, in which we have discussed the Mi'raj, the Splitting of the Moon, and worship at the poles.

Ayahs

وَءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ هُدًى لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَلَّا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ مِن دُونِى وَكِيلًا (٢)
We gave Moses the Book, and made it a guide for the Children of Israel—[saying,] ‘Do not take any trustee besides Me’— (2)
ذُرِّيَّةَ مَنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ عَبْدًا شَكُورًا (٣)
descendants of those whom We carried [in the ark] with Noah. Indeed, he was a grateful servant. (3)
وَقَضَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَتُفْسِدُنَّ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَرَّتَيْنِ وَلَتَعْلُنَّ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا (٤)
We revealed to the Children of Israel in the Book: ‘Twice you will cause corruption on the earth, and you will perpetrate great tyranny.’ (4)
فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ أُولَىٰهُمَا بَعَثْنَا عَلَيْكُمْ عِبَادًا لَّنَآ أُو۟لِى بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ فَجَاسُوا۟ خِلَـٰلَ ٱلدِّيَارِ ۚ وَكَانَ وَعْدًا مَّفْعُولًا (٥)
So when the first occasion of the two [prophecies] came, We aroused against you Our servants possessing great might, and they ransacked [your] habitations, and the promise was bound to be fulfilled. (5)
ثُمَّ رَدَدْنَا لَكُمُ ٱلْكَرَّةَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَمْدَدْنَـٰكُم بِأَمْوَٰلٍ وَبَنِينَ وَجَعَلْنَـٰكُمْ أَكْثَرَ نَفِيرًا (٦)
Then We gave you back the turn [to prevail] over them, and We aided you with children and wealth, and made you greater in number, (6)
إِنْ أَحْسَنتُمْ أَحْسَنتُمْ لِأَنفُسِكُمْ ۖ وَإِنْ أَسَأْتُمْ فَلَهَا ۚ فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لِيَسُـۥٓـُٔوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ وَلِيَدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْمَسْجِدَ كَمَا دَخَلُوهُ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَلِيُتَبِّرُوا۟ مَا عَلَوْا۟ تَتْبِيرًا (٧)
[saying,] ‘If you do good, you will do good to your [own] souls, and if you do evil, it will be [evil] for them.’ So when the occasion for the other [prophecy] comes, they will make your faces wretched, and enter the Temple just as they entered it the first time, and destroy utterly whatever they come upon. (7)
عَسَىٰ رَبُّكُمْ أَن يَرْحَمَكُمْ ۚ وَإِنْ عُدتُّمْ عُدْنَا ۘ وَجَعَلْنَا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ حَصِيرًا (٨)
Maybe your Lord will have mercy on you, but if you revert, We [too] will revert, and We have made hell a prison for the faithless. (8)

Commentary

Two Great Storms

Since the first verse of this Surah spoke of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) journey from "al-Masjid al-Haram" to "al-Masjid al-Aqsa" in a single night as a miracle and an honor for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and since such subjects were often denied by the polytheists and opponents—questioning how a prophet could arise from among them with such great distinctions—the verses under discussion refer to the call of Moses (عليه السلام) to his Divine Book. This is to make it clear that the program of messengership is not an unprecedented phenomenon, and that the stubborn and persistent opposition of the polytheists also has precedents in past history, especially the history of the Children of Israel.

For this reason, the first verse under discussion says: "And We gave Moses the Book" (وَآتَيْنَا مُوسَى الْكِتَابَ).

"And We made it a guidance for the Children of Israel" (وَجَعَلْنَاهُ هُدًى لِبَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ).

Undoubtedly, the "Book" here refers to the "Torah," which Allah placed at the disposal of Moses (عليه السلام) for the guidance of the Children of Israel.

Then, it points to the fundamental goal of the mission of the prophets, including Moses: "saying: 'Take not other than Me as a guardian (Wakil)'" (أَلَّا تَتَّخِذُوا مِنْ دُونِي وَكِيلًا) [1]. This is one of the primary branches of Monotheism (Tawhid)—Monotheism in action—which is a sign of Monotheism in belief. One who considers the only true effective agent in the universe to be Allah will not rely on anyone else; those who choose other supports for themselves demonstrate the weakness of their foundational Monotheism.

The most sublime manifestation of the guidance of Divine Books is the igniting of the light of Tawhid in hearts, the result of which is "detaching from all else and connecting to Allah" and relying solely upon Him.

[1]: Regarding the grammatical structure, some commentators consider the phrase allā tattakhidhū min dūnī wakīlā to be implicitly li-allā tattakhidhū... (so that you do not take...), while others consider an to be redundant and the phrase wa qulnā lahum (and We said to them) to be implicit, making the overall meaning: "And We said to them: do not take other than Me as a guardian."

In the next verse, in order to stir the emotions of the Children of Israel regarding gratitude for Divine blessings—especially the spiritual and intellectual blessing of the Divine Book—He addresses them, saying: "O descendants of those We carried [in the ship] with Noah" (ذُرِّيَّةَ مَنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ) [1]. Do not forget that "Indeed, he was a grateful servant" (إِنَّهُ كَانَ عَبْدًا شَكُورًا).

You, who are the descendants of Noah’s companions, why do you not follow the path of your faithful ancestors? Why step into the path of ingratitude?! "Shakur" is an intensive form meaning "one who is very grateful." The reason the Children of Israel are addressed as the descendants of those who were with Noah is perhaps because, according to well-known histories, Noah had three sons named "Shem," "Ham," and "Japheth," from whom the people of the earth spread after the Great Flood, including specifically the Children of Israel.

Undoubtedly, all prophets were grateful servants of Allah, but specific characteristics are mentioned in the Hadiths regarding Noah that made him worthy of this description. Among them is that whenever he put on clothes, drank water, ate food, or received any other blessing, he would immediately remember Allah and offer thanks.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) and Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read: "Noah used to recite this supplication every morning and evening:

Allahumma inni ushhiduka anna ma asbaha aw amsa bi min ni’matin fi dinin aw dunya fa minka, wahdaka la sharika lak, laka al-hamdu wa laka al-shukru biha ‘alayya hatta tarda, wa ba’da al-rida.

[1]: The phrase dhurriyyata man hamalnā ma’a Nūh is a "vocative phrase" (nida'), and it is implicitly yā dhurriyyata... (O descendants of...). The possibility that dhurriyyata is a substitute (badal) for wakīlā or the second object of tattakhidhū is very remote and does not align with the phrase innahu kāna ‘abdan shakūrā (reflect on this).

"O Allah, I call You to witness that whatever blessing reaches me morning or evening, whether religious or worldly, spiritual or material, it is all from You; You are One and have no partner. Praise is specifically Yours and gratitude belongs to You, such gratitude that You become pleased with me, and even after Your pleasure."

Then the Imam added: "This was the gratitude of Noah." [1]

Then, mentioning a portion of the eventful history of the Children of Israel, He says: "And We decreed for the Children of Israel in the Book: 'You will surely cause corruption on the earth twice, and you will surely reach [a state of] great arrogance'" (وَقَضَيْنَا إِلَىٰ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ فِي الْكِتَابِ لَتُفْسِدُنَّ فِي الْأَرْضِ مَرَّتَيْنِ وَلَتَعْلُنَّ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا).

"Qada" (decreed), although it has various meanings, here means "notified" or "announced."

The word "the earth" (al-ard), by the context of the subsequent verses, refers to the Holy Land of Palestine, where "al-Masjid al-Aqsa" is located.

In the following verses, He describes these two great corruptions and the events that occurred afterward as Divine punishment, saying: "So when the [time of] promise came for the first of them, We sent against you servants of Ours - those of great military might" to crush you as a punishment for your deeds (فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ أُولاهُما بَعَثْنا عَلَيْكُمْ عِباداً لَنا أُولِي بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ).

This warrior nation will attack you so fiercely that, to find your people,

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.

"they probed [even] through the homes" (فَجاسُوا خِلالَ الدِّيارِ).

"And it was a promise [to be] fulfilled" (وَكانَ وَعْداً مَفْعُولًا).

"Then We gave back to you a return victory over them" (ثُمَّ رَدَدْنا لَكُمُ الْكَرَّةَ عَلَيْهِمْ‌). "And We reinforced you with wealth and sons and made you more numerous in manpower" (وَأَمْدَدْناكُمْ بِأَمْوالٍ وَبَنِينَ وَجَعَلْنَاكُمْ أَكْثَرَ نَفِيرًا) [1].

These Divine favors are granted to you so that perhaps you might come to your senses, reform yourselves, abandon evils, and turn toward good, because: "If you do good, you do good for yourselves; and if you do evil, [you do it] to them [i.e., yourselves]" (إِنْ أَحْسَنْتُمْ أَحْسَنْتُمْ لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ وَإِنْ أَسَأْتُمْ فَلَها).

This is an eternal Sunnah (law): good and evil ultimately return to the human being. Every blow a person strikes is struck against their own body, and every service they render to another is, in reality, a service to themselves. But unfortunately, neither that punishment awakens you, nor does this renewed Divine blessing and mercy; you return to rebellion, take the path of oppression, tyranny, and transgression, create great corruption on the earth, and exceed all bounds in arrogance.

[1]: "Nafir" is a collective noun meaning a group of men. Some have said it is the plural of "nafar" and originally from the root nafr (on the pattern of 'afw), meaning to migrate or turn toward something; hence, a group that has the power to move toward something is called nafir.

Then the second Divine promise arrives: "So when the second promise came, [We sent them] to sadden your faces" (فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ الْآخِرَةِ لِيَسُوؤُا وُجُوهَكُمْ‌). They will bring such a calamity upon you that signs of grief and sorrow will pour from your faces.

They will even take your great temple, Jerusalem, from you "and enter the temple, as they entered it the first time" (وَ لِيَدْخُلُوا الْمَسْجِدَ كَما دَخَلُوهُ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ).

They will not be content with this; "and to destroy what they had taken over with [total] destruction" (وَ لِيُتَبِّرُوا ما عَلَوْا تَتْبِيراً).

Despite this, the doors of repentance and returning to Allah are not closed; still, "It may be that your Lord will have mercy upon you" (عَسى‌ رَبُّكُمْ أَنْ يَرْحَمَكُمْ‌).

"But if you return [to sin], We will return [to punishment]. And We have made Hell, for the disbelievers, a prison-bed" (وَ إِنْ عُدْتُمْ عُدْنا وَ جَعَلْنا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكافِرِينَ حَصِيراً) [1].

Key Points

1- The Two Great Historical Corruptions of the Children of Israel

In the above verses, mention is made of two social deviations of the Children of Israel that lead to corruption and arrogance. Following each of these two, Allah gave powerful and warrior men mastery over them to punish them severely and bring them to the retribution of their deeds.

Although the eventful history of the Children of Israel has many ups and downs, and many victories and defeats are seen in it, there is much discussion among commentators as to which of these events the Quran refers to. As an example, we provide several views below:

1- What can be gathered from the history of the Children of Israel is that the first person to attack them and destroy Jerusalem was Nebuchadnezzar, the King of Babylon. Jerusalem remained in that state for seventy years until the Jews rose up and rebuilt it. The second person to attack them was the Roman Emperor Vespasian, who commissioned his minister "Titus" for this task. He set out to destroy Jerusalem and weaken and kill the Children of Israel, which was about a hundred years before the birth of Christ.

Therefore, it is possible that the two events the Quran refers to are the same as those mentioned in the history of the Children of Israel, because other events in their history were not so severe as to completely dismantle their government. However, Nebuchadnezzar's attack completely crushed their power and glory. This continued until the time of "Cyrus," after which the Children of Israel regained power, and this situation continued until the Roman Emperor attacked them again and dismantled their government, and this wandering continued (until recently when, with the help of world-devouring and colonial powers, they scrambled to form a government for themselves) [1].

2- Al-Tabari narrates in his Tafsir that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: The first corruption refers to the killing of Prophet Zechariah and a group of other prophets, and the first promise refers to the promise of Divine vengeance against the Children of Israel by "Nebuchadnezzar." The second corruption refers to the rebellion they committed after "being freed by one of the Persian kings," and the second promise refers to the invasion by "Antiochus," the King of Rome.

This interpretation is somewhat compatible with the first, but both the narrator of this Hadith is not reliable, and the historical alignment of "Zechariah" and "John" with the history of "Nebuchadnezzar" and "Vespasian or Antiochus" is not established. Rather, according to some, Nebuchadnezzar was a contemporary of the Prophet Jeremiah or Daniel, and his uprising took place about six hundred years before the time of John. Therefore, how could Nebuchadnezzar's uprising have been to avenge the blood of John?

3- Others have said that Jerusalem was built once during the time of David and Solomon, and Nebuchadnezzar destroyed it, which is the first promise the Quran refers to. It was built and flourished again during the time of the Achaemenid kings and was destroyed by the Roman "Titus" (note that Titus or Tartuz mentioned above are compatible), and after that, it remained in ruins until the era of the second Caliph when that land was conquered by Muslims [1].

This interpretation also does not significantly contradict the two interpretations above.

4- In contrast to the above interpretations and others that are more or less aligned with them, we have another interpretation that Sayyid Qutb suggested in Fi Zilal al-Quran, which is completely different:

[1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 13, p. 46. [1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, Vol. 7, footnote p. 209, by the late scholar Sha'rani.

These two historical events had not occurred in the past or at the time of the Quran's revelation, but rather relate to the future. One of them was possibly their corruption at the beginning of Islam, which led to the uprising of the Muslims under the command of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) against them, and they were completely driven out of the Arabian Peninsula. The other relates to the uprising of the German Nazis led by Hitler against the Jews [1]. However, the problem with this interpretation is that in neither of these did the victorious people enter Jerusalem, let alone destroy it.

5- The final possibility is that some have suggested these two events relate to the occurrences after World War II and the formation of a party called "Zionism" and the establishment of a state called "Israel" in the heart of Islamic lands. The first corruption of the Children of Israel and their arrogance refers to this. The first vengeance refers to when the Islamic countries, upon becoming aware of this conspiracy, joined hands and were able to take Jerusalem and part of the cities and towns of Palestine out of the hands of the Jews, and Jewish influence was completely cut off from al-Aqsa Mosque.

The second corruption refers to the invasion by the Children of Israel relying on world-devouring colonial forces, the occupation of Islamic lands, and the taking of Jerusalem and al-Aqsa Mosque.

Accordingly, Muslims must await a second victory over the Children of Israel such that they take al-Aqsa Mosque out of their hands and completely cut off their influence from this Islamic land. This is what all the Muslims of the world are waiting for, and it is the promise of Divine victory and help for the Muslims [2].

[1]: Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 5, p. 308. [2]: From Maktab-e Islam magazine, No. 12 and Year 13, from the commentary discussion by Mr. Ibrahim Ansari.

And there are other interpretations whose mention is not particularly noteworthy.

Of course, in the fifth and fourth interpretations, the past tense verbs mentioned in the verse must all be understood in the future sense, and this meaning is not linguistically remote in Arabic when the verb follows conditional particles.

However, the apparent meaning of the verse "Then We gave back to you a return victory over them and We reinforced you with wealth and sons and made you more numerous in manpower" is that at least the first corruption of the Children of Israel and its vengeance occurred in the past.

Beyond all this, an important point to be considered here is that the apparent expression "We sent against you servants of Ours - those of great military might" (بَعَثْنا عَلَيْكُمْ عِباداً لَنا أُولِي بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ) indicates that the avenging group were men of faith who were worthy of the name "servants" ('ibad), the title "Ours" (lana), and also "We sent" (ba'athna). This meaning is not seen in many of the interpretations mentioned above.

Of course, it cannot be denied that the term ba'th (sending/arousing) is not always used for prophets and believers; it is also used for others in the Quran, such as what we read in the story of "Abel" and "Cain": "Then Allah sent a crow searching in the ground" (Surah Al-Ma'idah, Verse 31).

And this word has been used regarding earthly and heavenly punishments: "Say: He is the All-Capable to send upon you a punishment from above you or from beneath your feet" (Surah Al-An'am, 65).

Also, the word "'ibad" (servants) or "'abd" (servant) is occasionally used for individuals who are being censured, including the use of this word for sinners in Verse 58 of Surah Al-Furqan: "And sufficient is He as being Aware of the sins of His servants," and in Verse 27 of Surah Ash-Shura regarding rebels: "And if Allah had extended provision for His servants, they would have rebelled on the earth," and regarding wrongdoers and those who deviated from the principle of Monotheism in Verse 118 of Surah Al-Ma'idah: "If You punish them, they are indeed Your servants."

Yet, despite all these descriptions, it cannot be denied that unless a definitive context exists, the apparent meaning of the verses under discussion at first glance is that the avenging group are people of faith.

In any case, these verses tell us in summary that the Children of Israel twice committed great corruption and acted with arrogance, and Allah took severe revenge on them. The purpose of stating this subject is a lesson for them, for us, and for all humans, so that we know that oppressions and corruptions do not go unpunished before Allah. When we gain power, we should not forget the painful events that await us in the future, and we should learn this lesson from the histories of those who came before.

2- Whatever You Do, You Do to Yourselves!

In the above verses, this fundamental principle is mentioned: that your good and evil deeds return to you. Although the addressee of this sentence is apparently the Children of Israel, it is obvious that the Children of Israel have no exclusivity in this matter. This is an eternal law throughout human history, and history itself bears witness to it [1]. There were many who established ugly and improper traditions, oppressive laws, and inhumane innovations, and eventually, these caught up with them and their friends, and they fell into the very well they had dug for others.

[1]: In the above verses we read: in ahsantum ahsantum li-anfusikum wa in asa'tum fa-laha (if you do good, you do good for yourselves; and if you do evil, then for it), whereas normally it should say "'alayha" (against it), because doing evil is not to a person's benefit but to their detriment. This expression is either for the sake of harmony between the two parts of the sentence, or because the lam here means "exclusivity" rather than "benefit," and some commentators have also suggested that the lam here means "ila" (to).

Specifically, creating corruption on the earth, seeking superiority, and arrogance ('uluwwan kabira) are matters whose effects catch up with a person in this very world. For this reason, the Children of Israel were repeatedly afflicted with severe defeats, dispersion, and misery because they committed corruption on the earth.

Even now, a group from the Jewish people, namely the "Zionists," have taken action to usurp the lands of others, displace them from their homelands, and kill and destroy their children, and they have not even respected the sanctity of the House of Allah, Jerusalem.

In dealing with global issues, they have practically shown that they follow no law or standard. If, for example, a Palestinian fighter fires at them, they bomb refugee camps, kindergartens, and hospitals in return. In exchange for the killing of one of their own, they sometimes mow down hundreds of innocent people and blow up many houses! They do not consider themselves bound by any of the resolutions of international assemblies and openly and publicly trample them all. Undoubtedly, all this law-breaking, injustice, and inhumane acts are because they rely on a world-devouring power like "America." But it is also beyond doubt that this very people and group, from a moral and intellectual standpoint, are a complete example of criminality and the disregard of all human issues. This itself is an instance of corruption on the earth and seeking superiority and arrogance, and one must expect that again "servants of Ours - those of great military might" will overcome them and fulfill Allah's definitive promise regarding them.

3- Application of the Verses to Islamic History

In various narrations, we see that the above verses have also been applied to events in the history of Muslims. For instance, the first and second corruptions refer to the killing of Ali (عليه السلام) and the blow to the body of Imam Hasan (عليه السلام), or the meaning of "We sent against you servants of Ours - those of great military might" is Imam Mahdi (عليه السلام) (the Qa'im) and his companions.

In some others, it is taken to refer to a group that rises before the Mahdi (عليه السلام) [1]. It is very clear that the concept of these Hadiths is never the linguistic interpretation of the above verses, as these verses speak with total clarity about the Children of Israel. Rather, the intention is that similar programs of corruption and punishment occur in this Ummah. This is clear evidence that the above program, although mentioned regarding the Children of Israel, is a general law for all nations and peoples and a universal ongoing Sunnah throughout human history.

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 138.

Ayahs

إِنَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ يَهْدِى لِلَّتِى هِىَ أَقْوَمُ وَيُبَشِّرُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ أَجْرًا كَبِيرًا (٩)
Indeed this Quran guides to what is most upright, and gives the good news to the faithful who do righteous deeds that there is a great reward for them. (9)
وَأَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَعْتَدْنَا لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا (١٠)
As for those who do not believe in the Hereafter, We have prepared a painful punishment for them. (10)
وَيَدْعُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ بِٱلشَّرِّ دُعَآءَهُۥ بِٱلْخَيْرِ ۖ وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ عَجُولًا (١١)
Man prays for ill as [avidly as] he prays for good, and man is overhasty. (11)
وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ ءَايَتَيْنِ ۖ فَمَحَوْنَآ ءَايَةَ ٱلَّيْلِ وَجَعَلْنَآ ءَايَةَ ٱلنَّهَارِ مُبْصِرَةً لِّتَبْتَغُوا۟ فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَلِتَعْلَمُوا۟ عَدَدَ ٱلسِّنِينَ وَٱلْحِسَابَ ۚ وَكُلَّ شَىْءٍ فَصَّلْنَـٰهُ تَفْصِيلًا (١٢)
We made the night and the day two signs. Then We faded out the sign of the night, and made the sign of the day lightsome, so that you may seek from your Lord’s bounty and that you may know the number of years and calculation [of time], and We have elaborated everything in detail. (12)

Commentary

The Most Direct Path to Happiness

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the Children of Israel, their Heavenly Book (the Torah), their deviation from this Divine program, and the subsequent punishments they faced in this regard.

From that discussion, the discourse shifts to the Holy Qur'an—the Heavenly Book of Muslims and the final link in the chain of Divine Scriptures—stating: "Indeed, this Qur'an guides to that which is most upright and most enduring" (إِنَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ يَهْدِي لِلَّتِي هِيَ أَقْوَمُ).

"Aqwam" (أَقْوَمُ) is derived from the root "Qiyam" (standing/rising). Since a human being stands up and applies himself when he intends to pursue a continuous activity, "standing" has become a metaphor for the proper execution of affairs and readiness for action. Furthermore, "Istiqamah" (steadfastness/integrity), which is derived from the same root, and "Qayyim" (guardian/upright), also from this root, mean straight, direct, firm, and enduring.

Since "Aqwam" is a "superlative noun" (Ism al-Tafdil), it means straighter, more direct, and more enduring. Thus, the concept of the above verse is that "the Qur'an invites to a way that is the straightest, clearest, and most enduring of all ways."

It is clear and straight in terms of the beliefs it presents—beliefs that are lucid, comprehensible, free from any ambiguity or superstition; beliefs that call to action, mobilize human potential, and establish harmony between man and the laws of the natural world.

It is straighter and more direct in the sense that it creates a synthesis between the internal and external, belief and action, thought and program, inviting all toward "Allah."

It is straighter and more direct in terms of social and economic laws and the political systems that govern human society, nurturing both spiritual aspects and fostering evolution from a material perspective.

It avoids the extremes of excess and negligence in worship, as well as in moral programs that liberate man from any inclination toward greed, avarice, extravagance, wastefulness, stinginess, envy, weakness, or arrogance.

And finally, it is straighter and more direct in terms of the system of governance, which establishes justice and crushes oppression and oppressors.

Indeed, the Qur'an guides to a path and method that is the clearest, straightest, and most stable in all fields.

Here, a question arises: does the use of the "superlative" (Aqwam) imply that this uprightness and justice exist in the current religions and creeds of other nations, but are merely greater in the Qur'an? The answer becomes clear by considering a few points:

First: If the comparison is with other Heavenly religions, they were undoubtedly each a straight, clear, and enduring path in their own time and era. However, according to the law of evolution, when we reach the final stage—the stage of Finality (Khatamiyyah)—there exists a faith that is the straightest and most enduring of all.

Second: If the comparison is with non-Heavenly ideologies, the superlative still holds meaning because other schools of thought strive to possess a share of uprightness and clarity. However, when compared to their errors, and when their totality is compared to the Qur'an, it becomes clear that this faith is the most direct and clear of all, and most harmonious with the structure of the human soul, body, and spirit—and for this reason, it is the most enduring.

Third: As we have previously noted, the "superlative" does not always imply that the object of comparison necessarily possesses a share of that quality. As we read in the Holy Qur'an: "Is He then who guides to the truth more worthy to be followed, or he who finds not guidance unless he is guided?" (أَ فَمَنْ يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ أَحَقُّ أَنْ يُتَّبَعَ أَمَّنْ لا يَهِدِّي إِلَّا أَنْ يُهْدى‌) (Yunus: 35).

Additionally, it is necessary to note that considering one of the meanings of "Aqwam" is "more stable and enduring," and given that the object of comparison is not mentioned in the phrase—and as the rule goes, "the omission of the related object indicates generality"—it becomes clear that this verse is among those that allude to the issue of the Finality of Islam and the Prophet, as it states this faith is more stable and enduring than all others (reflect on this).

Then, since people's stances toward this direct Divine letter vary, He points to two distinct types of stances and their consequences, saying:

"Indeed, this Qur'an gives glad tidings to the believers who do righteous deeds that for them is a great reward" (وَ يُبَشِّرُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ الصَّالِحاتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ أَجْراً كَبِيراً).

"And that those who do not believe in the Hereafter and its Great Court (and naturally do not perform righteous deeds), We give them the 'glad tidings' that We have prepared for them a painful punishment" (وَ أَنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ أَعْتَدْنا لَهُمْ عَذاباً أَلِيماً).

The use of the term "glad tidings" (Bushra) regarding the believers is clear, but regarding the faithless and rebellious, it is actually a form of sarcasm, or it is glad tidings for the believers that their enemies will meet such a fate. [^1]

[^1]: Under verse 138 of Surah An-Nisa, we mentioned that "Bushra" is originally derived from "Basharah" meaning "face/skin," and it includes any news that affects the human face, making it either joyful or sorrowful.

Furthermore, He summarizes the reward of the believers in the short phrase "a great reward" (أَجْراً كَبِيراً) and the punishment of the faithless in the comprehensive phrase "a painful punishment" (عَذاباً أَلِيماً). The concept of both is so vast that it encompasses every kind of reward and punishment—spiritual and material, physical and psychological.

As for why, among the attributes of the people of Hell, He only points to "lack of belief in the Hereafter" and does not mention their deeds, it may be because belief in that Great Court, more than anything else, restrains a person from sins. Moreover, denial of the Resurrection leads back to the denial of God; for how is it possible for the Just and Wise God to leave the people of this world to themselves under the conditions we see, without another world existing? This is consistent neither with His Wisdom nor His Justice. Above all, since the discussion in the verse is about reward and punishment, it is fitting to the issue of the Hereafter and the Court of Justice of the Lord.

In the following verse, in connection with the previous discussion, He points to one of the major causes of lack of faith—which is the lack of sufficient study of affairs—stating: "Man prays for evil as he prays for good, due to his haste and lack of sufficient study" (وَ يَدْعُ الْإِنْسانُ بِالشَّرِّ دُعاءَهُ بِالْخَيْرِ). "For man is ever hasty" (وَ كانَ الْإِنْسانُ عَجُولًا).

"Dua" (prayer/call) here has a broad meaning that includes every kind of seeking and wanting, whether he asks with his tongue or practically rises and strives to obtain something.

In reality, man's hastiness to gain more benefits and his rush to acquire good and profit causes him not to examine all aspects of matters. Often, due to this haste, he cannot distinguish his true good; rather, rebellious whims and desires distort the face of reality in his eyes, and he pursues evil.

In this state, just as man requests good from God, due to his poor judgment, he requests evils from Him. And just as he strives for good, he pursues evil. This is a great calamity for mankind and a strange obstacle on the path to happiness.

How many are those who, due to their hastiness, have thrown themselves into dangerous precipices thinking they were going to a place of safety, or have stepped into detours imagining they were advancing toward the house of happiness, or have plunged into ugliness and misery under the impression that they were walking the path of glory? This is the bitter result of haste and precipitancy.

From what we have said, it is clear that the concept of the verse is limited neither to verbal prayer nor to practical seeking; rather, both are included in one comprehensive meaning. If some commentators have limited it to one part, there is no evidence for it.

Furthermore, if in some narrations only the issue of verbal prayer is mentioned, it is by way of providing an example, not the entire concept. As narrated in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"Know the path of your salvation and your destruction, lest you pray to Allah for something in which your destruction may lie, while you think your salvation is in it. Allah Almighty says: 'Man prays for evil as he prays for good, for man is hasty.'" [^1]

Therefore, the only way to reach good and happiness is for a person to examine all aspects with the utmost precision and alertness, far from any haste and precipitancy, in every step he takes. He must keep himself free from any prejudice or judgments mixed with whims and desires in choosing his path, seeking help from Allah in this way so that he may find the path of good and happiness and not step into precipices and detours.

The next verse speaks of the creation of night and day, the benefits and blessings of these two, and the existence of reckoning and accounting in the universe. This serves both as a proof of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the knowledge of God—completing the previous discussion on the Resurrection—and as a witness to the necessity of precision regarding the consequences of actions and the avoidance of haste. It says: "We have made the night and the day two signs [of Our power]" (وَ جَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ وَ النَّهارَ آيَتَيْنِ‌).

"Then We obscured the sign of the night, and We made the sign of the day illuminating" (فَمَحَوْنا آيَةَ اللَّيْلِ وَ جَعَلْنا آيَةَ النَّهارِ مُبْصِرَةً).

And We had two goals for this: "First, that you may seek bounty from your Lord" (لِتَبْتَغُوا فَضْلًا مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ‌).

Rest during the nights, and strive and work during the days, and in the light of that, benefit from Divine favors.

The other goal is: "That you may know the number of years and the account [of time-bound affairs]" (وَ لِتَعْلَمُوا عَدَدَ السِّنِينَ وَ الْحِسابَ‌).

"And We have explained everything in detail" (وَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ فَصَّلْناهُ تَفْصِيلًا).

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 141.

So that no room for doubt remains.

There is a discussion among commentators as to whether "the sign of the night" and "the sign of the day" refer to the night and day themselves—each being a sign from the Lord—or if "the sign of the night" refers to the moon and "the sign of the day" refers to the sun. [^1] However, precision in the verse itself shows that the first interpretation is correct, for the expression "We have made the night and the day two signs" is evidence that each is considered a sign to prove the existence of God. The meaning of "obscuring the sign of the night" is that the dark and gloomy veils of the night are erased and vanished under a covering of the light and brightness of the day, and what was hidden in the heart of the night becomes manifest in the light of the day.

And if in some other verses of the Qur'an (Surah Yunus, verse 5), the sun and the moon are made the means of identifying the year, month, and reckoning, it does not contradict what we said above, because the emergence of accounting and reckoning in the program of human life can be attributed to both the night and day and to the sun and the moon, as both are interconnected.

In Nahj al-Balagha, in the "Sermon of the Ghosts" (Khutbat al-Ashbah), while describing the signs of Allah's greatness, we read:

"He made its sun an illuminating sign for its day, and its moon an obscured sign for its night, and He made them run in the paths of their courses, and He ordained their movement in the stages of their progress, to distinguish between night and day through them, and so that the number of years and the reckoning might be known by their measures." [^1]

These expressions also do not contradict what was stated in the first interpretation, because as we said, the emergence of reckoning and the numbering of years can be attributed to both the night and day and to the sun and the moon, as both are related.

Key Points

1. Is Man Hasty by Nature?

Not only regarding haste and precipitancy, as described in the above verses, but in several other instances in the Qur'an, criticisms are seen regarding the title "Man" (al-Insan), such as being "unjust" (Zalum), "ignorant" (Jahul), "ungrateful" (Kafur), "rebellious" (Tughyan-gar), "impatient/anxious" (Halu'), "arrogant" (Maghrur), and the like.

These expressions sometimes raise the question: how can these matters be reconciled with the understanding we have from the Qur'an regarding the pure nature (Fitrah) of man and his being a carrier of the Divine Spirit?

In other words, from the perspective of the Islamic worldview, man is a very exalted being, to the extent that he is worthy of the station of the Vicegerency of Allah (Khalifatullah) and God's representation on earth; he is the teacher of the angels and superior to them. How is this consistent with the aforementioned reproaches?

The answer to this question can be given in one sentence: all that status, personality, and value of man is conditional upon one requirement, and that is "upbringing under the supervision of Divine leaders." Otherwise, man grows like a wild plant, plunging into whims and desires, losing the vast potential capital he possesses, and negative aspects manifest in his existence.

[^1]: In the first case, the genitive construction is "explanatory" (Idafah Bayaniyyah), and in the second case, it is "possessive/specific" (Idafah Ikhtisasiyyah). [^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 91 (Khutbat al-Ashbah).

Therefore, if the said condition is fulfilled, all the positive aspects mentioned in the Qur'an regarding man will bear fruit in his existence. If this condition is not met, the mentioned negative aspects will appear. Thus, we read in verses 19 to 24 of Surah al-Ma'arij: "Indeed, man was created anxious: when evil touches him, he is fretful; and when good touches him, he is withholding—except the observers of prayer, those who are constant in their prayer."

We also explained this subject in more detail under verse 12 of Surah Yunus (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 8, p. 239).

2. The Calamity of Precipitancy!

Fiery passion for a subject, shallow and limited thoughts, and sometimes the dominance of whims and desires over man, along with excessive optimism toward a matter, are factors of haste and precipitancy in actions. Since shallow and preliminary examinations are often insufficient to realize the truth of a matter and its profit or loss, haste and nervousness in performing tasks usually lead to regret, loss, and remorse.

To the extent that we read in the above verses that sometimes haste causes man to pursue evils as fast as he pursues goods! The bitterness, failures, and calamities that have afflicted man throughout history due to haste and precipitancy are more than can be counted, and we ourselves have experienced examples of it throughout life and tasted its bitter fruits!

The opposite of haste is "Thabat" (firmness) and "Ta'anni" (deliberation)—that is, pausing and performing a task with thought, reflection, and examination of all aspects.

In a hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated that he said: "Haste destroys people; if people were to act with deliberation, no one would be destroyed." [^1]

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "With deliberation comes safety, and with haste comes regret!" [^2]

And the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله also says: "Deliberation and the abandonment of precipitancy are from Allah, and haste is from Satan!" [^3]

Of course, in Islamic narrations, we have a chapter regarding "haste in good deeds." For instance, in a hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Indeed, Allah loves that which is hastened of good." [^4]

There are many narrations in this regard, where the meaning of "haste" in these narrations is "speed" (Sur'ah) as opposed to negligence, undue delays, and procrastination (putting things off until tomorrow), which often cause problems and obstacles to arise in affairs.

The witness to this is a hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام in this same chapter: "Whoever intends to do something good should hasten it, for in everything that is delayed, Satan has a look [an opportunity to intervene]." [^5]

Therefore, it must be said: "Speed and diligence in affairs, yes; but haste and precipitancy, no!"

In other words, blameworthy haste is that which occurs during the examination and study of the aspects of a task and its recognition. However, praiseworthy speed and haste is that which occurs after the necessary decision-making, where there is no delay in execution. Thus, we read in narrations: "Hasten in good deeds," meaning after the goodness of a task is proven, there is no longer room for negligence.

3. The Role of Numbering and Accounting in Human Life

The entire universe revolves around the axis of accounting and numbers, and none of the systems of this world are without calculation. Naturally, man, who is a part of this whole, cannot live without accounting and reckoning.

For this reason, in various verses of the Qur'an, one of the blessings of God is the existence of the moon and sun, or night and day, as a factor for creating order and accounting in human life, because chaos and the absence of accounting and order in life are factors of annihilation and destruction.

Interestingly, in the above verses, two benefits are mentioned for the blessing of night and day: one is "seeking the bounty of Allah" (ibtigha' fadl Allah), which in the Qur'an usually means useful and valuable work and earning; and the other is "knowing the number of years and the account." Perhaps mentioning these two together is evidence that "seeking the bounty of Allah" is not possible without the use of "accounting and reckoning."

Perhaps this statement was not as clear and obvious in past times as it is today. But in today's world, which is a world of statistics, numbers, and figures—where alongside every economic, social, political, military, scientific, and cultural organization, there exists a statistical organization—one can well understand the depth of this Qur'anic allusion and know that the Qur'an not only does not become old with the passage of time but becomes fresher the more time passes. [^1]

(1, 2, 3, 5) Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 129. [^4]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, Book of Faith and Disbelief, Chapter on Hastening Good Deeds. [^1]: Under verse 5 of Surah Yunus, we also had a detailed explanation in this regard (refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 8, p. 228).

Ayahs

وَكُلَّ إِنسَـٰنٍ أَلْزَمْنَـٰهُ طَـٰٓئِرَهُۥ فِى عُنُقِهِۦ ۖ وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ كِتَـٰبًا يَلْقَىٰهُ مَنشُورًا (١٣)
We have attached every person’s omen to his neck, and We shall bring it out for him on the Day of Resurrection as a book that he will find wide open. (13)
ٱقْرَأْ كِتَـٰبَكَ كَفَىٰ بِنَفْسِكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيبًا (١٤)
‘Read your book! Today your soul suffices as your own reckoner.’ (14)
مَّنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۗ وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًا (١٥)
Whoever is guided is guided only for [the good of] his own soul, and whoever goes astray, goes astray only to its detriment. No bearer shall bear another’s burden. We do not punish [any community] until We have sent [it] an apostle. (15)

Commentary

Four Important Islamic Principles

Since the previous verses discussed matters related to "Resurrection" and "Accountability," the verses under discussion address the issue of the "accounting of human deeds" and its nature on the Day of Judgment, stating: "We have fastened every man’s deeds to his neck."

(وَ كُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ‌).

"Tā’ir" literally means "bird," but here it refers to a practice common among the Arabs who used birds to seek omens of good or bad luck, drawing conclusions from the direction of their flight.

For instance, if a bird flew to their right, they took it as a good omen, and if it flew to the left, they took it as a bad omen.

Consequently, this word is often used to mean a bad omen (tiyarah), whereas "tafa’ul" is more commonly used for seeking a good omen.

In the verses of the Quran, "tatayyur" (taking an omen) has repeatedly appeared in the sense of a bad omen, such as:

وَ إِنْ تُصِبْهُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَطَّيَّرُوا بِمُوسى‌ وَ مَنْ مَعَهُ‌: "Whenever a calamity befell the people of Pharaoh, they ascribed it to the ill-fortune of Moses and those with him!" (Al-A'raf: 131). And in Surah An-Naml, verse 47, we read: قالُوا اطَّيَّرْنا بِكَ وَ بِمَنْ مَعَكَ‌:

The polytheists of the people of Salih عليه السلام said to this great Prophet: "We augur ill of you and those with you!" In Islamic hadiths, we read that "tatayyur" is forbidden, and the way to combat it is identified as "reliance upon Allah" (tawakkul).

In any case, "Tā’ir" in the verse under discussion also refers to this meaning, or to the concept of "fate and destiny," which is close in scope to the issue of good and bad omens.

The Quran, in reality, says: Good and bad omens, and auspicious or inauspicious destinies, are nothing but your own deeds which are hung around your necks!

The expression "Alzamnahu" (We have made it inseparable from him) and the expression "fi 'unuqihi" (in his neck) both serve as evidence that human deeds and their consequences in this world and the Hereafter are never separated from him, and he must be responsible and accountable for them in all circumstances. Whatever exists is the deed, and the rest is mere talk.

Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that the word "Tā’ir" is applied to human deeds because good and bad deeds seemingly take flight from one's being like a bird; hence, they are called "Tā’ir."

Commentators have mentioned several other possibilities for the meaning of "Tā’ir" in the verse under discussion: including "Tā’ir" meaning "man's portion of good and evil," or meaning "proof and guide," or meaning the "record of deeds," or meaning "blessing and misfortune."

However, some of these interpretations return to the same meaning we mentioned at the beginning, while others are quite distant from the concept of the verse.

The Quran then adds: "And on the Day of Resurrection, We shall bring out for him a book which he will find spread open before him" (وَ نُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ كِتاباً يَلْقاهُ مَنْشُوراً).

It is clear that the "book" refers to nothing other than the record of human deeds—the same record that exists in this world and in which his deeds are registered, except that here it is hidden and concealed, while there it is spread out and open.

The expression "nukhriju" (We bring out) and also the expression "manshura" (spread open) refer to this same meaning: that what is hidden and sealed here will be revealed and opened there.

We will discuss the record of deeds and its reality under these same verses.

At this moment, he is told: "Read your own record!" (اقرأ كتابك).

"Sufficient is your own self this day as a reckoner against you!" (كَفى‌ بِنَفْسِكَ الْيَوْمَ عليك حَسِيباً).

Meaning, the matters are so clear and evident, and the evidence and documents so vivid, that there is no room for debate. Anyone who looks at this record of deeds can pass judgment, even if the person is a "criminal," because this record—as will be explained—is a collection of the effects of the deed itself, or the deeds themselves. Thus, it is not something that can be denied.

If I hear my own voice from a tape recorder, or see an accurate photograph of myself while performing a good or bad deed, can I deny it? The quality of the formation of the record of deeds on the Day of Resurrection is even more vivid and precise than this.

The next verse states four fundamental and basic rulings regarding the issue of accounting and the recompense of deeds.

1- First, it says: "Whoever accepts guidance, he is guided only for [the benefit of] his own soul, and its result returns to him" (مَنِ اهْتَدى‌ فَإِنَّما يَهْتَدِي لِنَفْسِهِ‌).

2- "And whoever goes astray, he goes astray only to his own loss"; its grim consequences will afflict him (وَ مَنْ ضَلَّ فَإِنَّما يَضِلُّ عَلَيْها).

We read similar rulings to these two in verse 7 of this same Surah.

3- "No bearer of burdens shall bear the burden of another," and no one will be punished for the crime of another (وَ لا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى‌).

"Wizr" means a heavy load, and it also means responsibility, because that too is considered a heavy spiritual load upon a person's shoulders. If a minister is called a "wazir," it is because a heavy burden from the ruler or the people has been placed upon his shoulders.

Of course, this general law that "no one bears the burden of another" does not contradict what was mentioned in verse 25 of Surah An-Nahl, which says: "The misleaders also bear the burdens of responsibility of those they misled."

This is because, by taking action to mislead others, they are the doers of that sin, or are considered as the doers. Therefore, in reality, it is the burden of their own sins that they carry. In other words, the "cause" here is in the position of the "direct actor."

Similarly, the numerous narrations regarding good and bad traditions (sunnah), which imply that whoever establishes a good or bad tradition shares in the reward or punishment of those who act upon it, do not conflict with what we said above. This is because the "establisher of the tradition" is actually part of the complete cause of the deed and a partner in the agency.

4- Finally, it states the fourth ruling in this manner: "And We would never punish any person or people until We have sent a messenger to them" to fully explain their duties and complete the proof (وَ ما كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّى نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًا).

There is discussion among commentators as to whether "punishment" (adhab) here refers to any type of punishment in this world and the Hereafter, or specifically to "punishment of eradication" (i.e., destructive punishments like the Flood of Noah). However, without a doubt, the literal meaning of the verse is absolute and includes every type of punishment.

Furthermore, there is debate among commentators as to whether this ruling is specific to those religious matters that can only be understood through transmitted evidence (naqli), or whether it includes all matters, both fundamental and subsidiary, rational ('aqli) and transmitted.

However, if we wish to act upon the literal meaning of the verse, which is absolute, we must say it includes all rational and transmitted rulings regarding the principles and branches of religion. The implication of this is that even in matters where the intellect independently perceives their goodness or badness (such as the goodness of justice and the badness of oppression), Allah, out of His grace, will not punish anyone until the divine prophets come and confirm and strengthen the judgment of the intellect through transmitted judgment (reflect carefully).

But since this seems very unlikely—because independent rational judgments do not need religious explanation, and the judgment of the intellect is sufficient to complete the proof in such cases—we have no choice but to exclude independent rational judgments from the verse.

Or, if we do not exclude them, we must take "punishment" in this sentence to mean "punishment of eradication," the result of which is: Allah, out of His grace, does not destroy oppressors and the deviant until He sends prophets to show all paths of happiness, even confirming independent rational judgments with religious explanation, thereby completing the proof from both sides—intellect and revelation (again, reflect carefully).

Key Points

1- Tafa’ul and Tatayyur (Good and Bad Omens)

Taking "good" and "bad" omens has existed and continues to exist among all nations. It seems its source was the lack of access to realities and ignorance of the actual causes of events.
