In any case, undoubtedly these two have no natural effect, but they do have a psychological effect: a good omen creates hope, while a bad omen causes despair, hopelessness, and weakness.

Since Islam always welcomes positive matters, it has not forbidden good omens, but it has severely condemned bad omens.

In some narrations, it has even been considered on the verge of polytheism (shirk). It is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "Taking bad omens (and considering them effective in one's destiny alongside Allah) is a type of shirk against Allah."

We have discussed this in detail under verse 131 of Surah Al-A'raf [1]. Interestingly, in many cases, Islam has placed these types of imaginary and superstitious concepts into a correct and constructive channel and utilized them.

For example, regarding what is common among the masses who say a certain spouse was "lucky" or "unlucky," and that from the day she entered someone's house and became his wife, such and such happened—which in this form is certainly nothing more than a superstition—Islam has given it a constructive educational form. In a hadith from Imam As-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Among the misfortunes of a woman is the heaviness of her dowry and the excessiveness of her maintenance..." [2] And in another hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"As for the house, its misfortune is its narrowness and the wickedness of its neighbors" [3].

Observe carefully how He has employed the same terms used in people's superstitious concepts for real and constructive concepts, guiding the thought and mind that was going astray back to the right path.

[1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 6, page 316 onwards. [2]: Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 3, page 104. [3]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, page 680.

We conclude this discussion with a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله which confirms what we have said:

"O Allah, there is no good except Your good, and no omen except Your omen, and there is no Lord besides You."

2- The Extraordinary Record of Human Deeds

In many verses of the Quran and narrations, the record of human deeds is mentioned. From the sum of these verses and narrations, it is understood that all human actions, with all their details, are recorded in a book. On the Day of Resurrection, if a person is righteous, his record of deeds will be given to his right hand, and if he is wicked, his record of deeds will be given to his left hand.

In Surah Al-Haqqah, we read: فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ فَيَقُولُ هاؤُمُ اقْرَؤُا كِتابِيَهْ: "Then as for him who is given his record in his right hand, he will say [with pride and honor]: 'Here, read my record!'" (Al-Haqqah: 19). وَ أَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِشِمالِهِ فَيَقُولُ يا لَيْتَنِي لَمْ أُوتَ كِتابِيَهْ: "But as for him who is given his record in his left hand, he will say: 'I wish that I had not been given my record!'" (Al-Haqqah: 25).

And in Surah Al-Kahf, verse 49, we read: وَ وُضِعَ الْكِتابُ فَتَرَى الْمُجْرِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا فِيهِ وَ يَقُولُونَ يا وَيْلَتَنا ما لِهذَا الْكِتابِ لا يُغادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَ لا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّا أَحْصاها وَ وَجَدُوا ما عَمِلُوا حاضِراً وَ لا يَظْلِمُ رَبُّكَ أَحَداً: "And the record [of deeds] will be placed [open], and you will see the criminals fearful of that which is therein, and they will say: 'Woe to us! What kind of book is this that leaves out nothing small or great except that it has recorded it?' And they will find all that they did present [before them], and your Lord treats no one with injustice."

In a hadith regarding the verse under discussion (اقْرَأْ كِتابَكَ ...), we read from Imam As-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"On that day, the servant will remember all that he did and all that was written against him, as if he had done it that very hour! Therefore, the criminals will cry out: 'What kind of book is this that has omitted nothing small or great?'" [1]

Here the question arises: What is this record? And what is it like?

Undoubtedly, it is not of the nature of ordinary books, notebooks, or letters. Therefore, some commentators have said that this record of deeds is nothing other than the "human soul," in which the effects of all deeds are recorded [2], because every action we perform inevitably leaves an effect on our soul and spirit.

Or, this record of deeds consists of our body parts, even our skin, and beyond that, the earth, air, and space in which we perform our deeds. For our deeds, besides being imprinted on our bodies and every particle of our being, are reflected in the air and the earth.

Although these effects are not perceptible or understandable to us in this world, they undoubtedly exist, and on the day when we gain a new vision, we will see and read all of them.

The expression "reading" should never divert us from what was mentioned in the above interpretation, because reading also has a broad concept that encompasses every kind of observation in its wide sense. For example, in everyday expressions, we sometimes say: "I read in his eyes what he intended," or "I read the rest of his actions from what he did." Similarly, the term "reading" is commonly used today for X-rays or scans taken of patients.

For this reason, we read in the above verses that the lines of this record of deeds are in no way deniable, because they are the actual and existential (takwini) effects of the deed itself, just like a person's recorded voice, a photograph taken of him, or his fingerprint.

3- The Innocent and the Guilty are Not Burned Together!

Contrary to the common saying among the masses that "when the fire starts, the dry and the wet burn together," in the logic of reason and the teachings of the Prophets, no innocent person will be punished for the crime of another. In all the cities of Lot, there was one believing family; when Allah punished that deviant and polluted people, He saved that one family.

The above verses also explicitly state: "وَ لا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى‌".

Therefore, if in some unreliable hadiths something is seen contrary to this general law of Islam, it must certainly be set aside or reinterpreted, such as the narration that says: "The deceased is punished because of the weeping and lamentation of his survivors" (it is possible that "punished" here does not mean divine punishment, but rather the distress his soul feels upon becoming aware of his relatives' agitation).

It also becomes clear that the belief of those who say the children of disbelievers will be in the fire of Hell along with their fathers is not an Islamic belief, because no child is punished for the sin of the father or mother. For this reason, we have stated elsewhere that even illegitimate children personally have no sin, and the doors of happiness and salvation—if they so desire—are open to them, even though they may have a difficult educational background.

4- The Principle of Innocence (Bara'ah) and the Verse "وَ ما كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ..."

In the science of Usul (Principles of Jurisprudence), in the discussions on "Bara'ah" (Exemption), this verse is used as evidence. This is because the minimum concept of the verse is that in matters where the intellect is unable to perceive, Allah does not punish anyone without sending messengers—meaning the explanation of rulings and duties. This is evidence for the negation of punishment and penalty in cases where there is no explanation, and the Principle of Bara'ah says nothing other than that punishment without explanation (al-iqab bila bayan) is not valid.

As for those who have said that "punishment" in the above verse refers only to the "punishment of eradication"—meaning destructive punishments like the Flood of Noah—there is no evidence for it. Rather, as we said, the absolute nature of the verse negates this and encompasses every kind of punishment and penalty.

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, page 144. [2]: Tafsir al-Safi.

Ayahs

وَإِذَآ أَرَدْنَآ أَن نُّهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً أَمَرْنَا مُتْرَفِيهَا فَفَسَقُوا۟ فِيهَا فَحَقَّ عَلَيْهَا ٱلْقَوْلُ فَدَمَّرْنَـٰهَا تَدْمِيرًا (١٦)
And when We desire to destroy a town We command its affluent ones [to obey Allah]. But they commit transgression in it, and so the word becomes due against it, and We destroy it utterly. (16)
وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِنَ ٱلْقُرُونِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ نُوحٍ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِرَبِّكَ بِذُنُوبِ عِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرًۢا بَصِيرًا (١٧)
How many generations We have destroyed since Noah! Your Lord is sufficient as [a witness who is] well aware and percipient of His servants’ sins. (17)

Commentary

The Four Stages of Divine Punishment

Following the final verse of the previous discussion, which pointed out: "We never punish an individual or a group without first sending messengers and explaining Our commands," the first verse under discussion pursues this same fundamental principle in another form, stating:

"And when We intend to destroy a city, We first issue Our commands to its 'mutrafin' (the opulent ones) and their leaders; then, when they rise in opposition and depart from obedience, becoming deserving of punishment, We utterly crush and destroy them" (وَ إِذا أَرَدْنا أَنْ نُهْلِكَ قَرْيَةً أَمَرْنا مُتْرَفِيها فَفَسَقُوا فِيها فَحَقَّ عَلَيْهَا الْقَوْلُ فَدَمَّرْناها تَدْمِيراً) [^1]. Although many commentators have offered various possibilities in interpreting this verse, in our view, according to its apparent meaning, the verse has only one clear interpretation, which is:

Allah never calls anyone to account or punishes them before completing the proof (atmam al-hujjah) and explaining His commands. Rather, He first proceeds to explain His commands; if the people enter through the door of obedience and accept them, so much the better, for therein lies their worldly and otherworldly felicity. But if they rise in wickedness (fisq) and opposition and trample everything underfoot, it is here that the decree of punishment regarding them is realized, followed by destruction.

If we look closely at the verse, four distinct stages are described for this program:

1- The stage of commands (and prohibitions). 2- The stage of wickedness (fisq) and opposition. 3- The stage of deserving punishment. 4- The stage of destruction.

All these stages are linked to one another with the conjunction "fa" (denoting sequence and result).

Here, the question arises: why are the "mutrafin" the only ones mentioned as being commanded?

("Mutrafin" is derived from the root "taraf," meaning abundant blessings—referring to those living in luxury and the wealthy who are heedless of God).

In response to this question, one key point is helpful: in many societies (referring to unhealthy societies), the mutrafin are the social leaders, and others are their followers and subordinates.

Furthermore, this expression points to another nuance: the source of most social corruptions is those wealthy individuals heedless of God who are drowned in luxury, comfort, and desire. Every reformative, humane, and moral melody sounds discordant to their ears. For this reason, they have always stood in the front line against the Prophets, viewing their call—which was in favor of justice, equity, and support for the oppressed—as being against their own interests.

For these reasons, they are specifically mentioned because this group is the primary root of corruption.

Additionally, "dammarna" and "tadmin" are derived from the root "damar," meaning destruction.

In any case, the above verse is a warning to all people of faith to be vigilant, not to hand over their governance to the mutrafin and the wealthy who are intoxicated by lust, and not to follow them, for they will ultimately lead their society to ruin and destruction.

The next verse refers to examples of this issue as a general principle, saying: "And how many generations have We destroyed after Noah (according to this same Sunnah)!" (وَ كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا مِنَ الْقُرُونِ مِنْ بَعْدِ نُوحٍ‌). Then it adds: It is not the case that the injustice, oppression, and sin of an individual or a population remain hidden from the sharp eye of Allah's knowledge; "Sufficient is your Lord as All-Aware and All-Seeing of the sins of His servants" (وَ كَفى‌ بِرَبِّكَ بِذُنُوبِ عِبادِهِ خَبِيراً بَصِيراً).

"Qurun" is the plural of "qarn," meaning a population living in a single era, and subsequently, it has been applied to an entire era itself.

Regarding how many years a "qarn" (century/generation) consists of, various opinions have been given; some consider it forty years, some eighty, some a hundred, and finally, some have considered it one hundred and twenty years. However, it is self-evident that this is a conventional matter that varies according to agreements. But what is common in our era is that "qarn" is applied to one hundred years [^2]. The fact that "generations after Noah" are specifically emphasized may be because human life before Noah was very simple, and such differences—especially the division of societies into "mutraf" (opulent) and "mustad'af" (oppressed)—existed less, and for this reason, they were less frequently subjected to divine punishments.

The mention of "Khabir" (All-Aware) and "Basir" (All-Seeing) together indicates that "Khabir" refers to being aware of intentions and beliefs, while "Basir" refers to being seeing regarding actions. Therefore, Allah is informed of both the internal motives of people's deeds and the deeds themselves, and such a Being never permits any injustice or oppression toward anyone, and no one's right is wasted in His governance.

[^1]: "Qawl" (the word/decree), although it has a broad meaning, in such instances refers to the decree of punishment. [^2]: We also alluded to this subject in the commentary of Surah Yunus, verse 13 (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 8, p. 243).

Ayahs

مَّن كَانَ يُرِيدُ ٱلْعَاجِلَةَ عَجَّلْنَا لَهُۥ فِيهَا مَا نَشَآءُ لِمَن نُّرِيدُ ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا لَهُۥ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَىٰهَا مَذْمُومًا مَّدْحُورًا (١٨)
Whoever desires this transitory life, We expedite for him therein whatever We wish, for whomever We desire. Then We appoint hell for him, to enter it, blameful and spurned. (18)
وَمَنْ أَرَادَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ وَسَعَىٰ لَهَا سَعْيَهَا وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَانَ سَعْيُهُم مَّشْكُورًا (١٩)
Whoever desires the Hereafter and strives for it with an endeavour worthy of it, should he be faithful,—the endeavour of such will be well-appreciated. (19)
كُلًّا نُّمِدُّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ وَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ مِنْ عَطَآءِ رَبِّكَ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ عَطَآءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُورًا (٢٠)
To these and to those—to all We extend the bounty of your Lord, and the bounty of your Lord is not confined. (20)
ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَلَلْـَٔاخِرَةُ أَكْبَرُ دَرَجَـٰتٍ وَأَكْبَرُ تَفْضِيلًا (٢١)
Observe how We have given some of them an advantage over some others; yet the Hereafter is surely greater in respect of ranks and greater in respect of relative merit. (21)

Commentary

The Life Paths of Seekers of This World and the Hereafter

Since the previous verses spoke of the opposition of the rebellious against Divine commands and their subsequent destruction, the verses under discussion point to the real cause of this defiance and disobedience, which is the love of this world. It says: "Whoever desires only this fleeting life of the material world, We hasten for him therein what We will for whom We please; then We have settled for him Hell, which he will [enter to] burn, censured and banished [from God's mercy]" (مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعاجِلَةَ عَجَّلْنا لَهُ فِيها ما نَشاءُ لِمَنْ نُرِيدُ ثُمَّ جَعَلْنا لَهُ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلاها مَذْمُوماً مَدْحُوراً).

"Al-’Ajilah" (العاجلة) means fleeting blessings or the transitory world.

It is noteworthy that it does not say everyone who pursues the world will attain whatever they desire; rather, it sets two conditions: First, they will only reach a portion of what they want—that which We will (ما نَشاءُ).

Second: Not everyone will even reach this amount; rather, only a group of them will attain a portion of worldly goods—those whom We will (لِمَنْ نُرِيدُ).

Thus, neither do all world-seekers attain the world, nor do those who attain it reach everything they desire. Daily life clearly shows us these two limitations: how many there are who run day and night and reach nowhere, and how many have long-reaching aspirations in this world but obtain only a small fraction of them.

This is a warning to the world-seekers: if you imagine that by selling the Hereafter for this world you will achieve your entire goal, you have made a great mistake; rather, sometimes you access nothing, and sometimes only a little.

Fundamentally, the scope of human desires is so vast that it cannot be satiated by the limitations of the material world; if the entire world were given to one person, they would often still not be satisfied.

As for those who strive and reach nothing, it may be for various reasons: either because there is still hope for their awakening and salvation, and Allah shows them kindness, or because if they were to reach a certain status, they would rebel so greatly that they would make life unbearable for Allah's creation.

"Yasla" (يصلى) is derived from the root "Sala" (صلى), meaning to kindle a fire and to burn in fire, and the second meaning is intended here.

It is significant that the punishment for this group, while being the fire of Hell, is emphasized with two terms: "Madhmum" (مذموم) and "Madhur" (مدحور). The first means being blamed and censured, and the second means being kept far from the mercy of Allah.

In reality, the fire of Hell is their physical punishment, while being censured and banished is their spiritual punishment, for the Resurrection is both physical and spiritual, and its punishment and reward are likewise in both aspects.

Then, it proceeds to describe the state of the second group, so that through the principle of contrast—as is the method of the Quran—the matter becomes clearer. It says: "But whoever desires the Hereafter and exerts the effort due to it while he is a believer—it is those whose effort will be appreciated [by Allah]" (وَ مَنْ أَرادَ الْآخِرَةَ وَ سَعى‌ لَها سَعْيَها وَ هُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَأُولئِكَ كانَ سَعْيُهُمْ مَشْكُوراً).

Therefore, three fundamental matters are conditions for reaching eternal felicity:

1- The human will, specifically a will directed toward eternal life, and not toward fleeting pleasures, unstable blessings, and purely material goals. It must be backed by a high ambition and a lofty spirit that frees one from accepting any form of worldly attachment or dependency.

2- This will should not exist in a weak and feeble form within the realm of thought and soul; rather, it must set every particle of the human being into motion, exerting one's utmost effort in this path (note that the word "Sa'yaha" (سعيها), mentioned for emphasis, shows that he performs the ultimate effort and striving necessary to reach the Hereafter and leaves nothing undone).

3- All of this must be coupled with "Faith"—a firm and stable faith—because decision and effort bear fruit only when they spring from a correct motive, and that motive can be nothing other than faith in Allah.

It is true that striving for the Hereafter will not exist without faith, and thus the concept of faith is inherent within it; however, since faith is a fundamental principle and the primary foundation in this path, [the Quran] does not settle for that level of implicit indication and explicitly states faith as a condition.

It is noteworthy that regarding the world-seekers, He says: "We have settled Hell for them," but regarding the lovers of the Hereafter, He says: "Their effort will be appreciated (Mashkur)," meaning thanked and valued by the Lord.

This expression is much more comprehensive and loftier than saying their reward is Paradise, because the thanks and appreciation of anyone is proportionate to their personality and the vastness of their being, not just the size of the deed performed. On this account, the thanks and appreciation of Allah is proportionate to His Infinite Essence; all types of material and spiritual blessings, and whatever can or cannot be imagined by us, are included within it.

Although some commentators have taken "Mashkur" to mean "multiplied reward" [1] or "acceptance of the deed" [2], it is clear that "Mashkur" has a broader meaning than all of these.

Here, a misconception might arise that worldly blessings will only be the portion of world-seekers and that seekers of the Hereafter will be deprived of them. The next verse responds to this misconception: "To each [category] We extend—to these and to those—from the gift of your Lord" (كُلًّا نُمِدُّ هؤُلاءِ وَ هَؤُلاءِ مِنْ عَطاءِ رَبِّكَ‌).

"For the gift of your Lord is not restricted from anyone," and the unbeliever, the Christian, the believer, and the Muslim all receive their sustenance from the table of His bounty (وَ ما كانَ عَطاءُ رَبِّكَ مَحْظُوراً).

"Numiddu" (نمد) is from the root "Imdad," meaning to increase or extend help.

The next verse states a fundamental principle in this regard: Just as the difference in efforts in this world causes a difference in benefits, this same principle is fully dominant in the affairs of the Hereafter, with the difference that this world is limited and its differences are limited, but the Hereafter is unlimited and its differences are also unlimited. It says:

"See how We have favored some of them over others [due to the difference in their striving], but the Hereafter is greater in degrees and greater in favor" (انْظُرْ كَيْفَ فَضَّلْنا بَعْضَهُمْ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ وَ لَلْآخِرَةُ أَكْبَرُ دَرَجاتٍ وَ أَكْبَرُ تَفْضِيلًا).

It might be said that we see individuals in this world who obtain vast benefits without effort or striving, but undoubtedly these are exceptional cases and cannot be considered against the general principle of effort and its relationship with the level of success; such deviant gains do not contradict that general principle.

Additionally, it should be noted that by effort and striving, it is not only the quantity that is meant; sometimes a small effort with high quality has a much greater effect than a vast effort with low quality.

Key Points

1- Are This World and the Hereafter in Conflict?

In many verses, there is praise and commendation for the world or its material possibilities:

In some verses, wealth is introduced as "Khayr" (Good) (Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 180).

In many verses, material blessings are mentioned under the title of Allah's Grace: "And seek from the grace of Allah" (Surah Al-Jumu'ah, Verse 10).

Elsewhere He says: "He created for you all that is in the earth" (Al-Baqarah, 29).

In many verses, He mentions them under the title "Sakhara Lakum" (He has subjected them to you). If we were to collect all the verses related to respecting the material possibilities of this world, it would be a significant collection.

Yet, despite all this importance given to material blessings and favors, expressions that strongly belittle them are also found in the verses of the Quran.

In one place, He considers it a fleeting gain and enjoyment: "You desire the commodities of the worldly life" (An-Nisa, 94).

In another place, He considers it a source of delusion and negligence: "And the worldly life is nothing but the enjoyment of delusion" (Surah Al-Hadid, Verse 20).

In another instance, He considers it a means of diversion and play: "And this worldly life is nothing but diversion and play" (Al-Ankabut, 64).

And in another place, a cause of negligence from the remembrance of Allah: "[Those] men whom neither commerce nor sale distracts from the remembrance of Allah" (An-Nur, 37).

These dual expressions are exactly mirrored in Islamic narrations as well:

On one hand, the world is considered the farm of the Hereafter, the marketplace of the men of God, the mosque of the lovers of Truth, the place of descent for the Lord's revelation, and the abode of exhortation and counsel ("The mosque of the lovers of Allah, the place of prayer for the angels of Allah, the place of descent for the revelation of Allah, and the marketplace for the friends of Allah") [1]. On the other hand, it is a source of negligence and heedlessness of Allah's remembrance, the enjoyment of delusion, and the like.

Do these two groups of verses and narrations contradict each other?

The answer to this question can be found in the Quran itself.

For where it censures the world and its blessings, it refers to those for whom this life constitutes their only goal. In Surah An-Najm, Verse 29, we read: "And desired nothing but the worldly life."

In other words, it speaks of those who sell the Hereafter for the world and refrain from no wrongdoing or crime to reach material ends.

In Surah At-Tawbah, Verse 38, we read: "Are you satisfied with the worldly life instead of the Hereafter?"

The verses under discussion are themselves witnesses to this claim, where it says: "Whoever desires the fleeting [life]..." meaning their only goal is this transitory material life.

Fundamentally, the terms "farm" or "marketplace" and the like are living witnesses to this subject.

In short, the blessings of the material world—which are all from the favors of Allah and whose existence was and is certainly necessary in the system of creation—if utilized as a means to reach human spiritual felicity and perfection, are praiseworthy from every aspect.

But if they are regarded as an end and not a means, and are severed from spiritual and human values, then they will naturally be a source of delusion, negligence, rebellion, defiance, oppression, and injustice, and are deserving of every kind of censure and blame.

How beautifully Ali عليه السلام said in that short and profound statement: "Whoever looks with it, it gives him sight; and whoever looks at it, it blinds him." [1] In reality, the difference between the censured world and the praised world is exactly what is understood from "at it" (إليها) and "with it" (بها), where the former indicates the goal and the latter indicates the means.

2- The Role of Effort and Striving in Successes

This is not the first time the Quran, by emphasizing effort and striving, warns lazy and idle individuals that the felicity of the next abode cannot be obtained merely by professing faith and words; rather, the primary factor for felicity is effort and striving.

This truth is reflected in many verses of the Quran.

In one place, it considers man a hostage to his deeds: "Every soul, for what it has earned, is a hostage" (Al-Muddathir, 38).

In another place, it considers his benefit to be only in his effort: "And that there is not for man except that [good] for which he strives."

In many verses, after mentioning faith, it emphasizes righteous deeds.

This is so that everyone may remove the vain thought from their heads that one can reach anywhere without effort and striving. If the blessings of the material world cannot be obtained without effort, how can one expect eternal felicity to be obtained without it?

3- Divine Aids

"Numiddu" (نمد) is from the root "Imdad," meaning to provide help. Raghib says in the book Al-Mufradat: The word Imdad is mostly used for useful and effective help, while the word Madd is used in disliked and unpleasant cases.

In any case, in the verses under discussion, we read that Allah places a portion of His blessings at the disposal of everyone, and both the good and the wicked utilize them. This refers to that portion of blessings upon which the continuation of life depends and without which one cannot be a free agent.

In other words, this is the station of Allah's Rahmaniyyah (General Mercy), whose general grace encompasses both the believer and the disbeliever. But beyond these are endless blessings reserved for the believers and the righteous.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 6, p. 3852. [^2]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the verse. [^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 131. [^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 82.

Ayahs

لَّا تَجْعَلْ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ فَتَقْعُدَ مَذْمُومًا مَّخْذُولًا (٢٢)
Do not set up another god besides Allah, or you will sit blameworthy, forsaken. (22)
۞ وَقَضَىٰ رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَـٰنًا ۚ إِمَّا يَبْلُغَنَّ عِندَكَ ٱلْكِبَرَ أَحَدُهُمَآ أَوْ كِلَاهُمَا فَلَا تَقُل لَّهُمَآ أُفٍّ وَلَا تَنْهَرْهُمَا وَقُل لَّهُمَا قَوْلًا كَرِيمًا (٢٣)
Your Lord has decreed that you shall not worship anyone except Him, and [He has enjoined] kindness to parents. Should any of them or both reach old age at your side, do not say to them, ‘Fie!’ And do not chide them, but speak to them noble words. (23)
وَٱخْفِضْ لَهُمَا جَنَاحَ ٱلذُّلِّ مِنَ ٱلرَّحْمَةِ وَقُل رَّبِّ ٱرْحَمْهُمَا كَمَا رَبَّيَانِى صَغِيرًا (٢٤)
Lower the wing of humility to them, mercifully, and say, ‘My Lord! Have mercy on them, just as they reared me when I was [a] small [child]!’ (24)
رَّبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا فِى نُفُوسِكُمْ ۚ إِن تَكُونُوا۟ صَـٰلِحِينَ فَإِنَّهُۥ كَانَ لِلْأَوَّٰبِينَ غَفُورًا (٢٥)
Your Lord knows best what is in your hearts. Should you be righteous, He is indeed most forgiving toward penitents. (25)

Commentary

Monotheism and Kindness to Parents: The Beginning of a Series of Important Islamic Injunctions.

The verses under discussion serve as an introduction to a series of fundamental Islamic rulings that begin with the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and faith—a Monotheism that is the leaven for all positive activities and righteous, constructive deeds. This also establishes a connection between these verses and the preceding ones, which spoke of the fortunate and their three-fold program: "faith," "striving and effort," and "the intention for the Abode of the Hereafter."

Furthermore, it is a renewed emphasis on what was previously stated regarding the Quran and its invitation to the clearest and best of paths.

It begins first with Monotheism, saying: "Do not set up with Allah any other deity" (لَا تَجْعَلْ مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلَٰهًا آخَرَ).

It does not say "do not worship another deity with God," but rather "do not set up," so that it carries a broader meaning. That is, neither in belief, nor in action, nor in supplication and request, nor in worship, should you place another deity alongside "Allah."

Then, it proceeds to describe the lethal consequence of polytheism (shirk), saying: "lest you [thereby] sit down condemned and forsaken" (فَتَقْعُدَ مَذْمُوماً مَخْذُولًا).

The choice of the word qu'ud (sitting) here alludes to weakness and inability; in Arabic literature, this word is a metaphor for weakness, just as it is said: qa'ada bihi al-du'fu 'an al-qital ("Weakness caused him to sit back from fighting the enemy").

From the above phrase, it is understood that polytheism leaves three very negative effects on a human being's existence:

  1. Polytheism is a source of weakness, inability, abjectness, and humiliation, whereas Monotheism is the factor for rising, movement, and honor.

  2. Polytheism is a source of blame and reproach, for it is a clear deviant line against the logic of reason and an overt ingratitude toward the blessings of the Lord. He who submits to such deviation deserves condemnation.

  3. Polytheism causes Allah to abandon the polytheist to his fabricated deities and withdraw His support. Since fabricated deities are incapable of supporting anyone, and God has withdrawn His support from such people, they become makhdhul—meaning without a friend or helper.

In other verses of the Quran, this same meaning is visualized in another form. As we read in Surah Al-Ankabut, verse 41: "The example of those who take allies other than Allah is like that of the spider who takes a home. And indeed, the weakest of homes is the home of the spider, if they only knew" (مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ أَوْلِياءَ كَمَثَلِ الْعَنْكَبُوتِ اتَّخَذَتْ بَيْتاً وَ إِنَّ أَوْهَنَ الْبُيُوتِ لَبَيْتُ الْعَنْكَبُوتِ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ).

After the principle of Monotheism, the verse points to one of the most fundamental human teachings of the Prophets while re-emphasizing Monotheism, saying: "And your Lord has decreed that you worship none but Him, and to parents, good treatment" (وَ قَضى‌ رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ وَ بِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً).

Qada (decreed) carries a more emphatic concept than amr (commanded); it conveys a definitive and firm command, and this is the first emphasis on this matter.

Placing Monotheism—the most fundamental Islamic principle—alongside kindness to parents is another emphasis on the importance of this Islamic instruction.

The absolute nature of ihsan (kindness/good treatment), which encompasses every kind of goodness, and likewise "parents," which includes both Muslim and non-Muslim, constitute the third and fourth emphases in this sentence.

The indefinite form of ihsan (ihsanan), which in such cases is used to express greatness, is considered the fifth emphasis. [^1] It is also necessary to note that a command usually targets an affirmative matter, whereas here it targets a negation ("Your Lord has commanded that you do not worship except Him").

This may be because it is understood from the word qada that another phrase in affirmative form is implied, meaning: "Your Lord has given an emphatic command that you worship Him and do not worship other than Him."

Or, the entire phrase of "negation and affirmation" (أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ) is treated as an affirmative sentence: the affirmation of the exclusive worship of the Lord. Then, it proceeds to describe one of the clear instances of kindness to parents, saying:

"Whether one or both of them reach old age [while] with you (such that they require your constant care), do not withhold any kind of affection from

[^1]: Some believe that ihsan is usually transitive with ila (to), as in ahsana ilayhi. However, it is sometimes made transitive by the letter ba (with/by). This expression is perhaps used to express directness—meaning you should personally and without an intermediary treat them with affection and respect. This would be the sixth emphasis on this matter.

them, and do not show them the slightest insult; do not even say to them the lightest disrespectful expression, namely 'uff'" (إِمَّا يَبْلُغَنَّ عِنْدَكَ الْكِبَرَ أَحَدُهما أَوْ كِلاهُما فَلا تَقُلْ لَهُما أُفٍ) [^1] "and do not shout at them" (وَ لا تَنْهَرْهُما).

Rather, "speak to them a noble and gracious word" (وَ قُلْ لَهُما قَوْلًا كَرِيماً).

And show the utmost humility toward them, "and lower to them the wing of humility out of mercy" (وَ اخْفِضْ لَهُما جَناحَ الذُّلِّ مِنَ الرَّحْمَةِ). "And say, 'My Lord, have mercy upon them as they brought me up [when I was] small'" (وَ قُلْ رَبِّ ارْحَمْهُما كَما رَبَّيانِي صَغِيراً).

Extraordinary Precision in Respecting Parents.

In truth, in the two preceding verses, a portion of the subtleties of polite and extraordinarily respectful conduct of children toward fathers and mothers is recounted:

  1. On one hand, it places its finger on their states of old age—when they are more in need of support, affection, and respect than ever—saying: do not say the slightest insulting word to them!

Due to old age, they may reach a point where they cannot move or stand up without another's help, and they may even be unable to clean themselves. At this moment, the great trial for the children begins.

Do they consider the existence of such parents a source of mercy, or a trial, calamity, and torment? Do they have sufficient patience and endurance to maintain respectful care for such parents, or do they, at every moment, squeeze and hurt their hearts with the sting of their tongues, with light and insulting words, and sometimes even by asking God for their death?

  1. On the other hand, the Quran says: at this time, do not say "uff" to them—meaning do not express annoyance or show aversion. It further adds: do not speak to them with a loud, insulting voice or shouting. It emphasizes again: speak to them with qawlan kariman (noble speech) and gracious words, all of which signify the ultimate etiquette in speech, for the tongue is the key to the heart.

  2. On another hand, it commands modesty and humility—a humility that reflects affection and love, and nothing else.

  3. Finally, it says: even when you turn toward the threshold of God, do not forget your parents (whether in life or in death) and request the Lord's mercy for them.

Specifically, accompany this request with this reasoning and say: "O Allah, just as they raised me in childhood, grant them Your mercy."

An important point derived from this expression, in addition to what has been said, is that if parents become so old and weak that they are unable to move or clean themselves alone, do not forget that you were once like this in childhood, and they did not withhold any support or affection from you. Compensate for their love.

And since lapses may occur regarding the preservation of the rights of parents, the respect due to them, and the humility

required of the child—whether one is drawn toward it consciously or unconsciously—the final verse under discussion says: "Your Lord is most knowing of what is within yourselves" (رَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِما فِي نُفُوسِكُمْ), for His knowledge in all fields is present, fixed, eternal, and free from any error, whereas your knowledge does not possess these attributes.

Therefore, if a lapse occurs regarding respect and kindness to parents without the intention of rebellion or defiance against God's command, and you immediately become regretful and seek to compensate, you will certainly be included in God's forgiveness: "If you should be righteous [in intention]—then indeed He is ever, to the often returning [to Him], Forgiving" (إِنْ تَكُونُوا صالِحِينَ فَإِنَّهُ كانَ لِلْأَوَّابِينَ غَفُوراً).

Awwab is from the root awb, meaning a return accompanied by will, whereas ruju' is used for both willful and involuntary return. For this reason, repentance (tawbah) is called awbah, because the reality of repentance is a willful return toward Allah.

And since awwab is an intensive form (sighat al-mubalaghah), it is said of someone who, whenever a mistake escapes them, returns to the Lord.

It is also possible that the use of the intensive form refers to the multiplicity of factors for returning to God; for faith in the Lord on one hand, attention to the court of the Day of Resurrection on the other, an awakened conscience on a third, and attention to the consequences and effects of sin on a fourth, join hands and emphatically lead the human being from the path of deviation back to God.

Key Points

1. Respect for Parents in Islamic Logic

Although human emotions and the issue of gratitude are alone sufficient for observing respect toward parents, Islam does not remain silent even in matters where reason has complete independence and emotion clearly perceives them. Instead, as an emphasis, it issues the necessary instructions in such cases. Regarding respect for parents, it has emphasized it to an extent seen in few other issues.

As an example, we point to several parts:

A) In four surahs of the Holy Quran, kindness to parents is placed immediately after the issue of Monotheism. This pairing of the two issues expresses the extent to which Islam values respect for fathers and mothers.

In Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 83, we read: "Worship not except Allah and to parents do good" (لا تَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا اللَّهَ وَ بِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً).

In Surah An-Nisa, verse 36: "Worship Allah and associate nothing with Him, and to parents do good" (واعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ وَ لا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئاً وَ بِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً).

In Surah Al-An'am, verse 151, He says: "that you not associate anything with Him, and to parents, good treatment" (أَلَّا تُشْرِكُوا بِهِ شَيْئاً وَ بِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً).

And in the verses under discussion, we saw these two paired together: "And your Lord has decreed that you worship none but Him, and to parents, good treatment" (وَ قَضى‌ رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ وَ بِالْوالِدَيْنِ إِحْساناً).

B) The importance of this subject is to such a degree that both the Quran and narrations explicitly recommend that even if parents are disbelievers, observing respect for them is necessary.

In Surah Luqman, verse 15, we read: "But if they endeavor to make you associate with Me that of which you have no knowledge, do not obey them but accompany them in [this] world with appropriate kindness" (وَ إِنْ جاهَداكَ عَلى‌ أَنْ تُشْرِكَ بِي ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ فَلا تُطِعْهُما وَ صاحِبْهُما فِي الدُّنْيا مَعْرُوفاً).

C) Gratitude toward parents in the Holy Quran is placed in the same rank as gratitude for God's blessings, as we read: "Be grateful to Me and to your parents" (أَنِ اشْكُرْ لِي وَ لِوالِدَيْكَ) (Surah Luqman, verse 14). This is despite God's blessings being beyond count, and it serves as evidence for the depth and breadth of the rights of fathers and mothers.

D) The Quran does not permit even the slightest disrespect toward parents. In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said:

"If Allah knew of anything lower than 'uff,' He would have forbidden it, and it is among the lowest forms of being 'unfilial' (uquq). And it is part of uquq for a man to look at his parents with a sharp [angry] gaze." [^1]

E) Although Jihad is one of the most important Islamic programs, as long as it does not become an individual obligation (wujub 'ayni)—meaning there are sufficient volunteers—being in the service of parents is more important. If it causes them distress, it is not permissible.

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that a man came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "I am a vibrant and fit young man and I love Jihad, but I have a mother who is distressed by this." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"Return and be with your mother, for by the One who sent me with the truth, for her to be comforted by you for one night is better than a year of Jihad in the way of Allah." [^1]

Of course, when Jihad becomes an individual obligation and the Islamic country is in danger and everyone's presence is required, no excuse is accepted, not even the dissatisfaction of parents.

Regarding other collective obligations (wajibat kifa'i) and also recommended acts, the matter is the same as stated regarding Jihad.

F) The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"Beware of being unfilial (uquq) to parents, for the fragrance of Paradise is found from a distance of a thousand years, but it will not be found by the one who is aq [disowned/disobedient to parents]." [^2]

This expression is a subtle allusion to the fact that such persons not only do not step into Paradise but are at a very great distance from it and cannot even approach it.

Sayyid Qutb, in the commentary Fi Zilal, narrates a hadith to this effect from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that a man was performing circumambulation (tawaf) while carrying his mother on his shoulders. He saw the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in that state and asked: "Have I fulfilled my mother's right with this act?" He replied: "No, not even to compensate for one of her groans (during childbirth)!" [^3]

If we were to let the pen run free here, the discourse would become very lengthy and depart from the form of a commentary. However, it must be said explicitly that no matter how much is said in this regard, it is still little, for they have the right of life over the human being.

[^1]: Jami' al-Sa'adat, Vol. 2, p. 258. [^1]: Jami' al-Sa'adat, Vol. 2, p. 260. [^2]: Jami' al-Sa'adat, Vol. 2, p. 257. [^3]: Fi Zilal, Vol. 5, p. 318.

At the end of this discussion, we deem it necessary to mention that sometimes parents may make illogical suggestions or those contrary to Sharia. Obviously, obedience to them is not required in any of these cases. Nevertheless, one must deal with such suggestions through logical interaction and by performing the duty of "enjoining the good" in its best form. We conclude our words in this regard with a hadith from Imam al-Kadhim عليه السلام: The Imam says, "Someone came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and asked about the right of the father and the child. He said:

'He should not call him by his name (but rather say "my father"), he should not walk in front of him, he should not sit before him, and he should not do anything that causes people to insult his father' (they should not say 'May God not forgive your father for what you did!')." [^1]

2. Research into the Meaning of "Qada"

Qada is from the root qada', which originally means to separate or settle something, either by action or by word. Some have said it originally means to bring something to an end, and both meanings are actually close in scope.

Since bringing to an end and separating have a broad meaning, this word has been used in various concepts.

Qurtubi, in his commentary, mentions six meanings for it:

  • Qada meaning "command" and decree, such as: وَ قَضى‌ رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ ("And your Lord has commanded that you worship none but Him").

  • Qada meaning "creation," such as: فَقَضاهُنَّ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ فِي يَوْمَيْنِ ("So He completed them as seven heavens in two days") (Surah Fussilat, verse 12).

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 149.

  • Qada meaning "judgment" and adjudication, such as: فَاقْضِ ما أَنْتَ قاضٍ ("So decree whatever you are to decree") (Surah Taha, verse 72).

  • Qada meaning "completion of something," such as: قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ الَّذِي فِيهِ تَسْتَفْتِيانِ ("The matter has been concluded about which you both inquire") (Surah Yusuf, verse 41).

  • Qada meaning "will/intent," such as: إِذا قَضى‌ أَمْراً فَإِنَّما يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ ("When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, 'Be,' and it is") (Surah Al-Imran, verse 47).

  • And Qada meaning "covenant," such as: إِذْ قَضَيْنا إِلى‌ مُوسَى الْأَمْرَ ("When We decreed to Moses the matter") (Surah Al-Qasas, verse 44). [^1] Abu al-Futuh al-Razi adds to these meanings:

  • Qada meaning "informing and announcing," such as: وَ قَضَيْنا إِلى‌ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ فِي الْكِتابِ ("And We announced to the Children of Israel in the Scripture"). [^2] To this can be added:

  • Qada meaning "death," such as: فَوَكَزَهُ مُوسى‌ فَقَضى‌ عَلَيْهِ ("So Moses struck him and ended his life") (Surah Al-Qasas, verse 15).

Some commentators have even identified up to thirteen meanings for qada in the Holy Quran. [^3] However, these cannot be considered distinct meanings for the word qada, because they all share a common core. In reality, most of the meanings mentioned above are instances of "mistaking the application for the concept," as each of these is an instance of that general and comprehensive meaning, namely "bringing to an end and separating."

For example, a judge ends a dispute with his ruling; the Creator ends the creation of something with His act of creating; the informer ends the explanation of something with his report; the one who covenants or commands considers a matter concluded through their covenant or command, such that reversal is impossible.

However, it cannot be denied that in some of these instances, the term has been used so frequently that it has taken on a new meaning, such as qada meaning adjudication or meaning command and decree.

3. Research into the Meaning of "Uff"

Raghib says in the book Al-Mufradat: "Uff originally means anything dirty and contaminated, and it is also said as an insult. This word does not only have a noun meaning, but verbs are also derived from it; for example, they say: affaftu bi-kadha, meaning 'I considered such-and-forth dirty and expressed aversion toward it.'"

Some commentators, like Qurtubi in his commentary and Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, have said: "Uff and tuff originally mean the dirt that collects under the fingernail; it is both contaminated and insignificant. Some have even distinguished between uff and tuff, considering the first to be earwax and the second to be fingernail dirt. Then its concept expanded and was applied to anything that is a source of annoyance." [^1] Other meanings have also been stated for uff, including a small thing, annoyance, blame, and a bad smell.

Others have said the origin of this word is taken from the fact that whenever

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 6, p. 3853. [^2]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, Vol. 7, p. 188. [^3]: Wujuh al-Quran by Taflisi, p. 235. [^1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 20, p. 188.

a small amount of dust or ash falls on a person's body or clothes, the person blows it away. The sound that comes out of the human mouth at this moment is something like "ouf" or "uff," and it was later used to mean expressing annoyance and aversion, especially regarding small matters.

From a synthesis of what was mentioned above and other clues, it is inferred that this word was originally an "onomatopoeia" (the sound a person makes when expressing aversion, or minor pain and suffering, or blowing away something contaminated).

Then this "onomatopoeia" became a word, and even verbs were derived from it, and it was used for minor annoyances or expressing aversion due to small issues. The various meanings mentioned above seem to be instances of this same comprehensive and general meaning.

In any case, the above verse intends to convey, in a short phrase and with the utmost eloquence and rhetoric, that the respect for parents is so great that one should not even utter the slightest word that indicates annoyance with them or reluctance and aversion.

Ayahs

وَءَاتِ ذَا ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ حَقَّهُۥ وَٱلْمِسْكِينَ وَٱبْنَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَلَا تُبَذِّرْ تَبْذِيرًا (٢٦)
Give the relatives their [due] right, and the needy and the traveller [as well], but do not squander wastefully. (26)
إِنَّ ٱلْمُبَذِّرِينَ كَانُوٓا۟ إِخْوَٰنَ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينِ ۖ وَكَانَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لِرَبِّهِۦ كَفُورًا (٢٧)
Indeed the wasteful are brothers of satans, and Satan is ungrateful to his Lord. (27)
وَإِمَّا تُعْرِضَنَّ عَنْهُمُ ٱبْتِغَآءَ رَحْمَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكَ تَرْجُوهَا فَقُل لَّهُمْ قَوْلًا مَّيْسُورًا (٢٨)
And if you have to hold off from [assisting] them [for now], seeking your Lord’s mercy which you expect [in the future], speak to them gentle words. (28)
وَلَا تَجْعَلْ يَدَكَ مَغْلُولَةً إِلَىٰ عُنُقِكَ وَلَا تَبْسُطْهَا كُلَّ ٱلْبَسْطِ فَتَقْعُدَ مَلُومًا مَّحْسُورًا (٢٩)
Do not keep your hand chained to your neck, nor open it altogether, or you will sit blameworthy and regretful. (29)
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَقْدِرُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ بِعِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرًۢا بَصِيرًا (٣٠)
Indeed your Lord expands the provision for whomever He wishes, and tightens it. Indeed, He is well aware of His servants and a keen observer. (30)

Commentary

Observing Moderation in Spending and Charity

In these verses, another chapter of the fundamental principles of Islamic rulings is presented regarding the fulfillment of the rights of kinsmen, the needy, and the wayfarer, as well as general spending (Infaq), while remaining distant from any form of extravagance or wastefulness.

First, It says: "Give to the near of kin his due" (وَ آتِ ذَا الْقُرْبى‌ حَقَّهُ‌).

"And also to the needy and the wayfarer" (وَ الْمِسْكِينَ وَ ابْنَ السَّبِيلِ‌).

At the same time, "do not squander wastefully" (وَ لا تُبَذِّرْ تَبْذِيراً).

The word "Tabdhir" (squandering) originally comes from the root "Badhr" (seed) and means to scatter seeds. However, this word is specifically used for instances where a person consumes his wealth in an irrational and corrupt manner; its equivalent in modern Persian is "Rikht-o-pash" (lavish tossing/wasting).

In other words, Tabdhir is to spend wealth where it should not be spent, even if it is a small amount; and if it is spent where it should be, it is not Tabdhir, even if it is a large amount.

As we read in Tafsir al-Ayyashi from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, who said in response to a questioner regarding this verse:

"Whoever spends something in other than the obedience of Allah is a squanderer (mubadhir), and whoever spends in the way of Allah is moderate (muqtasid)." [^1] It is also narrated from him that one day he ordered dates to be brought for those present to eat. Some were eating the dates and throwing away the pits. He said: "Do not do this, for this is Tabdhir, and Allah does not love corruption." [^2] Precision regarding the issue of extravagance (Israf) and squandering (Tabdhir) is to such an extent that we read in a Hadith that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was passing by when one of his companions named Sa'd was performing ablution (Wudu) and was pouring a lot of water. He said: "Why this extravagance, O Sa'd?" Sa'd asked: "Is there extravagance even in the water of Wudu?" He replied:

"Yes, even if you are by a flowing river." [^3]

There is discussion among commentators as to whether "near of kin" (Dhi al-Qurba) here refers to all relatives or specifically the relatives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (since he is the addressee in the verse).

In several Hadiths, which will be discussed in the "Key Points" section, we read that this verse has been interpreted as referring to the kinsmen of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and in some, we even read that it refers to the event of gifting the land of Fadak to Fatimah al-Zahra عليها السلام.

However, as we have stated many times, such interpretations do not limit the broad concept of the verses; rather, they are expressions of the most prominent and clear examples.

The address to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the phrase "And give" (وَ آتِ) is not evidence that this ruling is exclusive to him, because other rulings mentioned in this series of verses—such as the prohibition of squandering, being gentle with the beggar and the needy, or the prohibition of stinginess and extravagance—are all mentioned as addresses to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, while we know these rulings are not exclusive and their concept is entirely general.

[^1], [^2], [^3]: Narrated according to Tafsir al-Safi under the verse being discussed.

It is also necessary to note that the prohibition of squandering after the command to give the rights of kinsmen, the needy, and the wayfarer is an indication that one should not be so influenced by the emotions of kinship or humanitarian feelings toward the poor and the wayfarer that they spend beyond their due and follow the path of extravagance, for extravagance and squandering are reprehensible in all cases.

The next verse serves as an argument and emphasis for the prohibition of squandering. It says: "Verily, the squanderers are brothers of the devils" (إِنَّ الْمُبَذِّرِينَ كانُوا إِخْوانَ الشَّياطِينِ‌). "And the Devil was ungrateful to his Lord" (وَ كانَ الشَّيْطانُ لِرَبِّهِ كَفُوراً).

As for the Devil being ungrateful for the blessings of his Lord, it is clear, because Allah had given him extraordinary power, strength, intelligence, and talent, yet he spent all these powers in the wrong place—namely, in the path of seduction and misguiding people.

As for squanderers being the "brothers of the devils," it is because they too are ungrateful for God-given blessings and spend them in ways that are not useful.

The expression "brothers" (Ikhwan) is either because their actions are in the same rank and harmony as the actions of the devils—like brothers who act alike—or because they are companions and associates of the Devil in Hell, just as in verse 39 of Surah Az-Zukhruf, after describing the Devil as a companion to polluted humans in general, He says: "And it will never profit you this Day, since you did wrong, that you are sharers in the punishment."

As for "devils" (Shayatin) being mentioned in the plural here, it may refer to what is understood from the verses of Surah Az-Zukhruf: that for every human who turns away from the remembrance of Allah, a devil is appointed who will be his companion and associate, not only in this world but also in the hereafter: "And whoever turns away from the remembrance of the Most Merciful - We appoint for him a devil, and he is to him a companion... Until, when he comes to Us, he says [to his companion], 'Oh, I wish there was between me and you the distance between the two easts - and wretched is the companion.'" (Surah Az-Zukhruf, verses 36 and 38).

Since sometimes a needy person approaches a human and there are no means available to respond to his need, the next verse explains the manner of direct interaction with the needy under such conditions. It says: "And if you turn away from them (due to lack of means) seeking mercy from your Lord which you expect, then speak to them a gentle word." Even if you can, give them a promise for the future and do not make them lose hope (وَ إِمَّا تُعْرِضَنَّ عَنْهُمُ ابْتِغاءَ رَحْمَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكَ تَرْجُوها فَقُلْ لَهُمْ قَوْلًا مَيْسُوراً).

"Maysur" comes from the root "Yusr," meaning ease and comfort, and here it has a broad meaning that includes any kind of good speech and interaction accompanied by respect and affection.

Therefore, if some have interpreted it as a specific phrase or as making a promise for the future, these are all examples of the concept.

In narrations, we read that after the revelation of this verse, whenever someone asked something of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and he had nothing to give, he would say:

"May Allah provide for us and you from His grace." [^1]

In our old traditions, when dealing with a beggar, it was and is such that when a petitioner came to the door and there was nothing to give, they would say: "Forgive [me]," implying that "your coming creates a right upon us, and morally you are owed something by us, and we request that you forgive us this moral debt because we have nothing available to serve as its reward!"

And since observing moderation in everything—even in spending and helping others—is a condition, the next verse emphasizes this issue, saying: "And do not make your hand tied to your neck" (وَ لا تَجْعَلْ يَدَكَ مَغْلُولَةً إِلى‌ عُنُقِكَ‌). This expression is a subtle metaphor for having a giving hand; do not be like the stingy who act as if their hands are tied to their necks with chains and are unable to help or spend.

On the other hand, "do not stretch it out to its utmost reach, and do not give without measure, lest you become blameworthy and destitute, and separated from the people" (وَ لا تَبْسُطْها كُلَّ الْبَسْطِ فَتَقْعُدَ مَلُوماً مَحْسُوراً).

Just as "the hand being tied to the neck" is a metaphor for stinginess, "stretching the hands out completely"—as understood from the phrase "do not stretch it out to its utmost reach"—is a metaphor for spending without measure.

And "Taq'uda," which comes from the root "Qu'ud" meaning to sit, is a metaphor for stopping and becoming incapacitated.

The expression "Malum" (blameworthy) indicates that sometimes excessive giving not only hinders a person from activity and the necessities of life but also opens the tongues of people to blame him.

"Mahsur" comes from the root "Hasr," which originally means to push aside clothing and expose the part underneath. For this reason, "Hasir" is said of a warrior who has no armor on his body or helmet on his head.

The words "Hasir" and "Hasur" are applied to animals that become exhausted and stranded due to excessive walking, as if all the flesh of their bodies or their strength has been stripped away and they have become "naked."

Later, this concept expanded to refer to any exhausted and stranded person who is unable to reach his destination.

"Hasrat," meaning grief and sorrow, is also derived from this root, because this state usually occurs to a person when they have lost the power to compensate for difficulties and failures, as if they have been stripped of ability and strength.

In the case of spending and charity, if it exceeds the limit and all of a person's strength and resources are absorbed by it, it is natural that the person will be unable to continue working, being active, and organizing his own life; he becomes stripped of power, full of grief, and naturally, his connection and bond with the people will also be severed.

In some narrations regarding the occasion of revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) of this verse, this point is clearly seen. In one narration, we read that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was at home when a petitioner came to the door. Since nothing was ready to be given and the person asked for a shirt, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله gave him his own shirt. This caused him to be unable to go to the mosque for prayer that day.

This incident opened the tongues of the disbelievers, who said: "Muhammad has overslept or is busy with play and amusement and has forgotten his prayer."

Thus, this act resulted in both the blame and mockery of the enemy and separation from friends, becoming an instance of "Malum Mahsur." The above verse was revealed and warned the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that this should not be repeated.

Regarding the apparent contradiction between this command and the issue of "Ithar" (self-sacrifice), we will discuss the answer in the upcoming "Key Points."

Some have also narrated that sometimes the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would give whatever he had in the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) to the needy such that if another needy person came to him later, he had nothing left and would feel ashamed; often the needy person would begin to blame him, causing distress to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Therefore, it was commanded that he should neither spend everything in the treasury nor keep it all, so that such problems would not arise.

[Why are some people deprived and needy?]

Here the question arises: why are some people deprived, needy, and poor at all, such that it is necessary for us to spend on them? Would it not have been better if Allah Himself gave them whatever was necessary so they would not need our charity?

The last verse under discussion seems to point to the answer to this very question, saying:

"Verily, your Lord expands the provision for whom He wills and restricts it; indeed, He is ever, of His servants, Aware and Seeing" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَقْدِرُ إِنَّهُ كانَ بِعِبادِهِ خَبِيراً بَصِيراً).

This is a test for you; otherwise, everything is possible for Him. He wants to train you through this means and to cultivate the spirit of generosity, sacrifice, and selflessness within you.

Furthermore, many people, if they were to become completely self-sufficient, would take the path of rebellion and transgression; it is better for them to have a certain level of provision—a level that leads neither to poverty nor to rebellion.

Beyond all this, the expansion and restriction of provision for individuals (except for exceptional cases like the incapacitated and the disabled) depends on the level of their effort and striving. When He says Allah restricts or expands provision for whom He wills, this "willing" is in harmony with His wisdom, and His wisdom requires that whoever strives more should have a greater share, and whoever strives less should be more deprived.

Some commentators have accepted another possibility regarding the connection of this verse with the preceding ones: that the final verse serves as a reason for the prohibition of excess and negligence in spending. It says that even Allah, with all His power and ability, observes the limit of moderation in granting provisions; He neither gives so much that they are led to corruption, nor so little that they fall into hardship. All of this is to observe the best interest of His servants.

Therefore, it is fitting that you too adopt this Divine character, follow the path of moderation, and avoid excess and negligence. [^1]

Key Points

1- Who is meant by "near of kin" (Dhi al-Qurba) here?

The word "Dhi al-Qurba," as we said, means relatives and kin. There is a discussion among commentators as to whether it refers to a general or specific meaning here.

1- Some believe the addressees are all believers and Muslims, and the meaning is to pay the rights of their own relatives to them.

2- Others say the addressee is the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the meaning is to pay the rights of the Prophet's relatives to them, such as the Khums of spoils and other items subject to Khums, and generally their rights in the public treasury.

Therefore, in numerous narrations reported through both Shi'a and Sunni sources, we read that upon the revelation of the above verse, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله called Fatimah عليها السلام and gifted her the land of "Fadak." [^1] In a Hadith narrated from Sunni sources from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, the famous companion of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"When the words of the Almighty 'And give to the near of kin his due' were revealed, the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله gave Fadak to Fatimah." [^2] From some narrations, it is understood that even Imam al-Sajjad عليه السلام, while being held captive in Damascus, argued with the people of Damascus using this very verse, saying: "The meaning of the verse 'Give to the near of kin his due' is us, whom Allah commanded His Prophet to fulfill our rights (and thus you people of Damascus have violated all these rights)." [^1] Yet, as we said before, these two interpretations are not contradictory. Everyone is duty-bound to pay the rights of their kin, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, as the leader of the Islamic society, is also duty-bound to fulfill this great Divine obligation. In fact, the Ahl al-Bayt of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله are among the clearest examples of "near of kin," and the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is among the clearest individuals addressed by this verse.

For this reason, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله gave them the rights of the near of kin, which included Khums as well as Fadak and the like, because receiving Zakat—which was considered part of the public wealth—was forbidden for them.

2- The Calamity of Extravagance and Squandering

Undoubtedly, the blessings and bounties present on Earth are sufficient for its inhabitants, but on one condition: that they are not wasted in vain, but rather utilized in a correct, rational manner, far from any excess or negligence. Otherwise, these bounties are not so vast and unlimited that they cannot be damaged by improper use.

And perhaps extravagance and squandering in one region of the earth causes deprivation in another region, or the extravagance and squandering of humans today causes the deprivation of future generations.

In those days when figures and statistics were not in human hands as they are today, Islam warned: do not allow extravagance and squandering in utilizing Allah's bounties on earth.

The Quran, in numerous verses, severely condemns the extravagant (Musrifin):

In one place it says: "Do not be extravagant; indeed, He does not love the extravagant" (Al-An'am 141, Al-A'raf 31).

In another place, it considers the extravagant to be "companions of the Fire" (Ghafir 43).

And it "prohibits obeying the command of the extravagant" (Ash-Shu'ara 151).

And it describes Divine punishment awaiting the extravagant (Adh-Dhariyat 34).

And it characterizes extravagance as a Pharaonic program (Yunus 83).

And it considers the lying extravagant to be deprived of Divine guidance (Ghafir 28).

And finally, it introduces their fate as destruction and ruin (Al-Anbiya 9).

And as we saw, the verses under discussion also consider squanderers to be the brothers of the Devil and his companions.

"Israf" (extravagance) in the broad sense of the word is any exceeding of the limit in an action a person performs, but this word is most often used regarding expenses and spending.

From the Quranic verses themselves, it is well understood that Israf is the opposite of being stingy and tight-fisted, where He says: "And those who, when they spend, are neither extravagant nor stingy, but hold a medium (way) between those (extremes)" (Al-Furqan 67).

3- The Difference Between Israf and Tabdhir

Regarding the difference between Israf (extravagance) and Tabdhir (squandering), we have not seen a clear discussion from commentators in this regard. However, considering the roots of these two words, it seems that when these two are placed against each other, "Israf" means going beyond the limit of moderation without apparently wasting something—for example, preparing our food so expensively that with its price, a large number of people could be fed respectably.

Here we have exceeded the limit, but apparently, nothing has been destroyed.

However, "Tabdhir" (squandering/tossing) is to consume in a way that leads to loss and waste, such as preparing food for ten people when there are only two guests—as some ignorant people do and take pride in—and then throwing the leftovers in the trash and wasting them.

But it should not go unmentioned that often these two words are used with the exact same meaning and are even placed one after the other for emphasis.

Ali عليه السلام, according to what is narrated in Nahj al-Balagha, says: "Behold, giving wealth where it is not due is squandering (Tabdhir) and extravagance (Israf); it may raise a person in this world but it will surely lower him in the Hereafter; it may bring him honor among the masses but it will cause his status to fall before Allah."

In the commentary of the verses under discussion, we read that Islamic instructions emphasize the negation of Israf and Tabdhir so much that they even prohibited pouring too much water for Wudu, even if it be by a river, and the Imam also prohibited throwing away a date pit.

Today's world, which feels the scarcity of certain materials, has paid serious attention to this subject to the point that it utilizes everything: they make the best fertilizer from trash, necessary tools from dregs, and even usable water for agriculture from sewage after purification. This is because they have realized that the materials present in nature are not unlimited such that they can be easily discarded; everything must be utilized in a "cyclical" manner.

4- Does Moderation in Spending Contradict Self-Sacrifice (Ithar)?!

Considering the above verses which command "observing moderation in spending," the question arises: in Surah Al-Insan (Ad-Dahr) and other verses of the Quran, as well as in narrations, we read praise and commendation for those who practice self-sacrifice (Ithar), who give to others even in their own extreme hardship. How are these two compatible?!

Precision regarding the occasion of revelation of the above verses, as well as other clues, clarifies the answer to this question: the command to observe moderation is for cases where excessive giving causes extraordinary disorder in the person's own life, making them, so to speak, "blameworthy and destitute" (Malum and Mahsur).

Or where self-sacrifice causes distress and pressure on one's children and endangers the family system. In cases where none of these occur, self-sacrifice is undoubtedly the best path.

Furthermore, observing moderation is a general ruling, while self-sacrifice is a specific ruling related to certain instances, and these two rulings do not contradict each other.

Ayahs

وَلَا تَقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَوْلَـٰدَكُمْ خَشْيَةَ إِمْلَـٰقٍ ۖ نَّحْنُ نَرْزُقُهُمْ وَإِيَّاكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ قَتْلَهُمْ كَانَ خِطْـًٔا كَبِيرًا (٣١)
Do not kill your children for the fear of penury: We will provide for them and for you. Killing them is indeed a great iniquity. (31)
وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ ٱلزِّنَىٰٓ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فَـٰحِشَةً وَسَآءَ سَبِيلًا (٣٢)
Do not approach fornication. It is indeed an indecency and an evil way. (32)
وَلَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلنَّفْسَ ٱلَّتِى حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ ۗ وَمَن قُتِلَ مَظْلُومًا فَقَدْ جَعَلْنَا لِوَلِيِّهِۦ سُلْطَـٰنًا فَلَا يُسْرِف فِّى ٱلْقَتْلِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ مَنصُورًا (٣٣)
Do not kill a soul [whose life] Allah has made inviolable, except with due cause, and whoever is killed wrongfully, We have certainly given his heir an authority. But let him not commit any excess in killing [the murderer], for he has been assisted [by law]. (33)
وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا۟ مَالَ ٱلْيَتِيمِ إِلَّا بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ أَشُدَّهُۥ ۚ وَأَوْفُوا۟ بِٱلْعَهْدِ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلْعَهْدَ كَانَ مَسْـُٔولًا (٣٤)
Do not approach the orphan’s property except in the best manner, until he comes of age. Fulfill the covenants; indeed all covenants are accountable. (34)
وَأَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ إِذَا كِلْتُمْ وَزِنُوا۟ بِٱلْقِسْطَاسِ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلًا (٣٥)
When you measure, observe fully the measure, [and] weigh with an even balance. That is better and more favourable with respect to the outcome [in the Hereafter]. (35)

Commentary

Six Important Commandments

Following the various sections of Islamic rulings mentioned in the previous verses, the verses under discussion address another part of these rulings, explaining six important commandments within five verses using short yet meaningful and pleasant expressions.

[1- The Prohibition of Killing Children]

First, it refers to a repulsive Ignorant (Jahili) practice which was among the most heinous sins, saying: "And do not kill your children for fear of poverty" (وَلَا تَقْتُلُوا أَوْلَادَكُمْ خَشْيَةَ إِمْلَاقٍ).

Their sustenance is not upon you; "We provide for them and for you" (نَحْنُ نَرْزُقُهُمْ وَإِيَّاكُمْ):

"For indeed, killing them has been and is a great sin" (إِنَّ قَتْلَهُمْ كَانَ خِطْأً كَبِيراً).

From this verse, it is clearly understood that the economic situation of the Arabs in the Age of Ignorance was so harsh and distressing that they would sometimes kill their beloved children out of fear of economic inability.

There is a discussion among commentators as to whether the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance only buried daughters alive, or if they also killed sons out of fear of poverty.

Some believe that all of this refers to the burying of daughters alive, which they did for two reasons: one was the fear that in future wars they might be captured by enemies and their honor would fall into the hands of strangers! The other was the pressure of poverty and the inability to provide for their living expenses, as a daughter in that society was not a producer but was mostly considered a consumer.

It is true that sons were also consumers at the beginning of their lives, but the Ignorant Arab always looked upon sons as a significant asset and was not willing to lose them.

Others believe that they practiced two types of child-killing: one type, based on their false imagination, was for the sake of preserving honor, and this was specific to daughters; the other type was carried out due to fear of poverty, which was general and did not differentiate between son and daughter.

The apparent phrasing of the verse, which uses the masculine plural pronoun (قَتْلَهُمْ - qatlahum), could be evidence for this view, as applying the masculine plural to both sons and daughters collectively is possible in Arabic grammar, but it seems unlikely to be used specifically for daughters alone.

As for the point that sons were capable of production and were considered an asset, this is entirely correct, but only if the parents had the ability to cover their expenses in the short term. However, they were sometimes under such pressure that they lacked the ability to manage their lives even in the short term (and for this reason, the second interpretation seems more accurate).

In any case, it was nothing more than an illusion that parents are the providers for their children; Allah announces that they should cast this satanic thought from their heads and rise to greater effort and striving, and Allah will help and manage their lives.

It is noteworthy that while we are horrified by this ugly and shameful crime, the same crime is carried out in another form in our era, even in so-called most "progressive" societies: the act of abortion on a very large scale for the sake of preventing population growth and economic shortages (for more explanation, refer to the commentary of Surah Al-An'am, verse 151, Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 33).

The expression "خَشْيَةَ إِمْلَاقٍ" (fear of poverty) is also a subtle reference to the negation of this satanic thought; in reality, He says it is only a fear that encourages you toward this great betrayal, not a reality.

Additionally, it should be noted that the phrase "كَانَ خِطْأً كَبِيراً" (was a great sin), considering that "كَانَ" is a past tense verb, is an indication and emphasis that the killing of children is a great sin that has been recognized among humans since ancient times, and its repulsiveness is rooted deep within human nature; therefore, it is not specific to any particular age or time.

[2- Adultery (Zina)]

Another great sin mentioned in the next verse is the issue of adultery and acts contrary to chastity; it says: "And do not approach adultery, for indeed it is an indecency and an evil way" (وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا الزِّنَىٰ ۖ إِنَّهُ كَانَ فَاحِشَةً وَسَاءَ سَبِيلًا).

In this brief statement, three points are mentioned:

A- It does not say "do not commit adultery," but rather "do not approach this shameful act." This expression, in addition to the emphasis inherent in its depth regarding the act itself, is a subtle indication that involvement in adultery often has preliminaries that gradually bring a person closer to it. Lustful glancing is one of its preliminaries; nudity and lack of Hijab is another; "indecent books," "corrupt films," "vile publications," and "centers of corruption" are each considered a preliminary for this act.

Likewise, being in seclusion with a stranger (meaning a non-mahram man and woman being alone in an empty place) is another provocative factor.

Finally, the abandonment of marriage for the youth and the groundless strictness of both parties in this regard are all factors of "approaching adultery," which the above verse forbids in one short sentence, and in Islamic narrations, each has been forbidden separately.

B- The phrase "إِنَّهُ كَانَ فَاحِشَةً" (indeed it is an indecency), which contains three emphases (the word inna, the use of the past tense verb, and the term fahishah), makes the magnitude of this manifest sin even clearer.

C- The phrase "سَاءَ سَبِيلًا" (it is an evil way) expresses the reality that this act opens a path to other corruptions in society.

The Philosophy of Prohibiting Adultery

1- The emergence of chaos in the family system and the loss of the relationship between children and fathers—a relationship whose existence is not only a cause for social identification but also leads to the full protection of children and establishes the foundations of affection that sustain this protection throughout life.

In short, in a society where illegitimate and fatherless children become numerous, social relations—which are built upon the foundation of family relations—become severely shaken.

To understand the importance of this issue, it is enough to think for a moment: if adultery were permitted in the entire human society and marriage were abolished, the identity-less children born under such conditions would be under no one's protection, neither at birth nor while growing up.

Beyond this, they would be deprived of the element of affection, which plays a decisive role in combating crime and violence, and human society would turn into a purely animalistic society coupled with violence in all dimensions.

2- This shameful act is the cause of various types of individual and social conflicts and struggles among the hedonistic. The stories that some have narrated and written about the conditions inside "red-light districts" and centers of corruption clearly demonstrate the reality that the worst crimes occur alongside sexual deviations.

3- Experience has shown and science has proven that this act causes the spread of various diseases.
