Despite all the organizations established today to combat its consequences and effects, statistics still show to what extent individuals have lost and continue to lose their health through this path.

4- This act often leads to abortion, the killing of children, and the termination of lineage, because such women are never willing to keep such children, and fundamentally, the existence of a child is a major obstacle in the path of continuing their sinister deeds; therefore, they always try to eliminate them.

The completely hollow hypothesis that such children can be gathered in state-supervised institutions has clearly failed in practice, and it has been proven how many problems arise from raising fatherless and motherless children in this manner; even then, the "product" is of very poor quality: cold-hearted, criminal, lacking personality, and devoid of everything!

5- It must not be forgotten that the goal of marriage is not merely the satisfaction of the sexual instinct; rather, sharing in the formation of a life, spiritual intimacy, mental peace, the upbringing of children, and cooperation in all aspects of life are the effects of marriage, none of which are possible without the mutual exclusivity of man and woman and the prohibition of adultery.

Imam Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام says in a Hadith: I heard the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saying:

"In adultery, there are six evil traits: three in this world and three in the Hereafter:

As for those in this world: it takes away the radiance of the face, cuts off sustenance, and hastens destruction.

As for those in the Hereafter: the wrath of the Lord, the severity of the reckoning, and entry into—or eternity in—the Fire." [^1]

[3- Homicide]

Another ruling mentioned in the next verse is the sanctity of human blood and the severe prohibition of homicide; it says: "And do not kill the soul which Allah has forbidden, except by right" (وَلَا تَقْتُلُوا النَّفْسَ الَّتِي حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ).

Respect for human blood and the prohibition of homicide are matters upon which all heavenly religions and human laws agree, considering it one of the greatest sins. However, Islam has given even greater importance to this issue, to the extent that it considers the killing of one human being equivalent to the killing of all humanity: "Whoever kills a soul unless for a soul or for corruption [done] in the land - it is as if he had slain mankind entirely" (Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 32).

And it is even understood from some Quranic verses that the punishment of eternity in the Fire—which is specific to disbelievers—has been designated for a murderer. As we said previously, this expression may be evidence that individuals whose hands are stained with the blood of the innocent will not leave this world with faith! "But whoever kills a believer intentionally - his recompense is Hell, wherein he will abide eternally" (Surah An-Nisa, verse 93).

Even in Islam, a heavy punishment has been designated for those who draw weapons against the people, under the title of "Muharib" (one who wages war against God and society), the details of which are found in books of jurisprudence and which we referred to under verse 33 of Surah Al-Ma'idah.

Not only homicide, but even the slightest and smallest harm to a human being is punishable in Islam. It can be said with certainty that such respect as Islam has accorded to the blood, life, and dignity of a human being does not exist in any other creed.

But for this very reason, cases arise where the sanctity of blood is removed, and this applies to those who have committed murder or a sin equivalent to it. Therefore, in the above verse, after mentioning a general principle regarding the prohibition of homicide, it immediately excludes such individuals with the phrase "إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ" (except by right).

In a famous Hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"The blood of a Muslim man who bears witness that there is no god but Allah and that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah is not lawful except in one of three cases: a life for a life, the married adulterer, and the one who leaves his religion and separates from the community." [^1]

As for the murderer, his case is clear; in his execution (Qisas) lies the life of society and the ensuring of the security of lives. If the right of Qisas were not given to the heirs of the victim, murderers would become bold and insolent, and the security of society would be disrupted.

As for the married adulterer, his execution is in response to one of the foulest sins, which is equated with blood.

And the execution of the apostate (murtad) prevents chaos in the Islamic society. As we have said before, this ruling is a political one for the preservation of the social order against matters that threaten not only social security but the very foundation of the Islamic system.

Fundamentally, Islam does not compel anyone to accept this religion; rather, its interaction with followers of other faiths is only a logical one accompanied by free discussion. However, if someone accepts Islam of their own will and becomes part of the Islamic society, and naturally becomes aware of the secrets of the Muslims, and then decides to turn back from this religion and practically weaken the foundation of the system and create instability in the pillars of the Islamic society, this act is certainly not tolerable, and under the conditions mentioned in Islamic jurisprudence, its ruling is execution. [^1]

Of course, respect for human blood in Islam is not exclusive to Muslims; rather, non-Muslims who are not at war with Muslims and live in peaceful coexistence with them have their lives, property, and honor protected, and any aggression against them is forbidden and prohibited.

Then, it refers to the right of Qisas (retaliation) established for the heirs of the blood, saying:

"And whoever is killed unjustly - We have given his heir authority" (authority to retaliate against the murderer) (وَمَنْ قُتِلَ مَظْلُومًا فَقَدْ جَعَلْنَا لِوَلِيِّهِ سُلْطَانًا).

But at the same time, "he should not exceed the limit in [taking] life, for indeed he is supported" (فَلَا يُسْرِفْ فِي الْقَتْلِ ۖ إِنَّهُ كَانَ مَنْصُورًا).

Yes, the heirs of the victim, as long as they walk within the boundaries of Islam and do not exceed their limit, are under divine support.

This phrase refers to practices that existed in the Age of Ignorance, and which sometimes occur today, where occasionally, in response to the killing of one person from a tribe, the tribe of the victim sheds much blood.

Or, in response to the killing of one person, they kill other innocent and defenseless individuals besides the murderer. As we read in the customs of the Age of Ignorance, whenever a prominent person from a tribe was killed, the victim's tribe was not satisfied with killing the murderer; rather, it was necessary to kill the chief of the murderer's tribe or another prominent individual, even if they had no part in the murder. [^1]

In our era as well, crimes sometimes occur that make the criminals of the Age of Ignorance look pale in comparison. We witness such scenes especially from the usurping Israel; whenever a Palestinian fighter kills one of their soldiers, they immediately drop their bombs on Palestinian women and children, sometimes slaughtering dozens of defenseless and innocent humans in exchange for one person.

We see the exact same meaning in the imposed war that the Ba'athist mercenaries have launched today against our Islamic country; let future history judge in this regard.

The issue of observing justice even regarding the murderer is at such a level that we read in the testament of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, where he said:

"O children of Abdul-Muttalib! Let me not find you wading through the blood of Muslims, saying 'The Commander of the Faithful has been killed,' and shedding blood on that pretext. Behold, none shall be killed for me except my killer (Abdur-Rahman ibn Muljam al-Muradi). Look, if I die from this stroke of his, then strike him one stroke for his stroke, and do not mutilate the man." [^1]

[4- The Importance of Preserving the Property of Orphans]

The next verse explains the fourth instruction in this series of rulings. First, it addresses the importance of preserving the property of orphans, and with a tone similar to what was mentioned regarding acts contrary to chastity in the previous verses, it says: "And do not approach the property of an orphan" (وَلَا تَقْرَبُوا مَالَ الْيَتِيمِ).

Not only should you not consume the property of orphans, but you must even respect its boundaries completely.

However, since this instruction might become a pretext for ignorant individuals who only look at negative aspects, causing them to leave the property of orphans without a guardian and abandon it to the elements, it immediately mentions a clear exception to this ruling, saying: "except in a way that is best" (إِلَّا بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ).

According to this comprehensive and eloquent expression, any disposal of the property of orphans intended for preservation, improvement, multiplication, and increase—where necessary aspects for preventing the waste of this property are considered—is permissible; rather, it is a manifest service to orphans who are unable to protect their own interests.

Of course, this situation continues until the orphan reaches the stage of intellectual and economic maturity, as the Quran mentions in the continuation of the verse: "until he reaches his maturity" (حَتَّىٰ يَبْلُغَ أَشُدَّهُ).

"Ashudd" is from the root "shadd," meaning a "firm knot." It was later expanded to refer to any kind of physical and spiritual strength. The meaning of "ashudd" here is reaching the age of puberty, but physical puberty alone is not sufficient here; rather, there must also be intellectual and economic maturity, such that the orphan can protect and maintain his own property. The choice of this expression is for this very purpose, which must, of course, be determined through definitive testing.

Undoubtedly, in every society, due to various events, there are orphans whom human considerations and every other calculation require to be placed under the protection of the well-wishers of society in all aspects. For this reason, Islam has given extraordinary importance to this issue, part of which we brought under verse 2 of Surah An-Nisa (refer to Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 249).

One thing to add here is that in some narrations, "orphan" is used in a broader sense, and it is applied to those who have been separated from their Imam and leader and the voice of truth does not reach their ears. This is a type of expansion in the concept of the orphan and a spiritual use of a material ruling.

[5- Fulfilling Covenants]

Then it addresses the issue of fulfilling covenants, saying: "And fulfill [every] covenant. Indeed, the covenant will be questioned" (وَأَوْفُوا بِالْعَهْدِ ۖ إِنَّ الْعَهْدَ كَانَ مَسْئُولًا).

Many social relations, the lines of the economic system, and political issues all revolve around the axis of covenants and pacts. If instability appears in them and the capital of trust is lost, the social order will soon collapse and a terrifying chaos will govern it. For this reason, in the verses of the Quran, great emphasis is placed on the issue of fulfilling covenants.

Covenant and pact have a broad meaning that includes private covenants among individuals regarding economic matters, business, marriage, and the like, as well as covenants and pacts established between nations and governments. Higher than that, it includes divine covenants and the covenants of heavenly leaders toward the nations, and the nations toward them. [^1]

[6- Combating Short-Changing]

The last ruling in the final verse under discussion relates to justice in measure and weight, observing the rights of the people, and combating short-changing. It says: "And give full measure when you measure" (وَأَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ إِذَا كِلْتُمْ).

"And weigh with an even and straight balance" (وَزِنُوا بِالْقِسْطَاسِ الْمُسْتَقِيمِ).

"For that is better and best in the end" (ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ وَأَحْسَنُ تَأْوِيلًا).

Key Points The Harm of Short-Changing

The first point to be noted here is that in the Holy Quran, the issue of combating short-changing and fraud in weight and measure is repeatedly emphasized. In one place, it places the observance of this order in the same rank as the order of creation in the expanse of the universe, saying: "And the heaven He raised and imposed the balance, that you not transgress within the balance" (Surah Ar-Rahman, verses 7 and 8).

This indicates that the issue of observing justice in measure and weight is not a small or unimportant matter; rather, it is part of the principle of justice and order that governs the entire existence.

In another place, it says with a severe and threatening tone: "Woe to those who give less [than due], who, when they take a measure from people, take in full. But if they give by measure or by weight to them, they cause loss. Do they not think that they will be resurrected for a tremendous Day?" (Surah Al-Mutaffifin, verses 1-4).

Even in the accounts of some prophets in the Holy Quran, we read that the sharp edge of their struggle, after the issue of polytheism, was directed toward short-changing. Ultimately, that oppressive people paid no heed and were caught and destroyed by severe divine punishment (refer to Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 249, under verse 85 of Surah Al-A'raf regarding the mission of Shu'ayb in Midian).

Fundamentally, right, justice, order, and calculation in everything and everywhere are a basic and vital principle. As we said, it is a principle that governs the entire world of existence. Therefore, any deviation from this principle is dangerous and has an evil end. Specifically, short-changing destroys the capital of trust and confidence, which is a major pillar of exchange, and disrupts the economic system.

It is very regrettable that we sometimes see non-Muslims being more proactive in observing this principle than some irresponsible Muslims, trying to send their goods to world markets with exactly the same weight and measure written on them, without any deficiency, and thereby gaining the trust of others.

Yes, they know that even if one is a person of this world, the way is not to betray in transactions.

It is also noteworthy that from a legal perspective, short-changers are guarantors and debtors to the buyers; therefore, their repentance is not possible except by paying the rights they have usurped. Even if they do not know the owners, they must give the equivalent as "Rad al-Mazalim" (restitution of wrongs) to the needy on behalf of the original owners.

2- Another point is that sometimes the issue of short-changing is generalized such that it includes any type of under-working or negligence in performing duties. In this way, a worker who falls short in his work, a teacher or professor who does not teach properly, or an employee who does not show up for work on time and does not show the necessary care—all are included in this ruling and share in its consequences.

Of course, the wording of the verses mentioned above does not directly include this generalization; rather, it is a rational expansion. However, the expression we read in Surah Ar-Rahman: "And the heaven He raised and imposed the balance, that you not transgress within the balance," hints at this generalization.

3- "Qistas" (pronounced with a kasra or damma on the qaf) means a scale. Some consider it a Roman word, and some Arabic. Sometimes it is said to be originally composed of two words: "qist" meaning justice and "tas" meaning the pan of a scale. Some have said "qistas" is a large scale, while "mizan" is also used for small scales. [^1] In any case, "al-qistas al-mustaqim" is a correct and sound scale that weighs justly, without any deficiency!

Interestingly, in a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of this word, we read:

"It is the scale that has a tongue (pointer)." [^1]

This indicates that scales without a pointer do not show the movements of the pans accurately, but when a scale has a pointer, the slightest movements of the pans are reflected on the pointer, and justice is fully observed.

[^1]: Tafsir Majma', Vol. 6, p. 414. [^1]: Sahih Bukhari and Sahih Muslim, as quoted in Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 5, p. 323. Ayahs

وَلَا تَقْفُ مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْبَصَرَ وَٱلْفُؤَادَ كُلُّ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَانَ عَنْهُ مَسْـُٔولًا (٣٦)
Do not pursue that of which you have no knowledge. Indeed hearing, eyesight, and the heart—all of these are accountable. (36)
وَلَا تَمْشِ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا ۖ إِنَّكَ لَن تَخْرِقَ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَلَن تَبْلُغَ ٱلْجِبَالَ طُولًا (٣٧)
Do not walk exultantly on the earth. Indeed, you will neither pierce the earth, nor reach the mountains in height. (37)
كُلُّ ذَٰلِكَ كَانَ سَيِّئُهُۥ عِندَ رَبِّكَ مَكْرُوهًا (٣٨)
The evil of all these is detestable to your Lord. (38)
ذَٰلِكَ مِمَّآ أَوْحَىٰٓ إِلَيْكَ رَبُّكَ مِنَ ٱلْحِكْمَةِ ۗ وَلَا تَجْعَلْ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ فَتُلْقَىٰ فِى جَهَنَّمَ مَلُومًا مَّدْحُورًا (٣٩)
These are among [precepts] that your Lord has revealed to you of wisdom. Do not set up another god besides Allah, or you will be cast into hell, being blameworthy and banished [from His mercy]. (39)
أَفَأَصْفَىٰكُمْ رَبُّكُم بِٱلْبَنِينَ وَٱتَّخَذَ مِنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِنَـٰثًا ۚ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَقُولُونَ قَوْلًا عَظِيمًا (٤٠)
Did your Lord prefer you for sons, and [Himself] adopt females from among the angels? Indeed, you say a monstrous word! (40)

Commentary

1- Follow Only Knowledge

In the preceding verses, we studied a series of the most fundamental Islamic teachings and rulings, ranging from Monotheism (Tawhid)—which is the essence of these teachings—to instructions regarding various aspects of the individual and social lives of human beings.

In the verses under discussion, we reach the final section of these rulings, in which several important commands are mentioned.

1- First, it speaks of the necessity of investigation in all matters, stating:

"And do not follow that of which you have no knowledge" (وَ لا تَقْفُ ما لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ).

Neither in your personal actions follow other than knowledge, nor when judging others; do not bear witness without knowledge, and do not adopt a belief without knowledge.

In this way, the prohibition against following other than knowledge has a broad meaning that encompasses matters of belief, speech, testimony, judgment, and action. The fact that some commentators have limited it to only a portion of these matters has no clear justification, for la taqfu is derived from the root qafw (rhyming with ‘afw), meaning to follow after something. We know that following other than knowledge is a broad concept that includes everything we have mentioned.

Based on this, the criterion for discernment in everything is knowledge and certainty; anything else—whether it be "conjecture and suspicion," "guesswork and estimation," or "doubt and probability"—is unreliable.

Those who form beliefs based on these things, or sit in judgment, or give testimony, or even behave according to them in their personal actions, have stepped contrary to this explicit Islamic command.

In other words, neither rumors can be the scale for judgment, testimony, and action, nor can conjectural evidence or uncertain reports that reach us from unreliable sources.

At the end of the verse, it states the reason for this prohibition: "Indeed, the hearing, the sight, and the heart—about all those [one] will be questioned" (إِنَّ السَّمْعَ وَ الْبَصَرَ وَ الْفُؤادَ كُلُّ أُولئِكَ كانَ عَنْهُ مَسْؤُلًا).

These responsibilities exist because the words a person utters without knowledge and certainty are either heard from unreliable individuals, or he claims to have seen what he did not see, or he has fallen into baseless and unfounded judgments in his thinking that do not correspond with reality. For this reason, his eyes, ears, thoughts, and intellect will be questioned: Did you truly have faith in these matters when you testified, or judged, or believed in them and aligned your actions with them?! Although some commentators have said that the purpose of questioning these organs is to question their owners, given that the Quran explicitly states in other verses (such as verse 21 of Surah Fussilat) that on the Day of Resurrection, the organs of the human body and even the skin will speak and reveal the truths, there is no reason for us to abandon the literal meaning of the verse and not say that these organs themselves will be questioned.

As for why, among the human senses, only the eyes and ears are mentioned, the reason is clear: because human sensory information is mostly obtained through these two channels, and the rest are overshadowed by them.

An Important Lesson for Establishing Social Order

In the verse we read, one of the most important principles of social life is mentioned, the disregard of which will result in nothing but social chaos and the destruction of human relationships and emotional bonds.

If this Quranic program were truly implemented precisely throughout human society and all human communities, many of the disorders originating from rumor-mongering, atmosphere-creating, hasty judgments, baseless suspicions, and doubtful or false news would be eliminated.

Otherwise, chaos in social relations will prevail everywhere; no one will be safe from the ill suspicions of another, no one will trust another, and the honor and reputation of individuals will always be at risk.

In many other verses of the Quran and Islamic narrations, this subject has been emphasized, including:

1- Verses that severely rebuke faithless individuals for following conjecture, such as: وَ ما يَتَّبِعُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ إِلَّا ظَنًّا إِنَّ الظَّنَّ لا يُغْنِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَيْئاً: "And most of them follow not except assumption. Indeed, assumption avails not against the truth at all" (Surah Yunus, verse 36).

2- Elsewhere, following conjecture is placed alongside following whims and desires: إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَ ما تَهْوَى الْأَنْفُسُ: "They follow not except assumption and what [their] souls desire" (An-Najm, verse 23).

3- In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "It is of the reality of faith that your speech does not exceed your knowledge [and you do not say more than what you know]." [^1]

4- In another hadith from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام, we read that he narrated from his forefathers: "It is not for you to speak whatever you wish, for Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, says: 'And do not follow that of which you have no knowledge.'" [^2]

5- In another hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said: "Beware of suspicion, for suspicion is the falsest of falsehoods." [^3]

6- Someone came to Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام and said: "I have neighbors who have singing slave-girls; they sing and play music, and sometimes when I go to relieve myself (to the restroom), I prolong my stay to listen to their melodies, even though I did not go for that purpose." Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said: "Have you not heard the words of Allah where He says: 'Indeed, the hearing, the sight, and the heart—about all those [one] will be questioned'?" He replied: "It is as if I had never heard this verse from anyone, neither Arab nor non-Arab, and I now abandon this act and repent to the threshold of Allah." [^4] In some sources of hadith, in the footnote of this narration, we read that the Imam commanded him: "Rise and perform the bath of repentance (Ghusl) and pray as much as you can, for you were doing a very evil thing, and had you died in that state, your responsibility would have been immense!"

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 18, p. 16. [^2]: Wasa'il, Vol. 18, p. 17. [^3]: Wasa'il, Vol. 18, p. 38. [^4]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 164.

From these verses and hadiths narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of Guidance عليهم السلام, it becomes clear how Islam holds the human eye and ear responsible: so that one does not speak until he sees, does not judge until he hears, and does not believe, act, or adjudicate without investigation, knowledge, and certainty.

Following conjecture, guesswork, estimation, rumors, and anything other than knowledge and certainty creates great dangers for the individual and society, each of which alone causes significant damage, including:

1- Relying on non-knowledge is the source of violating the rights of individuals or giving rights to those who do not deserve them. 2- Following non-knowledge endangers the reputation of honorable people and discourages those who serve. 3- Relying on non-knowledge makes the market for rumors and rumor-mongers hot and prosperous. 4- Following non-knowledge strips the spirit of investigation and curiosity from a person and makes him a gullible and simple-minded individual. 5- Following non-knowledge disrupts warm and friendly relations in the home, the market, the workplace, and everywhere, making people suspicious of one another. 6- Following non-knowledge destroys our intellectual independence and prepares the soul to accept any kind of poisonous propaganda. 7- Following non-knowledge is the source of hasty judgments and immediate choices regarding everyone and everything, which itself is the cause of various failures and regrets.

The Way to Combat Illusion-Oriented Thinking

The only question that remains here is how we can liberate ourselves and society from this ugly and disastrous habit and its painful consequences.

The answer to this question requires a lengthy discussion, but as a concise set of instructions, the following points should be noted:

A- The painful consequences of this act must be repeatedly pointed out to people through various means, and they should be asked to reflect on the sinister effects of following non-knowledge.

B- The Islamic worldview and way of thinking must be revived in people so they know that Allah is watching them at all times; He is All-Hearing and All-Seeing, and He is even aware of our thoughts: يَعْلَمُ خائِنَةَ الْأَعْيُنِ وَ ما تُخْفِي الصُّدُورُ (Surah Ghafir, verse 19): "He knows that which deceives the eyes and what the breasts conceal." Every word we speak is recorded, and every step we take is written in our account, and we are responsible for all our actions, judgments, and beliefs.

C- The level of intellectual growth must be raised, for following non-knowledge is often the work of the simple-minded masses and unaware individuals who, upon hearing a baseless rumor, immediately cling to it, judge by it, and take it as a model for their actions.

2- Do Not Be Arrogant!

The next verse rises to combat pride and arrogance, prohibiting believers from it with a vivid and clear expression. Addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says: "And do not walk upon the earth exultantly" (وَ لا تَمْشِ فِي الْأَرْضِ مَرَحاً) [^1] "Indeed, you will never tear the earth [apart], and you will never reach the mountains in height" (إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَخْرِقَ الْأَرْضَ وَ لَنْ تَبْلُغَ الْجِبالَ طُولًا).

[^1]: "Marah" (rhyming with farah) means intense joy over something false and baseless.

This refers to the fact that arrogant and proud individuals often stomp their feet firmly on the ground when walking to make people aware of their coming and going, and they stretch their necks toward the sky to distinguish their perceived superiority over those on earth! But the Quran says: If you stomp your foot on the ground, can you ever tear the earth apart, or are you but a tiny speck upon this massive earthen sphere?

Like an ant moving upon a massive rock, stomping its foot on that rock, while the rock laughs at its folly and lack of capacity.

Can you—no matter how much you stretch your neck—become equal to the mountains, or can you at most show your stature a few centimeters taller, while even the greatness of the highest mountain peaks on earth is nothing significant compared to this planet, and the earth itself is an insignificant speck in the collection of the universe?

So what is this pride and arrogance that you possess?!

It is noteworthy that the Quran does not directly discuss arrogance and pride as a dangerous internal trait, but rather points to its outward manifestations, even the simplest ones, and speaks of the manner of walking of the self-centered and brainless arrogant ones. This indicates that pride and arrogance, even at the level of their simplest effects, are reprehensible, unpleasant, and shameful.

It also indicates that the internal qualities of a human being, whatever they may be, will inevitably show themselves within his actions—in his way of walking, in his looking, in his speaking, and in all his deeds.

For this reason, as soon as we encounter the smallest manifestation of these traits in actions, we must realize that danger is near and that the reprehensible trait has nested in our soul, and we must immediately rise to combat it.

Furthermore, from what we have said, it can be clearly understood that the Quran's goal in what appears in the above verse (as well as in Surah Luqman and some other surahs) is to condemn pride and arrogance in general, not just in one specific form like walking.

For pride is the source of alienation from Allah and oneself, error in judgment, losing the path of truth, joining the line of Satan, and being tainted with various sins.

Ali عليه السلام says in the "Hammam" sermon regarding the qualities of the pious: "And their walking is with humility." [^1] Not only in the streets and markets, but their course of action in all life matters, and even in intellectual studies and the path of thoughts, is coupled with humility.

The practical program of the leaders of Islam is a very instructive model for every true Muslim in this regard.

In the biography of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: He never allowed people to walk on foot beside him while he was mounted; rather, he would say: "You go to such-and-such a place and I will come, and we shall meet there; walking on foot beside a rider causes pride in the rider and humiliation for the pedestrian!" We also read: The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would sit on the ground, eat simple food like the food of slaves, milk the sheep, and ride a bare donkey.

He performed such acts even when he reached the peak of power—such as the day of the Conquest of Mecca—so that people would not think that as soon as they reached a position, they should let the wind of pride and arrogance enter their heads, distance themselves from the people of the streets and the oppressed, and become strangers to the state of the hardworking masses.

In the accounts of Ali عليه السلام, we also read that he would bring water for the house and sometimes sweep the home.

And in the history of Imam al-Mujtaba عليه السلام, we read that despite having multiple mounts, he performed the pilgrimage to the House of Allah twenty times on foot and would say: "I perform this act out of humility before the presence of Allah." [^1]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 193. [^1]: In Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 106 and 107, we have also spoken about the harms of pride and arrogance.

The next verse, as an emphasis on all the rulings regarding the prohibition of Shirk (associating partners with Allah), murder, adultery, infanticide, misappropriating the property of orphans, harassing parents, and the like that passed in the previous verses, says: "All that—its evil is ever, in the sight of your Lord, detested" (كُلُّ ذلِكَ كانَ سَيِّئُهُ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ مَكْرُوهاً). [^2] From this expression, it becomes clear that contrary to the claims of the followers of the school of determinism (Jabr), Allah never willed for a sin to be committed by anyone, for if He had willed such a thing, it would not be consistent with the "detestation" and displeasure emphasized in this verse. Additionally, it becomes clear that the term makruh (detested/disliked) in the language of the Quran is used even for the greatest of sins.

[^2]: The pronoun in sayyi'uhu refers back to "dhalika" or to "kull," and its singular form is due to the singular form of these two words, even though it carries a plural meaning here.

3- Do Not Become a Polytheist!

Again, for further emphasis and to show that these wise rulings all originate from divine revelation, it adds: "That is from what your Lord has revealed to you, [O Muhammad], of wisdom" (ذلِكَ مِمَّا أَوْحى‌ إِلَيْكَ رَبُّكَ مِنَ الْحِكْمَةِ).

The expression "wisdom" (hikmah) indicates that these heavenly rulings, while originating from divine revelation, are also completely measurable and understandable by the scale of reason. Who can deny the ugliness of Shirk, or murder, or harassing parents, as well as the vileness of adultery, pride, arrogance, injustice to orphans, and the sinister consequences of breaking covenants and the like?

In other words, these rulings are proven both through rational wisdom and through divine revelation. The principles of all divine rulings are such, even though their details often cannot be discerned with the dim lamp of reason and must only be understood under the powerful spotlight of revelation.

Some commentators have also inferred from the term "wisdom" that the various rulings mentioned in the previous verses are among the fixed, firm, and non-abrogable rulings that have existed in all heavenly religions. For example, Shirk, murder, adultery, and breaking covenants are not things that have been permitted in any religion; thus, these rulings are considered part of the muhkamat (firmly established) and fixed laws.

Then, just as the beginning of these rulings started with the prohibition of Shirk, it ends them with an emphasis on the prohibition of Shirk, saying: "And [O man], do not make [as equal] with Allah another deity" (وَ لا تَجْعَلْ مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ).

"Lest you be thrown into Hell, blamed and banished" (فَتُلْقى‌ فِي جَهَنَّمَ مَلُوماً مَدْحُوراً).

In reality, Shirk and dualism are the essence of all deviations, crimes, and sins; therefore, the presentation of this series of fundamental Islamic rulings began with Shirk and ended with Shirk as well.

In the last verse under discussion, it refers to one of the superstitious thoughts of the polytheists and thereby clarifies the basis of their logic and thinking: many of them believed that the angels were the daughters of God, while they themselves felt shame and disgrace at hearing the name of a daughter and considered her birth in their home a cause of misery and humiliation!

The Quran takes evidence from their own logic, saying: "Then, has your Lord chosen you for [having] sons and taken from among the angels daughters?" (أَ فَأَصْفاكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ بِالْبَنِينَ وَ اتَّخَذَ مِنَ الْمَلايِكَةِ إِناثاً).

Undoubtedly, female children, like male children, are divine blessings and have no difference in terms of human value. Fundamentally, the survival of the human race is impossible without either of them, and for this reason, the belittling of daughters—which was and is specific to ignorant (Jahili) societies—is a superstitious thought whose roots we have explained in previous discussions. [^1] But the goal of the Quran is to condemn them using their own logic: what ignorant people you are to attribute to your Lord something that you yourselves are ashamed of.

Then, at the end of the verse, it states as a decisive judgment: "Indeed, you say a grave saying" (إِنَّكُمْ لَتَقُولُونَ قَوْلًا عَظِيماً).

A statement that is consistent with no logic and is baseless from several aspects, because:

1- The belief in the existence of a child for Allah is a great insult to His holy presence, for He is neither a body nor does He have bodily attributes, nor does He need the survival of lineage. Therefore, the belief in a child for Him stems purely from a lack of knowledge of His pure attributes.

2- How is it that you consider the children of Allah to be all daughters? While you grant the daughter the lowest status. This foolish belief is another insult to Allah according to your own perceptions.

3- Above all, this belief is an insult to the status of the divine angels, who are the obedient servants of Truth and those brought near to His presence. You are terrified of hearing the name of a daughter, yet you consider these near ones of Allah to be all daughters.

Yes, considering these matters, it becomes clearly evident that this saying is a very grave and "great" saying—great in terms of deviation from reality, great in terms of sin and punishment, and finally, great according to your own custom and habit, that same ugly custom and habit that belittled innocent daughters and diminished their respect.

As for why the polytheists—the Arab polytheists—imagined the angels to be daughters of God? And also why the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance buried daughters alive and were terrified of hearing their names?! And also the role of Islam in reviving the value and status of women and combating any form of belittling the female gender—detailed discussions have appeared in Volume 11 under verses 57 to 59 of Surah An-Nahl, which we recommend studying again.

[^1]: Refer to Volume 11 of Tafsir Nemouneh, under verses 58 and 59 of Surah An-Nahl.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَا فِى هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ لِيَذَّكَّرُوا۟ وَمَا يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا نُفُورًا (٤١)
Certainly We have variously paraphrased [the principles of guidance] in this Quran so that they may take admonition, but it increases them only in aversion. (41)
قُل لَّوْ كَانَ مَعَهُۥٓ ءَالِهَةٌ كَمَا يَقُولُونَ إِذًا لَّٱبْتَغَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ ذِى ٱلْعَرْشِ سَبِيلًا (٤٢)
Say, ‘Were there [other] gods besides Him, as they say, they would surely have encroached on the Lord of the Throne. (42)
سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا (٤٣)
Immaculate is He, and greatly exalted above what they say!’ (43)
تُسَبِّحُ لَهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ ٱلسَّبْعُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ وَمَن فِيهِنَّ ۚ وَإِن مِّن شَىْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِۦ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تَفْقَهُونَ تَسْبِيحَهُمْ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ حَلِيمًا غَفُورًا (٤٤)
The seven heavens glorify Him, and the earth [too], and whoever is in them. There is not a thing but celebrates His praise, but you do not understand their glorification. Indeed, He is all-forbearing, all-forgiving. (44)

Commentary

How Do They Flee from the Truth?!

Since the discussion in the previous verses concluded with the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and polytheism (Shirk), the verses under discussion pursue this matter with clear and decisive expression.

First, it speaks of the extraordinary stubbornness of a group of polytheists in the face of various proofs of Monotheism, saying: "We have brought forth in this Quran various types of arguments and effective statements so that they may take heed and step onto the path of truth, but all these explanations and arguments only increased their aversion and flight" (وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَا فِي هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنِ لِيَذَّكَّرُوا وَمَا يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا نُفُورًا).

"Sarf" (صرف) is derived from the root "Tasrif" (تصریف), meaning to change or transform, and especially considering it is in the "Taf'il" form, it also encompasses the meaning of frequency and abundance.

Since the Quranic statements regarding the proof of Monotheism and the negation of polytheism are sometimes presented in the guise of logical reasoning, sometimes through primordial nature (Fitrah), at times in the form of threats, and at others as encouragement—in short, utilizing various methods and arts of speech to inform and awaken the polytheists—the expression "Sarrafna" (We have explained in various ways) is highly appropriate.

With this expression, the Quran says: We entered through every door and utilized every path to ignite the lamp of Monotheism in the hearts of these blind-hearted people, yet a group of them is so stubborn, prejudiced, and headstrong that not only do these statements not bring them closer to the truth, but they increase their aversion and distance! Here, a question comes to mind: if these diverse statements yield the opposite result, what is the benefit of mentioning them?! The answer to this question is clear: the Quran was not revealed for a specific individual or group, but for the entire human society. Certainly, not all humans are like this; rather, there are many who hear these various proofs and find the path of truth. Each group of these seekers of truth benefits from one type of Quranic expression and awakens, and this effect alone is sufficient for the revelation of these verses, even if the blind-hearted derive the opposite result from them.

Furthermore, although this prejudiced and stubborn group is on the wrong path and is miserable, those who seek the truth can better find the path of truth by comparing themselves to them; for in the confrontation between light and darkness, the value of light is better realized, and "manners can be learned even from the ill-mannered."

Additionally, a lesson in the field of educational and missionary matters can be learned from this verse: to achieve high educational goals, one should not use only one method, but should benefit from various paths and different means, as people have different tastes and talents. To influence each one, one must enter through a specific way, and this is one of the arts of eloquence.

The Argument of Mutual Hindrance (Dalil al-Tamanu')

The next verse points to one of the proofs of Monotheism, which is known among scholars and philosophers as the "Argument of Mutual Hindrance" (Dalil al-Tamanu'). It says:

O Prophet, "Say to them: If there were other gods with the Almighty Allah—as they imagine—then these gods would have sought a way to the Great Lord of the Throne to overcome Him" (قُلْ لَوْ كَانَ مَعَهُ آلِهَةٌ كَمَا يَقُولُونَ إِذًا لَابْتَغَوْا إِلَىٰ ذِي الْعَرْشِ سَبِيلًا).

Although the phrase "then they would have sought a way to the Lord of the Throne" literally means they would find a way toward Him, the style of speech indicates that the intention is finding a way to overcome Him. Especially the use of the expression "Lord of the Throne" (Dhi al-Arsh) instead of "Allah" points to this very matter—meaning they too would want to become the "Owner of the Supreme Throne" and rule over the expanse of existence, and for this reason, they would rise to contend with Him.

In any case, it is natural that every power-holder wants to make his power more complete and expand his realm of governance. If there truly were multiple gods, this conflict and mutual hindrance over power and the expansion of rule would break out among them [1]. Here, it might be asked: what prevents multiple gods from ruling this world in cooperation with one another? And why is it necessary for them to rise in conflict?!

In response to this question, one must pay attention to the reality that—regardless of the fact that the desire for perfection and expansion of power is natural for every being, and regardless of the fact that the gods the polytheists believed in possessed many human traits, one of the clearest being the desire for more governance and power—regardless of all this, the requirement of multiplicity of existence is fundamentally "difference." For if there were no difference in approach, program, or other aspects, multiplicity would have no meaning; rather, both would be one and the same thing (reflect deeply).

A similar discussion appears in verse 22 of Surah Al-Anbiya, where it says:

لَوْ كَانَ فِيهِمَا آلِهَةٌ إِلَّا اللَّهُ لَفَسَدَتَا: "Had there been within the heavens and earth gods besides Allah, they both would have been ruined."

Make no mistake; although these two statements are similar in some respects, they point to two different proofs. One refers back to the "corruption of the world's order" due to the multiplicity of gods, while the other, regardless of the world's order, speaks of the existence of mutual hindrance and conflict among multiple gods (God willing, we will also speak about this under verse 22 of Surah Al-Anbiya).

And since in the expressions of the polytheists, the Great Allah has been lowered to the level of a party in a conflict, the next verse immediately says: "Exalted is He and high above what they say by great sublimity" (سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَىٰ عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا).

In fact, in this short sentence, with four different expressions, the purity of His Majesty from such improper attributions is stated:

  1. "Allah is pure from these deficiencies and improper attributions" (Subhanahu).
  2. "He is higher than what they say" (wa Ta'ala 'amma yaqulun).
  3. By mentioning the word "Uluwwan" (sublimity), which is an absolute object (maf'ul mutlaq) used for emphasis, this statement is reinforced.
  4. Finally, with the expression "Kabiran" (great), a new emphasis is added to it.

It is noteworthy that the phrase "from what they say" ('amma yaqulun) has a broad meaning that includes all their improper attributions and the implications they entail (reflect deeply).

Then, to prove the greatness of the Lord's station and that He is beyond the imagination, comparison, conjecture, and illusion of the polytheists, it proceeds to describe the glorification (tasbih) of the beings of the world before His Holy Essence, saying: "The seven heavens and the earth and whatever is in them glorify Him" (تُسَبِّحُ لَهُ السَّمَاوَاتُ السَّبْعُ وَالْأَرْضُ وَمَنْ فِيهِنَّ).

Not only the heavens and the earth, but "there is not a thing except that it exalts [Allah] with His praise, but you do not understand their glorification" (وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ وَلَٰكِنْ لَا تَفْقَهُونَ تَسْبِيحَهُمْ).

Despite this, "Indeed, He is ever Forbearing and Forgiving" (إِنَّهُ كَانَ حَلِيمًا غَفُورًا).

He does not immediately punish you for your polytheism and disbelief; rather, He gives sufficient respite and keeps the doors of repentance open for you so that the proof may be completed.

In other words, you have the ability to hear the murmur of the glorification of beings from within all the particles of the world and realize the One Almighty Allah, but you fall short. Allah does not immediately call you to account and punish you for this shortcoming; instead, He gives you the maximum opportunity and chance on the path of recognizing Monotheism and abandoning polytheism.

The Universal Glorification and Praise of the Beings of the World

In various verses of the Quran, the glorification and praise of the beings of the universe before the Great Allah is mentioned, and perhaps the most explicit is the verse under discussion, which, without any exception, considers all beings of the universe—the earth and heaven, stars and galaxies, humans and animals, the leaves of trees, and even the tiny particles of atoms—as participants in this universal glorification and praise.

The Quran says: The universe is a single murmur and clamor; every being is busy with the praise and glorification of the Truth in its own way, and a silent roar resonates across the expanse of existence that the heedless are unable to hear. However, the thinkers whose hearts and souls are alive and illuminated by the light of faith hear this sound well from every side with their ears and souls. As the poet says:

If an eye from the unseen opened for you, The particles of the world would become your confidants! The speech of water, the speech of earth, and the speech of clay, Are perceptible to the senses of the people of the heart! All particles in the hidden world, Speak to you day and night: "We are hearing, seeing, and conscious, With you strangers, we are silent." Go from the inanimate toward the Soul of souls, To hear the clamor of the components of the world. The glorification of inanimate objects will come to you openly, And will wipe away the whispers of interpretations.

However, there is much discussion among scholars, philosophers, and commentators regarding the interpretation of the reality of this praise and glorification:

Some have considered it a "situational" (hali) praise and glorification, and some "verbal" (qali). A summary of their views, along with what is acceptable to us, follows:

  1. A group believes that all particles of the beings of this world—whether those we consider rational or the inanimate and non-rational—all possess a type of perception and consciousness, and in their own world, they glorify and praise Allah, even though we are unable to understand the manner of their perception and feeling or hear the murmur of their praise and glorification.

Verses such as: وَإِنَّ مِنْهَا لَمَا يَهْبِطُ مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ: "And indeed, there are some [stones] which fall down for fear of Allah" (Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 74).

And: فَقَالَ لَهَا وَلِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيَا طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا قَالَتَا أَتَيْنَا طَائِعِينَ: "He said to it and to the earth, 'Come [into being], willingly or by compulsion.' They said, 'We have come willingly'" (Surah Fussilat, verse 11)... and the like can be taken as evidence for this belief.

  1. Many believe that this glorification and praise is what we call "the language of disposition" (zaban-e hal); it is real, not metaphorical, but it is through the language of disposition, not verbal speech (reflect deeply).

To explain: It often happens that we say to someone whose face and eyes show signs of discomfort, pain, suffering, and sleeplessness: "Even though you do not speak of your discomfort, your eyes say that you did not sleep last night, and your face bears witness that you are suffering from agonizing pain!" This "language of disposition" is sometimes so strong and powerful that it overshadows the "verbal language" and rises to contradict it. As the poet says:

I said that with trickery and charm, I would hide the secret within! It does not remain hidden when blood, Flows from my eyes!

This is the same thing that Ali عليه السلام says in his famous saying: "No one conceals a secret except that it appears in the slips of his tongue and the expressions of his face" [1].

On the other hand, can it be denied that a very beautiful painting, which is a masterpiece of true art, bears witness to the taste and skill of the painter and praises and lauds him?

Can it be denied that the collection of poems by great and famous poets tells of their high talent and constantly praises them?

Can it be denied that massive buildings, large factories, complex electronic brains, and the like, speak with a wordless language of their builder, inventor, and innovator, and each in its own capacity praises them?

Therefore, it must be accepted that the wondrous world of existence, with its amazing order, with all those secrets and mysteries, with its dazzling greatness, and with its awe-inspiring intricacies, all "glorify and praise" Allah.

Is "glorification" (tasbih) anything other than declaring Him pure and free from defects? The structure and order of this universe say that its Creator is free from any kind of deficiency or fault.

Is "praise" (hamd) anything other than the expression of attributes of perfection? The system of the world of creation speaks of Allah's attributes of perfection, His infinite knowledge, His boundless power, and His vast and all-encompassing wisdom.

Especially with the progress of human science and knowledge, and the unveiling of corners of the secrets and mysteries of this vast world, this universal praise and glorification of beings has become more apparent.

If one day that subtle poet considered every leaf of the green trees a book of the knowledge of the Creator, today's botanists have written not just a book but volumes about these leaves. They have discussed everything from the mysterious structure of its smallest components, namely the cells, to the seven layers of the leaf, its respiratory system, the channels for irrigation and nutrition, and other highly complex characteristics of leaves in these books.

Therefore, every leaf day and night sings the melody of Monotheism and spreads the loud voice of its glorification within the garden and forest, atop the mountains, and in the curves of the valleys, but the heedless understand nothing of it; they consider it silent and tongue-tied! This meaning for the universal glorification and praise of beings is completely understandable and does not require us to posit perception and consciousness for all particles of the universe, as there is no decisive proof for it, and the previous verses also most likely express the same language of disposition.

Answer to a Question

However, one question remains: if the purpose of glorification and praise is the narration of the creation's order regarding the purity, greatness, and power of Allah, and it explains His "negative" and "positive" attributes, then why does the Quran say "you do not understand their glorification"? If some do not understand, at least the scholars do.

But this question has two answers:

First, the address is to the majority of ignorant people, especially the polytheists, and the believing scholars, who are in the minority, are excluded from this generality, as every general rule has an exception.

Second, what we know of the secrets of this world compared to what we do not know is like a drop compared to the sea and a speck of straw compared to a massive mountain; if we think correctly, it cannot even be called science or knowledge.

My knowledge reached the point, Where I realized that I am ignorant!

Therefore, in reality, we do not hear the glorification and praise of these beings, even if we are scholars, because what we hear is only one word from a massive book. On this account, it can be said as a general judgment addressed to all the worlds: you do not understand the glorification and praise of the beings of the universe which they have through the language of disposition, because what you understand is so insignificant that it does not count.

  1. Some commentators have also suggested that the universal praise and glorification of beings here is a combination of the language of "disposition" and "speech," or in other words, "ontological" (takwini) and "legislative" (tashri'i) glorification. This is because many humans and all angels praise and laud Him out of perception and consciousness, and all particles of beings also discuss the greatness and majesty of the Creator with their language of disposition.

Although these two types of praise and glorification are different, they share a common ground in the broad concept of the words "praise" and "glorification."

However, as is evident, the second interpretation, with the explanation we provided, is the most appealing of all.

A Glimpse into the Narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt [Regarding the Universal Glorification of Beings]

In the narrations reached from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, interesting expressions are seen in this regard, including:

One of the companions of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: I asked about the interpretation of the verse وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ. The Imam عليه السلام said: "Yes, everything glorifies and praises Allah, even when a wall cracks and a sound is heard from it, that too is its glorification" [1]!

It is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that he said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله forbade branding animals on their faces and striking their faces, because they glorify and praise their Lord" [2].

And it is also narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "No bird is caught on land or sea, and no wild animal is trapped by a hunter, except due to its neglecting glorification" [3]!

Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام heard the sound of sparrows and said: Do you know what these are saying? Abu Hamza al-Thumali, who was one of the special companions of the Imam, says: I said no. He said: "They glorify their Lord, the Mighty and Majestic, and ask for their daily sustenance" [1].

In another hadith, we read that one day the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله came to Aisha and said: "Wash these two garments of mine." She said: "O Messenger of Allah, I washed them yesterday." He said: "Do you not know that a person's garment glorifies Allah, and when it becomes dirty and soiled, its glorification is cut off?" [2]!

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "An animal has six rights over its owner: he should not load it beyond its capacity, he should not use its back as a place for conversation (rather, when he meets someone and wants to talk, he should dismount and mount after finishing the talk), when he arrives at any station, he should first prepare its fodder, he should not brand its face, he should not strike it because it glorifies Allah, and he should offer it water when he passes by it" (so that if it is thirsty, it may drink) [3].

The collection of these narrations, some of which have subtle and delicate meanings, shows that this general rule of the glorification of beings encompasses everything without exception. All of this is completely consistent with what we said in the second interpretation (glorification in the sense of the language of disposition and ontology). The fact that we read in these narrations that when a garment becomes soiled and dirty, its glorification is cut off, may point to the fact that as long as beings have their pure natural face, they remind man of Allah, but when they lose their pure natural face, that reminder disappears.

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, No. 26. [1], [2], [3]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 168. [1], [2]: From Abu Nu'aym al-Isfahani in Hilyat al-Awliya (as cited in Tafsir al-Mizan). [3]: Al-Kafi, as cited in Tafsir al-Mizan.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا قَرَأْتَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ جَعَلْنَا بَيْنَكَ وَبَيْنَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ حِجَابًا مَّسْتُورًا (٤٥)
When you recite the Quran, We draw a hidden curtain between you and those who do not believe in the Hereafter, (45)
وَجَعَلْنَا عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَن يَفْقَهُوهُ وَفِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَقْرًا ۚ وَإِذَا ذَكَرْتَ رَبَّكَ فِى ٱلْقُرْءَانِ وَحْدَهُۥ وَلَّوْا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَـٰرِهِمْ نُفُورًا (٤٦)
and We cast veils on their hearts, lest they should understand it, and a deafness into their ears. When you mention your Lord alone in the Quran, they turn their backs in aversion. (46)
نَّحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَسْتَمِعُونَ بِهِۦٓ إِذْ يَسْتَمِعُونَ إِلَيْكَ وَإِذْ هُمْ نَجْوَىٰٓ إِذْ يَقُولُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ إِن تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلًا مَّسْحُورًا (٤٧)
We know best what they listen for, when they listen to you, and when they hold their secret talks, when the wrongdoers say, ‘[If you follow him] You will be following just a bewitched man.’ (47)
ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا۟ لَكَ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ فَضَلُّوا۟ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا (٤٨)
Look, how they coin epithets for you; so they go astray, and cannot find a way. (48)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of commentators, such as al-Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Fakhr al-Razi in al-Tafsir al-Kabir, and others, have stated regarding the occasion of revelation for the above verses that the first verse was revealed concerning a group of polytheists who used to harass the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله while he recited the Quran at night and offered prayers by the Kaaba. They would pelt him with stones and prevent people from being invited to Islam.

By His grace, Allah made it so they could not harm him (perhaps through the awe and terror He cast into their hearts regarding the Prophet) [1]. In another narration, we read that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recited the Quran, two polytheists would stand on his right and two on his left, clapping, whistling, and reciting poetry loudly so that the Prophet's voice would not reach the people's ears [2]. Ibn Abbas says: Abu Sufyan, Abu Jahl, and others would sometimes come to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and listen to his words. One day, one of them said to the others: "I don't understand at all what Muhammad is saying; I only see his lips moving!"

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan. [^2]: Tafsir al-Fakhr al-Razi, under the verse.

However, Abu Sufyan said: "I think some of his words are truth." Abu Jahl remarked: "He is mad," and Abu Lahab added: "He is a soothsayer." Another said: "He is a poet." Following these discordant words and false attributions, the above verses were revealed [1].

Commentary

The Arrogant Unaware and the Barriers to Recognition

Following the previous verses, a question arises for many: with the clarity of the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid)—such that all beings in the universe testify to it—why do the polytheists not accept this reality? Why do they hear these eloquent and expressive verses of the Quran and yet remain unawakened? The verses under discussion point toward the answer to this question.

The first verse says: "O Prophet! And when you recite the Quran, We place between you and those who do not believe in the Hereafter a hidden barrier" (وَ إِذا قَرَأْتَ الْقُرْآنَ جَعَلْنا بَيْنَكَ وَ بَيْنَ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ حِجاباً مَسْتُوراً).

This barrier and veil was the very stubbornness, prejudice, selfishness, pride, and ignorance that kept the truths of the Quran hidden from the perspective of their thought and intellect. It did not allow them to perceive clear realities such as Monotheism, the Resurrection, the truthfulness of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله call, and the like.

Regarding whether "hidden" (mastur) here is an adjective for the "barrier" (hijab), or for the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, or for the truths of the Quran, we have a discussion that you will read in the final points. Likewise, there is a discussion regarding the attribution of creating this barrier to Allah, which will follow there.

The next verse adds: "And We have placed over their hearts coverings lest they should understand it, and in their ears a heaviness" (وَ جَعَلْنا عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَنْ يَفْقَهُوهُ وَ فِي آذانِهِمْ وَقْراً).

And therefore, "when you mention your Lord alone in the Quran, they turn their backs in aversion" (وَ إِذا ذَكَرْتَ رَبَّكَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ وَحْدَهُ وَلَّوْا عَلى‌ أَدْبارِهِمْ نُفُوراً).

Truly, how strange is this flight from the truth, flight from salvation and rescue, and flight from prosperity, victory, and understanding.

A similar meaning appears in Surah al-Muddaththir, verses 50 and 51: كَأَنَّهُمْ حُمُرٌ مُسْتَنْفِرَةٌ فَرَّتْ مِنْ قَسْوَرَةٍ: "As if they were frightened donkeys, fleeing from a lion!"

It further adds: "We know best what they are listening for when they listen to you" (نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يَسْتَمِعُونَ بِهِ إِذْ يَسْتَمِعُونَ إِلَيْكَ‌). "And when they confer in private" (وَ إِذْ هُمْ نَجْوى‌).

"When the wrongdoers say to the believers: 'You follow none but a man who is bewitched'—meaning sorcerers have influenced his intellect and mind and impaired it" (إِذْ يَقُولُ الظَّالِمُونَ إِنْ تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلًا مَسْحُوراً).

In reality, they do not come to you to perceive the truth, nor do they listen to your words with the ear of the soul; rather, their goal is to come and cause disruption, attach labels, and—if they can—lead the believers astray. Fundamentally, one who has a covering over his heart and a heaviness in his ears (against hearing the truth) does not sit to listen to the words of men of truth except with such intentions.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr al-Razi, under the verse.

In the final verse, the address returns to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, providing a crushing response to this misguided group in a short phrase: "See how they strike similitudes for you (one calls you a sorcerer, another bewitched, one a soothsayer, and another mad), and so they have gone astray and cannot find a way [to the truth]" (انْظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا لَكَ الْأَمْثالَ فَضَلُّوا فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا).

It is not that the path is unfindable or the face of truth is hidden; rather, they lack seeing eyes, and they have disabled their own intellect and reason due to malice, ignorance, prejudice, and stubbornness.

Key Points

1- Summary and Review of These Verses

The above verses provide a precise depiction of the state of the misguided and the barriers to recognition. Generally, it states they have three major barriers to recognition; otherwise, seeing the face of truth is easy and simple.

First: A barrier has fallen between you and them. This barrier is in fact nothing but the grudges, jealousies, malice, and enmity they harbor toward you. These matters have caused them not to see your lofty personality and not to perceive the greatness of your speech and conduct—even virtues appear as vices in their eyes.

Second: Aside from the grudges and jealousy they had toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, a veil of ignorance and blind imitation had fundamentally fallen over their hearts, such that they were unwilling to hear the word of truth from anyone.

Finally, their third barrier to recognition was that even their tools of recognition, such as their ears, seemed to loathe the word of truth, casting it out and becoming "deaf" to it. Conversely, false words that suited their tastes and were pleasurable would quickly penetrate the depths of their souls.

Experience has specifically proven that a person hears with difficulty words they are not inclined toward, while they perceive words they love and adore with a special speed and sharp-earedness. It is as if internal inclinations affect a person's external senses and color them with their own hue.

The result of these three barriers was that, firstly, they fled from hearing the truth—especially when the mention of God's Oneness was made, which contradicted the foundation of all their polytheistic beliefs, they would flee rapidly. Secondly, to strengthen their deviant path, they resorted to false justifications regarding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his words, each accusing him of something: sorcerer, poet, or madman! Such is the fate of all enemies of truth, whose vile deeds and attributes act as a veil for them.

It is here that we say: if someone wishes to tread the Straight Path of truth and remain safe from deviation and misguidance, they must first and foremost strive to reform themselves.

One must cleanse the heart of malice, grudge, envy, and obstinacy, and the soul of pride, arrogance, and vanity—in short, cleanse one's existence of vile attributes. For whenever the mirror of the heart is cleansed of these vices and polished, all truths will reflect upon it. For this reason, sometimes pure-hearted unlettered individuals understand truths that an unrefined scholar lacks the power to perceive.

2- Why are these barriers attributed to Allah?

These verses, like many other verses of the Quran, attribute the existence of these barriers to Allah, saying: "We place a covering over their hearts," "We place a barrier between you and them," and "a heaviness in their ears."

These are expressions from which uninformed individuals might sense the school of predestination (determinism), whereas these are the very effects and properties of their own actions.

In reality, it is they themselves who create these barriers through their sins and ugly attributes. However, since the property of everything originates from Allah, and it is He who created this effect within evil deeds and vile attributes, it can also be attributed to Him.

We have spoken about this repeatedly in past discussions and have provided numerous evidences from the Quran.

3- What is the "Hidden Barrier" (Hijab Mastur)?

There is discussion among commentators regarding the meaning of "hijab mastur":

A- They consider "mastur" (hidden/veiled) as an adjective for "hijab" (barrier) and say the apparent meaning of the Quranic expression is that this barrier is hidden from sight. In fact, the barrier of malice, enmity, and jealousy is not something seen with the eye, yet it creates a thick veil between a person and the one they envy or hate.

B- Others have considered "mastur" to mean "satir" (veiling/covering), as the passive participle (ism al-maf'ul) sometimes carries the meaning of the active participle (ism al-fa'il), just as some commentators in this same series of verses have taken "mashur" (bewitched) to mean "sahir" (sorcerer) [1].

[^1]: It is narrated from al-Akhfash that he says: the passive participle sometimes means the active participle, such as "maymun" meaning "yamin" and "mash'um" meaning "sha'im."

C- Others consider the description of the barrier as "mastur" to be a metaphorical description, saying the intent is not that the barrier itself is hidden, but that the realities behind this barrier are hidden (such as the personality of the Prophet, the truth of his call, and the greatness of his words).

However, careful examination of these three interpretations shows that the first interpretation is more consistent with the apparent wording of the verse.

In some narrations, we also read that sometimes the staunch enemies of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would come to him while he was busy reciting the Quran with his companions, but they would not see him. It was as if the dazzling greatness of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله prevented these blind-hearted people from seeing, recognizing, or harming him.

4- What are "Akinnah" and "Waqr"?

"Akinnah" is the plural of "kinan," which originally means any covering used to hide something. However, "kinn" refers to a vessel in which something is kept protected. The plural of "kinn" is "aknan." This meaning then expanded to include anything that causes something to be hidden, such as a curtain, a house, or objects behind which a person hides.

"Waqr" refers to the heaviness that occurs in the ear, while "wiqr" refers to a heavy load.

5- Commentary on the phrase "بِما يَسْتَمِعُونَ بِهِ"

Two interpretations have been mentioned for the meaning of this phrase: Some, like al-Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan and Fakhr al-Razi in al-Tafsir al-Kabir, have taken it to mean the "motive for listening." That is, We know with what motive they listen to your words—not to perceive the truth, but for mockery, labeling, deviant justifications, and ultimately becoming lost and leading others astray.

Others (such as Allamah Tabataba'i in al-Mizan) have taken it to mean the "means of listening." That is, We are aware with what ears they listen to your words, and We are aware of their hearts and their private conversations (the first interpretation seems closer).

6- Why did they attribute being "bewitched" (mashur) to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله?

"Mashur" means "one who is bewitched," and "sahir" means "sorcerer."

The description of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as "mashur" by the enemies was either because they wanted to attribute madness to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله through this, saying that sorcerers had influenced his mind and intellect, causing—God forbid—a mental disturbance.

Some commentators have also suggested that "mashur" might mean "sahir" (as we said, the passive participle sometimes means the active participle). In this way, they attributed the extraordinary influence of the Prophet's words on the ready hearts of truth-seeking people to sorcery, which itself is an interesting implicit admission of the amazing influence of his speech.

7- The Polytheists' Terror of the Call to Monotheism

In the verses above, we read that the polytheists were particularly terrified by hearing the call to Monotheism and would flee, because the foundation of their entire life was polytheism and idolatry, and all the systems governing their society were polytheistic systems.

If Monotheism were established, not only their religious beliefs but also their social, economic, political, and cultural systems—which drew from polytheism—would collapse.

Governance would belong to the oppressed (mustad'afin), and the arrogant (mustakbirin) would fall. Exploitation, which is a result of polytheistic systems, would be abolished, and the negation of classes (the exploiting class and the exploited class) would take its place.

Therefore, the leaders of polytheism strove hard to ensure the call of Monotheism reached no one's ears. But they—as the above verses indicate—were wrongdoers and oppressors who oppressed both the masses of the weak and themselves, for every oppressor and deviant digs his own grave with his own hands.

Interestingly, the Quran says: the polytheists, in order to find a license for immorality and the continuation of sin, repeatedly asked when the Day of Resurrection would occur: بَلْ يُرِيدُ الْإِنْسانُ لِيَفْجُرَ أَمامَهُ يَسْئَلُ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ الْقِيامَةِ (Surah al-Qiyamah, verses 5 and 6), indicating that these are also excuses to flee from the burden of responsibility.

Ayahs

وَقَالُوٓا۟ أَءِذَا كُنَّا عِظَـٰمًا وَرُفَـٰتًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ خَلْقًا جَدِيدًا (٤٩)
They say, ‘What, when we have become bones and dust, shall we really be raised in a new creation?’ (49)
۞ قُلْ كُونُوا۟ حِجَارَةً أَوْ حَدِيدًا (٥٠)
Say, ‘[That is bound to happen] even if you should become stones, or iron, (50)
أَوْ خَلْقًا مِّمَّا يَكْبُرُ فِى صُدُورِكُمْ ۚ فَسَيَقُولُونَ مَن يُعِيدُنَا ۖ قُلِ ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ ۚ فَسَيُنْغِضُونَ إِلَيْكَ رُءُوسَهُمْ وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هُوَ ۖ قُلْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ قَرِيبًا (٥١)
or a creature more fantastic to your minds!’ They will say, ‘Who will bring us back?’ Say, ‘He who originated you the first time.’ They will nod their heads at you, and say, ‘When will that be?’ Say, ‘Maybe it is near! (51)
يَوْمَ يَدْعُوكُمْ فَتَسْتَجِيبُونَ بِحَمْدِهِۦ وَتَظُنُّونَ إِن لَّبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٥٢)
The day He calls you, you will respond to Him, praising Him, and you will think you remained [in the world] only for a little while.’ (52)

Commentary

Resurrection is Certain

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on "Monotheism" (Tawhid) and the struggle against polytheism. However, in the verses under discussion, the topic shifts to the "Resurrection" (Ma'ad), which serves as the universal complement to the issue of Monotheism. As we have previously stated, the most fundamental Islamic beliefs consist of faith in the Origin (the Creator) and the Resurrection. It is the belief in these two principles that cultivates a human being practically and morally, restrains them from impurity and sin, calls them to fulfill their duties, and propels them along the path of perfection. In these verses, three questions or three objections raised by the deniers of the Resurrection are answered:

First, it says: "And they said, 'When we have become bones and these bones have decayed and scattered, will we really be raised as a new creation?'" (وَ قالُوا أَ إِذا كُنَّا عِظاماً وَ رُفاتاً أَ إِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ خَلْقاً جَدِيداً) [^1]. Is it even possible for decayed and disintegrated bones, every particle of which has fallen in a different corner, to be gathered anew and then clothed in the garment of life?

What comparison is there between decayed, disintegrated bone and a living, powerful, and rational human being?

This expression, like many other Quranic expressions regarding the Resurrection, indicates that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله always spoke of "Physical Resurrection" in his invitation—that this body returns after disintegrating. Otherwise, had the discussion been solely about a spiritual resurrection, such objections from the opponents would have made no sense at all.

[^1]: "Rufat" (رُفات), on the weight of "kurāt," refers to anything that has become old, decayed, and disintegrated.

The Quran says in response to them: Say that even if you were more than decayed bones and dust, "even if you were stone or iron, Allah is still capable of clothing your bodies in the garment of life" (قُلْ كُونُوا حِجارَةً أَوْ حَدِيداً).

"Even if it be a creation that is harder than stone and iron, and further removed from life and vitality, and for this reason appears very great in your minds, Allah can still clothe it in the garment of life" (أَوْ خَلْقاً مِمَّا يَكْبُرُ فِي صُدُورِكُمْ).

It is clear that bones, after decaying, turn into dust, and dust always contains traces of life; plants grow from the soil, living creatures are nurtured in the soil, and the very origin of man's existence is from the soil. In short, soil is the gateway to life and vitality.

However, stone and iron, or entities harder than them, are much further removed from life and vitality. A plant never rises from the heart of stone or iron. Yet, the Quran states that in the presence of Allah's power, these things are of no consequence; whatever you are and whatever you become, the possibility of your return to life and vitality remains preserved.

Stones decay and turn into soil, and the buds of life rise from the heart of the soil.

Iron also decays and disintegrates, combining with other elements of this earthly sphere to become the source of life and vitality.

Every element imaginable on this earth—from metals and metalloids to organic substances—has been used in the construction of the human body. This shows that the capacity to transform into a living being exists in all entities of this world, though some are at a closer stage, such as soil, and some are further away, such as stone and iron.

Their second objection was: Very well, if we accept that these decayed and disintegrated bones are capable of returning to life, who has the power to perform this task? For they considered this transformation an extremely complex and difficult matter. "They say, 'Who will bring us back?'" (فَسَيَقُولُونَ مَنْ يُعِيدُنا).

The Quran provides the answer to this question as follows: "Say to them: The One who created you the first time" (قُلِ الَّذِي فَطَرَكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ).

If you doubt the "capacity of the recipient," you were dust in the beginning; what prevents you from becoming dust once more and returning to life?

And if you doubt the "power of the Agent," the same God who created you from dust in the beginning can repeat this act once more, for "the ruling regarding similar cases is the same in what is possible and what is impossible."

Finally, it addresses their third objection and says: "They will shake their heads at you in wonder and denial and say: 'When will this Resurrection occur?'" (فَسَيُنْغِضُونَ إِلَيْكَ رُؤُسَهُمْ وَ يَقُولُونَ مَتى‌ هُوَ).

"Fasayunghidun" (فَسَيُنْغِضُونَ) is derived from the root "inghad," meaning to move the head toward the opposite party out of amazement.

In reality, with this objection, they wanted to convey that even assuming this earthy matter is capable of being transformed into a human, and even if we accept the power of God, this is nothing more than a "deferred promise," and it is not known when it will occur.

If it is to be thousands or millions of years later, what impact does it have on our life today? Speak of the "cash" (the present) and leave the "credit" (the future).

The Quran says in response to them: "Say to them: Perhaps its time is near" (قُلْ عَسى‌ أَنْ يَكُونَ قَرِيباً).

And indeed, it is near, for whatever the total lifespan of this world may be, it is nothing more than a fleeting moment compared to the endless life in the other abode.

Furthermore, if the Day of Judgment seems distant by our small and limited scales, the threshold of the Resurrection—which is death—is near to all of us, for death is the "Minor Resurrection" (idha mata al-insan qamat qiyamatuh - "When a person dies, his resurrection begins"). It is true that death is not the "Great Resurrection," but it is a reminder of it.
