Additionally, the use of the expression "perhaps" ('asa) may be an indication that no one knows the exact date of the Day of Resurrection, not even the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself; this is among the sciences exclusive to the Essence of the Lord, and none but He is aware of it.

In the following verse, without mentioning a specific date for the Resurrection, it describes some of its characteristics as follows: "This return to life will be on the day He calls you from your graves, and whether you wish it or not, you will respond to His call and return to life while praising Allah" (يَوْمَ يَدْعُوكُمْ فَتَسْتَجِيبُونَ بِحَمْدِهِ).

And it is on that day that you will consider the interval between death and the Resurrection—that is, the period of Barzakh (the Purgatory)—to be short, and you will imagine that you tarried but a little while in the world of Barzakh (وَ تَظُنُّونَ إِنْ لَبِثْتُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا).

Yes, on that day, man will have the feeling that the period of Barzakh, however long it may have been, is nothing more than fleeting moments compared to the infinite life in the eternal world.

Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that this refers to the stay in this world; meaning, it is on that day that you will realize the life of this world was not very long—it was but a few short and very fleeting hours.

Ayahs

وَقُل لِّعِبَادِى يَقُولُوا۟ ٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ يَنزَغُ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ كَانَ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ عَدُوًّا مُّبِينًا (٥٣)
Tell My servants to speak in a manner which is the best. Indeed Satan incites ill feeling between them, and Satan is indeed man’s manifest enemy. (53)
رَّبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ ۖ إِن يَشَأْ يَرْحَمْكُمْ أَوْ إِن يَشَأْ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ ۚ وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكِيلًا (٥٤)
Your Lord knows you best. He will have mercy on you if He wishes, or punish you, if He wishes, and We did not send you to watch over them. (54)
وَرَبُّكَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَلَقَدْ فَضَّلْنَا بَعْضَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ زَبُورًا (٥٥)
Your Lord knows best whoever is in the heavens and the earth. Certainly, We gave some prophets an advantage over the others, and We gave David the Psalms. (55)
قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ فَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ كَشْفَ ٱلضُّرِّ عَنكُمْ وَلَا تَحْوِيلًا (٥٦)
Say, ‘Invoke those whom you claim [to be gods] besides Him. They have no power to remove your distress, nor to bring about any change [in your state]. (56)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ يَبْتَغُونَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِمُ ٱلْوَسِيلَةَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ وَيَرْجُونَ رَحْمَتَهُۥ وَيَخَافُونَ عَذَابَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ عَذَابَ رَبِّكَ كَانَ مَحْذُورًا (٥٧)
They [themselves] are the ones who supplicate, seeking a recourse to their Lord, whoever is nearer [to Him], expecting His mercy and fearing His punishment.’ Indeed your Lord’s punishment is a thing to beware of. (57)

Commentary

Logical Engagement with All Opponents

Since the preceding verses discussed the Origin (Creation) and the Resurrection, providing proofs for these two fundamental matters of faith, the verses under discussion teach the method of dialogue and reasoning with opponents, especially the polytheists. This is because no matter how sublime a school of thought may be, or how strong and powerful its logic, it will be ineffective if it is not coupled with the correct method of discussion and debate, or if harshness prevails instead of kindness and affection.

Therefore, in the first verse, He says: "Tell My servants to say that which is best" (وَ قُلْ لِعِبادِي يَقُولُوا الَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ‌).

"Best" in terms of content, "best" in terms of the manner of expression, and "best" in terms of being coupled with moral virtues and humane methods.

"For if they abandon the best speech and resort to harshness in words, contention, and obstinacy, Satan will sow discord and sedition among them" (إِنَّ الشَّيْطانَ يَنْزَغُ بَيْنَهُمْ‌).

And do not forget that Satan is lying in wait and is not idle, "for Satan has been an open enemy to man from the beginning" (إِنَّ الشَّيْطانَ كانَ لِلْإِنْسانِ عَدُوًّا مُبِيناً). Regarding who is meant by "servants" (عباد) in this verse, there are two different opinions among the commentators, each supported by certain indications:

  1. The term "servants" refers to the polytheistic servants. Although they have taken the path of error, Allah mentions them with the expression "My servants" (عبادى) to stir their human emotions, inviting them to seek the "best"—namely, the word of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the negation of polytheism—and to be wary of Satan's whispers. Thus, the purpose of stating these verses after mentioning the proofs of Monotheism and the Resurrection is to penetrate the hearts of the polytheists so that those who are ready may awaken and find the way.

The subsequent verses—as will be seen—are also consistent with this meaning.

The fact that this Surah is Meccan, considering that the command for Jihad had not yet been revealed and the only way of struggle was logical discourse, also supports this meaning.

  1. The word "My servants" refers to the believers, teaching them the method of debating with enemies. This is because sometimes novice believers, following their previous habits, would treat anyone who disagreed with them in belief with harshness, explicitly calling them people of Hell, punishment, wretchedness, and misguidance, while considering themselves the people of salvation. This caused the opponents to adopt a negative stance toward the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله call.

Furthermore, the insulting expressions used by the opponents toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—some of which were mentioned in previous verses, such as "bewitched," "madman," "soothsayer," or "poet"—sometimes caused the believers to lose their self-control and engage them in harsh verbal disputes, saying whatever they wished.

The "Quran" restrains the believers from this and invites them to gentleness and refinement in speech and the selection of the best words to avoid the corruption of Satan.

Of course, the word "between them" (بينهم) according to this interpretation means that Satan tries to sow discord between the believers and their opponents, or he tries to secretly penetrate the hearts of the believers and invite them to corruption and causing mischief (since yanzaghu is from the root nazgh, meaning to enter into an affair with the intention of causing corruption).

However, considering the sum of the indications, the second interpretation is more consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse, as the word "My servants" (عبادى) in the Quran is usually mentioned for the believers.

Additionally, in the occasion of revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) narrated by some commentators, we read that the polytheists in Mecca used to harass the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. When they were under pressure, they would insist to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Give us permission for Jihad (or permission for harshness in speech and retaliation)." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would say: "I have not yet been given such commands." At this time, the above verses were revealed (ordering them to continue only with logical struggle). [1]

The next verse adds: "Your Lord is most knowing of you. If He wills, He will have mercy on you, or if He wills, He will punish you" (رَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ إِنْ يَشَأْ يَرْحَمْكُمْ أَوْ إِنْ يَشَأْ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ‌). In connection with the two interpretations of the previous verse, this verse also allows for two interpretations:

First: O polytheists and faithless individuals, your God possesses both vast mercy

[1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and Tafsir al-Qurtubi, under the verses discussed.

and painful punishment; whomever He deems worthy of either, He will make them subject to it. It is better that you cling to His vast mercy and avoid His punishment.

But according to the second interpretation, the meaning is: Do not think that you believers are the only ones saved and others are the people of Hell; Allah is most knowing of your deeds and your hearts. If He wills, He will punish you for your sins, and if He wills, He will encompass you in His mercy. Reflect a little on your own state and judge yourselves and others more justly.

In any case, at the end of the verse, the address is directed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as a consolation and to prevent the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله extreme distress over the polytheists' lack of faith, saying:

"We have not sent you as a guardian over them [to be responsible for making them believe]" (وَ ما أَرْسَلْناكَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكِيلًا).

Your duty is clear proclamation and diligent invitation toward the Truth. If they believe, so much the better; otherwise, no harm will come to you—you have fulfilled your duty! Although the addressee in this sentence is the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself, it is not unlikely—like many other Quranic addresses—that the target is all believers. In this case, it would be another indication for the second interpretation, as the Quran says: Your duty as Muslims is to invite to the Truth, whether they believe or not. Therefore, there is no reason for excessive agitation that leads to resorting to harshness in speech, profanity, and insults.

The next verse takes the matter further and says: Allah is not only aware of your state, but "your Lord is most knowing of all who are in the heavens and the earth" (وَ رَبُّكَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌).

Then it adds: "We have preferred some of the prophets over others, and We gave David the Psalms" (وَ لَقَدْ فَضَّلْنا بَعْضَ النَّبِيِّينَ عَلى‌ بَعْضٍ وَ آتَيْنا داوُدَ زَبُوراً).

This sentence is actually a response to one of the objections of the polytheists, who said in a belittling manner: "Did Allah have no one else to choose for prophethood but the orphan Muhammad? Furthermore, how did he become the foremost of all prophets and their Seal?!"

The Quran says: This is not a matter of surprise. Allah is aware of the human value of everyone and has chosen His prophets from among these very masses of people, preferring some over others. He honored one as the "Friend of Allah" (Khalil-Allah), another as the "One who spoke with Allah" (Kalim-Allah), and another as the "Spirit of Allah" (Ruh-Allah). He chose the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله as the "Beloved of Allah" (Habib-Allah). In short, He preferred some over others according to the standards He knows and as His wisdom dictates.

As for why, among all prophets, "David" and the "Psalms" (Zabur) are mentioned, it may be due to the following reasons:

  1. The Psalms of David, among the books of the prophets, has the unique characteristic of being entirely supplications, prayers, and advice, which is most consistent with the "best speech" and "good words" commanded in the previous verses.

  2. In the Psalms of David, news was given of the rule of the righteous and the good, even if they appeared to be destitute, poor, or orphaned. [1]

[1]: In the Book of Psalms (Zabur) available today, in Psalm 37, we read: "...For evildoers shall be cut off; but those who wait on the Lord, they shall inherit the earth. For yet a little while and the wicked shall be no more; indeed, you will look carefully for his place, but it shall be no more. But the meek (the righteous) shall inherit the earth." In the same Psalm, in verses 22 and 29, expressions very similar to this are also found. This is the same thing that the Holy Quran states in Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 105: "And We have already written in the Psalms after the [previous] mention that the land is inherited by My righteous servants."

This is perfectly aligned with the call of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the true believers, who were mostly among the destitute, and it is a response to the polytheists' objection.

  1. Although David possessed a great government and a vast country, Allah does not make this his pride; rather, He considers the Book of Psalms his pride, so that the polytheists may know that the greatness of a human being is not in wealth, property, or having outward power and government, and being an orphan or without wealth is no reason for belittlement.

  2. Some of the Jews said: After Moses, the revelation of another book is not possible. The Quran answers them that We gave David the Psalms; why are you surprised at the revelation of the Quran? (Of course, David's book was not a book of laws but a book of ethics, but whatever it was, it was revealed after the Torah and from Allah).

In any case, there is no obstacle to all four of the above reasons being the cause for choosing David and the Psalms in this sentence from among all prophets and all heavenly books.

In the next verse, the discourse returns to the polytheists, and following the previous discussions, it says to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Tell them: Call upon those you imagine [to be worthy of worship] besides Allah; they have no power to remove hardships and afflictions from you, nor to change them" (قُلِ ادْعُوا الَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ فَلا يَمْلِكُونَ كَشْفَ الضُّرِّ عَنْكُمْ وَ لا تَحْوِيلًا).

In reality, this verse, like many other verses of the Quran, invalidates the logic and belief of the polytheists by pointing out that the worship and service of deities is either for gaining benefit or averting harm, whereas these [deities] have no power of their own to remove a problem, nor even to shift a problem—that is, to reduce it from a more severe degree to at least a milder one—which would show they have some power of their own.

Therefore, the mention of the phrase "nor to change them" (وَ لا تَحْوِيلًا) after "they have no power to remove hardships" (فَلا يَمْلِكُونَ كَشْفَ الضُّرِّ) indicates that they have neither the ability for a complete effect in removing problems nor a partial effect in altering them.

"Za'amtum" is from the root za'm, which is usually said of a false notion. Thus, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that wherever the word za'm is used in the Quran, it means a lie and falsehood (and a baseless belief).

Raghib says in the book Al-Mufradat: "Al-Za'm is the narration of a statement that is a site for suspicion of lying... Za'm means quoting a speech (or belief) in which there is a possibility of falsehood; therefore, in all instances where it is used in the Quran, it is in the context of blame and reproach."

The word kashf originally means to set aside a curtain or garment and the like from over something. If this expression (kashf durr) is also applied to the removal of grief, sorrow, illness, and discomfort, it is because it is as if grief, sorrow, and illness fall like a curtain over the face of the human soul and body, covering the true face, which is health, comfort, and peace. For this reason, removing this sorrow and discomfort is regarded as "removing hardship" (kashf durr).

It is also necessary to note that the expression "those who" (alladhina) in this verse indicates that it does not refer to all deities besides Allah, but rather to deities such as the angels, Jesus عليه السلام, and their like (because alladhina is usually used for a plural of rational beings).

The next verse is actually a proof for what was said in the previous verse. This verse says: Do you know why they are unable to lift a problem from your shoulders without the permission of the Lord? Because they themselves go to the door of Allah's house to solve problems; they themselves strive to seek proximity to His Pure Essence and ask Him for whatever they want: "Those are the ones who call upon Allah and seek the means of access to their Lord through obedience to Him" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ يَبْتَغُونَ إِلى‌ رَبِّهِمُ الْوَسِيلَةَ), "seeking the nearest means" (أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ‌).

"And they hope for His mercy" (وَ يَرْجُونَ رَحْمَتَهُ‌).

"And they fear His punishment" (وَ يَخافُونَ عَذابَهُ‌).

"For the punishment of your Lord is so severe that everyone avoids it and is in awe of it" (إِنَّ عَذابَ رَبِّكَ كانَ مَحْذُوراً). In the interpretation of the phrase "which of them is nearest" (أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ), the great commentators of Islam have various interpretations:

A group says: This phrase indicates that each of these saints of the Lord—from the angels and prophets who have been made objects of worship—the closer they are to Allah, the more they go to His threshold. Therefore, they have nothing of their own; whatever they have is from Allah, and the higher their rank rises, the more their obedience and servitude increase. [1] Others believe that the concept of the sentence is this: They strive so that each may precede the other in proximity to the Lord, as if they have entered a spiritual race in the path of obedience to the Lord and proximity to His Pure Essence, where each tries to surpass the other in this field. Can those who are like this be objects of worship and possess independence? [2] But the interpretation that they seek proximity to Allah by whatever means is nearest is a very remote possibility, because the pronoun "them" (hum) in "which of them" (ayyum), which is usually for the masculine plural, is not consistent with this meaning; rather it should be ayyuha. [3] Furthermore, "ayyum aqrab" is in the form of subject and predicate, whereas according to this meaning, it should be used as an object or a substitute for the object (reflect on this).

What is "Wasila" (The Means)?

This word is used in two places in the Holy Quran: one here and the other in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 35. As we have stated under verse 35 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, wasila means "seeking proximity" or something that causes proximity (or the result obtained from proximity).

In this way, "wasila" has a very broad concept that includes every good and righteous deed and every outstanding attribute is included in its concept, because all of these cause proximity to the Lord.

In the very profound sentences narrated from Ali عليه السلام in Sermon 110 of Nahj al-Balagha, we read: "The best means by which servants seek proximity to the threshold of Allah is faith in Allah, establishing prayer, paying Zakat, fasting in the month of Ramadan, Hajj and Umrah, maintaining family ties, spending and giving in the way of Allah secretly and openly, and all good deeds that save a person from falling and baseness." [4] Likewise, the intercession of the prophets, the righteous servants of Allah, and those near to His threshold—which according to the explicit verses of the Quran is accepted in His presence—is also one of the means of proximity to Him.

Let there be no mistake: the purpose of resorting to those near to the threshold of the Lord is not that a person should request something from a prophet or Imam independently or ask him to solve a problem. Rather, the goal is to place oneself in their line, become harmonious with their programs, and call upon Allah by their status so that Allah may grant permission for intercession regarding them (for more explanation, refer to Volume 4 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 363 onwards).

[1]: According to this interpretation, "ayyum" is a substitute for the pronoun in "yabtaghun," or it is a subject for a deleted part, and the estimation was: "which of them is nearest, he is more frequent in supplication and seeking the means." [2]: In this case, "ayyum" grammatically can only be a substitute for the pronoun in "yabtaghun." [3]: Besides this, "ayyum aqrab" is in the form of subject and predicate, whereas according to this meaning, it should be used as an object or a substitute for the object. [4]: Summarized from Sermon 110 of Nahj al-Balagha (we have provided its explanation in Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 4, page 364 onwards).

Ayahs

وَإِن مِّن قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا نَحْنُ مُهْلِكُوهَا قَبْلَ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُوهَا عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا ۚ كَانَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مَسْطُورًا (٥٨)
There is not a town but We will destroy it before the Day of Resurrection, or punish it with a severe punishment. That has been written in the Book. (58)
وَمَا مَنَعَنَآ أَن نُّرْسِلَ بِٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ إِلَّآ أَن كَذَّبَ بِهَا ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ ۚ وَءَاتَيْنَا ثَمُودَ ٱلنَّاقَةَ مُبْصِرَةً فَظَلَمُوا۟ بِهَا ۚ وَمَا نُرْسِلُ بِٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ إِلَّا تَخْوِيفًا (٥٩)
Nothing keeps Us from sending signs except that the former peoples denied them. We gave Thamud the she-camel as an eye-opener, but they wronged her. We do not send the signs except as warning. (59)
وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لَكَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ أَحَاطَ بِٱلنَّاسِ ۚ وَمَا جَعَلْنَا ٱلرُّءْيَا ٱلَّتِىٓ أَرَيْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِّلنَّاسِ وَٱلشَّجَرَةَ ٱلْمَلْعُونَةَ فِى ٱلْقُرْءَانِ ۚ وَنُخَوِّفُهُمْ فَمَا يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا طُغْيَـٰنًا كَبِيرًا (٦٠)
When We said to you, ‘Indeed your Lord encircles those people,’ We did not appoint the vision that We showed you except as a tribulation for the people and the tree cursed in the Quran. We warn them, but it only increases them in their outrageous rebellion. (60)

Commentary

Do Not Yield to the Excuse-Seekers!

Following the discussions regarding monotheism and the Resurrection with the polytheists in the preceding verses, the first verse under discussion admonishes them with awakening words. It visualizes the end and annihilation of this world before the eyes of their intellect so they may realize that this abode is a transient one, while the abode of permanence lies elsewhere, and thus prepare themselves to face the consequences of their deeds. It says: "There is no town [on earth] but We shall destroy it before the Day of Resurrection or punish it with a severe punishment" (وَ إِنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا نَحْنُ مُهْلِكُوها قَبْلَ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُوها عَذاباً شَدِيداً).

The wicked oppressors and rebellious tyrants We shall destroy through punishment, and others through natural death or common incidents.

Ultimately, this world will come to an end and all shall tread the path of annihilation, "and this is a definitive and certain principle recorded in the Divine Book" (كانَ ذلِكَ فِي الْكِتابِ مَسْطُوراً).

This "Book" is the Preserved Tablet (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz), the infinite knowledge of the Lord, and the collection of Allah's infallible laws in the universe.

In light of this definitive and unchangeable principle, the misguided polytheists and tainted oppressors must settle their accounts from now; for even if they remain alive until the end of this world, the ultimate end is still annihilation, followed by a return for reckoning and recompense.

At this point, an objection remains for the polytheists: "Well, we have no argument against believing, but on the condition that the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) performs every miracle we propose," effectively demanding that he yield to their excuse-seeking.

The Quran responds to them: "Nothing prevented Us from sending such miracles except that the former peoples denied them" (وَ ما مَنَعَنا أَنْ نُرْسِلَ بِالْآياتِ إِلَّا أَنْ كَذَّبَ بِهَا الْأَوَّلُونَ).

This indicates that the miracles which serve as proof of the Prophet's truthfulness have been sent sufficiently. As for the miracles you suggest and propose out of caprice, they are not something to be granted, because even after witnessing them, you will not believe. If they ask: "On what grounds?"

The answer is: On the grounds that past nations, who were in circumstances exactly similar to yours, made such excuse-seeking proposals and subsequently did not believe.

Then, the Quran points to a clear example of this issue, saying: "We gave the people of Thamud the she-camel as a visible sign" (وَ آتَيْنا ثَمُودَ النَّاقَةَ مُبْصِرَةً).

That very camel which emerged from the mountain by Allah's command, because they had requested such a miracle—a clear and enlightening miracle! "But despite this, they did not believe" and they wronged that she-camel "and slaughtered her" (فَظَلَمُوا بِها).

Fundamentally, Our program is not such that whenever someone proposes a miracle, the Prophet must yield to him. "We do not send signs and miracles except to warn people and to complete the proof" (وَ ما نُرْسِلَ بِالْآياتِ إِلَّا تَخْوِيفاً).

Our Prophets are not "miracle-performers" who sit and perform whatever anyone suggests. Their duty is to convey the Divine call, to educate and nurture, and to establish a government of justice and equity. However, they must provide sufficient miracles to prove their connection with Allah, and that is all.

Then, He comforts His Prophet in the face of the enemies' stubbornness and persistence, saying: If they show such obstinacy against your words and do not believe, it is nothing new. "Remember when We said to you that your Lord encompasses the people [in His knowledge and power]" (وَ إِذْ قُلْنا لَكَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ أَحاطَ بِالنَّاسِ).

In the face of the Prophets' call, there has always been a group of pure-hearted people who believed, and a group of prejudiced and stubborn people who rose up with excuse-seeking, sabotage, and enmity. It was so in the past, and it is so today.

Then He adds: "And We did not make the vision which We showed you except as a trial for the people" (وَ ما جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْناكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ).

"And likewise, the 'Cursed Tree' which We mentioned in the Quran is also a trial for the people" (وَ الشَّجَرَةَ الْمَلْعُونَةَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ).

Regarding the commentary on this "Vision" (Ru'ya) and the "Cursed Tree," we shall discuss them in the Key Points.

In conclusion, He adds: "We warn these stubborn, blind-hearted ones through various means, but Our constructive programs only increase them in great rebellion and defiance" (وَ نُخَوِّفُهُمْ فَما يَزِيدُهُمْ إِلَّا طُغْياناً كَبِيراً).

For if a person's heart and soul are not ready to accept the truth, not only does the word of truth fail to affect them, but it often produces the opposite result, increasing their misguidance and stubbornness due to their persistence and negative resistance (reflect on this).

Key Points

1- The Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) Vision and the Cursed Tree

There is much discussion among commentators regarding the interpretation of this "Vision" (Ru'ya):

A: A group of commentators have said that Ru'ya here does not mean a dream, but rather a physical observation with the eyes, and they consider it an allusion to the story of the Mi'raj (Ascension) mentioned at the beginning of this very Surah.

According to this interpretation, the Quran says: The event of the Mi'raj was a trial for the people because when the Prophet recounted the story of the Mi'raj in the morning, an uproar arose around it. The enemies mocked it, those with weak faith looked upon it with doubt and hesitation, and the true believers accepted it completely, for in the face of Allah's power, all such matters are easy.

The only major objection to this interpretation is that Ru'ya usually means a dream, not a waking observation.

B: It is narrated from Ibn Abbas that the vision refers to a dream the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) had in Medina during the year of "Hudaybiyyah" (the 6th year of Hijra), in which he gave glad tidings to the people that they would soon be victorious over the Quraysh and enter Masjid al-Haram in total peace. However, we know this dream was not realized that year, but rather two years later during the Conquest of Mecca. Yet, this delay was enough to put the believers through a trial; those with weak faith fell into doubt and suspicion, while the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) explicitly told them: "Did I not tell you that we would go to Mecca this year? I said it would happen in the near future" (and so it did).

The objection to this interpretation is that Surah Bani Isra'il is a Meccan Surah, while the event of Hudaybiyyah occurred in the 6th year of Hijra!

C: A group of Shi'a and Sunni commentators have narrated that this dream refers to a famous incident where the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) saw in a dream monkeys climbing up and down his pulpit. He became very saddened by this, so much so that he rarely laughed thereafter. (These monkeys have been interpreted as the Umayyads, who sat in the Prophet's place one after another, imitating each other, being individuals devoid of character who corrupted the Islamic government and the Caliphate of the Messenger of Allah).

This narration has been cited by Fakhr al-Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir, the famous Sunni commentator al-Qurtubi in al-Jami', al-Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, and others. The late Fayd Kashani says in Tafsir al-Safi: "This narration is among the well-known reports among both the commonality (Ammah) and the elite (Khassah)."

Of course, these three interpretations are not mutually exclusive; it is possible for all three to be encompassed by the meaning of the verse, though as mentioned, the second interpretation is inconsistent with the Surah being Meccan.

As for the "Cursed Tree," we also encounter several interpretations:

A- The "Cursed Tree" mentioned in the Quran is the "Tree of Zaqqum." This is the tree that, according to verse 64 of Surah al-Saffat, grows at the bottom of Hell and has a disagreeable and painful fruit (إِنَّها شَجَرَةٌ تَخْرُجُ فِي أَصْلِ الْجَحِيمِ). According to verses 46 to 47 of Surah al-Dukhan, this tree is the food of sinners—not like the foods of this world, but boiling in the belly like molten metal. Its full commentary will, Allah willing, come under the verses of Surah al-Dukhan.

Undoubtedly, the Tree of Zaqqum bears no resemblance to the trees of this world, and for this reason, it grows from the midst of fire. It is obvious that we can only perceive a shadow of such matters pertaining to the other world.

However, the polytheists of Quraysh mocked this Quranic expression due to their ignorance. Abu Jahl would say: "Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) threatens you with a fire that burns stones, then he imagines that a tree grows in Hell!" It is also narrated that Abu Jahl, as a mockery, would bring dates and butter, eat them, and say to his companions: "Eat this, for this is the Zaqqum!" (Ruh al-Ma'ani, under the verse). For this reason, in the verses under discussion, the Quran introduces this Cursed Tree as a means of trial for the people, as the stubborn polytheists mock it while the true believers submit to it.

It might be asked: where is this tree referred to as the "Cursed Tree" in the Quran?

In response, we say it might refer to the cursing of those who eat from it. Furthermore, a "curse" (la'n) is nothing but being distanced from Allah's mercy, and it is obvious that such a tree is very far from the Lord's mercy.

B- The "Cursed Tree" refers to the rebellious Jewish people; they are like a tree with many branches and leaves but are rejected from the presence of the Lord.

C- In many Shi'a and Sunni commentaries, it is narrated that the "Cursed Tree" refers to the Umayyads (Bani Umayya).

Fakhr al-Razi, in his commentary, narrates a report in this regard from Ibn Abbas, the famous Islamic commentator.

This interpretation is consistent with the narration mentioned above regarding the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) vision, and also with the hadith narrated from Aisha, who turned to Marwan and said: "Allah cursed your father while you were in his loins; therefore, you are a part of someone whom Allah has cursed" [^1]!

Again, the question arises: where in the Quran is the "Evil Tree" of the Umayyads cursed?

In response, we say: in Surah Ibrahim, verse 26, where the "Evil Tree" (Shajarah Khabithah) is mentioned. Given the broad concept of the "Evil Tree" and the narrations interpreting it as the Umayyads, and considering that "evil" (khabithah) is semantically linked to "cursed" (mal'unah), it can be said to be mentioned in the Quran [^2]. It is noteworthy that many of these interpretations, or all of them, are not mutually exclusive. The "Cursed Tree" in the Quran may refer to every hypocritical, evil, and rejected group, especially groups like the Umayyads, the hard-hearted and stubborn Jews, and all those who walk in their line. The Tree of Zaqqum in the Resurrection is a manifestation of the existence of these evil trees in the other world, and all these evil trees are a source of trial and testing for the true believers in this world.

The Jews who have today usurped control over sensitive Islamic centers, and who ignite fires in some corner of the world every hour, committing all those crimes and injustices—

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 6, p. 3902; Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 20, p. 237. [^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 538.

—as well as the hypocrites who have made and continue to make political and non-political deals with them, and all the hegemons who follow the line of the Umayyads against Islam across all Islamic countries—those who push the virtuous out of the social arena, impose the base upon the people's backs, martyr the friends of truth and true mujahideen, and appoint the remnants of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) to positions of power—all of these are the branches and leaves of the Cursed Evil Tree and a trial for the people!

2- The Excuses of Those Who Deny Miracles

We know that some negligent people of our time have started the tune that the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) had no miracle at all (except the Quran). They resort to any excuse for their claim, including taking the above verse ("Nothing prevented Us from sending miracles...") as proof that the Prophet of Islam, unlike previous Prophets, did not perform miracles.

But the surprise is that they have clung to the beginning of the verse and abandoned its end. The end of this very verse says: "We do not send signs except to warn [the deniers]" (وَ ما نُرْسِلَ بِالْآياتِ إِلَّا تَخْوِيفاً).

This expression shows that miracles are of two types:

The first category consists of miracles necessary to prove the truthfulness of the Prophet's call, to encourage the believers, and to warn the deniers.

The second category consists of miracles that are "provisional" or "capricious proposals"—that is, "excuse-seeking suggestions." In the history of the Prophets, we see several examples of these performed for deniers, yet they never believed, and for that reason, they were struck by Divine punishments (because if proposed miracles are performed and yet people do not believe, they deserve immediate punishment).

Therefore, what the Quran denies in the above verse regarding the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) is only the second category of miracles, not the first, the existence of which is inseparable from the claim of Prophethood.

It is true that the Quran alone is a clear, eternal miracle, and even if there were no other miracle for the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), we could still realize the truth of his call. Undoubtedly, the Quran is a spiritual and intellectual miracle and the best witness for those who are people of thought and reflection. However, it cannot be denied that supplementing this miracle with physical, perceptible miracles was of utmost importance for ordinary people and the masses. Especially since the Quran repeatedly reports such miracles regarding other Prophets, this would undoubtedly cause people to request similar miracles from the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), saying: "How do you claim to be the greatest and last of the Divine Prophets yet cannot perform the smallest of their miracles?"

Certainly, in the face of this question, there was no convincing answer other than for the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) to provide examples of the miracles of previous Prophets.

Successive (mutawatir) Islamic histories also state that the Prophet of Islam provided such miracles.

In various verses of the Quran, we encounter examples of these miracles, such as various prophecies regarding future events, the assistance of angels to the Islamic army to overthrow enemies, and other supernatural events that occurred especially during Islamic battles.

3- What Does the Denial of Past Generations Have to Do with Future Ones?!

Sometimes the question arises: the Quran says in the above verses that because past generations proposed miracles and then turned to denial and rejection after they were performed, this caused Us not to act upon your proposals in this regard.

Does the denial of predecessors cause the deprivation of subsequent generations?

The answer to this question is clear in light of what we said above. This is a common expression; for example, we say: "We cannot yield to your excuse-seeking." If the other party asks why, we say: "There is a long history of others making such proposals but then not submitting to the truth; your conditions and situation are just like theirs."

Furthermore, you endorse their method and agree with it, and you have practically proven that you are not seeking to investigate the truth, but rather your goal is excuse-seeking followed by stubbornness, obstinacy, and the denial of everything. Therefore, fulfilling your proposals would be meaningless.

Hence, as soon as the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) informed them that the inhabitants of Hell feed from a tree called Zaqqum that grows in the depths of Hell and has such-and-such qualities, they immediately—as mentioned before—rose up in mockery and derision. Sometimes they said: "Zaqqum is nothing but dates and butter! Come, let us eat this rich and sweet food in memory of Zaqqum!" And at other times they said: "How can a tree grow in Hell, whose fire comes from the heart of stones?"—even though it was clear that this tree was not like the trees of this world.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ قَالَ ءَأَسْجُدُ لِمَنْ خَلَقْتَ طِينًا (٦١)
When We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they [all] prostrated, but not Iblis: he said, ‘Shall I prostrate before someone whom You have created from clay?’ (61)
قَالَ أَرَءَيْتَكَ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى كَرَّمْتَ عَلَىَّ لَئِنْ أَخَّرْتَنِ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ لَأَحْتَنِكَنَّ ذُرِّيَّتَهُۥٓ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٦٢)
Said he, ‘Do You see this one whom You have honoured above me? If You respite me until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely lay my yoke on his progeny, [all] except a few.’ (62)
قَالَ ٱذْهَبْ فَمَن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ جَزَآؤُكُمْ جَزَآءً مَّوْفُورًا (٦٣)
Said He, ‘Begone! Whoever of them follows you, indeed the hell shall be your requital, an ample reward. (63)
وَٱسْتَفْزِزْ مَنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتَ مِنْهُم بِصَوْتِكَ وَأَجْلِبْ عَلَيْهِم بِخَيْلِكَ وَرَجِلِكَ وَشَارِكْهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَمْوَٰلِ وَٱلْأَوْلَـٰدِ وَعِدْهُمْ ۚ وَمَا يَعِدُهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ إِلَّا غُرُورًا (٦٤)
Instigate whomever of them you can with your voice; and rally against them your cavalry and your infantry, and share with them in wealth and children, and make promises to them!’ But Satan promises them nothing but delusion. (64)
إِنَّ عِبَادِى لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَـٰنٌ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِرَبِّكَ وَكِيلًا (٦٥)
‘As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them.’ And your Lord suffices as trustee. (65)

Commentary

The Snares of Iblis

These verses refer to the issue of Iblis’s defiance of Allah’s command to prostrate before Adam and his wretched fate following this event.

Presenting this narrative after the previous discussions regarding the stubborn polytheists is, in fact, an indication that the ultimate example of arrogance, disbelief, and rebellion was Satan; see where his fate led him—you, his followers, will share that same destiny.

Furthermore, if these blind-hearted individuals persist so much in the path of opposing the Truth, it is no surprise, for according to these verses, Satan has risen to seduce and mislead them through several avenues. Indeed, his statement—"I will lead most of the children of Adam astray, except for the sincere and true believers"—has come to fruition.

First, He says: "And [mention] when We said to the angels, 'Prostrate to Adam,' and they prostrated, except for Iblis" (وَ إِذْ قُلْنا لِلْمَلائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ‌).

As we have previously mentioned in the commentary on the verses related to the creation of Adam, this prostration was a form of humility and modesty due to the greatness of Adam's creation and his distinction over other beings, or it was a prostration of worship before Allah for the creation of such a marvelous creature.

We also noted that although Iblis is mentioned here as an exception among the angels, according to the testimony of the Quran, he was never part of the angels; rather, due to his devotion to Allah, he stood in their ranks. He was of the Jinn and had a material creation.

In any case, Iblis, whose head was filled with the wind of pride and arrogance, and whose intellect and perception were veiled by selfishness and vanity, assumed that earth and clay—which are the source of all blessings and the fountainhead of life—were of less importance than fire. As a protest before Allah, "he said, 'Should I prostrate to one whom You created from clay?'" (قالَ أَ أَسْجُدُ لِمَنْ خَلَقْتَ طِيناً).

But when he saw that due to this arrogance and rebellion against Allah’s command, he was banished from His holy presence forever: "He said, 'Do You see this one whom You have honored above me? If You delay me until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely destroy his descendants, [leading them astray] except for a few!'" (قالَ أَ رَأَيْتَكَ هذَا الَّذِي كَرَّمْتَ عَلَيَّ لَئِنْ أَخَّرْتَنِ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ لَأَحْتَنِكَنَّ ذُرِّيَّتَهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا) [1].

"Ihtanakan" (لَأَحْتَنِكَنَّ) is derived from the root "Ihtinak," which means to uproot something. Thus, when locusts completely consume a crop, the Arabs say: Ihtanaka al-jaradu al-zar'a. Therefore, this statement indicates: "I will uproot all the children of Adam from the path of Your obedience, except for a few."

There is also the possibility that "Ihtanakan" is derived from "Hanak," meaning the area under the throat. When a rope or bridle is placed around an animal's neck, the Arabs use the expression Hanakat al-dabbah. In reality, Satan wants to say: "I will cast the rope of temptation around all their necks and pull them toward the path of error."

At this point, in order for a field of testing to be established for everyone, and to provide a means for the cultivation of true believers—since a human being is always tempered in the furnace of events and becomes strong and heroic in the face of a powerful enemy—Satan was given the possibility of survival and activity: "[Allah] said, 'Go, for whoever of them follows you, then indeed Hell will be your recompense—an ample recompense!'" (قالَ اذْهَبْ فَمَنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ جَزاؤُكُمْ جَزاءً مَوْفُوراً).

[1]: A group of commentators have said that the Kaf in the phrase "A-ra'aytaka" is a redundant particle or a particle of address added for emphasis, and the phrase "A-ra'aytaka" means "Inform me," where its answer is omitted. The implied meaning was: "Inform me about this one whom You honored above me, why did You honor him over me when You created me from fire?" However, it is also possible that "A-ra'ayta" retains its original meaning and the sentence has no omitted parts. Collectively, it means: "Did You observe this creature whom You have preferred over me? If You leave me alive, You will see that I will mislead most of his children." (The second possibility regarding the structure and meaning of the verse seems more correct).

In this way, He announced the trial and stated the ultimate outcome of victory or defeat in this great divine test.

Then, He describes the ways of Satan's influence and the means and tools he resorts to in his temptations in a striking and expressive manner: "And incite whomever you can among them with your voice" (وَ اسْتَفْزِزْ مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتَ مِنْهُمْ بِصَوْتِكَ‌).

"And assault them with your cavalry and your infantry with your shout!" (وَ أَجْلِبْ عَلَيْهِمْ بِخَيْلِكَ وَ رَجِلِكَ‌).

"And partner with them in wealth and children" (وَ شارِكْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْوالِ وَ الْأَوْلادِ).

"And make promises to them [with your false promises]!" (وَ عِدْهُمْ‌).

Then He warns: "And Satan promises them nothing but delusion" (وَ ما يَعِدُهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ إِلَّا غُرُوراً).

But know that "Indeed, over My [true] servants there is for you no authority" (إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ‌). "And sufficient is your Lord as a Guardian [and Protector for these servants]" (وَ كَفى‌ بِرَبِّكَ وَكِيلًا).

Key Points

1- The Meaning of Several Words

"Istafziz" (اسْتَفْزِزْ) is from the root "Istifzaz," meaning to incite or provoke—a quick and simple provocation. However, originally it means to cut or tear something; thus, whenever a cloth or garment is torn, the Arabs say Tafazzaza al-thawb. The use of this word to mean incitement is because it "cuts" someone off from the Truth and drags them toward falsehood.

"Ajlib" (أَجْلِبْ) is from the root "Ijlab," originally from "Jalbah," meaning a loud shout. "Ijlab" means to drive or move something with a roar or shout. The prohibition of "Jalb" in some narrations either means that the Zakat collector, when coming to pastures to take the religious right, should not shout and frighten the livestock in their grazing land, or it refers to horse racing, where no participant should shout at another's mount to advance himself [1].

"Khayl" (خَيْل) has two meanings: "horses" and "cavalry" (horse riders). Here, it carries the second meaning and refers to the mounted army.

"Rajil" (رَجِل), conversely, means the infantry (foot soldiers). Of course, Satan does not have a cavalry and infantry in the sense used in a literal army, but we know he has many assistants from his own kind and from mankind who help him in seducing people. Some are faster and more powerful, like the cavalry, and some are slower and weaker, like the infantry!

[1]: Refer to Mufradat al-Raghib and Majma' al-Bayan.

2- The Diverse Means of Satanic Temptation

Although in the above verses the address is to Satan, and Allah speaks to him in the form of a threatening command—telling him to do whatever he can and to rise to seduce the children of Adam with various means—it is, in reality, a warning to all humans to recognize Satan's paths of influence and to become aware of the diversity of his tools of temptation.

Interestingly, in the above verse, the Quran points to four important and fundamental categories of these means, telling humans to guard themselves from four directions:

A: Propaganda Programs – The phrase "And incite whomever you can among them with your voice" (وَ اسْتَفْزِزْ مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتَ مِنْهُمْ بِصَوْتِكَ‌), which some commentators have interpreted only as lust-inciting melodies of music and singing, has a broad meaning that includes any misleading propaganda using audio and visual means.

Thus, Satan's first program is the use of these tools. This issue is clearer and more evident than ever in today's world, which is a world of radio transmitters and extensive audio-visual propaganda. Satans and their parties in the East and West rely on this effective tool, spending a huge portion of their capital in this way to colonize Allah’s servants, divert them from the path of Truth—which is the path of freedom, independence, faith, and piety—and turn them into weak, will-less slaves.

B: Use of Military Force – It is not unique to our era that satans resort to military power to find zones of influence. The military arm has always been one of the most important and dangerous arms of all the tyrants and oppressors of the world. They suddenly shout to their armed forces and dispatch them to regions that might regain their freedom and independence through fierce resistance. Even in our own time, we see the planning of "rapid deployment" programs, which is exactly the concept of "Ijlab." Certain world-devouring powers of the West have prepared special forces to be sent in the shortest time to any region of the world where their illegitimate satanic interests are endangered, to stifle any truth-seeking movement in its infancy.

And before the arrival of this rapid army, they prepare the ground with their skilled spies, who are in fact the "infantry." They are oblivious to the fact that Allah has promised His true servants in these very verses that Satan and his army will never have authority over them!

C: Economic and Seemingly Humanitarian Programs – Another effective means of Satan's influence is through partnership in wealth and souls. Here again, we see some commentators interpreting partnership in wealth exclusively as "usury" (riba) and partnership in children only as "illegitimate children" [1]. However, these two words have a much broader meaning that includes all forbidden wealth, illegitimate children, and beyond.

For example, in our own time, we see world-devouring satans constantly proposing investments, establishing companies, and creating various factories and production centers in weak countries. Under the cover of these companies, they carry out various dangerous and harmful activities. They dispatch their spies to these countries under the titles of technical experts, economic consultants, engineers, and technicians. With subtle tricks, they suck their last breath and prevent their economic growth and independence.

They also partner in their children through the establishment of schools, universities, libraries, hospitals, and tourism. They attract a group of them toward themselves; sometimes, with generous aid through scholarships provided to the youth, they fully attract them to their culture and programs, becoming partners in their thoughts.

Creating centers of corruption under the guise of building international hotels, recreational clubs, cinemas, and misleading films is one of the most common destructive programs of these satans. Not only do they promote immorality through this and become a factor in the increase of illegitimate children, but they also cultivate a generation that is deviant, weak, will-less, indifferent, and hedonistic. The more we scrutinize their programs, the more we will become acquainted with the depth of the dangers of these satanic temptations.

D: Destructive Psychological Programs – The use of deluding promises and various deceptions and tricks is another of Satan's programs. They have trained skilled psychologists and psychoanalysts to deceive simple-hearted and even alert people. Sometimes, by claiming that the "gate of a great civilization" is just a few steps away, or that in the near future they will be among the first-tier civilized and progressive countries, or that their generation is a unique and exemplary one that can reach the peak of greatness under their programs—and similar fantasies and delusions—they keep them occupied. All of this is summarized in the phrase "And make promises to them" (وَ عِدْهُمْ‌)!

Conversely, they sometimes discourage them from any effort by belittling and weakening their morale, claiming they can never fight against the great global powers and that there is a gap of hundreds of years between their civilization and that of advanced countries.

This story is long, and the ways of influence for Satan and his armies are not just one or two. It is here that the Ibad Allah (servants of Allah) and true believers, heartened by His definitive promise in these verses, rise to battle these satans. They harbor not the slightest fear, knowing that no matter how loud the noise of the satans may be, it is hollow and empty. With the power of faith and reliance on Allah, one can triumph over all of them and turn their plans to dust, as the Quran says: "And sufficient is your Lord as a Guardian" (وَ كَفى‌ بِرَبِّكَ وَكِيلًا)—Allah is their best Protector, Guardian, and Helper.

3- Regarding why Allah created Satan, we have discussed this in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 39. Also, regarding Satan's temptations in various guises and the meaning of "Satan" in the Quran, it has been discussed in Volume 6, page 115, and Volume 1, page 136.

[1]: Several narrations have also been reported regarding these verses interpreting Satan's partnership in children as illegitimate children, or children whose essence is formed from forbidden wealth, or when the parents do not remember Allah at the time of conception. However, as we have said many times, such interpretations explain a portion of the clear instances and are not evidence of exclusivity (Refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 184).

Ayahs

رَّبُّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى يُزْجِى لَكُمُ ٱلْفُلْكَ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ لِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيمًا (٦٦)
Your Lord is He who drives for you the ships in the sea that you may seek of His bounty. Indeed, He is most merciful to you. (66)
وَإِذَا مَسَّكُمُ ٱلضُّرُّ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ ضَلَّ مَن تَدْعُونَ إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ ۖ فَلَمَّا نَجَّىٰكُمْ إِلَى ٱلْبَرِّ أَعْرَضْتُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ كَفُورًا (٦٧)
When distress befalls you at sea, those whom you invoke besides Him are forsaken. But when He delivers you to land, you are disregardful [of Him]. Man is very ungrateful. (67)
أَفَأَمِنتُمْ أَن يَخْسِفَ بِكُمْ جَانِبَ ٱلْبَرِّ أَوْ يُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ حَاصِبًا ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُوا۟ لَكُمْ وَكِيلًا (٦٨)
Do you feel secure that He will not make the coastland swallow you, or He will not unleash upon you a rain of stones? Then you will not find any defender for yourselves. (68)
أَمْ أَمِنتُمْ أَن يُعِيدَكُمْ فِيهِ تَارَةً أُخْرَىٰ فَيُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ قَاصِفًا مِّنَ ٱلرِّيحِ فَيُغْرِقَكُم بِمَا كَفَرْتُمْ ۙ ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُوا۟ لَكُمْ عَلَيْنَا بِهِۦ تَبِيعًا (٦٩)
Do you feel secure that He will not send you back into it another time and unleash against you a shattering gale and drown you because of your unfaith? Then you will not find for yourselves any redresser against Us. (69)

Commentary

With All These Blessings, Why Such Ingratitude?!

These verses follow the discussions we previously had regarding monotheism (Tawhid) and the struggle against polytheism, approaching this subject through two different paths: the path of reasoning and logic, and the path of conscience and the inner self.

First, referring to logical monotheism, it says: "Your Lord is He who causes the ship to move for you in the sea, a continuous and steady movement!" (رَبُّكُمُ الَّذِي يُزْجِي لَكُمُ الْفُلْكَ فِي الْبَحْرِ).

It is evident that for ships to move in the sea, various systems have worked together to make this possible: on one hand, water has been created as a smooth vessel; on the other hand, the specific gravity of some objects is lighter than water so they remain afloat, or if they are heavier, they can be shaped in a way that their effective specific gravity becomes less than water, allowing them to bear heavy loads and many people.

Thirdly, a driving force is required. In ancient times, there were regular winds that blew across the oceans with a specific order, and familiarity with the timing, path, and speed of these winds allowed captains to use their immense power to move sailing ships. Today, steam power—which is a sibling to wind—is used to move massive vessels.

Fourthly, there is a need for navigation tools, which in the past were the sun and the stars, and today are compasses and maps.

In any case, if these four factors did not work together and harmonize for the orderly movement of ships, humanity would have been deprived of this vital means of transport and smooth-riding vessel.

Of course, you know that ships have always been, and remain, the largest means of transport for humans; today we have giant ships that are as vast and carry as many passengers as a small city.

Then it adds: "The purpose of this arrangement is so that you may seek of His bounty" (لِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ).

For your travels, for the transport of trade goods, and for whatever aids your religion and your worldly life.

"For indeed, He is ever Merciful toward you" (إِنَّهُ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً).

From this logical monotheism, which shows a small corner of the system of creation indicating the source of knowledge, power, and wisdom of the Creator, the discourse shifts to innate (fitari) reasoning and says: Do not forget: "And when distress touches you at sea (and you are caught in storms and crushing, terrifying waves), those you invoke vanish from you, except Him" (وَ إِذا مَسَّكُمُ الضُّرُّ فِي الْبَحْرِ ضَلَّ مَنْ تَدْعُونَ إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ).

And they must vanish, for the storm of events brushes aside the veils of imitation and prejudice that have fallen over human nature, and the light of the fitrah—which is the light of monotheism and the worship of the One God—shines forth. Yes, in such moments, all imaginary and fabled deities, to whom the power of human illusion had given strength, melt like snow in the summer sun and fade from the mind, and only the light of Allah shines within it.

This is a universal law that almost everyone has experienced: in hardships, when the knife reaches the bone, when outward means fail, and material aids become powerless, man remembers a Great Source of knowledge and power who is capable of solving the most difficult problems.

We need not concern ourselves with what name they give this Source; it is enough to know that a window of hope opens to the heart and a gentle yet powerful light is sprinkled within. This is one of the closest paths to God—a path from within the soul and the depths of the heart. [^1]

Then it adds: But you forgetful ones, "when He delivers you to the land, you turn away, and man is ever ungrateful" (فَلَمَّا نَجَّاكُمْ إِلَى الْبَرِّ أَعْرَضْتُمْ وَ كانَ الْإِنْسانُ كَفُوراً).

Once again, the veils of pride, negligence, imitation, and prejudice cover this divine light, and the dust of rebellion, sin, and the distractions of material life hide its radiant face.

But do you think that Allah cannot afflict you with severe punishments on land and in the heart of the desert? "Do you then feel secure that He will not cause a side of the land to swallow you up?" (أَ فَأَمِنْتُمْ أَنْ يَخْسِفَ بِكُمْ جانِبَ الْبَرِّ).

And "Do you feel secure that He will not send against you a storm of stones and bury you under them (a punishment far more severe than drowning in the seas), then you will find for yourselves no protector"?! (أَوْ يُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ حاصِباً ثُمَّ لا تَجِدُوا لَكُمْ وَكِيلًا).

Desert dwellers, who were particularly familiar with the fact that sometimes storms would blow in the heart of the wilderness, carrying masses of sand and pebbles and depositing them elsewhere to form a massive mound—such that sometimes camel caravans were buried beneath them—understood the weight of this threat more deeply.

Then it adds: O forgetful ones, did you imagine this was the last time you would need to travel by sea? "Or do you feel secure that He will not send you back into it another time, and send upon you a crushing gale and drown you because of your disbelief, then you will not find for yourselves against Us any avenger?!" (أَمْ أَمِنْتُمْ أَنْ يُعِيدَكُمْ فِيهِ تارَةً أُخْرى‌ فَيُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ قاصِفاً مِنَ الرِّيحِ فَيُغْرِقَكُمْ بِما كَفَرْتُمْ ثُمَّ لا تَجِدُوا لَكُمْ عَلَيْنا بِهِ تَبِيعاً).

Key Points

1- People of Shallow Capacity

This state—remembering God when problems arise and forgetting Him in times of ease—is the condition of many people. In such individuals, their "second nature" is forgetfulness and a lack of attention to the realities of life.

Therefore, turning toward Allah and the realities of life is considered an exceptional state for them that requires extraordinary factors. As long as that extraordinary state exists, they remember Allah, but as soon as it is removed, they return to their deviant nature and consign Allah to oblivion.

In short, one can find few people who do not bow before the magnificent threshold of Allah during severe and grueling hardships, but we know that this awakening and "forced attention" is of little value.

The people of faith and true Muslims are those who remember Him in ease and affliction, in health and sickness, in abundance and famine, in prison and upon the throne of power—in short, in all circumstances. Fundamentally, these changes in state do not alter them at all.

Their souls are so great that they absorb all of this within themselves, like Ali عليه السلام, whose worship, asceticism, and care for the suffering while on the throne of power were the same as when he was in seclusion.

As he himself says regarding the qualities of the pious: Nazalat anfusuhum minhum fil-bala’i kallati nazalat fir-rakha’i: "Their state in affliction and ease is the same." [^1]

The short of it is: faith, turning to Allah, seeking intercession, worship, repentance, and submission to the Lord are all valuable only if they are permanent and stable. However, seasonal faith, seasonal repentance, and seasonal worship performed under duress or where human interests dictate are useless or of very little value, and the verses of the Quran repeatedly rebuke such people.

2- Escape from the Domain of Allah's Sovereignty is Impossible

Some, like the idolaters of the Age of Ignorance, only turn to Allah when, for example, they are caught in the middle of the ocean, on a dangerous precipice, or in a state of severe illness. Yet, if we think correctly, man is extremely vulnerable at all times and in all places; sea and land, health and sickness, the precipice and elsewhere are actually all the same.

A minor earthquake can turn our safe, peaceful, and quiet home into a terrifying ruin. A small blood clot can block a major artery of the heart or brain, and with a heart attack or stroke, death can arrive in a single second. Given these facts, how ignorant is it to be negligent of Allah and to forget His Pure Essence?!

Proponents of the hypothesis that religion is based on fear might seize upon this subject and say: fear of various natural factors drives man toward God and strengthens such a notion in his mind.

But the verses of the Quran answer such delusions, for the Quran never bases the foundation of knowing God on this issue. Rather, it places the foundation on the study of the system of creation and discovering His Pure Essence through this study. Even in the verses above, we saw that before mentioning innate monotheism, it addresses logical faith and, in reality, considers these events as "reminders" of God, not the "cause" of knowing Him—for knowing Him is manifest to truth-seekers through both logic and nature (fitrah).

3- Meaning of Several Words

"Yuzji" (يُزْجِي), as we mentioned, comes from the root izja’, meaning to move something continuously.

"Hasib" (حاصِب) refers to a wind that moves pebbles and strikes them repeatedly against a place; it is originally derived from hasba’, meaning pebbles.

"Qasif" (قاصِف) means "breaker," and here it refers to a severe storm that breaks everything apart.

"Tabi‘" (تَبِيع) means "follower," and here it refers to someone who rises to demand blood or blood money and pursues the matter.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 193. [^1]: For a full explanation of innate monotheism, please study the book The Creator of the World, and we have also referred to this issue in the commentary of Surah An-Nahl, verse 14.

Ayahs

۞ وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنَا بَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ وَحَمَلْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ وَرَزَقْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ وَفَضَّلْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَىٰ كَثِيرٍ مِّمَّنْ خَلَقْنَا تَفْضِيلًا (٧٠)
Certainly We have honoured the Children of Adam, and carried them over land and sea, and provided them with all the good things, and preferred them with a complete preference over many of those We have created. (70)
يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا۟ كُلَّ أُنَاسٍۭ بِإِمَـٰمِهِمْ ۖ فَمَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِيَمِينِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَقْرَءُونَ كِتَـٰبَهُمْ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا (٧١)
The day We shall summon every group of people along with their imam, then whoever is given his book in his right hand—they will read it, and they will not be wronged so much as a single date-thread. (71)
وَمَن كَانَ فِى هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أَعْمَىٰ فَهُوَ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَعْمَىٰ وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا (٧٢)
But whoever has been blind in this [world], will be blind in the Hereafter, and [even] more astray from the [right] way. (72)

Commentary

Man, the Crown of Creation

Since one of the methods of education and guidance is to bestow a sense of dignity and character upon individuals, the Holy Quran—following the discussions regarding the polytheists and the deviant in the preceding verses—proceeds here to describe the lofty status of the human species and the Divine favors bestowed upon him. This is done so that, by recognizing this extraordinary value, man may not easily sully his essence or sell himself for a meager price. It says: "We have honored the children of Adam" (وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنَا بَنِي آدَمَ).

Then, it points to three categories of Divine favors toward human beings, stating:

"We carried them on land and sea" through the various means of transport placed at their disposal (وَحَمَلْنَاهُمْ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ). Furthermore, "We provided them with good things" (وَرَزَقْنَاهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّبَاتِ).

Considering the breadth of the word "Tayyib" (good/pure), which encompasses every wholesome and pure being, the vastness of this great Divine blessing becomes apparent.

Thirdly: "We preferred them over many of those We created with a marked preference" (وَفَضَّلْنَاهُمْ عَلَىٰ كَثِيرٍ مِمَّنْ خَلَقْنَا تَفْضِيلًا).

Key Points

1- Transportation: Man's First Blessing

A point of interest here is why, among all the favors bestowed upon man, Allah first mentions his movement on land and sea.

This may be because benefiting from "good things" and various types of sustenance is impossible without movement, and man's movement across the earth requires suitable means of transport. Indeed, "movement" (harakat) is the prelude to every "blessing" (barakat).

Alternatively, it may be to highlight his dominion over the entire expanse of the earth, including both sea and desert. While other species are confined to specific regions of the earth, only man governs this entire terrestrial globe—over the sea, the desert, and the heights of the air.

2- Allah’s Honoring of Mankind

Commentators have discussed what exactly Allah has honored man with, as the verse states broadly, "We have honored the children of Adam." Some believe it refers to the gift of intellect, speech, various talents, and freedom of will.

Some point to his balanced physique and upright stature.

Others mention the gift of fingers, which allow man to perform delicate and precise tasks and grant him the power to write.

Some suggest it is because man is nearly the only creature that can eat his food with his hands.

Some attribute it to his dominion over all creatures on earth.

And some believe it is due to his knowledge of God and his capacity to obey His commands.

However, it is clear that these favors are all combined in man and are not contradictory. Therefore, God's honoring of this great creature encompasses all these favors and more.

In short, man possesses numerous advantages over other creatures, each more fascinating and loftier than the last. Beyond physical advantages, the human soul is a collection of sublime talents and immense capabilities for traversing the path of perfection infinitely.

3- The Difference Between "Karramna" and "Faddalna"

Various opinions have been expressed regarding the difference between these two terms.

Some have said that "Karramna" (We honored) refers to the favors Allah has inherently given to man, while "Faddalna" (We preferred) refers to the virtues man acquires through Divine success.

It also seems very likely that the phrase "Karramna" refers to material aspects, while "Faddalna" refers to spiritual favors, as the word "Fadl" is frequently used in the Quran in this sense.

4- What Does "Many" (Kathir) Mean Here?

Some commentators have taken this verse as evidence of the superiority of angels over all children of Adam, because the Quran says, "We preferred them over many of those We created," implying there is a group over whom man is not superior, and this group must be the angels.

However, considering the verses regarding the creation of Adam, the prostration and submission of the angels to him, and Adam teaching them the "names," there remains no doubt that the [perfected] human is superior to the angel. Therefore, "many" (kathir) here means "all." According to the great commentator Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, it is very common in the Quran and Arabic speech for this word to mean "all."

Tabarsi states: The meaning of the sentence is: "We preferred them over those We created, and they are many."

Regarding the devils, the Quran says: "And most of them are liars" (Surah Ash-Shu'ara, Verse 223). It is obvious that all devils are liars, not just most of them.

In any case, even if we consider this meaning contrary to the literal sense, the verses on the creation of man serve as a clear contextual proof for it.

5- Why is Man the Best of God's Creatures?

The answer to this question is not very complex. We know that the only being composed of various forces—material and spiritual, physical and metaphysical—who can grow amidst contradictions and possesses the potential for infinite perfection and progress, is man.

A famous Hadith narrated from Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام is a clear witness to this claim:

"Allah created the creation in three types: angels, animals, and humans. Angels have intellect without desire or anger; animals are a collection of desire and anger without intellect; but man is a collection of both. Whichever prevails, he belongs to that side. If his intellect overcomes his desire, he is superior to the angels, and if his desire overcomes his intellect, he is lower than the animals." [^1]

One question remains: Are all humans superior to angels? While there are faithless, wicked, and oppressive groups who are considered the lowest of God's creation. In other words, does "children of Adam" in the verse include all humans or only a group of them?

The answer can be summarized in one sentence: Yes, all humans are superior, but potentially and by capacity. That is, everyone has this background and merit; if they do not utilize it and fall, it is due to their own actions.

Although man's fundamental superiority over other beings lies in his spiritual and human aspects, it is worth noting that, according to scientists, man is even physically stronger and more powerful than other living beings in certain respects (though he may seem weaker in others).

Alexis Carrel, the author of Man, The Unknown, writes:

"The human body possesses extraordinary durability and delicacy, resisting all kinds of accidents. It shows amazing endurance against lack of food, sleep, fatigue, extreme grief, pain, illness, suffering, and overwork, and in maintaining the balance of its body and soul. It can even be said that man is more durable and hardworking than all animals. With this marvelous physical and intellectual ability, he has been able to create all these affairs, industries, and current civilization, proving his superiority over all living beings." [^1]

The next verse points to another Divine favor toward man, and subsequently, the heavy responsibilities that parallel these favors:

At the beginning, it addresses the "issue of leadership" and its role in the destiny of humans, saying: "The Day We shall call every people with their Imam (leader)" (يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُنَاسٍ بِإِمَامِهِمْ). This means those who accepted the leadership of the Prophets and their successors in every age will be with their leader, and those who chose the leadership of Satan, the leaders of misguidance, and tyrannical leaders will be with them.

In short, the bond of "leadership" and "followership" in this world is fully reflected in that world, and based on that, the groups belonging to salvation or punishment are identified.

Although some commentators have sought to interpret "Imam" here exclusively as the Prophets, or the Heavenly Books, or the scholars, it is clear that "Imam" here has a broad meaning encompassing every leader—including Prophets, the Imams of Guidance, scholars, the Book, and the Sunnah, as well as the leaders of disbelief and misguidance. Thus, everyone there will be aligned with the leadership they chose in this world.

This expression, while describing one of the means of human perfection, is a warning to all individuals to be exceptionally careful and rigorous in choosing a leader and not to entrust the reins of their thought and life to just anyone.

Then it says: There, people will be divided into two groups: "Those who are given their record in their right hand, they will read their record with pride, honor, and joy, and not the slightest injustice will be done to them" (فَمَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتَابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ فَأُولَٰئِكَ يَقْرَءُونَ كِتَابَهُمْ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ فَتِيلًا). [^1]

"But those who were blind-hearted in this world will also be blind in the Hereafter" (وَمَنْ كَانَ فِي هَٰذِهِ أَعْمَىٰ فَهُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ أَعْمَىٰ).

And it is natural that these blind-hearted ones are the most astray (وَأَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا).

They neither find the path of guidance in this world nor the path to Paradise and happiness in the Hereafter, because they closed their eyes to all realities and deprived themselves of seeing the face of Truth, the signs of Allah, and that which brings guidance and lessons, despite all the favors Allah had bestowed upon them. Since the Hereafter is a massive reflection of this world, what wonder is it that these blind-hearted ones enter the gathering place of Resurrection as blind?!

Key Points

1- The Role of Leadership in Human Life

Accepting collective life in human existence cannot be separated from the issue of leadership, for to determine the main course of a population, there is always a need for a leader. Fundamentally, traversing the path of perfection is impossible without utilizing the existence of a leader; this is the secret behind sending Prophets and choosing successors for them.

In the science of theology (Kalam), using the "Rule of Grace" (Lutf) and considering the role of the leader in social order and preventing deviations, the mission of the Prophets and the necessity of an Imam's existence in every age have been proven.

Just as a Divine, knowledgeable, and righteous leader makes the path to the ultimate goal easy and swift, submitting to the leadership of the Imams of disbelief and misguidance throws one into the abyss of misery and wretchedness.

Regarding the interpretation of this verse in Islamic sources, several Hadiths have been narrated that clarify the concept of the verse and the purpose of Imamate:

In a Hadith narrated by both Shi'a and Sunni sources from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام through authentic chains, we read that the Imam narrated from his forefathers from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the interpretation of this verse:

"Every people will be called with the Imam of their time, the Book of their Lord, and the Sunnah of their Prophet." [^1]

We also read from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام:

"Do you not praise Allah? When the Day of Resurrection comes, Allah calls every group to the one they took as a guardian (Wali); He calls us to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله and you seek refuge with us. So where do you think they will take you? To Paradise, by the Lord of the Kaaba—the Imam repeated this three times." [^1]

2- The Dignity of the Children of Adam

"Children of Adam" (Bani Adam) is usually a title in the Quran for humans accompanied by praise, commendation, and respect, whereas the word "Insan" (Man) is often described with attributes such as "unjust" (Zalum), "ignorant" (Jahul), "impatient" (Halu'), "weak," "rebellious," "ungrateful," and the like. This indicates that "Children of Adam" refers to the educated human or at least looks at the positive potentials of man. (The reference to the honors of Adam and his superiority over angels inherent in the term "Children of Adam" also supports this). In contrast, the word "Insan" refers to man in an absolute sense and sometimes points to his negative aspects. Therefore, in the verses under discussion, which speak of the dignity, nobility, and preference of man, the term "Bani Adam" is used.

3- The Role of Leadership in Islam

In a famous Hadith from Imam Baqir عليه السلام, when discussing the main pillars of Islam, he introduces "Wilayah" (Leadership) as the fifth and most important pillar, while Prayer (representing the bond between creature and Creator), Fasting (the symbol of struggle against desires), Zakat (the bond between creature and creature), and Hajj (representing the social aspects of Islam) are the other four main pillars.

Then the Imam عليه السلام adds: "Nothing has been emphasized as much as Wilayah and Leadership." (Because the implementation of the other principles occurs under its shadow). [^1]

For this same reason, it is narrated in a famous Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Whoever dies without an Imam dies the death of Jahiliyyah (Ignorance)." [^2]

History also remembers well that sometimes a nation, under the leadership of a great and worthy leader, has stood in the first rank of the world, and sometimes that same nation, with the same human resources and other assets, has fallen so low due to weak and incompetent leadership that one might not believe it is the same progressive nation.

Was it not the pre-Islamic Arabs who were wallowing in ignorance, misery, corruption, humiliation, and fragmentation? It was because they lacked a worthy leader. But with the appearance of the Divine leader, Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, they traversed the path of progress, perfection, and greatness so rapidly that it left the world in wonder. Yes, this is the role of the leader in that time, this time, and all times.

Of course, Allah has placed a leader for every age and time for the salvation and guidance of mankind, for His wisdom requires that the decree of happiness not be without a guarantor of implementation. However, the important thing is for people to recognize their leader and not fall into the trap of misguided, corrupt, and corrupting leaders, from whose clutches escape is difficult.

The Shi'a belief in the existence of an infallible Imam in every age has this very philosophy, as Ali عليه السلام said:

"O Allah, yes! The earth shall never be void of one who stands for Allah with a proof (Hujjah), whether manifest and famous or (due to lack of sufficient followers) fearful and obscure, so that Allah's proofs and signs may not be nullified." [^1]

4- The Blind-Hearted!

The Quran uses a striking expression for the polytheists and wrongdoers in the above verses, describing them as "blind" (A'ma). This points to the reality that the face of Truth is manifest everywhere if there is a seeing eye—an eye that sees the signs of God in the expanse of this world, an eye that reads the lessons of history, an eye that observes the fate of tyrants and oppressors; in short, an open and truth-seeking eye! But when thick veils of ignorance, pride, prejudice, stubbornness, and lust disable this seeing eye of the human heart, it can no longer see. Even though the beauty of Truth has no veil, he is unable to witness it.

In a Hadith from Imam Baqir عليه السلام, we read regarding the interpretation of the above verse:
