"He whom the creation of the heavens and the earth, the alternation of night and day, the rotation of the spheres, the sun, the moon, and the wondrous signs do not guide to the fact that behind all this is a matter greater than it, he is blind in the Hereafter and more astray." [^1]

Also, in several narrations, this verse is interpreted as referring to someone who has the means for Hajj but does not perform it until the end of his life. [^2] Undoubtedly, this is one of the instances of this verse, not all of it. Perhaps this instance is mentioned because participating in the Hajj ceremonies and witnessing that great Islamic congress and the devotional and political secrets hidden within it opens a person's eyes and teaches him many truths.

In some other narrations, the worst blindness is considered the blindness of the heart: "The worst blindness is the blindness of the heart." [^3]

In any case, as we have said many times, the world of Resurrection is a reflection of this world and our beliefs and actions here. For this reason, we read in Surah Taha, verses 124 to 126:

"And whoever turns away from My remembrance—indeed, he will have a depressed life, and We will gather him on the Day of Resurrection blind. He will say, 'My Lord, why have you raised me blind while I was [once] seeing?' [Allah] will say, 'Thus did Our signs come to you, and you forgot them; and thus will you this Day be forgotten!'"

Ayahs

وَإِن كَادُوا۟ لَيَفْتِنُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ لِتَفْتَرِىَ عَلَيْنَا غَيْرَهُۥ ۖ وَإِذًا لَّٱتَّخَذُوكَ خَلِيلًا (٧٣)
They were about to beguile you from what Allah has revealed to you so that you may fabricate something other than that against Us, whereat they would have befriended you. (73)
وَلَوْلَآ أَن ثَبَّتْنَـٰكَ لَقَدْ كِدتَّ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْـًٔا قَلِيلًا (٧٤)
And had We not fortified you, certainly you might have inclined toward them a bit. (74)
إِذًا لَّأَذَقْنَـٰكَ ضِعْفَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ وَضِعْفَ ٱلْمَمَاتِ ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُ لَكَ عَلَيْنَا نَصِيرًا (٧٥)
Then We would have surely made you taste a double [punishment] in this life and a double [punishment] after death, and then you would have not found for yourself any helper against Us. (75)

Circumstances of Revelation

Regarding these controversial verses, various circumstances of revelation (shan nuzul) have been narrated, some of which are entirely inconsistent with the historical timeline of their revelation. However, since these narrations have become a pretext for some deviants, we shall mention all of them:

The late Tabarsi in Mجمع al-Bayan has cited five opinions in this regard:

  1. The Quraysh said to the Prophet: "We will not allow you to touch the Black Stone (al-Hajar al-Aswad) unless you at least look upon our gods with an eye of respect." The Prophet thought to himself: "Allah knows that I detest these idols, but what harm is there if I cast a glance toward them so they allow me to perform the istilam (kissing/touching) of the Black Stone?" These verses were then revealed, forbidding the Prophet from doing so.

  2. The Quraysh proposed: "Stop insulting our gods and belittling our intellects, and remove these slaves and low-born individuals—from whom a foul odor emanates—from your presence, so that we may attend your assembly and listen to your words." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, hoping they might become Muslim, considered accepting their request (even if temporarily), when these verses were revealed to warn him.

  3. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله cleared the idols from the Sacred Mosque (al-Masjid al-Haram). The Quraysh requested that he allow an idol located on Mount Marwa near the House of Allah to remain. Initially, for the sake of advancing political goals, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله decided to act on this proposal but later reconsidered and ordered that idol to be broken as well. At this time, the above verses were revealed.

  4. A group of representatives from the Thaqif tribe came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "We will pledge allegiance to you on three conditions: First, we will not bow for ruku' or sujud during prayer! Second, we will not break our idols with our own hands; you break them yourself! Third, allow the idol 'Lat' to remain for one year!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied: "A religion without ruku' and sujud is of no use. As for breaking the idols with your own hands, if you wish to do it, do so; if not, we will break them ourselves! But as for the worship of 'Lat,' I will not grant you such permission."

At this moment, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله stood up to perform ablution, and Umar turned to the people and said: "Why do you trouble the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله? He will never allow idols to remain in the land of the Arabs." Yet, the petitioners continued their request until these verses were revealed.

  1. A group of representatives from the Thaqif clan came to him and said: "Give us a one-year respite so we may collect the gifts brought for the idols. Once this is done, we ourselves will break the idols and embrace Islam." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was considering granting them this respite for certain reasons when the verses were revealed, strictly forbidding it.

Other circumstances of revelation similar to those mentioned above have also been narrated.

However, it perhaps requires no explanation that the inaccuracy of most of them is inherent within them. This is because the arrival of tribal representatives and their requests to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, or the removal of idols from the Sacred Mosque and their destruction, all occurred after the Conquest of Mecca in the eighth year of the Hijra. Meanwhile, this Surah was fundamentally revealed before the Prophet's migration (Hijra), at a time when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not possess such outward power that the polytheists would show such humility before him.

Regardless of this issue, the baselessness of some others will become clear from the explanations that follow.

Commentary

The Punishment for the Slightest Flexibility Toward Polytheism!

In light of the previous discussion in the verses regarding polytheism and the polytheists, the verses under discussion warn the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to beware of the whisperings of this group, lest he allow the slightest weakness in the struggle against polytheism and idol-worship, which must be pursued with the utmost decisiveness.

First, He says: "Indeed, they were about to tempt you away from what We revealed to you, to fabricate something else against Us, and then they would have surely taken you as a friend" (وَ إِنْ كادُوا لَيَفْتِنُونَكَ عَنِ الَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ لِتَفْتَرِيَ عَلَيْنا غَيْرَهُ وَ إِذاً لَاتَّخَذُوكَ خَلِيلًا).

"And had We not firmed your heart upon the truth and reality (and had you not become steadfast in the light of the light of Infallibility), you would have nearly inclined to them a little" (وَ لَوْ لا أَنْ ثَبَّتْناكَ لَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئاً قَلِيلًا).

"And if you had done so, We would have made you taste double the punishment of the polytheists in the life of this world, and double after death; then you would not find for yourself any helper against Us" (إِذاً لَأَذَقْناكَ ضِعْفَ الْحَياةِ وَ ضِعْفَ الْمَماتِ ثُمَّ لا تَجِدُ لَكَ عَلَيْنا نَصِيراً).

Key Points

1- Did the Prophet Show Favor to the Polytheists?

Although fault-finders have sought to use the above verses as a pretext to deny the infallibility ('ismah) of the Prophets, claiming that according to these verses and the narrated circumstances of revelation, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله showed flexibility toward the whisperings of the idolaters and was immediately rebuked by Allah.

However, the verses themselves are so eloquent that they spare us from providing other evidence for the falsity of this way of thinking. This is because the second verse under discussion explicitly states: "Had We not firmed you, you would have nearly inclined to them," which implies that Divine stabilization—which we refer to as the "Station of Infallibility"—prevented this inclination. It does not mean that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had shown flexibility and Allah then forbade and rebuked him.

To explain: The first and second verses actually refer to two different states of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

The first state, which is the human state and that of an ordinary person, described in the first verse, necessitates that the whisperings of enemies might affect him, especially if there appear to be apparent benefits in such flexibility—such as the hope that the leaders of polytheism might embrace Islam after this flexibility, or the prevention of further bloodshed and conflict. Any ordinary human, no matter how strong, has the potential to be influenced by such whisperings.

However, the second verse describes the spiritual aspect, Divine Infallibility, and the special grace of the Lord, which encompassed the Prophets, especially the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, during the most critical moments of life.

The conclusion is that the Prophet, by his human nature, came to the very edge of the precipice of accepting the polytheists' whisperings, but Divine support protected and saved him.

This expression is exactly like the one we read in Surah Yusuf, where at the most critical moment, the Divine Proof came to him, and had he not witnessed this proof, he would have succumbed to the exceptionally powerful whisperings of the wife of the Aziz of Egypt: (وَ لَدَقْ هَمَّتْ بِهِ وَ هَمَّ بِها لَوْ لا أَنْ رَأى‌ بُرْهانَ رَبِّهِ كَذلِكَ لِنَصْرِفَ عَنْهُ السُّوءَ وَ الْفَحْشاءَ إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبادِنَا الْمُخْلَصِينَ‌) "And she certainly desired him, and he would have desired her had he not seen the proof of his Lord. Thus [it was] that We might avert from him evil and immorality. Indeed, he was of Our chosen servants" (Surah Yusuf, Verse 24).

In our view, these verses are not only not evidence for the denial of infallibility, but are among the verses proving infallibility. This is because this Divine stabilization (stabilization in terms of thought, emotions, and practical steps) was certainly not limited to this instance, as the reason for it exists in similar cases as well. Thus, it is considered a living witness to the infallibility of the Prophets and Divine leaders.

As for the third verse under discussion, which says: "If you had inclined toward them, you would have been severely punished," it serves as evidence for what has been stated in discussions regarding the infallibility of the Prophets: that their being infallible is not a matter of compulsion (idtirari). Rather, it is coupled with a type of self-awareness that is performed with choice and freedom of will. Therefore, committing a sin in such a state is not intellectually impossible, but practically, due to that specific awareness and faith, it will never manifest externally. And if, hypothetically, it were to manifest, it would be subject to those same Divine penalties and punishments. (Reflect carefully) [1]

2- Why Double the Punishment?

It is clear that the higher a person's station in terms of knowledge, awareness, cognition, and faith, the deeper and more valuable their good deeds will be, and naturally, the greater the reward. Thus, we read in some narrations: "Indeed, the reward is in proportion to the intellect" [2]. Penalties and punishments will also increase in the same proportion. If an illiterate person with weak faith commits a major sin, it is not entirely unexpected, and for this reason, they receive a lesser punishment. However, if a person of faith and long-standing scholarly standing commits even a minor sin, it is a cause for wonder, and perhaps their punishment for this small sin will be more severe and heavy than the punishment of that illiterate commoner for a major sin!

For this reason, we read in the Holy Quran regarding the wives of the Prophet: "O wives of the Prophet, whoever of you should commit a clear immorality - for her the punishment would be doubled two fold, and that is ever easy for Allah. And whoever of you devoutly obeys Allah and His Messenger and does righteousness - We will give her her reward twice over, and We have prepared for her a noble provision" (Surah Al-Ahzab, Verses 30 and 31).

In narrations, we also read: "Allah forgives seventy sins of the ignorant before He forgives one sin of the scholar!" [3]

The above verses also point to this reality, telling the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that if you were to find any deviation or inclination toward polytheism and the polytheists, We would double your punishment in this world and the hereafter.

3- "Di'f" Means Two or Multiple Times

It is necessary to note precisely that "Di'f" (ضعف) in the Arabic language does not only mean "twofold," but rather means "two or multiple times."

"Fayruzabadi," the famous lexicographer of the eighth century AH, says in the book Al-Qamus that sometimes "Di'f of such-and-such a thing" is said, and twofold or threefold is intended, because this word is used for unlimited addition.

The evidence for this is that in the verses of the Quran, regarding "good deeds" (hasanat), it sometimes says: "If there is a good deed, He multiplies it (yuda'ifha)" (Surah An-Nisa, Verse 40), and sometimes it says: "Whoever comes [on the Day of Judgment] with a good deed will have ten times the like thereof" (Al-An'am, 160).

In Islamic narrations from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of Verse 261 of Surah Al-Baqarah, we also read: "When a believer performs his deed well, Allah multiplies his deed by seven hundred times for every good deed, and that is the word of Allah: 'And Allah multiplies for whom He wills'" [4]. However, this does not prevent the word from meaning "twofold" when it is used in the dual form (di'fan or di'fayn), or meaning "threefold" when it is used in an additive construction, such as saying "di'f al-wahid" (Reflect carefully).

4- Interpretation of the Phrase "إِذاً لَاتَّخَذُوكَ خَلِيلًا"

The famous view among commentators is that the Quran says: "If you had inclined toward the desires of the polytheists, they would have chosen you as their friend." However, some have suggested that the meaning of this phrase is that they would have made you poor, dependent, and needy of them. (In the first case, Khalil is from the root khullah meaning friendship, and in the second case, it is from the root khallah meaning need and poverty). But it is clear that the first interpretation is the correct one.

5- O Allah, Do Not Leave Me to Myself

In Islamic sources, we read that when the above verses were revealed, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "O Allah, do not leave me to myself for even the blink of an eye, ever!" This meaningful prayer of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله teaches an important lesson to all of us: that in every situation, one must seek refuge in Allah and rely on His grace, for even the infallible Prophets would not be immune in slippery places without His help, let alone us in the face of all these satanic whisperings.

[1]: Read more on this subject in the book The Great Leaders (Rahbaran-e Bozorg). [2]: Usul al-Kafi, Volume 1, Book of Intellect and Ignorance, page 9, Hadith 8. [3]: Usul al-Kafi, Volume 1, page 37. [4]: Tafsir al-Ayyashi (as cited in Tafsir al-Mizan, Volume 2, page 424).

Ayahs

وَإِن كَادُوا۟ لَيَسْتَفِزُّونَكَ مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ لِيُخْرِجُوكَ مِنْهَا ۖ وَإِذًا لَّا يَلْبَثُونَ خِلَـٰفَكَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٧٦)
They were about to hound you out of the land, to expel you from it, but then they would not have stayed after you but a little. (76)
سُنَّةَ مَن قَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ مِن رُّسُلِنَا ۖ وَلَا تَجِدُ لِسُنَّتِنَا تَحْوِيلًا (٧٧)
A precedent concerning those We have sent before you from among Our apostles, and you will not find any change in Our precedent. (77)

Occasion of Revelation

The famous view is that the above verses were revealed regarding the people of Mecca, who sat together and decided to expel the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله from Mecca. Later, this decision was revoked and changed to a decision to execute the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in Mecca. Following this, the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله house was besieged from all sides, and as we know, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله emerged from this circle of siege in a miraculous manner and moved toward Medina, which became the beginning of the Hijrah (Migration).

However, some have said that these verses were revealed in relation to a proposal by the Jews of Medina. They came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to expel him from Medina, saying: "This land is not the land of the Prophets; the land of the Prophets is Sham (the Levant). If you want your mission to progress, you must go there."

But considering that this Surah is Meccan, it does not align with this occasion of revelation. Furthermore, the phrasing of the above verses, as we shall see, is also not consistent with the content of this second occasion of revelation.

Commentary

Another Sinister Plot?

In the previous verses, we saw that the polytheists wanted to influence the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله through various temptations and divert him from His straight path, but Divine grace came to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله aid and their plans were thwarted.

Following that incident, according to the verses under discussion, they devised another plan to neutralize the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله call, which was to exile Him from His birthplace to a point that was likely obscure and remote. That too was neutralized by the grace of the Lord.

The first verse under discussion says: "And they were about to provoke you [to leave] the land to expel you from it with a calculated and skillful plan and incitement" (وَ إِنْ كادُوا لَيَسْتَفِزُّونَكَ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ لِيُخْرِجُوكَ مِنْها).

Considering that "yastafizzunaka" is derived from the root "istifzaz," which sometimes means uprooting and sometimes means inciting with speed and skill, it becomes clear that the polytheists had laid a calculated conspiracy to make the environment so unbearable for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, or to incite the common masses against Him so intensely, that they could easily expel Him from Mecca. However, they did not know that above their power is the power of the Great Allah, before whose Will they are extremely weak and helpless.

Then the Quran warns them: "And if they had done so, they would not have remained after you except for a little while" (وَ إِذاً لا يَلْبَثُونَ خِلافَكَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا) and they would have soon been destroyed. This is because it is a very grave sin for a people to expel their compassionate and life-saving leader from their city, thereby showing ingratitude for the greatest Divine blessing. Such a population would no longer have the right to live, and Allah's destructive punishment would come upon them.

This is not limited only to the Arab polytheists: "[This is] the way of those We sent before you of Our messengers; and you will not find in Our way any alteration" (سُنَّةَ مَنْ قَدْ أَرْسَلْنا قَبْلَكَ مِنْ رُسُلِنا وَ لا تَجِدُ لِسُنَّتِنا تَحْوِيلًا).

This Sunnah (tradition/way) stems from a clear logic: such an ungrateful people who break their own lamp of guidance, destroy their own fortress of salvation, and harass the physician of their agonizing pains—yes, such a people are no longer worthy of Divine mercy, and punishment will overtake them. We know that Allah does not practice discrimination among His servants; that is, He ordains the same punishment for identical actions (under identical conditions).

And this is the meaning of the non-violation of the Lord's Sunnahs, unlike the traditions of autocratic humans who, one day, find that their interests require them to establish a rule, and the next day, when their interests dictate otherwise, they abolish it and sometimes even put its opposite in its place.

Fundamentally, the change of traditions in human societies is either due to unknown issues that become clear over time—showing man that he was caught in mistakes in the past—or due to the requirements of specific interests, living conditions, or autocracy. We know that none of these issues find their way into the Pure Essence of Allah. A Sunnah that He has established according to wisdom has always been and will always be in effect under similar conditions.

Ayahs

أَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ لِدُلُوكِ ٱلشَّمْسِ إِلَىٰ غَسَقِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَقُرْءَانَ ٱلْفَجْرِ ۖ إِنَّ قُرْءَانَ ٱلْفَجْرِ كَانَ مَشْهُودًا (٧٨)
Maintain the prayer [during the period] from the sun’s decline till the darkness of the night, and [observe particularly] the dawn recital. Indeed the dawn recital is attended [by angels]. (78)
وَمِنَ ٱلَّيْلِ فَتَهَجَّدْ بِهِۦ نَافِلَةً لَّكَ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَبْعَثَكَ رَبُّكَ مَقَامًا مَّحْمُودًا (٧٩)
And keep vigil for a part of the night, as a supererogatory [devotion] for you. It may be that your Lord will raise you to a praiseworthy station. (79)
وَقُل رَّبِّ أَدْخِلْنِى مُدْخَلَ صِدْقٍ وَأَخْرِجْنِى مُخْرَجَ صِدْقٍ وَٱجْعَل لِّى مِن لَّدُنكَ سُلْطَـٰنًا نَّصِيرًا (٨٠)
And say, ‘My Lord! ‘Admit me with a worthy entrance, and bring me out with a worthy departure, and render me a favourable authority from Yourself.’ (80)
وَقُلْ جَآءَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَزَهَقَ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلْبَـٰطِلَ كَانَ زَهُوقًا (٨١)
And say, ‘The truth has come, and falsehood has vanished. Indeed falsehood is bound to vanish.’ (81)

Commentary

The End of Falsehood is Destruction

Following the previous verses which discussed Monotheism (Tawhid) and polytheism, and subsequently the whispers and conspiracies of the polytheists, the verses under discussion address the issue of prayer, turning toward God, and supplication. These are effective factors in the struggle against polytheism and means to repel any satanic whispers from the heart and soul of man.

Indeed, it is prayer that reminds man of Allah, washes the dust of sin from his heart and soul, and repels satanic whispers.

First, He says: "Establish prayer at the sun's decline until the darkness of the night, and [also] the Qur'an of dawn (the morning prayer). Indeed, the recitation of dawn is ever witnessed [by the angels of night and day]" (أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِدُلُوكِ الشَّمْسِ إِلى‌ غَسَقِ اللَّيْلِ وَ قُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ إِنَّ قُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ كانَ مَشْهُوداً).

"Duluk al-shams" means the decline of the sun from the meridian, which is the time of noon (Zuhr). Originally, it is derived from the root "dalk," meaning to rub, because at that time, due to the intensity of the sun's radiance, man rubs his eyes. Alternatively, it is from dalk meaning to incline, as the sun at this moment inclines from the meridian toward the west; or because man shields his eyes with his hand against the sun, as if he is pushing its light away from his eyes and causing it to incline.

In any case, in a narration reached to us from the sources of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, "duluk" has been interpreted as this very meaning of the sun's decline. In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that 'Ubayd ibn Zurarah asked the Imam عليه السلام about the interpretation of this verse. The Imam عليه السلام said: "Allah has made four prayers obligatory upon Muslims, the beginning of which is the time of the sun's decline (Zuhr) and the end of which is midnight." [1] In another narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of this verse, when Zurarah—the great Hadith scholar of the Shi'a—asked about it, he said:

Dulukuhu zawaluhu, ghasaq al-layl ila nisf al-layl, dhalika arba'u salawat wada'ahunna Rasul Allah (ص) wa waqqatahunna lin-nas wa Qur'an al-fajr salat al-ghadah:

"The decline of the sun (duluk) means its passing (from the meridian), and the darkness of the night (ghasaq al-layl) means midnight. These are four prayers that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله established for the people and timed for them, and 'the Qur'an of dawn' refers to the morning prayer." [2] Of course, some commentators have suggested other possibilities for the meaning of duluk, which are not noteworthy.

As for "ghasaq al-layl," considering that ghasaq is the intensity of darkness, and the darkness of the night is most concentrated at midnight, this phrase altogether signifies "midnight."

"Qur'an" means that which is recited, and "Qur'an al-fajr" altogether refers to the Fajr (dawn) prayer.

For this reason, the above verse is among those that provide a general indication of the times for the five daily prayers. When joined with other verses of the Qur'an regarding prayer times and the numerous narrations transmitted in this regard, the times for the five prayers are precisely determined.

It is necessary to note that some verses of the Qur'an refer only to one prayer, such as: "Guard strictly your prayers and the middle prayer" (Al-Baqarah: 238), where the "middle prayer" (al-salat al-wusta), according to the correct interpretation, is the Zuhr prayer.

Sometimes it refers to the times of three of the five prayers, such as: "And establish prayer at the two ends of the day and at the approach of the night" (Hud: 114), where "the two ends of the day" refers to the Morning and Maghrib prayers, and "the approach of the night" refers to the 'Isha prayer.

And sometimes it mentions all five prayers in summary, like the verse under discussion (we have provided further explanation on this in Volume 9 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 114 of Surah Hud, page 265 onwards).

In any case, there is no doubt that the details of the times for the five prayers are not stated in these verses; rather, like many other Islamic rulings, only the generalities are settled upon, and their detailed explanation has come in the Sunnah of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the truthful Imams عليهم السلام.

Another point remains here: the above verse says: إِنَّ قُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ كانَ مَشْهُوداً: "The recitation of dawn is witnessed." Now the question arises: witnessed by whom?

The narrations that have reached us in the interpretation of this verse say: It is witnessed by the angels of the night and the day. This is because at the start of the morning, the angels of the night who guard the servants of Allah hand over their duty to the angels of the day. Since the morning prayer is performed at the very beginning of dawn, both groups witness it and testify to it.

These narrations have been narrated by both Shi'a and Sunni scholars. Among them (as narrated in Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani), Ahmad, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, al-Tirmidhi, and al-Hakim have narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that in interpreting this sentence he said: Tashhaduhu mala'ikat al-layl wa mala'ikat al-nahar (The angels of the night and the angels of the day witness it). [1]

The famous Sunni Hadith scholars, al-Bukhari and Muslim, have also narrated this same meaning in their Sahih collections. [2]

For awareness of the Hadiths of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 3, under the verse in question.

From this expression, it becomes clearly evident that the best time for performing the morning prayer is those very moments at the beginning of the break of dawn.

After mentioning the five obligatory prayers, He adds: "And during a part of the night, awake from sleep and recite the Qur'an" (وَ مِنَ اللَّيْلِ فَتَهَجَّدْ بِهِ‌). [1] Famous Islamic commentators have considered this expression an indication of the Night Supererogatory Prayer (Nafilah al-Layl), regarding the virtue of which countless narrations have been transmitted. Although the verse is not explicit on this matter, with the various clues at hand, this interpretation seems clear.

Then He says: "This is an additional program for you, beyond the obligatory prayers" (نافلة لك). Many have considered this sentence as evidence that the Night Prayer was obligatory upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, because "nafilah" means "excess" or "addition," indicating that this additional obligation pertains to you.

Others believe that the Night Prayer was previously obligatory upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله by the evidence of the verses of Surah al-Muzzammil, and then the above verse abrogated that and announced its recommendatory (mustahabb) nature.

However, this interpretation seems weak, because nafilah originally did not have today's technical meaning of "recommended prayer," but rather meant "excess" and "addition." We know that if the Night Prayer was obligatory for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is an addition to the daily obligations.

In any case, at the end of the verse, He describes the result of this spiritual and soul-purifying divine program as such: "It may be that your Lord will raise you to a Praised Station" (عَسى‌ أَنْ يَبْعَثَكَ رَبُّكَ مَقاماً مَحْمُوداً).

Undoubtedly, the "Praised Station" (Maqam Mahmud) is a very prominent position that evokes praise (since Mahmud is from the root hamd, meaning praise).

Since this word is used in an absolute sense, it perhaps indicates that it draws the praise of everyone from the first to the last toward you.

Islamic narrations, both from the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام and those narrated through Sunni channels, have interpreted the "Praised Station" as the station of the Great Intercession (Shafa'at al-Kubra), for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is the greatest of intercessors in the other world, and those worthy of intercession will be included in this great intercession.

The next verse refers to one of the fundamental instructions of Islam, which springs from the spirit of faith and Monotheism, saying: "Say: My Lord! Cause me to enter with a truthful entry" (وَ قُلْ رَبِّ أَدْخِلْنِي مُدْخَلَ صِدْقٍ وَ أَخْرِجْنِي مُخْرَجَ صِدْقٍ‌). [1] Let me not begin any individual or social task except with truth and sincerity, and likewise, let me not end any program except with truth. Let truth, sincerity, honesty, and trustworthiness be my main line in all affairs, and let the beginning and end of everything take place with them.

Although some commentators have sought to limit the broad concept of this verse to a specific instance or instances—including the entry into Medina and the exit from it to Mecca, or entry into the grave and exit from it at the time of Resurrection, or the like—it is quite clear that the above comprehensive expression has no limitation. It is a request for truthful entry and exit in everything, in every task, and in every program.

In truth, the main secret of victory lies right here, and the way and method of the Prophets and the Saints of Allah was that their thoughts, speech, and actions be pure from any kind of deception, fraud, trickery, and anything contrary to truth and sincerity.

Fundamentally, many of the misfortunes we see today afflicting individuals, tribes, and nations are due to deviation from this very principle. Sometimes the main foundation of their work is based on lies and trickery, and at times when their entry into affairs is based on truth, they do not maintain this original line until the end, and this will be the factor of their defeat.

The second principle, which from one perspective is the fruit of the tree of Monotheism and from another is the result of truthful entry and exit in affairs, is what is mentioned at the end of the verse:

"O Allah, grant me from Yourself a supporting authority" (وَ اجْعَلْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ سُلْطاناً نَصِيراً).

For I am alone, and one cannot accomplish anything alone, and by relying on my own power, I will not be victorious against all these difficulties. You help me, and You provide helpers for me.

Grant me a powerful logic, crushing arguments against enemies, devoted friends, a strong will, a clear mind, and an abundant intellect—all of which will be my helpers on this path—for none but You is capable of this.

And since, following "sincerity" and "trust" (tawakkul) mentioned in the previous verse, the hope for certain victory is itself another factor for success, in the last verse under discussion, He says to His Prophet: "Say: Truth has come and falsehood has vanished" (وَ قُلْ جاءَ الْحَقُّ وَ زَهَقَ الْباطِلُ‌). [1] Fundamentally, the nature of falsehood is such that it is bound to vanish and be destroyed (إِنَّ الْباطِلَ كانَ زَهُوقاً). Falsehood may have a brief surge, but it will have no permanence or survival, and ultimately, victory belongs to the truth and the supporters and followers of the truth.

Key Points

1. The Night Prayer: A Great Spiritual Worship

The clamor of daily life attracts man's attention from various directions and draws his thoughts into different valleys, such that concentration and complete presence of heart are very difficult during it. However, in the heart of the night and at the time of dawn, when the clamor of material life subsides and man's soul and body find peace through some sleep, a state of special attention and vitality comes to man that is unparalleled.

Indeed, in this calm environment, far from any kind of hypocrisy, ostentation, or showing off, and coupled with presence of heart, a state of attention comes to man that is extraordinarily soul-nurturing and perfection-creating.

For this reason, the friends of Allah have always drawn strength from late-night worship for the purification of the soul, the life of the heart, the strengthening of the will, and the completion of sincerity.

At the beginning of Islam, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله also nurtured the Muslims using this spiritual program and raised their character so much that it was as if they were not the same previous people; that is, he created new human beings out of them—determined, brave, faithful, pure, and sincere. Perhaps the "Praised Station" mentioned in the above verses as the result of the Night Supererogatory Prayer also refers to this reality.

An examination of the narrations transmitted in Islamic sources regarding the virtue of the Night Prayer also clarifies this reality. For example:

  1. The Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله says: "The best of you are those who are polite in speech, feed the hungry, and pray at night while the people are asleep." [1]
  2. The Commander of the Faithful, 'Ali عليه السلام, says: "Standing at night [for prayer] is a source of health for the body, a means of pleasing the Lord, the Mighty and Majestic, an exposure to mercy, and an adherence to the character of the Prophets." [2]
  3. Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said to one of his companions: "Do not abandon the night vigil, for the loser is he who is deprived of the night vigil." [3]
  4. The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله says: "Whoever prays at night, his face (and character) will be beautiful during the day." [4]

We even read in some narrations that this worship is so important that none but the pure and the good succeed in performing it!

  1. A man came to 'Ali, the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام, and said: "I have been deprived of the Night Prayer." 'Ali عليه السلام said: "You are a man whom your sins have shackled!" [1]
  2. In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "A man sometimes tells a lie and is deprived of the Night Prayer because of it; when he is deprived of the Night Prayer, he is also deprived of sustenance (and material and spiritual blessings)." [2]
  3. While we know that someone like 'Ali عليه السلام never abandoned the Night Prayer, the importance of the subject is to such an extent that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to him in his testament: "I advise you regarding yourself with certain traits, so preserve them—then he said: O Allah, help him—... and upon you is the Night Prayer, and upon you is the Night Prayer, and upon you is the Night Prayer!" [3]
  4. The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله said to Gabriel: "Admonish me." Gabriel said: "O Muhammad, live as long as you wish, for you shall surely die; love whatever you wish, for you shall surely part from it; and do whatever you wish, for you shall surely meet it. And know that the honor of the believer is his prayer at night, and his dignity is his refraining from [tarnishing] the reputation of the people." [1]

These celestial counsels of Gabriel, which are all calculated, show that the Night Prayer gives such character, training, spirituality, and faith to man that it is the source of his honor and reputation, just as refraining from bothering people will be the cause of dignity.

  1. Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: "Three things are the pride of the believer and an adornment in this world and the Hereafter: prayer at the end of the night, despairing of what is in the hands of people, and the Wilayah (guardianship) of the Imam from the Family of Muhammad." [2]
  2. It is narrated from the same Imam that he said: "Every good deed that a person of faith performs has its reward explicitly mentioned in the Qur'an, except for the Night Prayer, which Allah, due to its extraordinary importance, did not state explicitly. He only said: 'Their sides forsake their beds, they call upon their Lord in fear and hope, and they spend out of what We have provided them. No soul knows what comforts of the eyes are hidden for them as a reward for what they used to do.'" [3]

Of course, the Night Prayer has many etiquettes, but it is not bad to mention its simplest form here so that lovers of this spiritual act can benefit more. The Night Prayer in its completely simple form is eleven units (rak'ahs), which are divided into three parts as follows: A. Four two-unit prayers, totaling eight units, called the Nafilah of the Night. B. One two-unit prayer called the Nafilah of Shaf'. C. A one-unit prayer called the Nafilah of Witr. The method of performing these prayers is exactly like the morning prayer, but they have no Adhan or Iqamah, and the longer the Qunut of the Witr is made, the better it is. [1]

2. What is the Praised Station?

"The Praised Station" (Maqam Mahmud), as is evident from its wording, has a broad meaning that includes any station worthy of praise. But certainly, here it refers to an excellent and extraordinary station that was attained for the Prophet in the shadow of nightly worship and supplication in the heart of dawn.

The well-known view among commentators—as we said previously—is that this station is the Great Intercession of the Prophet.

This interpretation has also been mentioned in several narrations: in Tafsir al-Ayyashi, we read from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام or Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that in interpreting the phrase "It may be that your Lord will raise you to a Praised Station," he said: "It is the Intercession."

Some commentators have tried to derive this reality from the concept of the verse itself: they believe that the phrase "It may be that He will raise you" is evidence that this is a station that God will give you in the future. It is a station that evokes the praise of everyone, because its benefit reaches everyone (since Mahmud in the above sentence is absolute and has no condition or restriction).

Beyond this, Hamd (praise) is in response to a voluntary act, and something that possesses all these attributes is nothing other than the general intercession of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. [1] The possibility also exists that the Praised Station is the ultimate proximity to the Lord, one of the effects of which is the Great Intercession (reflect on this).

Although the addressee in this verse is apparently the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, from one perspective its ruling can be generalized, and it can be said that all faithful individuals who carry out the divine spiritual program of recitation and Night Prayer will have a share of the Praised Station. They will find a path to the court of proximity to the Lord in proportion to their faith and action, and to that same extent, they can be intercessors and helpers for those lagging on the path.

For we know that every believer will enjoy the station of intercession within the radius of their faith, but the most complete and perfect instance of this verse is the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

3. The Three Factors of Victory

Often in the battlefields of truth and falsehood, the army of falsehood enjoys greater numbers and equipment, yet the army of truth, despite few personnel and a lack of outward means, enjoys striking victories. We see examples of this in the Islamic battles of Badr, Ahzab, and Hunayn, and the like, as well as in our own era in the victorious revolutions of oppressed nations against arrogant superpowers.

This is because the supporters of truth enjoy a special spiritual power that turns one "individual" into an "Ummah" (nation).

In the above verses, three important factors of victory were mentioned—factors from which today's Muslims have often distanced themselves, and for this reason, they witness successive defeats from arrogant enemies.

These three factors are: truthful and sincere entry into affairs, and the continuation of this program until the end of the task (رَبِّ أَدْخِلْنِي مُدْخَلَ صِدْقٍ وَ أَخْرِجْنِي مُخْرَجَ صِدْقٍ‌).

Reliance on the power of the Lord, self-confidence, and the abandonment of any reliance or dependence on others (وَ اجْعَلْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ سُلْطاناً نَصِيراً).

In this way, no policy is more effective on the path to victory than truth and sincerity, and no support is superior to independence, the rejection of dependency, and trust in Allah. How do Muslims expect to be victorious over enemies who have usurped their lands and are plundering their vital resources, while they are militarily, economically, and politically dependent on those very enemies?

Can one be victorious over the enemy with weapons purchased from the enemy? What a naive fantasy and false thought!

4. Truth is Victorious and Falsehood is Perished

In the above verses, we encounter another general and fundamental principle and an eternal divine law that is a source of encouragement for all followers of truth: that ultimately truth is victorious and falsehood is definitely bound to perish. Falsehood has a surge and a state, it flashes like lightning, it shows pomp and power, but its life is short, and eventually, it falls into the valley of non-existence.

Or as the Qur'an says, like the foam on the water, it blinks, makes a clamor, and is extinguished, while the water, which is the source of life, remains (As for the foam, it passes away as scum, but as for that which benefits mankind, it remains on the earth) (Al-Ra'd: 18). The reason for this is hidden within the essence of the word "falsehood" (batil), for it is something that is not harmonious with the laws of the universe and has no share of reality and truth.

It is fabricated, fake, rootless, and hollow; and surely something possessing these attributes cannot remain for a long time.

But truth is the very essence of reality, coupled with honesty and correctness, possessing depth and roots, and harmonious with the laws of creation—and such a thing must remain!

The followers of truth rely on the weapon of faith, logic, fulfillment of covenants, truthfulness in speech, sacrifice and selflessness, and readiness for devotion to the point of martyrdom. The light of awareness has illuminated their hearts; they fear nothing but Allah and rely on none but Him—and this is the secret of their victory!

5. The Verse "Truth has come..." and the Uprising of the Mahdi عليه السلام

In some narrations, the sentence "Truth has come and falsehood has vanished" is interpreted as the uprising of the Mahdi. Once Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام said: "The concept of this divine word is that: 'When the Qā'im (the one who rises) rises, the state of falsehood will pass away.'" [1] In another narration, we read that the Mahdi, at the time of his birth, had this sentence inscribed on his arm: "Truth has come and falsehood has vanished; indeed, falsehood is ever bound to vanish." [2]

Certainly, the concept of these Hadiths is not the restriction of the broad meaning of the verse to this instance; rather, the uprising of the Mahdi is among its clearest instances, the result of which is the final victory of truth over falsehood throughout the world.

In the accounts of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that on the day of the Conquest of Mecca, he entered the Masjid al-Haram and there were 360 idols arranged by the Arab tribes around the Kaaba. He toppled them one after another with his staff and continuously recited: "Truth has come and falsehood has vanished; indeed, falsehood is ever bound to vanish."

In short, this general divine law and the infallible law of creation has an instance in every age and time. The uprising of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his victory over the army of polytheism and idolatry, as well as the uprising of the Mahdi عليه السلام (may our souls be sacrificed for him) against the oppressors and tyrants of the world, are among the clear and radiant faces of this general law.

It is this very divine law that keeps the travelers on the path of truth hopeful, powerful, strong, and steadfast in the face of difficulties, and gives us vitality and strength in all our Islamic efforts.

Ayah

وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ ٱلْقُرْءَانِ مَا هُوَ شِفَآءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۙ وَلَا يَزِيدُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ إِلَّا خَسَارًا (٨٢)
We send down in the Quran that which is a cure and mercy for the faithful; and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss. (82)

Commentary

The Quran, a Healing Prescription

Since the preceding verses discussed monotheism, truth, and the struggle against polytheism and falsehood, the first verse under discussion addresses the extraordinary impact of the Quran and its constructive role in this regard, stating: "And We send down of the Quran that which is a healing and a mercy for the believers" (وَ نُنَزِّلُ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ ما هُوَ شِفاءٌ وَ رَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

"But it does not increase the wrongdoers (who, as always, instead of benefiting from this means of guidance) in anything except loss" (وَ لا يَزِيدُ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا خَساراً).

Key Points

1- The Meaning of the Word "Min" (from/of) in "Min al-Quran"

We know that the word "min" (مِن) in such contexts usually denotes "partitive" (tab'id), but since healing and mercy are not restricted to a specific part of the Quran—rather, they are the definitive effect of all Quranic verses—great commentators have considered "min" here to be "explicative" (bayaniyyah).

However, some have suggested the possibility that "min" here retains its partitive meaning, referring to the gradual revelation of the Quran (especially since the verb nunazzilu is in the present continuous tense). In this case, the overall meaning of the sentence would be: "We reveal the Quran, and every part of it that is revealed is, by itself, a source of healing and mercy..." (Reflect on this).

2- The Difference Between Healing (Shifa) and Mercy (Rahmah)

We know that "healing" (shifa) is typically used in contrast to diseases, defects, and shortcomings. Therefore, the first action the Quran performs within human beings is purification from various intellectual and moral diseases of the individual and society.

After that comes the stage of "mercy" (rahmah), which is the stage of adopting Divine ethics and the blossoming of human virtues in those who have come under the cultivation of the Quran.

In other words, "healing" refers to "purification" (paksazi), and "mercy" refers to "reconstruction" (nosazi). Or, in the terms of philosophers and mystics, the former refers to the station of "evacuation" (takhliyah - clearing out vices) and the latter to the station of "embellishment" (tahliyah - adorning with virtues).

3- Why Do the Wrongdoers Get the Opposite Result?

Not only in this verse but in many other verses of the Quran, we read that the enemies of truth, instead of illuminating their hearts and souls with the light of Divine verses and dispelling darkness, find their ignorance and wretchedness increased.

This is because the essence of their being has been reshaped by disbelief, injustice, and hypocrisy. Thus, wherever they see the light of truth, they rise to contend with it, and this confrontation and struggle against the truth increases their filth and strengthens the spirit of rebellion and defiance within them.

If we give a nutritious food to a striving scholar and a combatant intellectual, he derives from it sufficient strength for teaching, training, or Jihad in the way of truth. But if we give that same nutritious food to a cruel oppressor, he uses its strength for further oppression. The difference is not in the food; the difference is in the temperaments and ways of thinking!

According to a famous proverb, the verses of the Quran are like life-giving drops of rain that cause tulips to grow in gardens and weeds in salt marshes! For this very reason, to benefit from the Quran, one must first develop the readiness to receive it; as the saying goes, in addition to the "efficiency of the actor" (fa'iliyyat al-fa'il), the "receptivity of the site" (qabiliyyat al-mahall) is also a condition.

From here, the answer to the question of how the Quran—which is a source of guidance—does not guide these individuals becomes clear. For the Quran is undoubtedly a source of guidance for the strayed, but on one condition: those strayed ones who are searching for the truth. They come to the call of the Quran with this motivation and employ their intellect to understand the truth. But the stubborn bigots and sworn enemies of truth, who approach the Quran with a hundred percent negative attitude, will certainly not benefit from it; rather, their obstinacy and disbelief will increase, because repeating a wrong action deepens it within the human soul.

4- An Effective Medicine for All Social and Moral Ails

Undoubtedly, the spiritual and moral diseases of man bear a great resemblance to his physical diseases. Both are lethal, both require a physician, treatment, and abstinence, both sometimes spread to others, and both must be traced to their roots; after identifying the primary root, both must be treated.

Both sometimes reach a stage where they are incurable, but in most cases, they can be treated.

What a beautiful, meaningful, and rich comparison! Yes, the Quran is a life-giving prescription for those who wish to struggle against ignorance, pride, arrogance, envy, and hypocrisy.

The Quran is a healing prescription for removing weaknesses, meanness, and groundless fears, as well as divisions and fragmentations.

The Quran is a healing medicine for those suffering from the disease of love for the world, attachment to materialism, and unconditional surrender to lusts.

The Quran is a healing prescription for a world where the fires of wars are ignited on every side, whose back is bent under the burden of the arms race, and which pours its most important economic and human capital at the feet of the monster of war and weaponry.

And finally, the Quran is a healing prescription for those whom the dark veils of desires have prevented from reaching proximity to the Lord.

In Surah Yunus, verse 57, we read: "There has come to you an admonition from your Lord and a healing for what is in the hearts" (قَدْ جاءَتْكُمْ مَوْعِظَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ وَ شِفاءٌ لِما فِي الصُّدُورِ).

In Surah Fussilat, verse 44, we also read: "Say: It is for those who believe a guidance and a healing" (قُلْ هُوَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا هُدىً وَ شِفاءٌ).

Imam Ali عليه السلام, in his very comprehensive discourse in Nahj al-Balagha, expressed this reality with the most eloquent phrases:

"Seek healing from it for your ailments and seek its assistance in your hardships, for indeed in it is healing for the greatest disease, which is disbelief, hypocrisy, rebellion, and misguidance" [1].

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 176.

And in another statement from the same Imam, we read:

"Behold, in it is the knowledge of what is to come, the account of the past, the medicine for your disease, and the order of what is between you" [1]. And elsewhere from the same great Imam, we read:

"And you must adhere to the Book of Allah, for it is the strong rope, the manifest light, the beneficial healing, the thirst-quenching drink, a protection for the one who holds fast to it, and salvation for the one who clings to it. It does not crook so as to need straightening, and it does not deviate so as to be blamed. Its frequent repetition and its reaching the ears do not wear it out (and however much it is read, it becomes sweeter and more pleasant). Whoever speaks by it speaks the truth, and whoever acts upon it takes the lead" [2]. These expressive and eloquent descriptions—the likes of which are not few in the words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and other sayings of Ali عليه السلام and the Imams of Guidance عليهم السلام—clearly prove that the Quran is a prescription for organizing all disorders and for the recovery of the individual and society from all types of moral and social diseases.

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 158. [2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 156.

The best evidence to prove this reality is a comparison between the state of the pre-Islamic Arabs and those trained in the school of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله at the dawn of Islam. We saw how that bloodthirsty, ignorant, and unlearned people, whose entire existence was permeated by various social and moral diseases, were not only treated by using this healing prescription but became so strong and powerful that they brought the tyrannical superpowers of the world to their knees.

And this is exactly the truth that today's Muslims have forgotten, and they have fallen into this state and condition that we and you know.

Discord clamors among them, plunderers have dominated their resources, their destiny is determined by others, and various dependencies have dragged them into weakness, meanness, and humiliation.

And this is the end result for those who have the healing prescription in their homes yet stretch out their hands for the healing of their pains toward those who are more diseased than themselves! The Quran not only heals but, after recovery—that is, during the period of convalescence—it strengthens the patients with its various messages, because after "healing" comes "mercy."

Interestingly, medicines for physical pains often leave undesirable effects on the body's organs, to the extent that it is stated in a famous Hadith: "There is no medicine except that it itself is a source of another disease" (ما من دواء الا و يهيج داء) [1]. But this healing medicine has no undesirable effect whatsoever on the soul, thought, and spirit of man; on the contrary, it is entirely goodness and blessing.

In one of the phrases of Nahj al-Balagha, we read:

"A healing from which no ailments are feared" [2]. It is enough to commit ourselves for one month to following this healing prescription, accepting its commands in the fields of knowledge, awareness, justice, equity, piety, godliness, unity, sincerity, self-sacrifice, Jihad, and so on; we will see how quickly our disorders become ordered.

It is also necessary to mention this point: this prescription, like any other, is effective only when it is acted upon. Otherwise, if we read the best healing prescriptions a hundred times and place them on our heads but do not act upon them, we will achieve no result.

[1]: Safinat al-Bihar. [2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 198.

Ayahs

وَإِذَآ أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَى ٱلْإِنسَـٰنِ أَعْرَضَ وَنَـَٔا بِجَانِبِهِۦ ۖ وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ كَانَ يَـُٔوسًا (٨٣)
When We bless man, he is disregardful and turns aside; but when an ill befalls him, he is despondent. (83)
قُلْ كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلَىٰ شَاكِلَتِهِۦ فَرَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ هُوَ أَهْدَىٰ سَبِيلًا (٨٤)
Say, ‘Everyone acts according to his character. Your Lord knows best who is better guided with regard to the way.’ (84)

Commentary

Everyone Acts According to Their Own Disposition

Next, it points to one of the most deep-rooted moral ailments of "unrefined human beings," stating: "And when We bestow favor upon man, he turns away (out of pride and arrogance) and distances himself with haughtiness" (وَ إِذا أَنْعَمْنا عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ أَعْرَضَ وَ نَأى‌ بِجانِبِهِ‌).

"But when evil touches him—even a slight distress—he is filled with despair and hopelessness" (وَ إِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ كانَ يَؤُساً). The word "A'rad" (أعرض) is derived from "I'rad," meaning to turn away, and here it refers to turning away from Allah and the Truth.

"Na'a" (نأى) is derived from "Na'y" (rhyming with Ra'y), meaning to become distant. When the phrase "bi-janibihi" (with his side) is added, it conveys the meaning of arrogance, pride, and taking a hostile stance.

From the entirety of this sentence, it is understood that people with no faith or weak faith become so arrogant when blessings come their way that they completely forget the Bestower of blessings. Not only do they forget Him, but they also adopt an attitude of indifference, opposition, and haughtiness toward Him.

The phrase "massahu al-sharr" (evil touches him) refers to the slightest discomfort that reaches a person. This means they have such little capacity (low tolerance) that with a minor hardship, they lose their composure, their train of thought completely unravels, and the darkness of despair and hopelessness casts a shadow over their hearts.

The second verse addresses the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "Say: Everyone acts according to his own manner and disposition" (قُلْ كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلى‌ شاكِلَتِهِ‌).

The believers who seek healing and gain mercy from the verses of the Quran, the wrongdoers who gain nothing from it but loss and ruin, and the low-capacity individuals who are arrogant in times of blessing and despairing and abject in times of trouble—all of these act according to their inner dispositions; dispositions that have been shaped by education, upbringing, and the repeated actions of the person himself.

And in the midst of this, Allah is the Witness and Observer of everyone's state: "Indeed, your Lord is most knowing of who is best guided in way" (فَرَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ هُوَ أَهْدى‌ سَبِيلًا).

Key Points

1- Arrogance and Despair: Two Dangerous Moral Diseases

We have often heard it said of others, or said it ourselves, that such-and-all a person "no longer recognizes God as his Master" because he has attained some wealth or status.

We have also frequently seen that these same "newly-enriched" people who forgot Allah, when they fall from that state or are caught in hardships, become so wretched, abject, panicked, and hopeless that one cannot believe these are the same people as before! Indeed, such is the state of all narrow-minded, faithless, and low-capacity individuals. In contrast, the friends of Allah have souls like the ocean; the fiercest storms do not affect them. They stand like mountains against harsh events and like a straw before the command of Allah. If you give them the world, they do not lose their composure, and if you take the world from them, they do not even frown!

The strange thing is that these self-lost, low-tolerance humans—whose states are mentioned in many Surahs of the Quran (Yunus: 12, Luqman: 32, Al-Fajr: 14-15, Fussilat: 48-49)—become God-worshippers in times of hardship and return to their divine primordial nature (Fitrah) and their true selves. However, as soon as the storm of the incident subsides, they change direction so completely as if they had never heard the name of Allah.

This is a great calamity, for it causes them to never be able to maintain an independent and correct stance in life. The only way to treat this dangerous disease is to raise the level of thought in the light of knowledge and faith, to break the bonds of dependency and captivity in the clutches of materialism, and to accept asceticism (Zuhd) and piety in its constructive sense.

Furthermore, the answer to a question regarding these verses becomes clear from the above explanation. Some might ask: The verses under discussion describe such individuals as "Ya'us" (despairing) during hardships, while other verses (such as Surah Al-Ankabut, verse 65) describe them as "sincere to Him in religion" (مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ‌), which indicates ultimate attention to Allah in such a state.

However, these two states are not contradictory; rather, one is a prelude to the other. When faced with problems, such individuals completely despair of their [material] life, and this state of despair causes the veils to be lifted from their Fitrah, leading them to turn to the threshold of Allah. But this forced attention is neither a credit to them nor evidence of their awakening, for as soon as the problems are removed, they return to the same previous state that has become their second nature.

But the guardians of Truth and the true servants of Allah not only do not despair upon seeing the face of hardship, but these events increase their level of steadfastness. Due to their reliance on Allah and self-confidence, they adopt a more proactive stance toward problems because despair has no path into their being. They have not recognized Allah only in times of trouble; they are alive with His remembrance at all times, rely on His Pure Essence, and the light of His mercy always shines in their hearts.

2- What is "Shakilah"?!

"Shakilah" (شاكلة) is originally derived from the root "Shakl," meaning to tether or restrain an animal, and "Shikal" refers to the tether itself. Since the inner dispositions, traits, and habits of every human being restrict them to a certain course of action, it is called "Shakilah." The word "Ishkal" is used for questions, needs, and all issues that in some way restrict a person [^1]. Thus, the concept of "Shakilah" is not at all exclusive to a person's innate nature. Therefore, the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan mentioned two meanings for it: nature and creation, as well as method, religion, and tradition (since each of these matters restricts a person in terms of action in some way).

From this, it becomes clear how mistaken are those who take the above verse as evidence for the dominance of innate traits over man and perceive it as a proof for determinism (Jabr), going so far as to disbelieve in education and self-purification.

This way of thinking—which, due to various political, social, and psychological reasons discussed in the debates on determinism and free will, dominates the literature of many nations and is used to justify their shortcomings—is one of the most dangerous beliefs that can drag a society into humiliation and abjectness, keeping it in a state of backwardness for years or centuries.

Consider carefully the following poem, which expresses this thought regarding education: A tree that is bitter in its very nature, Even if you plant it in the Garden of Paradise, And at the time of watering from the stream of Eternity, You pour pure honey and nectar at its roots, In the end, it will manifest its essence, And bear that same bitter fruit!

If this logic were truly the foundation of educational and social issues, the futility of all education and training would be inevitable.

For this reason, we believe that the school of determinism has always been a tool for colonial powers to remain safe from the intense reactions of the people they have enslaved.

The famous saying: "Determinism (Jabr) and anthropomorphism (Tashbih) are Umayyad, while Justice ('Adl) and Monotheism (Tawhid) are Alawid" expresses this reality.

In short, "Shakilah" never means innate nature; rather, any habit, path, religion, or method that gives direction to a person is called "Shakilah." Therefore, the habits and traditions that a person has acquired through the repetition of a voluntary act, as well as beliefs accepted through reasoning or prejudice—all of these play a decisive role and are considered "Shakilah."

Fundamentally, human traits and dispositions are usually voluntary in nature, because when a person repeats an action, it first becomes a "state," then a "habit," and then gradually transforms into a "trait" (Malakah). It is these traits that give shape to human actions and define one's path in life, while their origin is rooted in voluntary factors.

In some narrations, "Shakilah" is interpreted as "intention" (Niyyah). In Usul al-Kafi, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"Intention is superior to action; indeed, intention is the action itself." Then he recited the words of Allah, the Almighty: "Say: Everyone acts according to his Shakilah," meaning according to his intention [^1]. This interpretation contains an interesting point: a person's intention, which arises from his beliefs, gives shape to his action. Fundamentally, the intention itself is a type of "Shakilah," meaning a restricting factor. Thus, sometimes intention is interpreted as the action itself, and sometimes it is recognized as superior to action, because in any case, the "line of action" branches out from the "line of intention."

[^1]: Mufradat al-Raghib, root "Shakl". [^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 114.

In another narration, we read that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام was asked if it is permissible to pray in Jewish temples and Christian churches. He replied: "Pray in them." Someone asked: "Should we pray in them even if they are also praying there?"

He replied: "Yes, do you not read the Quran where it says: 'Say: Everyone acts according to his own manner (Shakilah), and your Lord is most knowing of who is best guided in way'? Then he said: 'Pray toward your Qiblah and leave them be'" [^1].

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 114.

Ayah

وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلرُّوحِ ۖ قُلِ ٱلرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّى وَمَآ أُوتِيتُم مِّنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا (٨٥)
They question you concerning the Spirit. Say, ‘The Spirit is of the command of my Lord, and you have not been given of the knowledge except a few [of you].’ (85)

Commentary

What is the Spirit?

Following the previous verses, the Quran addresses some of the important questions posed by the polytheists or the People of the Book, stating: "And they ask you about the spirit. Say, 'The spirit is of the command of my Lord, and you have not been given of knowledge except a little'" (وَ يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي وَ ما أُوتِيتُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا).

Great commentators, past and present, have spoken extensively regarding the meaning of "spirit" (ruh) and the interpretation of this verse. We shall first address the linguistic meaning of "spirit," then its usage in the Quran, and finally the commentary on the verse and the narrations transmitted in this regard:

1- Linguistically, "spirit" (ruh) originally means "breath" and "movement." Some have explicitly stated that ruh (spirit) and rih (wind) are both derived from the same root. If the human spirit—which is an independent, incorporeal essence—is called by this name, it is because it resembles breath and wind in terms of its mobility, life-giving nature, and invisibility. This is the linguistic perspective.

2- Its usage in the Quran is highly diverse:

Sometimes it refers to the "Holy Spirit" that strengthened the prophets in fulfilling their missions, such as in verse 253 of Surah Al-Baqarah: وَ آتَيْنا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ الْبَيِّناتِ وَ أَيَّدْناهُ بِرُوحِ الْقُدُسِ: "And We gave Jesus, the son of Mary, clear proofs and supported him with the Holy Spirit."

Sometimes it refers to the divine spiritual power that strengthens believers, as in verse 22 of Surah Al-Mujadila: أُولئِكَ كَتَبَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْإِيمانَ وَ أَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ: "Those—He has decreed within their hearts faith and supported them with a spirit from Him."

At times, it refers to the "special Angel of Revelation" and is described with the title "the Trustworthy," as in verse 193 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara: نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ عَلى‌ قَلْبِكَ لِتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُنْذِرِينَ: "The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down upon your heart, [O Muhammad], that you may be of the warners."

And sometimes it refers to a great angel among God's special angels or a creature superior to the angels, as in: تَنَزَّلُ الْمَلائِكَةُ وَ الرُّوحُ فِيها بِإِذْنِ رَبِّمْ مِنْ كُلِّ أَمْرٍ: "The angels and the Spirit descend therein by permission of their Lord for every matter" (Surah Al-Qadr, verse 4). We also read in Surah An-Naba (verse 38): يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الرُّوحُ وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ صَفًّا: "The Day that the Spirit and the angels will stand in rows." Occasionally, it refers to the Quran or heavenly revelation, as in: وَ كَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ رُوحاً مِنْ أَمْرِنا: "And thus We have revealed to you an inspiration [spirit] of Our command" (Ash-Shura, 52).

Finally, it sometimes refers to the human spirit, as we read in the verses regarding the creation of Adam: ثُمَّ سَوَّاهُ وَ نَفَخَ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِهِ: "Then He proportioned him and breathed into him from His [created] spirit" (Surah As-Sajdah, verse 9).

And also: فَإِذا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَ نَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي فَقَعُوا لَهُ ساجِدِينَ: "So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My [created] spirit, then fall down to him in prostration" [1] (Surah Al-Hijr, verse 29).

3- Now the question is: what is meant by "spirit" in the verse under discussion?

Which spirit is this that a group of inquisitive people asked about, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied to them: "The spirit is of the command of my Lord, and you have not been given of knowledge except a little"?! From the sum of the clues present within the verse and outside of it, it is inferred that the questioners were asking about the reality of the human spirit—this great spirit that distinguishes us from animals and is our highest honor, from which all our power and activity originate, and with whose help we make the earth and sky our playground, unravel the secrets of science, and find a path to the depths of existence. They wanted to know: what is the reality of this wonder of the world of creation?

Since the spirit has a structure different from that of matter, and the principles governing it are other than the principles governing matter and its physical and chemical properties, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was commanded to say in one short and meaningful sentence: "The spirit is from the realm of Command (Amr), meaning it has a mysterious creation."

Then, so they would not be surprised by this answer, he adds: your share of science and knowledge is very small and insignificant; therefore, why be surprised that you do not know the secrets of the spirit, even though it is closer to you than anything else?

[1]: We have previously stated that the attribution of the spirit to Allah here is an "honorific attribution" (idafah tashrifi), and it refers to a great and holy divine spirit that Allah has bestowed upon human beings.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the verse يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ that they said: انما الروح خلق من خلقه، له بصر و قوة و تاييد، يجعله فى قلوب الرسل و المؤمنين: "The spirit is but a creature among His creatures; it has sight, power, and support. Allah places it in the hearts of the Messengers and the believers." [1]

In another hadith from one of those two great Imams, it is narrated: هى من الملكوت من القدرة: "It is from the Malakut (Celestial Realm) and from the Power of Allah." [2] In several narrations found in both Shi'a and Sunni books, we read that the polytheists of Quraysh took this question from the scholars of the People of the Book to test the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. They had been told that if Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله provided extensive information about the spirit, it would be proof of his lack of sincerity. Thus, the Prophet's short and meaningful sentence was astonishing to them.

However, in another part of the narrations reached us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the above verse, we see the "Spirit" introduced as a creature superior to Gabriel and Michael, who was always with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams, keeping them from any deviation in their path. [3] These narrations not only do not contradict what we said in the commentary of the verse but are in harmony with it, because the human spirit has levels and degrees. That level of the spirit present in the prophets and Imams is an extraordinarily high level, the effects of which include being infallible from error and sin, as well as possessing extraordinary awareness and knowledge. Certainly, such a level of the spirit would be superior to all angels, even Gabriel and Michael! (Reflect on this).

[1] and [2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 216. [3]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 215.

The Primacy and Independence of the Spirit

As far as the history of human science and knowledge shows, the issue of the spirit, its structure, and its mysterious characteristics has always been of interest to scholars, and every scholar has strived in his own way to step into the mysterious environment of the spirit.

Precisely for this reason, the opinions expressed about the spirit by scholars and scientists are very numerous and diverse.

It is possible that our current science—and even the science of future generations—may not be sufficient to discover all the secrets of the spirit. Although our spirit is closer to us than anything else in this world, because its essence differs fundamentally from what we are accustomed to in the material world, we should not be too surprised if we do not grasp the secrets and the core of this wonder of creation and supra-material creature.

However, in any case, this will not prevent us from seeing the vista of the spirit with the sharp eye of reason and becoming aware of the general principles and systems governing it.

The most important principle that must be recognized here is the issue of the primacy and independence of the spirit, in contrast to materialist schools that consider the spirit to be material and a property of brain matter and nerve cells, and nothing beyond that!

We will focus mostly on this discussion here, because the discussion of the "survival of the spirit" and the "issue of complete incorporeality or barzakhi (intermediate) incorporeality" depends on it.

But before entering this discussion, we find it necessary to mention this point: the connection of the spirit to the human body—as some have imagined—is not a connection of the type of "indwelling" (hulul), like wind entering a leather bag, for example. Rather, it is a type of connection and bond based on the spirit's sovereignty over the body and its management and governance, which some have likened to the connection of "meaning" to a "word."

Of course, this issue will become clear within the discussion of the independence of the spirit.

Now let us return to the main point.

There is no doubt that a human differs from a soulless stone or piece of wood, for we feel clearly that we are different from inanimate objects and even from plants. We understand, we imagine, we decide, we have will, we love, we hate, and so on.

But plants and stones have none of these feelings. Therefore, there is a fundamental difference between us and them, and that is the possession of a human spirit.

Neither materialists nor any other group ever denies the basic existence of the "spirit" and "psyche," and for this reason, they all recognize psychology and psychoanalysis as positive sciences. Although these two sciences are, in some ways, still in their infancy, they are nonetheless among the sciences pursued in the world's great universities by professors and researchers. As we shall see, the "psyche" and the "spirit" are not two separate realities but different stages of one reality.

Where the discussion is about the connection of the spirit with the body and the mutual influence of these two on each other, we call it the "psyche" (ravan), and where spiritual phenomena are discussed separately from the body, we use the name "spirit" (ruh).

In short, no one denies that a reality called the spirit and psyche exists within us.

Now we must see where the extensive battle lies between the "materialists" on one side and the "metaphysical philosophers and spiritualists" on the other.

The answer is: Divine scholars and spiritualist philosophers believe that besides the materials that make up the human body, another reality and essence is hidden within him that is not of the nature of matter, yet the human body is under its direct influence.

In other words: the spirit is a supernatural reality whose structure and activity are different from the structure and activity of the material world. It is true that it is constantly in contact with the material world, but it is not matter or a property of matter! On the opposite side stand the materialist philosophers: they say: we do not know of any being independent of matter called the spirit or by any other name; whatever exists is this physical matter or its physical and chemical effects.

We have a system called the "brain and nerves" that performs a major part of our vital actions, and like other systems of the body, they are material and operate under the laws of matter.

We have glands under the tongue called "salivary glands" that have both physical and chemical activities. When food enters the mouth, these "artesian wells" start working automatically and are so calculating that they spray exactly as much water as is needed to chew and soften the food. Juicy, low-water, or dry foods each receive a quota of saliva according to their need.

Acidic substances, especially when highly concentrated, increase the activity of these glands so they receive a larger share of water to be sufficiently diluted and not harm the stomach walls! And when we swallow the food, the activity of these wells shuts off. In short, a wonderful system governs these bubbling springs; if their balance and calculation were disrupted for one hour, either water would constantly flow from our lips, or our tongue and throat would become so dry that the morsel would get stuck in our throat!

This is the "physical" work of saliva, but we know the more important work of saliva is its chemical work; various substances are mixed with it that combine with food and reduce the stomach's burden.

Materialists say: our nervous system and brain, like the salivary glands and the like, have physical and chemical activities (collectively called "physicochemical"), and it is these "physicochemical" activities that we call "spiritual phenomena" or the "spirit."

They say: when we are busy thinking, a series of specific electrical waves arise from our brain. Today, these waves are captured with devices and recorded on paper; especially in psychiatric hospitals, by studying these waves, they find ways to diagnose and treat mental patients. This is the physical activity of our brain.

In addition, brain cells undergo a series of chemical reactions during thinking or other psychological activities.

Therefore, the spirit and spiritual phenomena are nothing but the physical properties and chemical reactions of our brain and nerve cells.

From this discussion, they conclude:

1- Just as the activity of the salivary glands and its various effects did not exist before the body and will not exist after it, our spiritual activities also come into existence with the emergence of the brain and nervous system and die with its death! 2- The spirit is a property of the body, so it is material and has no supernatural aspect. 3- The spirit is subject to all the laws that govern the body. 4- The spirit has no independent existence without the body and cannot have one.

The Materialists' Arguments Against the Independence of the Spirit
