Materialists have brought forth evidence to prove their claim that the spirit, thought, and other spiritual phenomena are all material—meaning they are physical and chemical properties of brain and nerve cells—which are mentioned below:

1- "It can easily be shown that with the failure of a part of the centers or the nervous system, a set of spiritual effects is suspended." [1] For example, it has been tested that if specific parts of a pigeon's brain are removed, it does not die, but it loses much of its knowledge. If they give it food, it eats and digests, but if they do not and only pour grain in front of it, it does not eat and dies of hunger! Similarly, in some brain injuries that occur to humans, or due to certain diseases where parts of the brain fail, it has been seen that the person loses part of his knowledge.

A while ago, we read in the newspapers that a young educated man, due to a brain injury in an accident near Ahvaz, forgot all the past events of his life and did not even recognize his mother and sister! When they took him to the house where he was born and raised, it was completely unfamiliar to him! These and similar cases show that a close relationship exists between "brain cell activity" and "spiritual phenomena."

[1]: Psychology, Dr. Arani, p. 23.

2- "During thinking, material changes on the surface of the brain increase; the brain takes in more food and gives off more phosphorus materials. During sleep, when the brain does not perform the work of thinking, it takes in less food. This itself is proof of the material nature of thought effects." [1]

3- Observations show that the brain weight of thinkers is generally above average (the average weight of male brains is about 1400 grams, and the average for women is slightly less). This is another sign of the material nature of the spirit.

4- If the power of thought and spiritual manifestations were proof of the existence of an independent spirit, we would have to accept this for animals as well, for they too have perceptions within their own limits. In short, they say: we feel that our spirit is not an independent entity, and the known progress in anthropology also confirms this reality.

From the sum of these arguments, they conclude that the progress and development of human and animal physiology day by day makes it clearer that a close relationship exists between spiritual phenomena and brain cells.

The Dark Points of This Argumentation

The great mistake that has plagued materialists in these types of arguments is that they have confused the "tool of the work" with the "agent of the work."

To understand how they have confused the tool with the doer, let us provide an example (pay attention).

From the time of Galileo onwards, a transformation occurred in the study of the heavens. Galileo, the Italian, succeeded in making a small telescope with the help of an eyeglass maker. Galileo was very happy, and at night, when he used it to study the stars, an amazing scene appeared before his eyes that no other human had seen until that day. He realized he had made an important discovery, and from that day on, the study of the secrets of the world above fell into human hands! Until then, man was like a butterfly that only saw a few branches around itself, but when he put the telescope to his eye, he observed a significant amount of the trees around him in this great forest of creation.

This matter continued its evolution until large astronomical telescopes were built with lens diameters of five meters or more. They were installed atop high mountains in regions suitable for clear air. These telescopes, whose entire apparatus was sometimes the size of a multi-story building, showed man realms of the world above that the naked eye had not seen even one-thousandth of.

Now think: if one day human technology allows the construction of telescopes with a diameter of one hundred meters and equipment the size of a city, what realms will be discovered for us?! Now this question arises: if you take these telescopes away from us, certainly a part or parts of our knowledge and observations regarding the heavens will be suspended. But is the primary observer "us" or is it the telescope?! Is the telescope an instrument of our work through which we see, or is it the agent of the work and the real observer?!

In the case of the brain as well, no one denies that without brain cells, performing thought and the like is impossible. But is the brain the tool of the spirit's work, or is it the spirit itself?!

In short: all the arguments the materialists have brought here only prove that there is a connection between brain cells and our perceptions, but none of them prove that the brain is the performer of perceptions rather than the tool of perception (pay attention).

From here it becomes clear that if the dead do not understand anything, it is because the connection of their spirit with the body has been severed, not because the spirit has perished—just like a ship or an airplane whose radio equipment has all failed. The ship, the guides, and the captains exist, but those on the shore cannot establish a connection with them because the means of communication has been destroyed.

Arguments for the Independence of the Spirit

The discussion was about the issue of the spirit and how materialists insist on considering spiritual phenomena as properties of brain cells, placing "thought," "memory," "innovation," "love," "hatred," "anger," and "sciences and knowledge" all in the category of laboratory issues subject to the laws of the material world. However, philosophers who support the independence of the spirit have eloquent arguments for rejecting and discarding this belief, some of which are mentioned below:

1- The Property of Representing Reality (Awareness of the External World)

The first question that can be asked of materialists is: if thoughts and spiritual phenomena are the same as the "physicochemical" properties of the brain, there should be no "fundamental difference" between the work of the brain and the work of the stomach, kidney, or liver, for example. For the work of the stomach is a combination of physical and chemical activities, and with its specific movements and secretion of acids, it digests food and prepares it for absorption by the body. Similarly, the work of saliva, as mentioned, is a combination of physical and chemical work. Yet we see that spiritual work is different from all of them.

The actions of all body systems more or less resemble one another except for the "brain," whose situation is exceptional. They are all related to internal aspects, whereas spiritual phenomena have an external aspect and make us aware of the state outside of our existence.

To explain this, several points must be noted:

First: Is there a world outside of our existence or not? Certainly, such a world exists. Idealists who deny the existence of the external world and say "whatever exists is us and our perceptions," and that the external world is just like the scenes we see in a dream, are gravely mistaken. We have proven their mistake in its proper place, showing how idealists become realists in practice and forget everything they thought in their library when they step into the street and the environment of ordinary life.

Second: Are we aware of the existence of the external world or not? Certainly, the answer to this question is also positive, for we have much awareness of the world outside ourselves, and we have abundant information about the beings around us or in distant places.

Now this question arises: does the external world enter into our existence? Certainly not; rather, its map is with us, and by using the property of "representing reality," we discover the world outside our existence.

This representation of reality cannot be merely the physicochemical properties of the brain, because these properties are born from our impressions of the external world—they are, so to speak, its effects, just like the effects food has on our stomach. Does the effect of food on the stomach and its physicochemical reaction cause the stomach to have awareness of the food? Then how can our brain be aware of the world outside itself?!

In other words: to be aware of external and objective beings, a type of encompassment over them is necessary, and this encompassment is not the work of brain cells. Brain cells are only affected by the outside, and this impression is like the impression of other body systems from the external state. We understand this subject well.

If being affected by the outside were proof of our awareness of the outside, it would be necessary for us to understand with our stomach and tongue as well, whereas this is not the case. In short, the exceptional state of our perceptions is proof that another reality is hidden within them, whose system is completely different from the system of physical and chemical laws (pay attention).

2- Unity of Personality

Another argument that can be mentioned for the independence of the spirit is the issue of the unity of personality throughout a person's life.

To explain: whatever we may doubt, we do not doubt this subject: that "we exist."

"I am," and I do not doubt my own existence. My knowledge of my own existence is what is called "knowledge by presence" (ilm huduri), not "acquired knowledge" (ilm husuli); that is, I am present to myself and not separate from myself.

In any case, our awareness of ourselves is the clearest of our knowledge and does not need argumentation at all. The famous argument that the renowned French philosopher Descartes made for his existence—"I think, therefore I am"—seems to be a redundant and incorrect argument, because before proving his existence, he confessed to his existence twice! (Once when he says "I" and again when he says "think"). This is on one hand.

On the other hand, this "I" is no more than a single unit from the beginning to the end of life. "The I of today" is the same as "the I of yesterday" and the same as "the I of twenty years ago." I have been no more than one person from childhood until now. I am the same person I was and will be this same person until the end of life, not another person. Of course, I have studied, become literate, evolved, and will continue to do so, but I have not become another person. For this reason, all people recognize me as one person from the beginning to the end of life; I have one name, one identity card, and so on.

Now let us calculate and see what this single entity that covers our entire life is. Is it the particles and cells of our body, or the collection of brain cells and their reactions? These are changed many times during our life, and approximately every seven years, all cells are replaced. For we know that every twenty-four hours, millions of cells in our body die and millions of new cells take their place, just like a building whose bricks are gradually removed and new bricks are put in their place; this building is completely changed after a while, even if superficial people do not notice. Or like a large pool where water slowly enters from one side and exits from the other; obviously, after a while, all the water in the pool is changed, even if short-sighted individuals do not pay attention and see it as the same stable state.

Generally, any being that receives food and, on the other hand, consumes food will gradually be "renewed" and "replaced."

Therefore, a seventy-year-old person has likely had all the components of his body changed ten times. On this account, if we—like the materialists—consider the human to be only the body and the brain and nervous systems and their physicochemical properties, this "I" must have changed ten times in 70 years and not be the same former person, whereas no conscience will accept this statement.

From here it becomes clear that besides the material components, there is a single, stable reality throughout life that is not replaced like the material components, and it constitutes the foundation of existence, and it is the factor of our unity of personality.

Avoiding a Mistake: Some imagine that brain cells do not change and say: we have read in physiology books that the number of brain cells is the same from the beginning to the end of life—meaning they never increase or decrease, but only grow larger and do not reproduce. For this reason, if an injury occurs to them, they are not repairable. Therefore, we have one stable unit in the whole body, which is the brain cells, and this preserves our unity of personality.

But this is a great mistake, because those who say this have confused two issues. What has been proven in modern science is that brain cells are stable in terms of number from the beginning to the end of life and do not increase or decrease.

It is not that the particles making up these cells are not replaced. For as we said, the body's cells constantly receive food and also gradually lose old particles. They are exactly like someone who constantly has income on one side and expenditure on the other; certainly, the capital of such a person will gradually change even if its amount does not change—just like that pool of water where water flows in from one side and out from the other; after a while, its contents are completely replaced, even if the amount of water remained stable.

(This issue is also mentioned in physiology books; for example, refer to the book Hormones, p. 11, and the book Animal Physiology by Dr. Mahmoud Behzad and colleagues, p. 32). Therefore, brain cells are also not stable and change just like other cells.

3- The Non-Correspondence of the Large to the Small

Suppose we are sitting by a beautiful sea; a few small boats and a massive ship are moving on the waves. We see the sun setting on one side and the moon rising on the other. Beautiful seagulls constantly land on the water and take flight. In one direction, a massive mountain rises toward the sky.

Now, for a few moments, we close our eyes and visualize what we have seen in our minds: the mountain with the same grandeur, the sea with the same vastness, and the massive ship with the same greatness it possesses are visualized in our minds—meaning they exist like an extraordinarily large painting before our spirit or within our spirit.

Now, this question arises: where is the place of this large map? Can extraordinarily small brain cells accept such a massive map within themselves?

Certainly not. Therefore, we must possess another part of existence that is above this physical matter and is so vast that it accommodates all these maps within itself.

Can the map of a 500-meter building be implemented on a few-millimeter piece of land?

Certainly, the answer to this question is negative, because a larger entity, while maintaining its greatness, does not correspond to a small entity. The requirement for correspondence is that it be either equal to it or smaller than it so that it can be laid upon it.

Nevertheless, how can we place extraordinarily large mental maps in our small brain cells?

We can depict the Earth with its forty-million-meter belt in our minds; we can visualize the Sun, which is one million two hundred thousand times larger than the Earth, and also galaxies that are millions of times vaster than our Sun—all in our thought. If these maps were to be implemented in our small brain cells, it would be impossible according to the law of the non-correspondence of the large to the small. Thus, we must confess to an existence above this body that is the center for receiving these large maps.

A Necessary Question and Answer: Our mental maps are like microfilms and geographical maps

It might be said that our mental maps are like "microfilms" or "geographical maps" next to which a fractional number is written, such as 1/1,000,000 or 1/100,000,000, which shows the scale of its reduction and makes us understand that we must enlarge this map by that same ratio to obtain the real map. Also, we have often seen a photo taken of a giant ship that cannot show the grandeur of that ship by itself; therefore, before taking the photo, to show its grandeur, they place a human on the deck of the ship and take a photo of both together so that by comparison, the grandeur of the ship becomes clear.

Our mental maps are also very small images that have been reduced by certain scales. When we enlarge them by that same ratio, the real map is obtained, and certainly, these small maps can be placed in some way in our brain cells (pay attention).

Answer:

The important issue here is that microfilms are usually either enlarged by projectors and reflected on a screen, or in geographical maps, the number written under them helps us multiply the map by that number and reflect the real large map in our minds. Now, this question arises: where is that large screen on which our mental microfilms are reflected in a massive form?

Is this large screen the brain cells? Certainly not. And that small geographical map that we multiply by a large number and transform into a massive map certainly requires a place; can it be the small brain cells?

In clearer terms: in the example of the microfilm and the geographical map, what exists externally are the small films and maps. But in our mental maps, these maps are exactly the size of their external existence and certainly require a place as large as themselves, and we know that brain cells are too small to reflect them with that grandeur.

In short: we imagine these mental maps with the same greatness they have externally, and this massive imagination cannot be reflected in a small cell. Therefore, it needs a place, and from here we discover the true reality above these cells.

4- Spiritual Phenomena are Not Identical to Material Qualities

Another argument that can lead us to the independence of the spirit and its non-material nature is that in spiritual phenomena, we see properties and qualities that have no similarity to the properties and qualities of material beings, because:

First: Material beings require "time" and have a gradual nature. Second: They wear out with the passage of time. Third: They are divisible into multiple parts.

But mental phenomena do not possess these properties and effects. We can depict a world like the current world in our minds without needing the passage of time or gradual aspects.

Furthermore, scenes that, for example, were imprinted in our minds from childhood do not become old or worn out with the passage of time; they have maintained their form. A person's brain may wear out, but with the wearing out of the brain, the map of a house that was recorded in our mind twenty years ago does not wear out; it enjoys a type of stability that is a property of the world beyond matter.

Our spirit has a wonderful creativity regarding patterns and images, and in an instant, we can depict any kind of pattern in the mind without any preamble: celestial bodies, galaxies, or earthly beings, seas, mountains, and the like. This is not a property of a material being, but a sign of a supra-material being.

Additionally, we know, for example, that 2 + 2 = 4. There is no doubt that we can divide the sides of this equation—meaning we can divide the number two or the number four—but we can never divide this "equality" and say "equality has two halves, and each half is different from the other." Equality is an indivisible concept; it either exists or it does not; it can never be halved.

Therefore, these types of mental concepts are not divisible, and for this reason, they cannot be material, because if they were material, they would be divisible. For the same reason, our spirit, which is the center of such non-material concepts, cannot be material; therefore, it is above matter (pay attention). [1]

[1]: Summarized from the book Resurrection and the World After Death—Section on the Independence of the Spirit.

Ayahs

وَلَئِن شِئْنَا لَنَذْهَبَنَّ بِٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُ لَكَ بِهِۦ عَلَيْنَا وَكِيلًا (٨٦)
If We wish, We would take away what We have revealed to you. Then you would not find for yourself any defender against Us, (86)
إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۚ إِنَّ فَضْلَهُۥ كَانَ عَلَيْكَ كَبِيرًا (٨٧)
except a mercy from your Lord. Indeed His grace has been great upon you. (87)

Commentary

Whatever You Possess is from the Blessing of His Mercy!

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the Quran; the two verses under discussion also speak on the same subject.

First, it states: "And if We willed, We could surely take away that which We have revealed to you" (وَ لَئِنْ شِئْنا لَنَذْهَبَنَّ بِالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ).

"Then you would not find for yourself therein any advocate against Us" to reclaim it (ثُمَّ لا تَجِدُ لَكَ بِهِ عَلَيْنا وَكِيلًا). Thus, it is We who have bestowed these sciences upon you so that you may be a leader and guide for the people, and it is We who, whenever We deem it wise, can take them back from you, and no one has any authority or influence in this regard!

Regarding the connection of these verses to the previous verse, in addition to what has been mentioned, there is also the possibility that in the final sentence of the previous discussion we read: "And you have been given of knowledge but a little."

And in the verses under discussion, we read that Allah can even take back the portion of knowledge and insight He has granted to His Prophet; therefore, everything you possess, even your knowledge and awareness, is from Him.

The next verse, which appears as an exception, says: "Except [it is] a mercy from your Lord" that We do not take this knowledge and insight away from you (إِلَّا رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ). It is a mercy for your own guidance and salvation, and a mercy for the guidance and salvation of humanity, and this mercy is, in fact, a continuation of the mercy of creation.

That God who, by the requirement of His general and specific mercy, created human beings and clothed them in the garment of existence—which is the highest garment of perfection—that same God, by the requirement of His mercy, helps them traverse this path. He appoints conscious, infallible, tireless, compassionate, kind, and steadfast leaders for their guidance. It is this very mercy that necessitates that the earth never remains devoid of a Divine Proof (Hujjah).

And at the end of the verse, as an emphasis or as an explanation of the reason for the preceding sentence, it says:

"Indeed, His favor upon you has ever been great" (إِنَّ فَضْلَهُ كانَ عَلَيْكَ كَبِيراً).

The existence of the capacity for this favor within your heart—which has been nourished by the water of worship, self-purification, and struggle (jihad)—on one hand, and the dire need of the servants for such a leader on the other, has necessitated that Allah's favor upon you be exceptionally great:

That He opens the doors of knowledge to you, informs you of the secrets of human guidance, and protects you from errors, so that you may be a model and an exemplar for all human beings until the end of the world.

Additionally, it is necessary to mention that the aforementioned exception clause is connected to what appeared in the previous verse, and the meaning of the exception and the antecedent is as follows: "If We willed, We could take away this revelation which We have sent to you, but We do not do so because Divine mercy encompasses you and the people." [1] It is clear that such exceptions are not evidence that Allah might actually one day take this mercy away from His Prophet; rather, they are evidence that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله also possesses nothing of his own; his knowledge, insight, and heavenly revelation are all from Allah and dependent upon His Will.

[1]: In reality, the meaning of the sentence is: "But We do not will to take away that which We revealed to you, as a mercy from your Lord."

Ayahs

قُل لَّئِنِ ٱجْتَمَعَتِ ٱلْإِنسُ وَٱلْجِنُّ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتُوا۟ بِمِثْلِ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ لَا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِۦ وَلَوْ كَانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيرًا (٨٨)
Say, ‘Should all humans and jinn rally to bring the like of this Quran, they will not bring its like, even if they assisted one another.’ (88)
وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَا لِلنَّاسِ فِى هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ مِن كُلِّ مَثَلٍ فَأَبَىٰٓ أَكْثَرُ ٱلنَّاسِ إِلَّا كُفُورًا (٨٩)
We have certainly interspersed this Quran with every [kind of] parable for the people, but most people are only intent on ingratitude. (89)

Commentary

You Shall Never Bring the Like of the Quran

Considering that the preceding and succeeding verses relate to discussions about the Quran, the connection of the verse under discussion—which explicitly speaks of the miraculous nature (I’jaz) of the Quran—to them requires no further explanation.

Furthermore, in the upcoming verses, there is a detailed discussion regarding the excuses made by the polytheists concerning miracles and their demands for various signs. The verse under discussion is, in fact, a prelude to that future discussion, aiming to show these excuse-seekers that the most sublime and living document of the truthfulness of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, which shines throughout history as an eternal miracle, is this very Quran. In the presence of this Quran, such excuses are misplaced!

Some have also sought to explain the connection of this verse with the previous ones by comparing the mysterious nature of the soul (Ruh) with the mysterious nature of the Quran [^1], but the connection mentioned above seems clearer.

In any case, Allah addresses the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله here, saying:

"Say: 'If all of mankind and the jinn were to gather together to produce the like of this Quran, they could not produce the like thereof, even if they backed up each other with help and support.'" (قُلْ لَئِنِ اجْتَمَعَتِ الْإِنْسُ وَ الْجِنُّ عَلى‌ أَنْ يَأْتُوا بِمِثْلِ هذَا الْقُرْآنِ لا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ وَ لَوْ كانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيراً).

This verse, with total clarity, invites all the inhabitants of the world—whether small or great, Arab or non-Arab, humans and even non-human intelligent beings, scholars, philosophers, literati, historians, geniuses, and non-geniuses—in short, everyone without exception, to challenge the Quran. It says that if you think the Quran is not the word of Allah and is a product of the human brain, you too are human; bring the like of it. And whenever, after collective effort and striving, you find yourselves incapable, this is the best proof of the Quran being a miracle.

This invitation to challenge, which in the terminology of theologians is called "Tahaddi" (Challenge), is one of the pillars of every miracle. Wherever such an expression is used, we clearly understand that the subject is among the miracles.

In this verse, several points attract attention: [The Universality of the Challenge]

1- First of all, the universality of the invitation to challenge, which encompasses all humans and other intelligent beings.

2- The eternal nature of the invitation is another point, as there is no restriction in terms of time. Thus, this call and invitation, just as it existed during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, exists today and will exist tomorrow as well.

[^1]: Fi Zilal, Vol. 5, p. 358, under the verse discussed.

3- The expression "gathering" (اجتماع) refers to the issue of cooperation, collective thinking, and mutual assistance, which certainly increases the output of human work hundreds or thousands of times over.

4- The phrase "even if they backed up each other with help and support" (وَ لَوْ كانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيراً) is a renewed emphasis on the issue of collective thinking and cooperation, and simultaneously a subtle hint at the importance and impact of this work in advancing goals.

5- The expression "the like of this Quran" (بِمِثْلِ هذَا الْقُرْآنِ) is a comprehensive term that implies similarity and likeness in all fields—meaning like it in terms of eloquence, like it in terms of content, and like it in terms of human-building, scientific discussions, life-giving social laws, history free from superstitions, prophecies regarding the future, and the like.

6- The invitation to all humans is proof that in the matter of the miracle, it is not only the aspect of the Quran's wording, eloquence, and rhetoric that is considered; for if that were the case, inviting those unfamiliar with the Arabic language would be useless.

7- A clear and expressive miracle is one whose bringer not only invites opponents to challenge it but also stimulates and encourages them to do so through various means, "provoking their pride," so to speak, so that they employ whatever power they have. Then, when their incapacity becomes apparent, the depth and greatness of the miracle become clear.

In the verse under discussion, this has been fully implemented. On one hand, it involves all humans, and by explicitly stating their inability through the phrase "they could not produce the like thereof" (لا يَأْتُونَ بِمِثْلِهِ), it has provoked them, and with the phrase "even if they backed up each other with help and support" (وَ لَوْ كانَ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ ظَهِيراً), it has provided further stimulation.

The next verse is, in fact, an explanation of one of the aspects of the Quran's miraculous nature, namely its "comprehensiveness." It says: "And We have certainly explained for mankind in this Quran every kind of example" (وَ لَقَدْ صَرَّفْنا لِلنَّاسِ فِي هذَا الْقُرْآنِ مِنْ كُلِّ مَثَلٍ).

"But even so, most of the ignorant and unlearned people showed no reaction except denying the truth and ignoring the proofs of guidance" (فَأَبى‌ أَكْثَرُ النَّاسِ إِلَّا كُفُوراً). "Sarrafna" (صرفنا) comes from the root "Tasrif," meaning to change, transform, or turn from one state to another.

"Kufur" (كفور) means the denial of truth.

Truly, this diversity of the Quran's contents, coming from an unlettered person, is astonishing. For in this Heavenly Book, there are solid rational arguments with their specific subtleties regarding beliefs, as well as the expression of firm and stable laws based on human needs in all fields. The historical discussions of the Quran are unique in their kind—exciting, awakening, pleasant, soul-stirring, and free from any superstition.

And its moral discussions do to ready hearts what spring rain does to dead lands! The scientific matters raised in the Quran unveil realities that were unknown to any scientist, at least at that time.

In short, whichever path the Quran treads, it presents the most sublime example.

Considering that human knowledge is limited (as hinted in the previous verses), and especially considering that the Prophet of Islam was raised in an environment where there was no news even of the limited human knowledge of that time, is the existence of all this diverse content in monotheistic, moral, social, political, and military fields not proof that it did not emanate from a human brain but rather from Allah?

And for this reason, if jinn and men were to gather to bring the like of it, they would not be able to.

Suppose all today's scientists and specialists in various sciences were to gather to compile an encyclopedia and cast it in the mold of the best expressions; it is possible that this collection might be comprehensive for today, but certainly, fifty years from now, it would not only be incomplete and inadequate but would also reek of obsolescence.

In contrast, the Quran, in whatever age or time it is read—especially in our age—is such that it is as if it were revealed "today" and "for today," and no trace of the passage of time is seen in it.

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ لَن نُّؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّىٰ تَفْجُرَ لَنَا مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ يَنۢبُوعًا (٩٠)
They say, ‘We will not believe you until you make a spring gush forth for us from the ground. (90)
أَوْ تَكُونَ لَكَ جَنَّةٌ مِّن نَّخِيلٍ وَعِنَبٍ فَتُفَجِّرَ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرَ خِلَـٰلَهَا تَفْجِيرًا (٩١)
Or until you have a garden of date palms and vines and you make streams gush through it. (91)
أَوْ تُسْقِطَ ٱلسَّمَآءَ كَمَا زَعَمْتَ عَلَيْنَا كِسَفًا أَوْ تَأْتِىَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ قَبِيلًا (٩٢)
Or until you cause the sky to fall in fragments upon us, just as you have averred. Or until you bring Allah and the angels [right] in front of us. (92)
أَوْ يَكُونَ لَكَ بَيْتٌ مِّن زُخْرُفٍ أَوْ تَرْقَىٰ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَلَن نُّؤْمِنَ لِرُقِيِّكَ حَتَّىٰ تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنَا كِتَـٰبًا نَّقْرَؤُهُۥ ۗ قُلْ سُبْحَانَ رَبِّى هَلْ كُنتُ إِلَّا بَشَرًا رَّسُولًا (٩٣)
Or until you have a house of gold, or you ascend into the sky. And we will not believe your ascension until you bring down for us a book that we may read.’ Say, ‘Immaculate is my Lord! Am I anything but a human apostle?!’ (93)

Circumstances of Revelation

In Islamic traditions, as well as in the writings of renowned commentators, various accounts regarding the circumstances of the revelation of the above verses have been narrated, the summary of which is as follows:

"A group of the polytheists of Mecca, including Walid ibn Mughirah and Abu Jahl, gathered by the Kaaba and spoke with one another regarding the Prophet's mission. Finally, they concluded that someone should be sent to Muhammad with a message: 'The nobles of Quraysh, your own tribe, have gathered and are ready to speak with you; come to us.'

The Prophet, hoping that perhaps the light of faith had begun to shine in their hearts and that they were ready to accept the truth, immediately hurried to them.

However, he was met with these words:

'O Muhammad! We have called you here to finalize the argument against you. We know of no one who has caused as much distress to his own people and tribe as you have: you have insulted our gods, criticized our religion, called our wise men fools, and sowed the seeds of discord among the community.

Tell us, what is your ailment?! Do you want money? We will give you so much that you will be beyond need! Do you want status? We will give you a great position! Are you ill? (And suffer from a mental affliction?) We will invite the best physicians to treat you!'

The Prophet replied: 'It is none of these matters. Allah has sent me to you and has revealed a Heavenly Book to me. If you accept it, it will be to your benefit in this world and the Hereafter; and if you do not accept it, I shall be patient until Allah judges between me and you.'

They said: 'Very well, since you say such things, no city is more cramped than ours (Mecca is surrounded by closely situated mountains). Ask your Lord to push these mountains back and make rivers flow in this dry and barren land, like the rivers of Syria and Iraq.

And also ask Him to bring our ancestors back to life, and Qusayy ibn Kilab must certainly be among them, for he was a truthful old man! So that we may ask them whether what you say is truth or falsehood?!'

The Prophet replied with indifference: 'I am not commanded to do these things.'

They said: 'If you will not do that, then at least ask your Lord to send an angel to testify for you, and to provide for us gardens, treasures, and palaces of gold!' He replied: 'I have not been sent for these matters either. I have an invitation from Allah; if you accept it, so much the better, otherwise Allah will judge between me and you.'

They said: 'Then cause pieces of the sky to fall upon us—as you claim your God can do whenever He wills!' He replied: 'That is up to Allah; if He wills, He shall do it.'

One among them shouted: 'We will not believe even with these things! We will only believe when you bring Allah and the angels and place them before us!' The Prophet (upon hearing these absurdities) stood up to leave the gathering. Some of that group followed him and said:

'O Muhammad, your people made every proposal and you did not accept; then they asked for things regarding themselves and you did not perform them; finally, they asked you to bring down the punishment with which you threaten them, and you did not do that either. By Allah, we will never believe in you until you place a ladder to the sky and climb it before our eyes, and bring back several angels with you upon your return! And you must have a letter in your hand testifying to the truth of your call!'

Abu Jahl said: 'Leave him alone! He knows nothing but insulting our idols and ancestors! And I have made a covenant with God to pick up a boulder and, when he prostrates, smash it upon his brain!!' The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله returned from them while his heart was shrouded in a halo of grief and sorrow because of the ignorance, stubbornness, and arrogance of these people...

At this moment, the above verses were revealed and responded to their dialogues." [1]

Commentary

Various Excuses!

After explaining the greatness and miraculous nature of the Quran in the preceding verses, the verses under discussion address a portion of the excuses that demonstrate that the stance of this group of disbelievers against the life-giving call of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was nothing but stubbornness, enmity, rebellion, and arrogance. For, in the face of the Prophet's logical proposal and the living evidence he carried, they made the most unreasonable requests.

These requests are described in the above verses in six parts:

[1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan under the verses in question—this circumstance of revelation is also mentioned in Ad-Durr al-Manthur with some variations under these same verses.

1- First, it says: "And they say: 'We will never believe in you until you cause a spring to gush forth for us from the earth!'" (وَ قالُوا لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لَكَ حَتَّى تَفْجُرَ لَنا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ يَنْبُوعاً).

"Fajr" and "Tafjir" mean to split or break open, whether it is splitting the earth by springs or splitting the horizon by the light of dawn (though tafjir implies a greater intensity than fajr).

"Yanbu'" is derived from the root "nab'" meaning the place where water gushes out. Some have said that yanbu' is a spring of water that never runs dry.

2- "Or [until] you have a garden of date-palms and grapes and cause rivers to gush forth in their midst abundantly!" (أَوْ تَكُونَ لَكَ جَنَّةٌ مِنْ نَخِيلٍ وَ عِنَبٍ فَتُفَجِّرَ الْأَنْهارَ خِلالَها تَفْجِيراً).

3- "Or you cause the sky to fall upon us in pieces, as you have claimed," (أَوْ تُسْقِطَ السَّماءَ كَما زَعَمْتَ عَلَيْنا كِسَفاً).

4- "Or you bring Allah and the angels before us face to face," (أَوْ تَأْتِيَ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْمَلائِكَةِ قَبِيلًا).

"Qabil" has sometimes been interpreted as a guarantor or sponsor, and sometimes as something that stands in front of a person, face to face. Some have also considered it the plural of "qabilah," meaning a group or tribe.

According to the first meaning, the interpretation of the verse would be: "You must bring Allah and the angels as guarantors of the truth of your words."

According to the second meaning: "You must bring Allah and the angels and place them before us."

And according to the third meaning: "You must bring Allah and the angels to us in groups!"

It should be noted that these three concepts are not contradictory or in conflict, and it is possible for all of them to be contained within the meaning of the verse, as we find no obstacle in using a single word for more than one meaning.

5- "Or you have a house of gold—a house full of ornamentation and decoration," (أَوْ يَكُونَ لَكَ بَيْتٌ مِنْ زُخْرُفٍ‌).

"Zukhruf" originally means ornament, and since gold is one of the well-known ornamental metals, it is called zukhruf. Highly decorated houses are also called zukhruf, and similarly, colorful and deceptive speech is called muzakhraf (absurd/embellished).

6- "Or you ascend into the sky! But we will never believe in your ascension until you bring down to us a book that we may read!" (أَوْ تَرْقى‌ فِي السَّماءِ وَ لَنْ نُؤْمِنَ لِرُقِيِّكَ حَتَّى تُنَزِّلَ عَلَيْنا كِتاباً نَقْرَؤُهُ‌).

At the end of these verses, we read that Allah commands His Prophet to say in response to these contradictory, baseless, and sometimes ridiculous proposals: "Say: 'Glory be to my Lord!'" (قُلْ سُبْحانَ رَبِّي‌).

"Am I anything but a human messenger?" (هَلْ كُنْتُ إِلَّا بَشَراً رَسُولًا).

Key Points

1- The Prophet's Response to the Excuse-Seekers

As the tone of the verses themselves, in addition to the circumstances of revelation, testifies, these strange and bizarre requests of the polytheists never originated from a spirit of seeking the truth. Rather, their entire goal was to ensure that the system of idolatry and polytheism—which formed the foundations of the power of the Meccan leaders—remained in place, and to stop the Prophet of Islam from continuing the path of monotheism by any means possible.

However, the Prophet gave them two logical and clear answers in one short phrase.

First, that the Lord is far removed from such matters; He is too exalted to be under the command of this person or that, or to submit to the vain and baseless proposals of the light-minded (سُبْحانَ رَبِّي‌).

Second: Regardless of the above, fundamentally, bringing miracles is not my work. I am a human being like you, with the difference that I am the Messenger of Allah. Sending miracles is His work, and it is done by His will and command. I do not even have the right to make such a request on my own; whenever He deems it necessary to prove the truth of His Prophet's call, He sends whatever miracle is required (هَلْ كُنْتُ إِلَّا بَشَراً رَسُولًا).

It is true that these two responses are interconnected, yet they are considered two distinct answers: one proves the weakness of man in the face of this matter, and the other proves the sanctity of the God of man from accepting such arbitrary miracles.

Fundamentally, a Prophet is not a miracle-performer who sits in a place so that anyone who enters can propose a miracle of their own liking, and if they do not like it, propose another; in short, to play with the laws and traditions of creation, and after all this, accept if he wishes or dodge with an excuse if his noble desire does not so dictate.

The Prophet's duty is to prove his connection to Allah by bringing a miracle, and once he has shown sufficient miracles, he has no further duty in this regard.

He may not even be able to predict the time of the descent of miracles and only requests a miracle from Allah in a situation where he knows Allah is pleased with it.

2- Limited Minds and Unreasonable Demands:

Everyone speaks according to the extent of their own thought, and for this reason, the words of any person are an indication of the level of their intellect.

People who think of nothing but wealth and status imagine that anyone who speaks does so in the same context.

For this reason, the narrow-minded nobles of Quraysh sometimes offered the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله wealth, and sometimes status, so that he would abandon his call. They were measuring the vast soul of the Prophet with the very small cup of their own thought.

They even thought that if someone's effort was not for wealth or status, they must certainly be insane, and there was no fourth option! Therefore, they said: "If you want neither this nor that, accept the third option: allow us to invite physicians to treat you!" Like someone imprisoned in a tiny room who has not seen the vast sky, the shining sun, and all these mountains, seas, and deserts to realize the greatness of the universe, they wanted to measure the boundless soul of the Prophet with their own scales.

Furthermore, let us see what they were asking of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that was not present in Islam. They requested developed lands, springs full of water, gardens of palms and grapes, and comfortable houses; and we know that Islam, in its path of progress, created such a flourishing civilization that all kinds of economic advancement were possible within it. We saw that the Muslims, under the light of this very program of the Quran, went far beyond what the Arab polytheists, with their deficient thinking, were seeking.

If they had eyes that saw the truth, they would have seen both spiritual progress in this magnificent religion and material victories, for the Quran is the guarantor of human happiness in both fields.

Let us set aside their childish or foolish proposals, such as: "If you are truthful, send the divine punishment upon us and drop pieces of celestial stones upon our heads and brains!"

Or: "Set up a ladder and ascend to the sky, and from there bring us a letter of 'I am your devotee'!" Or: "Summon Allah and the angels collectively before us."

They did not even suggest "take us to Him." What ignorance, pride, and arrogance this brainless human has?!

3- Another Pretext for Denying Miracles

Although the concept of the above verses is not complex, and it is clear what kind of requests the polytheists of "Mecca" had for the Prophet of Islam and why the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله negative encounter with them occurred, we nevertheless see that these verses have become a pretext for the excuse-seekers of our time, some of whom insist on denying any kind of miracle for the Prophet of Islam.

They consider these verses the clearest verses that negate miracles from the Prophet, because the opponents asked for six different types of miracles from the earth and sky, beneficial and life-giving or death-dealing, yet he did not yield to any of them. His only answer was: "Glory be to my Lord! Am I anything but a human who is a messenger of Allah?"

But if these excuse-seekers of our time are not like their excuse-seeking friends in the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, their answer is clearly stated in these very verses, because:

1- Some of these six requests were fundamentally ridiculous and unreasonable, such as summoning Allah and the angels, or bringing a specific letter from the sky, presumably with their names and addresses! Others were arbitrary demands that, if carried out, would have left no trace of the requesters to believe or not (such as the descent of celestial stones upon their heads).

The rest of their proposals were summarized in having a comfortable and completely luxurious life with abundant wealth and riches, while we know that Prophets did not come for such a purpose.

And if we assume that some of these had none of these flaws, we know they were merely for the sake of seeking excuses, evidenced by other parts of these verses. We know it is not the Prophet's duty to submit to the proposals of excuse-seekers; rather, his duty is to present a miracle to the extent that the truth of his call is proven, and beyond this, nothing is required of him.

2- Some of the expressions in these verses themselves explicitly state to what extent these requesters were excuse-seekers and stubborn. While they propose ascending to the sky to the Prophet, they explicitly say: "Even if you ascend to the sky, we will not believe unless you bring a letter for us from the sky."

If they truly requested a miracle, then why do they say ascending to the sky is not enough for us? Is there any clearer evidence than this for their lack of logic?

3- Beyond all this, we know that a miracle is the work of Allah, not the work of the Prophet, while the tone of these excuse-seekers clearly shows that they considered the miracle to be the Prophet's work. Therefore, they attributed all actions to the person of the Prophet:

"You must split this earth and make rivers of water flow in it; you must drop the stones of the sky upon our heads; you must make Allah and the angels appear before us!"

Whereas it is necessary for the Prophet to remove this notion from their brains and prove to them: "I am neither God nor a partner to God, and miracles are His work alone. I am a human like other humans, with the difference that revelation is sent to me. And the amount of miracles that was necessary, He Himself has placed at my disposal; beyond this, nothing is within my power." Specifically, the phrase "سُبْحانَ رَبِّي‌" (Glory be to my Lord) is a testimony to this very meaning, as it purifies the station of the Lord from having any partner or peer.

For this reason, although several miracles are attributed to Jesus in the Quran—such as raising the dead, healing incurable diseases or the blind from birth—in all these cases, the word "bi-idhni" (by My permission) or "bi-idhnillah" (by Allah's permission) is mentioned, which makes it exclusively dependent on the command of the Lord, so it becomes clear that these miracles, although they appeared at the hand of the Messiah, were not from him himself but were all by the command of Allah. [1]

4- Furthermore, what intellect would believe that a person would claim prophethood and even consider himself the Seal of the Prophets, mentioning all kinds of miracles for past prophets in his Heavenly Book, yet refuse to bring any miracle himself?! Would people not say: "What kind of prophet are you that you cannot bring any of the miracles you yourself attribute to others?"

"You claim to be superior and the foremost of them all, while you would not even be their student."

What could the Prophet say in response to such words?

[1]: Refer to Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 110, and Surah Al-Imran, verse 49.

And this itself is a living sign that he presented miracles when necessary. Therefore, it becomes clear that if the Prophet did not submit to their proposals in these verses, they must have been baseless proposals and groundless excuses; otherwise, he was submissive to logical and reasonable proposals.

Ayahs

وَمَا مَنَعَ ٱلنَّاسَ أَن يُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ إِذْ جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوٓا۟ أَبَعَثَ ٱللَّهُ بَشَرًا رَّسُولًا (٩٤)
Nothing has kept these people from believing when guidance came to them, but their saying, ‘Has Allah sent a human as an apostle?!’ (94)
قُل لَّوْ كَانَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَلَـٰٓئِكَةٌ يَمْشُونَ مُطْمَئِنِّينَ لَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَلَكًا رَّسُولًا (٩٥)
Say, ‘Had there been angels in the earth, walking around and residing [in it like humans do], We would have sent down to them an angel from the heaven as apostle.’ (95)

Commentary

The Excuse of Homogeneity!

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the polytheists' excuses regarding Monotheism (Tawhid), and in the verses under discussion, it points to the excuse of homogeneity, stating:

"And nothing prevented the people from believing when guidance came to them except that they said: 'Has Allah sent a human being as a messenger?'" (وَ ما مَنَعَ النَّاسَ أَنْ يُؤْمِنُوا إِذْ جاءَهُمُ الْهُدى‌ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا أَ بَعَثَ اللَّهُ بَشَراً رَسُولًا).

Is it believable that this lofty and highly significant position be entrusted to a human being?

Should this great mission not be placed upon the shoulders of a superior species such as the angels?

So that they may fulfill it properly; where is a man of clay and where is the Divine mission? The celestial beings are worthy of this station, not the terrestrial ones!

This weak and baseless logic was not exclusive to one or two groups; rather, perhaps most faithless individuals throughout history have resorted to it against the prophets:

The people of Nuh, in their opposition to this great prophet, would cry out: "ما هذا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ‌" — "This is but a man like yourselves" (Surah Al-Mu'minun, Verse 24).

The faithless people contemporary to Hud said: "ما هذا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يَأْكُلُ مِمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ مِنْهُ وَ يَشْرَبُ مِمَّا تَشْرَبُونَ‌" — "This is but a man like yourselves; he eats of that which you eat and drinks of what you drink" (Surah Al-Mu'minun, Verse 33).

They even added that if you obey a human like yourselves, you will indeed be losers: (وَ لَئِنْ أَطَعْتُمْ بَشَراً مِثْلَكُمْ إِنَّكُمْ إِذاً لَخاسِرُونَ‌) (Surah Al-Mu'minun, Verse 34).

They raised this very same objection against the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "ما لِهذَا الرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ الطَّعامَ وَ يَمْشِي فِي الْأَسْواقِ لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُ نَذِيراً" — "Why does this messenger eat food and walk in the markets? Why was an angel not sent down to him to be a warner with him?" (Surah Al-Furqan, Verse 7).

The Quran, in a short and clear sentence, gives a meaningful response to all of them, saying:

"Say: 'If there were upon the earth angels walking securely, We would have sent down to them from the heaven an angel as a messenger'" (قُلْ لَوْ كانَ فِي الْأَرْضِ مَلائِكَةٌ يَمْشُونَ مُطْمَئِنِّينَ لَنَزَّلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ مَلَكاً رَسُولًا).

This means that a leader must always be of the same species as his followers: a human for humans and an angel for angels.

The reason for this homogeneity between the leader and the followers is also clear; because, on one hand, the most important part of a leader's propagation is his practical propagation—being a role model and an exemplar. This is only possible if he possesses the same instincts, feelings, and the same physical and spiritual structure. Otherwise, the purity of an angel who has neither sexual desire nor a need for shelter, clothing, and food, and in whom other human instincts do not exist, can never be a model for humans. Rather, people would say: "He is unaware of our hearts and does not know what storms rage in our souls due to lust and anger; he only speaks for himself. If he had our feelings, he would be like us or worse! No credit can be given to his words!"

But when a leader like Ali عليه السلام says: "Indeed, it is my soul which I discipline with God-wariness (Taqwa) so that it may arrive in safety on the Day of the Greatest Fear" [1]—"I too have a soul like yours, but I restrain it through Taqwa so that it may be in peace and security on the Day of Resurrection"—in such a state, they can be a role model and an exemplar.

On the other hand, a leader must well understand all the pains, needs, and desires of his followers to be ready to treat and respond to them, and not fall under the poet's saying: "You are unaware of my state; that is the problem."

It is specifically for this reason that the prophets rose from among the masses of the people,

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 45.

and they often endured periods of the hardest types of life, so that they might taste all the bitterness of life, touch the painful realities, and prepare and equip themselves to treat them.

Key Points

1- The phrase "وَ ما مَنَعَ النَّاسَ‌ ..." states that the only obstacle to their faith was this excuse-making, but this expression, of course, is not a proof of exclusivity; rather, it is for emphasis and to express the importance of the subject.

2- The expression "مَلائِكَةٌ يَمْشُونَ مُطْمَئِنِّينَ‌" (angels walking securely) has been discussed among commentators, and several interpretations have been given for it:

Some have considered it an allusion to the speech of the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) who said: "We were settled in this peninsula and had a peaceful life; Muhammad came and disturbed our peace." The Quran says that even if there were angels on earth and they had such a false peace as they claim, We would have sent a messenger of their own kind to them.

Others have interpreted it to mean "contentment with the world and its pleasures and turning away from any creed or religion."

And finally, some have taken it to mean "residing and inhabiting" the earth.

However, this possibility seems strong: that the meaning is that even if angels lived on earth and had a peaceful life free from collision, hostility, and conflict, they would still have a need for a leader of their own kind. For the sending of prophets is not only to end unrest and create peace in material life; rather, all of these are a prelude to traversing the path of perfection and cultivation in spiritual and human fields, and this requires a Divine leader.

3- Allamah Tabataba'i in Tafsir al-Mizan infers from the word "ارض" (earth) in the above verse that the nature of material life on earth is a nature of need for the existence of a prophet, and without it, life will never be organized.

Furthermore, he considers this word a subtle allusion to the issue of Earth's gravity, since walking with peace and security is not possible without its existence.

Ayahs

قُلْ كَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًۢا بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ بِعِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرًۢا بَصِيرًا (٩٦)
Say, ‘Allah suffices as witness between me and you. Indeed He is well aware His servants and a keen observer.’ (96)
وَمَن يَهْدِ ٱللَّهُ فَهُوَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِ ۖ وَمَن يُضْلِلْ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِهِۦ ۖ وَنَحْشُرُهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ عَلَىٰ وُجُوهِهِمْ عُمْيًا وَبُكْمًا وَصُمًّا ۖ مَّأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ كُلَّمَا خَبَتْ زِدْنَـٰهُمْ سَعِيرًا (٩٧)
Whomever Allah guides is rightly guided, and whomever He leads astray—you will never for find them any guardians besides Him. On the Day of Resurrection, We shall muster them [scrambling] on their faces, blind, dumb, and deaf. Their refuge shall be hell. Whenever it subsides, We shall intensify the blaze for them. (97)

Commentary

The Truly Guided

Following the previous discussions regarding Monotheism (Tawhid), Prophethood, and the debates with opponents, the above verses serve as a declaration of the conclusion of the argument at this stage and a drawing of results from it.

First, it says: If they do not accept your clear proofs regarding Monotheism, Prophethood, and the Resurrection, declare to them and "Say: Allah is sufficient as a witness between me and you; surely He is Aware of His servants, Seeing their actions" (قُلْ كَفَى بِاللَّهِ شَهِيداً بَيْنِي وَ بَيْنِكُمْ إِنَّهُ كَانَ بِعِبَادِهِ خَبِيراً بَصِيراً) [^1]. In reality, two objectives were intended by stating this phrase: First:

To threaten the stubborn and prejudiced opponents that the All-Knowing and All-Seeing Allah is a witness and observer of our deeds and yours; do not imagine that you will escape the realm of His power, or that any of your actions remain hidden from Him.

Second, that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, by stating this phrase, manifests his firm faith in what he has said, because the speaker's decisiveness in his words has a deep psychological effect on the listener. Perhaps this firm and decisive expression, which is also accompanied by a kind of mild threat, will affect them, shake their hearts, awaken their thoughts, and call them to the right path.

Then it adds: "Only he is guided whom Allah casts the light of guidance into his heart" (وَ مَنْ يَهْدِ اللَّهُ فَهُوَ الْمُهْتَدِ). "But those whom Allah has led astray (due to their own actions), you will never find for them any protectors, guides, or guardians besides Him" (وَ مَنْ يُضْلِلْ فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لَهُمْ أَوْلِيَاءَ مِنْ دُونِهِ).

The only way is for them to return to Him once more and seek the light of guidance from Him.

These two sentences are, in fact, an indication that strong and crushing arguments alone are not sufficient for bringing about faith; rather, until Divine success (tawfiq) and the merit for guidance are found within a person,

[^1]: From a grammatical perspective, the "ba" in "كَفَى بِاللَّهِ" is extra (za'idah), "Allah" is the subject (fa'il) of "kafa," and "shahidan" is a specification (tamyiz) or, according to some, a circumstantial qualifier (hal).

it is impossible for them to believe.

This expression is similar to when we invite individuals to perform a significant good deed, or point out the importance of a subject through various arguments, yet some accept while others oppose. We say: Not everyone is worthy of performing such a task! A pure seed is needed to become capable of grace, Otherwise, every stone and clod would not become pearl and coral! It is a reality that not every heart is worthy of the Light of Truth, and not every head is a place for His passion. Furthermore, this speech has a stimulating effect on the listener, and it often happens that one abandons stubbornness and submits to the Truth to prove their own merit.

We have also stated many times that Divine guidance and misguidance never have a compulsory (jabr) nature; rather, they are the direct effect of the human being's own actions and attributes:

Those who rise to strive (jihad) in His way and make every kind of sacrifice to reach the Truth are certainly worthy of being included in His guidance: "And those who strive in Our cause, We will surely guide them to Our paths" (Surah Al-Ankabut, Verse 69).

But those who, by following the path of enmity and stubbornness and becoming tainted with every kind of injustice, corruption, and sin, have killed the merit within themselves and deserved the withdrawal of success and the falling into misguidance—Allah certainly leads these individuals astray, as He says: "And Allah leads the wrongdoers astray" (Ibrahim, 27); "And He misleads thereby only the rebellious" (Surah Al-Baqarah, 26); "Thus does Allah lead astray him who is a transgressor and a doubter" (Ghafir, 34).

As for "protectors" (awliya) being mentioned in the plural form, it may refer to the multiplicity of imaginary gods, or the variety of means and causes to which they used to take refuge. That is, none of these means and causes, nor humans, non-humans, or imaginary gods, can come to their aid or save them from misguidance and misery.

Then, in the form of a decisive and crushing threat, it shows them a scene from the scenes of the Resurrection, which is the definite result of their actions:

"And We shall gather them on the Day of Resurrection on their faces" (وَ نَحْشُرُهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ عَلَى وُجُوهِهِمْ):

Instead of walking upright and standing, the angels of punishment drag them on their faces upon the ground.

Some have also suggested the possibility that since these sinners are unable to walk there, they crawl on their faces and chests like reptiles, moving forward in a painful and humiliating state.

Yes, they are deprived of great blessings such as legs for walking because, in this world, they did not use these means in the way of prosperity; rather, they employed them in the path of sin.

And also "they shall enter that great court in a state of being blind, dumb, and deaf" (عُمْياً وَ بُكْماً وَ صُمّاً).

Here, the question arises that it is understood from other verses of the Quran that the criminals and the people of Hell both see, hear, and speak [^1]. How then does the above verse say they are blind, deaf, and dumb?! Commentators have mentioned several interpretations in response to this question, the best of which are the following two:

[^1]: In Verse 53 of Surah Al-Kahf we read: "وَ رَأَى الْمُجْرِمُونَ النَّارَ" (And the criminals shall see the Fire); and in Verse 13 of Surah Al-Furqan we read: "دَعَوْا هُنَالِكَ ثُبُوراً" (They will cry out therein for destruction); and in Verse 12 of Surah Al-Furqan we read: "سَمِعُوا لَهَا تَغَيُّظاً وَ زَفِيراً" (They shall hear its raging and roaring).

1- The stations and stages of the Resurrection are various. In some stages and stations, they are blind, deaf, and dumb, and this itself is a kind of punishment for them (because they did not make proper use of these great Divine blessings in the world). But in other stages, their eyes become seeing, their ears hearing, and their tongues loosened, so that they may see the scenes of punishment, hear the reproaches of the blamers, and engage in wailing, crying out, and expressing weakness—which is itself another type of punishment for them.

2- The criminals are deprived of seeing what brings joy, hearing what brings delight, and saying what leads to salvation. Conversely, they see, hear, and say that which causes torment and distress.

And at the end of the verse, it says: "Their permanent abode is Hell" (مَأْوَاهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ).

But do not imagine its fire, like the fires of the world, eventually tends toward extinction; no, "whenever its flame abates, We increase for them the blazing fire" (كُلَّمَا خَبَتْ زِدْنَاهُمْ سَعِيراً).

Ayahs

ذَٰلِكَ جَزَآؤُهُم بِأَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَقَالُوٓا۟ أَءِذَا كُنَّا عِظَـٰمًا وَرُفَـٰتًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ خَلْقًا جَدِيدًا (٩٨)
That is their requital because they defied Our signs and said, ‘What, when we have become bones and dust, shall we really be raised in a new creation?’ (98)
۞ أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ قَادِرٌ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَخْلُقَ مِثْلَهُمْ وَجَعَلَ لَهُمْ أَجَلًا لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهِ فَأَبَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ إِلَّا كُفُورًا (٩٩)
Do they not see that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth, is able to create the like of them? He has appointed for them a term, in which there is no doubt; yet the wrongdoers are only intent on ingratitude. (99)
قُل لَّوْ أَنتُمْ تَمْلِكُونَ خَزَآئِنَ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّىٓ إِذًا لَّأَمْسَكْتُمْ خَشْيَةَ ٱلْإِنفَاقِ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ قَتُورًا (١٠٠)
Say, ‘Even if you possessed the treasuries of my Lord’s mercy, you would withhold them for the fear of being spent, and man is very niggardly.’ (100)

Commentary

How is Resurrection Possible?

In the preceding verses, we saw the grim fate awaiting the criminals in the other world—a fate that would make any rational person pause and reflect. The verses under discussion explain the cause of this matter in another way.

First, it says: "That is their recompense because they disbelieved in Our signs and said, 'When we are reduced to bones and decayed particles, shall we really be raised up as a new creation?'" (ذلِكَ جَزاؤُهُمْ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِنا وَ قالُوا أَ إِذا كُنَّا عِظاماً وَ رُفاتاً أَ إِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ خَلْقاً جَدِيداً).

"Rufat" (رُفات), as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, refers to pieces of straw that do not break but rather scatter. It is evident that a human being under the earth first turns into decayed bones and then into dust, and these particles of dust eventually disintegrate and scatter.

The Quran immediately responds with a clear explanation to their statement—those who viewed the issue of physical resurrection with astonishment or deemed it impossible—saying: "Do they not see that Allah, who created the heavens and the earth, is able to create the like of them?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّ اللَّهَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ قادِرٌ عَلى‌ أَنْ يَخْلُقَ مِثْلَهُمْ‌).

However, they should not be in a hurry; this Resurrection and Judgment Day, though it may seem delayed, will eventually come. "He has appointed for them a fixed term, about which there is no doubt, [but the wrongdoers refuse everything except disbelief]" (وَ جَعَلَ لَهُمْ أَجَلًا لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ‌).

Yet, these oppressors and wrongdoers, even after hearing these verses, continue in their deviant path and "the wrongdoers refuse everything except disbelief" (فَأَبَى الظَّالِمُونَ إِلَّا كُفُوراً).

Since they insisted that the Prophet should not be of the human species, it was as if a kind of jealousy and stinginess prevented them from believing that Allah could bestow this blessing upon a human being. Therefore, in the last verse under discussion, He says: "Say, 'If you possessed the depositories of the mercy of my Lord, you would surely withhold them out of fear of spending, lest spending lead to poverty and destitution'" (قُلْ لَوْ أَنْتُمْ تَمْلِكُونَ خَزائِنَ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّي إِذاً لَأَمْسَكْتُمْ خَشْيَةَ الْإِنْفاقِ‌).

"And man is ever stingy" (وَ كانَ الْإِنْسانُ قَتُوراً).

"Qatur" (قتور) is derived from the root "qatr" (قتر), which means withholding in spending. Since "Qatur" is an intensive form (sighah al-mubalaghah), it conveys the meaning of extreme withholding and narrow-mindedness.

Key Points

1- Physical Resurrection

The above verses are among the clearest regarding the proof of physical resurrection. This is because the polytheists' astonishment was about how Allah could once again clothe decayed bones and dust with the garment of life. The Quran's response is directly related to this: the God who created the heavens and the earth has the power to gather the scattered parts of a human being and grant them new life.

It is unclear how, despite these clear verses and many others like them, some claim to be Muslims yet consider resurrection to be exclusively spiritual! Furthermore, arguing from the universality of Allah's power to prove the issue of resurrection is one of the proofs the Quran repeatedly emphasizes, including at the end of Surah Ya-Sin, where several proofs for physical resurrection are stated, one of which is this very issue of the universality of Allah's power. [1]

2- Which Signs?

Regarding what is meant by "signs" in the phrase "disbelieved in Our signs" (كفروا بآياتنا), there are several possibilities: the signs of Monotheism (Tawhid), the proofs of Prophethood, or the verses related to Resurrection. However, considering that this phrase occurs within the discussion of Resurrection, it seems to refer to the verses of Resurrection, serving as a preamble to responding to those who deny it.

3- What is Meant by "The Like of Them" (Mithlahum)?

Normally, it should be said that Allah, with His power, can bring these humans back on the Day of Judgment, yet in the above verses we read that He can create "the like of them" (مثلهم). This expression has sometimes caused confusion or at least led some to ask: Are the humans who step into the Resurrection not the same humans?

Some commentators have said that "like" (مثل) here means "the very same" (عين), because sometimes we say, "The like of you should not do such a thing," meaning "You should not do this." However, this interpretation seems very unlikely, as such expressions have other contexts that do not fit our discussion.

The apparent meaning is that the use of "like" in the above verse refers to the restoration and renewal of life. The second creation is certainly not identical to the first in every respect, as it occurs at a different time and under different conditions, making it a new form, even if the matter is the same old matter. For example, if we mold a decayed and disintegrated brick into a new mold identical to the first, it cannot be said to be the exact same brick, though it is not something else either; rather, it is "the like of it." This shows how precise the Quran is in its choice of expressions. (Reflect on this).

[1]: Read more on this subject in the book "Resurrection and the World After Death."

Of course, it is certain that a person's identity lies in their soul and spirit, and we know that the original soul returns at the time of Resurrection. However, physical resurrection tells us that the soul will be with its original vehicle—meaning the disintegrated matter is gathered and reconstructed to harmonize with the soul. In discussions on Resurrection, we have proven that once a human soul is formed, it cannot harmonize with any body other than the original body in which it developed. That "robe" fits only this "stature," and this "stature" is for that "robe." This is the secret behind the necessity of the resurrection of both soul and body together (physical and spiritual resurrection).

4- What is the "Ajal" (Term)?

We know that "Ajal" means the end of the lifespan of something. But does "Ajal" in the above verses refer to the end of a person's life, or the end of the world's lifespan and the beginning of Resurrection?

Given that the topic is Resurrection, the second interpretation seems more correct.

As for what some great commentators have said—that this statement is inconsistent with the phrase "about which there is no doubt" (لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ‌) because the deniers of Resurrection certainly had doubts—this does not seem correct.

This is because the meaning of such expressions is that one should not allow any doubt into this matter, and fundamentally there is no room for doubt, not that no one actually doubts it.

Therefore, the overall meaning of the verse is: the God who created the heavens and the earth can certainly clothe these humans in life again. However, if this does not happen immediately, it is because Divine Wisdom has set a definite timeline for this matter, about which there is no doubt.

The conclusion is: the primary argument against the deniers of Resurrection is the issue of Power, while the phrase "He has appointed for them a fixed term about which there is no doubt" is an answer to a question that used to be raised regarding the delay of Resurrection. (Reflect on this).

5- The Connection Between the Verses

Studying the above verses raises the question: what is the connection between the issue of human stinginess mentioned in the last verse and the preceding discussions?

Some commentators have said this phrase refers to what was mentioned a few verses earlier by the idolaters—that they demanded the Prophet turn the land of Mecca into springs and gardens. The Quran responds to them by saying that even if all the Divine treasures were given to you, you would still not cease your withholding and stinginess.

However, this interpretation seems very unlikely, because their discussion was not about the ownership of these gardens and springs, but rather they were demanding the performance of these extraordinary acts.

The second interpretation given for this connection, which seems correct, is what we mentioned above: out of stinginess and narrow-mindedness, they were astonished that the blessing of Prophethood could be given to a human being. This verse actually responds to them, saying that your narrow-mindedness is such that even if you became the owners of the entire world, you would still not abandon your ugly and undesirable ways.

6- Are All Humans Stingy?

We have said many times that in many verses of the Quran, "man" (insan) is subjected to various types of blame in an absolute and unconditional manner, described with attributes such as stinginess, ignorance, being oppressive, and being hasty.

These expressions never contradict the fact that believers and those who are properly refined stand in direct opposition to these traits. Rather, it points to the fact that human nature is such that if it is not placed under the training of Divine leaders and is left to itself like a wild plant, it has the potential to adopt all these ugly traits.

It does not mean that man was inherently created this way or that everyone will ultimately be like this. [1]

7- The Expression "Fear of Spending" (Khashyat al-Infaq)

This means the fear of poverty—poverty that, in their imagination, results from excessive spending.

[1]: We have also discussed this in detail in previous sections.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ تِسْعَ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۖ فَسْـَٔلْ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ إِذْ جَآءَهُمْ فَقَالَ لَهُۥ فِرْعَوْنُ إِنِّى لَأَظُنُّكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ مَسْحُورًا (١٠١)
Certainly We gave Moses nine manifest signs. So ask the Children of Israel. When he came to them, Pharaoh said to him, ‘O Moses, indeed I think you are bewitched.’ (101)
قَالَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَآ أَنزَلَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِلَّا رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ بَصَآئِرَ وَإِنِّى لَأَظُنُّكَ يَـٰفِرْعَوْنُ مَثْبُورًا (١٠٢)
He said, ‘You certainly know that no one has sent these [signs] except the Lord of the heavens and the earth, as eye-openers, and I, O Pharaoh, indeed think you are doomed.’ (102)
فَأَرَادَ أَن يَسْتَفِزَّهُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥ جَمِيعًا (١٠٣)
He desired to exterminate them from the land, so We drowned him and all those who were with him. (103)
وَقُلْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ لِبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱسْكُنُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْضَ فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ جِئْنَا بِكُمْ لَفِيفًا (١٠٤)
After him We said to the Children of Israel, ‘Take up residence in the land, and when the occasion of the other [promise] comes, We shall gather you in mixed company.’ (104)

Commentary

Despite All These Signs, They Did Not Believe

In the preceding verses, we read about the strange and eccentric demands the polytheists made of the Prophet ﷺ. Since their motive—by the testimony of their own statements—was not seeking the truth but rather excuse-making and stubbornness, the Prophet ﷺ gave them a negative response.

In the verses under discussion, a sample of this scene is presented from previous nations: how they witnessed various extraordinary acts and miracles yet continued to make excuses and persist in their path of denial.

In the first verse, it says: "And We certainly gave Moses nine clear signs" (وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ تِسْعَ آيَاتٍ بَيِّنَاتٍ).

We will refer to what these nine signs were at the end of this discussion.

Then, for further emphasis, it adds: If your opponents even deny this matter, then to complete the proof against them, "ask the Children of Israel [how it was] when he came to them" (فَسْئَلْ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ إِذْ جَاءَهُمْ). Yet, despite this, the neck-stretching, tyrannical, and rebellious Pharaoh did not submit; instead, he accused Moses of being a sorcerer or insane, saying: "I think, O Moses, that you are indeed bewitched [or a sorcerer]" (فَقَالَ لَهُ فِرْعَوْنُ إِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يَا مُوسَىٰ مَسْحُوراً). In explaining the meaning of "mas-hur" (مَسْحُور), commentators have offered two interpretations: some consider it to mean "sorcerer" (sahur), based on other verses of the Quran which state that Pharaoh and his folk everywhere accused him of being a sorcerer. The use of the passive participle (ism al-maf'ul) to mean the active agent (fa'il) exists in the Arabic language, such as "mash'um" meaning "sha'im" (one who brings misfortune) and "maymun" meaning "yamin" (one who brings good fortune). However, another group of commentators has kept "mas-hur" in its literal passive sense, meaning someone who has been affected by magic, as can be inferred from verse 39 of Surah Adh-Dhariyat, which indicates they attributed both sorcery and madness to him.

In any case, this is the perennial method of the arrogant: they accuse men of God of sorcery or madness because of their innovations, their movement against the current of corrupt societies, and their display of extraordinary miracles, all to influence the minds of simple-hearted people and scatter them from around the Prophets.

But Moses did not remain silent in the face of this unjust accusation and, with total decisiveness, "said: You have already known that none has sent down these [signs] as insights except the Lord of the heavens and the earth" (قَالَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَا أَنْزَلَ هَٰؤُلَاءِ إِلَّا رَبُّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ بَصَائِرَ). Therefore, you deny the truths despite your knowledge and awareness; you know well that these are from Allah, and I also know that you know!

These are "basa'ir" (insights)—clear proofs through which people find the path of truth and gain vision to traverse the road of felicity.

And for this reason, because you knowingly deny the truth, "I think, O Pharaoh, that you are indeed doomed to destruction" (وَإِنِّي لَأَظُنُّكَ يَا فِرْعَوْنُ مَثْبُوراً). "Mathbur" (مَثْبُور) is derived from "thubur," meaning destruction.

When Pharaoh could not resist Moses' crushing arguments, he resorted to the same thing that all illogical tyrants throughout the centuries and ages have resorted to: "So he intended to drive them out of the land, but We drowned him and those with him, all together" (فَأَرَادَ أَنْ يَسْتَفِزَّهُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ فَأَغْرَقْنَاهُ وَمَنْ مَعَهُ جَمِيعاً). "Yastafizza" is from "istifzaz," meaning to drive out by force and violence.

Following this great victory and salvation, "We said to the Children of Israel after him: Dwell in the land (the land of Egypt and Sham)" (وَقُلْنَا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ لِبَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ اسْكُنُوا الْأَرْضَ). "But when the promise of the Hereafter comes, We will bring you forth as a mixed crowd" (فَإِذَا جَاءَ وَعْدُ الْآخِرَةِ جِئْنَا بِكُمْ لَفِيفاً).

"Lafif" (لَفِيف) is from "laff," meaning to wrap or fold, and here it refers to a group that is completely intermingled and entwined such that individuals and their tribes cannot be distinguished.

Key Points
