1- What is Meant by the Nine Signs?

In the Holy Quran, many signs and miracles are mentioned for Moses, including:

  1. The staff turning into a great serpent and swallowing the sorcerers' tools (فَإِذَا هِيَ حَيَّةٌ تَسْعَىٰ) (Ta-Ha: 20).
  2. The "White Hand" or the shining of Moses' عليه السلام hand like a source of light (وَاضْمُمْ يَدَكَ إِلَىٰ جَنَاحِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضَاءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوءٍ آيَةً أُخْرَىٰ) (Ta-Ha: 22).
  3. Crushing floods (فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ الطُّوفَانَ) (Al-A'raf: 133).
  4. "Locusts" which dominated their crops and trees and became a pest to their cultivated lands (وَالْجَرَادَ) (Al-A'raf: 133).
  5. "Qummal" (lice/vermin), a type of plant pest that destroyed grains (وَالْقُمَّلَ) (Al-A'raf: 133).
  6. "Frogs" (dhafadi') that emerged from the Nile and multiplied so much that they made life miserable and difficult (وَالضَّفَادِعَ) (Al-A'raf: 133).
  7. "Blood" (dam), either a general affliction of nosebleeds or the Nile turning the color of blood such that it was unusable for drinking or agriculture (وَالدَّمَ آيَاتٍ مُفَصَّلَاتٍ) (Al-A'raf: 133).
  8. The parting of the sea so that the Children of Israel could cross (وَإِذْ فَرَقْنَا بِكُمُ الْبَحْرَ) (Al-Baqarah: 50).
  9. The descent of "Manna" and "Salwa" (quail), the explanation of which was given under verse 57 of Surah Al-Baqarah (وَأَنْزَلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَنَّ وَالسَّلْوَىٰ) (Al-Baqarah: 57).
  10. Springs gushing from the stone (فَقُلْنَا اضْرِبْ بِعَصَاكَ الْحَجَرَ فَانْفَجَرَتْ مِنْهُ اثْنَتَا عَشْرَةَ عَيْناً) (Al-Baqarah: 60).
  11. A portion of the mountain being detached and held over them like a canopy (وَإِذْ نَتَقْنَا الْجَبَلَ فَوْقَهُمْ كَأَنَّهُ ظُلَّةٌ) (Al-A'raf: 171).
  12. Famine, drought, and shortage of fruits (وَلَقَدْ أَخَذْنَا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ بِالسِّنِينَ وَنَقْصٍ مِنَ الثَّمَرَاتِ) (Al-A'raf: 130).
  13. The return of life to a slain person whose murder caused great dispute among the Children of Israel (فَقُلْنَا اضْرِبُوهُ بِبَعْضِهَا كَذَٰلِكَ يُحْيِي اللَّهُ الْمَوْتَىٰ) (Al-Baqarah: 73).
  14. Utilizing the shade of clouds which miraculously stood over their heads in the intense heat of the desert (وَظَلَّلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ الْغَمَامَ) (Al-Baqarah: 57).

However, the question here is: what is meant by the "nine signs" mentioned in the verses under discussion?

The expressions used in these verses indicate that they refer to the miracles performed in connection with Pharaoh and his folk, not those related to the Children of Israel themselves, such as the descent of Manna and Salwa, the gushing of springs from the stone, and the like.

Given this point, it can be said that the five subjects mentioned in verse 133 of Surah Al-A'raf are part of these nine signs (flood, plant pests, locusts, abundance of frogs, and blood).

Additionally, without doubt, the two famous miracles of Moses عليه السلام—the matter of the "staff" and the "white hand"—are part of these nine signs, especially since in Surah An-Naml, verses 10 to 12, the same expression "nine signs" (تسع آيات) is mentioned after describing these two great miracles.

This brings the total to seven extraordinary matters. Let us see what the other two signs are.

Undoubtedly, the drowning of Pharaoh's folk and the like cannot be among these signs, because the goal is to mention signs that came for the guidance of Pharaoh's folk, not those that caused their destruction.

A careful look at the verses of Surah Al-A'raf, where many of these signs are mentioned, shows that the other two signs were "drought" and "shortage of various fruits." This is because after the miracle of the staff and the white hand, and before the mention of the five signs of the flood, locusts, etc., we read: "And We certainly seized the people of Pharaoh with years of famine and a deficiency in fruits that perhaps they would be reminded" (Al-A'raf: 130).

Although some might imagine that drought is not separate from the shortage of fruits and thus they count as one sign, as we said in the commentary of verse 130 of Surah Al-A'raf, temporary and limited droughts might affect trees less, but when prolonged, they will also cause the destruction of trees. Therefore, drought alone does not always cause the destruction of fruits.

Furthermore, the loss of fruits might have been due to pests other than drought.

The conclusion is that the nine extraordinary acts referred to in the verses under discussion are: the staff, the white hand, the flood, locusts, a type of plant pest called qummal, the abundance of frogs, blood, drought, and the shortage of fruits.

In the same Surah Al-A'raf, we read that after mentioning these nine signs, it says that since they ultimately did not believe despite witnessing all these signs, We took retribution from them and drowned them in the sea because they denied Our signs and were heedless of them (Al-A'raf: 136).

Of course, in our hadith sources, narrations have been quoted regarding the interpretation of this verse, but since these narrations differ from one another, they cannot be used as a definitive criterion for judgment or relied upon with certainty.

2- Is the Questioner the Prophet ﷺ?

The apparent meaning of the above verses is that the Prophet ﷺ was commanded to ask the Children of Israel regarding the nine signs revealed to Moses—how Pharaoh's folk, with various excuses, refused to accept the truth of Moses عليه السلام despite all these signs. However, since a person like the Prophet ﷺ, with his knowledge and intellect, had no need for such a question, some commentators have considered the one commanded to ask to be other addressees. Yet, considering that the Prophet's ﷺ question was not for himself but for the acceptance of the polytheists, there is no obstacle to the questioner being the Prophet ﷺ himself, so that the polytheists would know that if the Prophet ﷺ does not submit to their various proposals, it is because these proposals are not motivated by seeking truth, but rather by stubbornness, prejudice, and enmity—just as we read in the story of Moses عليه السلام and Pharaoh.

3- Which Land is Meant by "Al-Ard" (The Land) in These Verses?

In the above verses, we read that Allah commanded the Children of Israel to dwell in the promised land now that they had triumphed over the enemy. Is the meaning the land of Egypt? (The same word in the previous verse, which says Pharaoh wanted to drive them out of "the land," carries this meaning, and other verses of the Quran also say that the Children of Israel became the inheritors of Pharaoh's folk).

Or is it a reference to the holy land of Palestine? Because after this event, the Children of Israel went toward the land of Palestine and were commanded to enter it.

However, we do not find it unlikely that both lands were intended, as the Children of Israel, by the testimony of Quranic verses, became inheritors of the lands of Pharaoh's folk and also owners of the land of Palestine.

4- Does the Phrase "Wa'd al-Akhirah" in the Above Verses Mean the Abode of the Hereafter?

The answer to this question is apparently positive, as the phrase "We will bring you forth as a mixed crowd" (جِئْنَا بِكُمْ لَفِيفاً) is a contextual clue for this subject.

However, some great commentators have suggested the possibility that the expression "Wa'd al-Akhirah" refers to the same thing we read at the beginning of this Surah—that Allah had promised two victories and defeats to the Children of Israel, referring to one as "the first promise" (wa'd ula) and the other as "the second/last promise" (wa'd al-akhirah). But this possibility seems very unlikely given the phrase "We will bring you forth as a mixed crowd." (Reflect on this).

Ayahs

وَبِٱلْحَقِّ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ وَبِٱلْحَقِّ نَزَلَ ۗ وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا (١٠٥)
With the truth did We send it down, and with the truth did it descend, and We did not send you except as a bearer of good news and as a warner. (105)
وَقُرْءَانًا فَرَقْنَـٰهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ عَلَىٰ مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْنَـٰهُ تَنزِيلًا (١٠٦)
We have sent the Quran in [discrete] parts so that you may recite it for the people a little at a time, and We have sent it down piecemeal. (106)
قُلْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَوْ لَا تُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦٓ إِذَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ يَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقَانِ سُجَّدًا (١٠٧)
Say, ‘Whether you believe in it, or do not believe in it, indeed when it is recited to those who were given knowledge before it, they fall down in prostration on their faces, (107)
وَيَقُولُونَ سُبْحَـٰنَ رَبِّنَآ إِن كَانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّنَا لَمَفْعُولًا (١٠٨)
and say, ‘‘Immaculate is our Lord! Indeed Our Lord’s promise is bound to be fulfilled.’’ (108)
وَيَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقَانِ يَبْكُونَ وَيَزِيدُهُمْ خُشُوعًا ۩ (١٠٩)
Weeping, they fall down on their faces, and it increases them in humility.’ (109)

Commentary

Lovers of the Truth!

Once again, the Quran addresses the importance and greatness of this Heavenly Book and responds to some of the objections or excuses raised by the opponents.

First, It says: "We have sent it down with the truth" (وَبِالْحَقِّ أَنْزَلْنَاهُ) and immediately adds, "and with the truth it has descended" (وَبِالْحَقِّ نَزَلَ).

"And We have not sent you except as a bringer of glad tidings and a warner," and you have no right to make any changes to the content of the Quran (وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَاكَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا). Regarding the difference between the first phrase (وَبِالْحَقِّ أَنْزَلْنَاهُ) and the second phrase (وَبِالْحَقِّ نَزَلَ), commentators have offered various explanations, including:

  1. The first phrase means that We decreed for the Quran to be sent down with the truth, and the second phrase adds that this decree was realized. Thus, one refers to the decree (pre-ordination), and the other to the stage of actualization. [^1]
  2. The first phrase means that the material and content of the Quran is truth, while the second phrase indicates that its result and fruit are also truth. [^2]
  3. The first phrase means that We sent down the Quran with the truth, and the second phrase says that because the Prophet did not interfere with the truth himself, it descended as pure truth.

However, there is another possibility here which is clearer than the previous interpretations:

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 6, p. 3955. [^2]: Fi Zilal, under the verse.

Sometimes a person starts a task, but because their power is limited, they cannot carry it out correctly until the end. However, He who is aware of everything and capable of all things starts the beginning correctly and brings the end to complete fruition. For example, sometimes a person releases pure water from a spring, but because they cannot protect it from pollution along the way, it does not reach the consumer pure and clear. But he who is in control of his work in every respect both brings it out pure from the spring and delivers it pure into the vessels of the thirsty and the drinkers.

The Quran is exactly such a book; it was sent down with the truth from Allah's side, and throughout the entire process of communication—whether at the stage where Gabriel was the intermediary or at the stage where the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was the recipient—He protected and guarded it in every way. Even with the passage of time, according to the verse: "Indeed, it is We who sent down the message [i.e., the Quran] and indeed, We will be its guardian" (إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَ إِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ), no hand of distortion has reached or will ever reach its hem, for Allah has taken its guardianship upon Himself.

Therefore, this pure water of Divine revelation reaches all hearts untouched from the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله until the end of the world.

In the next verse, it addresses one of the excuses of the opponents who asked why the Quran was not revealed to the Prophet all at once, and why its method of revelation is necessarily gradual (as referred to in Surah Al-Furqan, verse 32). It says: "We have revealed a Quran to you which is in the form of separate verses, so that you may recite it to the people with deliberation and gradually," so that it may be well absorbed by hearts and minds and fully implemented in practice (وَ قُرْآناً فَرَقْناهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلى‌ مُكْثٍ) [^1]. And again, for further emphasis, it says that We have certainly revealed all of this Quran (وَ نَزَّلْناهُ تَنْزِيلًا).

Undoubtedly, for superficial individuals—especially if they are looking for excuses—this objection regarding the manner of the Quran's revelation will arise: Why was this great Heavenly Book, which is the foundation and essence of Islam, the guide for all humanity, and the axis of all legal, social, political, and devotional laws of Muslims, not revealed to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله all at once in its entirety, so that people could read it from beginning to end and be informed of its conclusion?! However, a little reflection is sufficient to resolve this objection because:

Firstly, although the Quran is called a "Book," it is not like books authored by humans where they sit down, consider a subject, organize its chapters and sections, and write it out. Rather, it is a book that has an inseparable link and connection with the events of its time—namely, the twenty-three years of the Prophet of Islam's صلى الله عليه وآله mission—and its occurrences.

How is it possible for a book that was connected to the events of twenty-three years to be revealed all at once in a single day?! Is it possible to gather all the events of 23 years into one day so that the issues related to them could be revealed in the Quran at once?! For example, many parts of the Quran relate to Islamic battles, some sections concern the actions of the hypocrites, and other issues involve the delegations from various tribes who came to

[^1]: According to many commentators, the accusative case (nasb) of "Qur'anan" in the above verse is due to a hidden verb explained by "faraqnahu," and the underlying structure was "wa faraqna Qur'anan."

the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله took actions regarding them by Divine command. Is it possible for all of these to be written on the first day?

Secondly, the Quran is a book that does not only have an educational aspect; rather, every verse of it must necessarily be implemented after its revelation. If the entire Quran were revealed at once, it would have to be implemented at once, and we know that implementing it all at once was impossible. This is because the reform of a society that is corrupt from head to toe cannot be achieved in one day, and an illiterate child cannot be taken from the first grade to a doctorate in a single day. For this reason, the Quran was revealed gradually so that it could be properly implemented and, so to speak, become fully established, without facing any instability, and so that society would have the capacity to absorb, accept, and preserve it.

Thirdly, the Prophet himself, who was the leader of this great revolution, would undoubtedly have had more strength and readiness if he were to focus his mind on implementing the Quran part by part rather than trying to implement the whole at once. It is true that he was the Messenger of Allah and possessed unique intellect and capability, but despite all this, the gradual acceptance and implementation of the Quran were carried out in a more perfect manner.

Fourthly, gradual revelation meant a permanent connection between the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Source of Revelation, whereas a one-time revelation would not guarantee the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله connection more than once.

The end of verse 32 of Surah Al-Furqan, which says: "Thus [it is revealed] that We may strengthen thereby your heart, and We have spaced it distinctly" (كَذلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ وَ رَتَّلْناهُ تَرْتِيلًا), refers to the third philosophy, while our verse under discussion refers more to the second philosophy. In any case, the collection of these factors is a living and clear proof for the gradual revelation of the Quran and its philosophy.

The next verse, to break the pride of the ignorant opponents, says: "Whether you believe in it or do not believe, those who were given knowledge before it, when it is recited to them, fall down on their faces in prostration" and bow their heads in submission before it (قُلْ آمِنُوا بِهِ أَوْ لا تُؤْمِنُوا إِنَّ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ إِذا يُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِمْ يَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقانِ سُجَّداً).

In this verse, several points should be noted

  1. Commentators generally believe that the phrase "Believe in it or do not believe" (آمِنُوا بِهِ أَوْ لا تُؤْمِنُوا) has an omitted conclusion that is clear from the context. This conclusion has been mentioned in various ways:

Some have said it means that whether you believe or not, the miraculous nature of the Quran and its attribution to Allah is clear.

Others have said the completion of the sentence was: whether you believe or not, its benefit or harm returns to yourselves.

However, there is also the possibility that the subsequent sentence itself completes the previous one, as we have similar expressions in Persian: we say, "Whether you want to accept my words or not, those who are people of knowledge and understanding accept them," which is an allusion to the fact that the reason for your non-acceptance is your lack of awareness and knowledge; if you had knowledge, you would accept.

In other words, if you do not believe, the aware and knowledgeable people will believe.

  1. The meaning of "those who were given knowledge before it" (الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ) refers to a group of Jewish and Christian scholars who, after hearing the verses of the Quran and observing the signs they had read in the Torah and the Gospel, believed and joined the ranks of the true believers, becoming part of the scholars of Islam.

This subject is also mentioned in other verses of the Quran, such as: "They are not all the same; among the People of the Book is a community standing [in obedience], reciting the verses of Allah during periods of the night and prostrating" (Surah Al-Imran, verse 113).

  1. "Yakhirrun" means they fall to the ground involuntarily. Using this expression instead of "yasjudun" (they prostrate) points to a subtle point: that the awakened and conscious-hearted, upon hearing the verses of the Quran, become so attracted and enamored by the Divine words that they involuntarily fall into prostration and surrender their hearts and souls to it. [^1]
  1. "Adhqan" is the plural of "dhaqan," meaning chin. We know that when prostrating, no one places their chin on the ground, but the expression in the verse indicates that they fall to the ground with their entire face before Allah; even their chin, which is the last part of the face that might reach the ground during prostration, is placed on the ground in His majestic presence. Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that in a normal prostration, a person first places their forehead on the dust, but someone who falls to the ground like those who are faint or overwhelmed, their chin hits the ground first. Using this expression in the verse is an emphasis on the meaning of "yakhirrun." [^2]

[^1]: Raghib says in "Al-Mufradat": "Yakhirrun" is originally from the root "kharir," which refers to the sound of water or similar things falling from a height. Using this expression for those prostrating is a sign that they fall to the ground before the Lord while their voices are raised in glorification (Tasbih). [^2]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 15, p. 175.

The next verse recounts their words when they fall into prostration:

"They say: Exalted is our Lord! Indeed, the promise of our Lord has been fulfilled" (وَ يَقُولُونَ سُبْحانَ رَبِّنا إِنْ كانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّنا لَمَفْعُولًا) [^1]. With these words, they express the ultimate degree of their faith and trust in the Lordship of the Creator and His pure attributes, as well as the promises He has made. This is a statement in which faith in Monotheism (Tawhid), the attributes of Truth and His Justice, as well as the Prophethood of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Resurrection (Ma'ad) are included. In this way, they gather the principles of religion in a single sentence.

Again, for further emphasis on the impact of those Divine verses and this passionate prostration, the next verse says: "They fall down on their faces weeping, and it increases them in humility" (وَ يَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقانِ يَبْكُونَ وَ يَزِيدُهُمْ خُشُوعاً). The repetition of the phrase "they fall down on their faces" is proof of both emphasis and continuity. Similarly, the use of the present tense verb "yabkun" (they weep) is evidence of the continuation of their passionate weeping.

Furthermore, the use of the present tense in "it increases them in humility" (وَ يَزِيدُهُمْ خُشُوعاً) is another proof that they never remain stationary in one state and are always moving toward the peak of perfection, and at every moment their humility increases (Khushu' is a state of physical and spiritual modesty and etiquette that a person has before a personage or a reality).

[^1]: "In" in the phrase "in kana wa'du Rabbina" is not conditional, but rather emphatic (mukhaffafah min al-thaqilah).

Key Points

1. Planning for Education and Training

One of the important lessons that the above verses teach us is the necessity of planning for any cultural, intellectual, and social revolution, and any educational program. For if such a program is not organized and implemented in its various stages at the appropriate time, failure is certain. Even the Holy Quran was not revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله all at once—though in the knowledge of Allah it was one, and it was presented to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as a whole once on the Night of Power (Laylat al-Qadr)—but its operational revelation over 23 years occurred at different points in time with precise planning.

When Allah, with His infinite power and knowledge, acts in this way, the duty of human beings is clear. Fundamentally, this is a Divine law and tradition that is manifested not only in the world of "Legislation" (Tashri') but also in the world of "Creation" (Takwin). Have you ever heard of a child being born from a mother overnight? Or a fruit on a tree becoming sweet and ripe in an hour? Therefore, how can one expect everything to be reformed overnight, especially in the stage of building a society intellectually and culturally, or economically and politically?

This statement also means that if we do not get results from our efforts in the short term, we must never allow despair and hopelessness to creep in, nor should we stop our continued efforts and endeavors. We should note that final and complete victories are often achieved in the long term.

2. The Relationship Between Knowledge and Faith

Another lesson that can be clearly learned from the above verses is the relationship between knowledge and faith. It says: whether you believe in these Divine verses or not, the "scholars" not only believe in them, but such a love for "Allah" burns within them that they involuntarily fall into prostration before Him, and a flood of tears flows down their faces, and at every moment their submissiveness and humility increase, and their etiquette and respect toward these verses grow!

It is only the ignorant and base individuals who, when encountering truths, sometimes pass by them with indifference and sometimes with mockery and derision. If such individuals are occasionally drawn toward faith, they will have a weak, unstable faith, devoid of love, passion, and fervor.

Moreover, this is a renewed emphasis on the invalidation of the hollow hypothesis of those who imagine that religion is linked to human ignorance. The Holy Quran, contrary to this claim, emphasizes in various places that knowledge and faith are always together; deep and stable faith is not possible except in the shadow of knowledge, and knowledge also seeks help from faith in its higher and more advanced stages (reflect on this).

Ayahs

قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ أَوِ ٱدْعُوا۟ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنَ ۖ أَيًّا مَّا تَدْعُوا۟ فَلَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَلَا تَجْهَرْ بِصَلَاتِكَ وَلَا تُخَافِتْ بِهَا وَٱبْتَغِ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ سَبِيلًا (١١٠)
Say, ‘Invoke ‘‘Allah’’ or invoke ‘‘the All-beneficent.’’ Whichever [of His Names] you may invoke, to Him belong the Best Names.’ Be neither loud in your prayer, nor murmur it, but follow a middle course between these, (110)
وَقُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَدًا وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ شَرِيكٌ فِى ٱلْمُلْكِ وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ وَلِىٌّ مِّنَ ٱلذُّلِّ ۖ وَكَبِّرْهُ تَكْبِيرًۢا (١١١)
and say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has neither taken any son, nor has He any partner in sovereignty, nor has He any wali out of weakness,’ and magnify Him with a magnification [worthy of Him]. (111)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation for the first verse mentioned above, commentators have narrated from Ibn Abbas that one night in Mecca, the Prophet was in prostration, calling upon Allah by the names "Ya Rahman" (O Most Gracious) and "Ya Rahim" (O Most Merciful). The pretext-seeking polytheists seized the opportunity and said, "Look at this man (who reproaches us for being polytheists), yet he himself worships two gods, while he imagines himself to be a monotheist who has only one deity!" The above verse was revealed and answered them (explaining that these multiple names signify a single Pure Essence). [^1]

Commentary

The Final Pretexts

Following the weak and baseless objections raised by the polytheists in the previous verses and subsequently answered, we arrive at their final pretexts in this series of verses. They used to ask why the Prophet calls Allah by various names despite claiming monotheism. In response to them, the Quran says:

"Say, 'Call upon Allah or call upon the Most Gracious (al-Rahman). Whichever [name] you call—to Him belong the best names'" (قُلِ ادْعُوا اللَّهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى‌).

These blind-hearted people were even heedless of their own daily lives, where they sometimes give different names to a single person or place, each representing a different aspect of its existence.

In such a case, is it surprising that God—whose existence is infinite in every respect, who is the source and origin of all perfections, all blessings, and all goodness, and the primary mover of all the wheels of this universe—should have a specific name corresponding to every action He performs and every perfection His Holy Essence possesses?!

Fundamentally, Allah cannot be called by just one name, nor can He be fully known through one; rather, His names, like His attributes, must be endless to be able to express that Essence. However, since our words, like everything else of ours, are limited, we cannot find more than a limited number of names for Him. Therefore, our knowledge and understanding, whatever they may be, are limited. Even the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, with his vastness of soul, says: "We have not known You as You truly deserve to be known."

But this is no reason not to know Him to the extent of our intellect and understanding, especially since He Himself has greatly assisted us in knowing His Essence and has mentioned Himself by various names in His Book. In the statements of the guardians of His religion (the Awliya), we encounter even more names, reaching up to a thousand.

It is obvious that all of these are "names," and one meaning of "name" (ism) is a sign or mark. All of these are signs of His Pure Essence, and all these lines lead to a single point, in no way diminishing the Oneness (Tawhid) of His Essence and Attributes.

Among all these names, some hold greater importance and majesty because they provide us with deeper knowledge and awareness; these are referred to as "al-Asma al-Husna" (the Most Beautiful Names) in the Quran and Islamic narrations. According to a famous narration from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Indeed, Allah has ninety-nine names; whoever counts them will enter Paradise!" Regarding the meaning of the Most Beautiful Names and these 99 names, we have provided a detailed discussion in Volume 7, page 25 onwards, under verse 180 of Surah Al-A'raf (وَ لِلَّهِ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى‌ فَادْعُوهُ بِها).

However, it is important to know that "counting" these names does not mean merely reciting them or calling Allah by them to become a dweller of Paradise or one whose prayers are answered. Rather, the goal is to "adopt the character" (takhalluq) of these names and to manifest a reflection of the names "All-Knowing," "Most Gracious," "Most Merciful," "Generous," and "Noble" within our own being, so that it may both lead us to Paradise and ensure our prayers are answered in all circumstances!

In a tradition narrated by the late Saduq in the book Al-Tawhid from Hisham ibn al-Hakam, we read: Hisham says: I asked the Imam عليه السلام about the names of Allah and their etymological origins (the root from which the name is derived), and I asked, "From what is 'Allah' derived?"

The Imam عليه السلام said: "O Hisham! it is derived from 'ilah' (which implies bewilderment), and the concept of 'ilah' is that it must have a 'ma'luh' (one who is bewildered and lost in trying to understand the depth of His Essence).

But O Hisham! You must know that the 'name' is different from the 'named' (al-musamma). Whoever worships the name alone without the meaning and content is a disbeliever and has in reality worshipped nothing. And whoever worships both the name and the named is also a disbeliever, for he is a dualist!

But he who worships only the 'named' and not the 'name' (rather, he considers the name a sign and a mark to reach that meaning), this is the reality of true monotheism—do you understand, O Hisham?" Hisham says: I said, "Please explain a bit more for me."

He said: "Allah the Great has 99 names; if every name had its own 'named' entity, we would have 99 gods! But 'Allah' is a name that points to all these attributes, and in any case, all His names are other than His Essence.

O Hisham! 'Bread' is the name of something edible, 'water' is the name of something drinkable, 'clothing' is the name of something wearable, and 'fire' is the name of that burning substance (but all these are names; what we eat, drink, wear, and whose burn we fear is not the name, but the external reality itself)." [^1]

Moving past this discussion: at the end of the verse under discussion, in view of the talk of the polytheists in Mecca regarding the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله prayer—where they said he recites his prayer loudly and disturbs us, asking "What kind of worship is this? What kind of program is this?"—it commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "And do not recite your prayer [too] loudly nor [too] quietly, but seek between that an intermediate way" (وَ لا تَجْهَرْ بِصَلاتِكَ وَ لا تُخافِتْ بِها وَ ابْتَغِ بَيْنَ ذلِكَ سَبِيلًا).

Therefore, the above verse is not concerned with the issue of "loud" (jahri) and "silent" (ikhfati) prayers in the well-known jurisprudential sense; rather, it addresses excess and negligence in the volume of recitation. It says: do not recite so loudly that it becomes shouting, nor so quietly that only the movement of the lips is visible and no sound is heard.

The occasion of revelation narrated by many commentators from Ibn Abbas also confirms this meaning.

Multiple narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding this verse also point to this same interpretation. [^1] Therefore, other interpretations mentioned for this verse seem foreign to the subject.

As for what the moderate limit is here, and what constitutes the "loudness" (jahr) and "silence" (ikhfat) that are forbidden? It appears that "jahr" means shouting, and "ikhfat" means reciting so quietly that even the person himself cannot hear it.

In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that in interpreting the verse, he said: Al-jahru biha raf'ul-sawt, wal-tukhafatu biha ma lam tusmi' nafsak, waqra' bayna dhalik: "Jahr is to raise the voice excessively, and ikhfat is when you do not even hear yourself; do neither of these, but rather choose a middle path between the two." [^1]

As for "ikhfat" and "jahr" in the daily and nightly prayers—as we mentioned above—that is a different ruling with a different concept, which has separate proofs, and our jurists (may Allah be pleased with them) have provided the evidence for it in the "Book of Prayer" (Kitab al-Salat).

Key Point

This Islamic ruling, namely moderation in loudness and silence, provides us with insight and understanding from two perspectives:

First, it says: Do not perform your acts of worship in a way that gives pretexts to the enemies or drives them to mockery and criticism. It is far better that worship be accompanied by dignity, tranquility, and etiquette, so that not only can they find no fault in it, but it becomes an example of Islamic splendor, etiquette, and the majesty of worship.

Those who try to show the presence of their gatherings by making ear-splitting noises from loud speakers at times when people are resting, imagining that by this act they are bringing the voice of Islam to the ears of others—this is not only not the voice of Islam, but it causes people to distance themselves from Islam and is consequently a blow to religious propagation.

Secondly: This should be a model for all our actions and all social, political, and economic programs. All of these must be far from excesses and radicalism, as well as from negligence and laxity. The fundamental principle "seek between that an intermediate way" mentioned in the verse above must be observed everywhere.

Finally, we reach the last verse of this Surah (Surah al-Isra), a verse that ends the Surah with the praise of Allah, just as the Surah began with the glorification (tasbih) of His Pure Essence. In reality, this verse is a conclusion to all the monotheistic discussions of this Surah and the content of all those monotheistic concepts.

Addressing the Prophet, it says: "And say, 'Praise be to Allah, who has not taken a son and has had no partner in [His] dominion and has no protector [to support Him] out of weakness'" (وَ قُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَداً وَ لَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ شَرِيكٌ فِي الْمُلْكِ وَ لَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ وَلِيٌّ مِنَ الذُّلِ‌).

And such a God with such attributes is higher and loftier than anything you can imagine; "therefore, magnify Him with [worthy] magnification" (وَ كَبِّرْهُ تَكْبِيراً).

Key Points

1- The Proportion of the Three Attributes

In the above verse, three categories of Allah's attributes are mentioned, which, considering the command at the end of the verse, are completed by a fourth attribute:

First is the negation of a son, because having a son is evidence of both need and physicality, as well as having a peer or likeness; and Allah is neither a body, nor is He in need, nor does He have a peer or likeness!

Second is the negation of a partner, because the existence of a partner is evidence of a limitation in power and sovereignty, or weakness and inability, or the existence of a peer or likeness; and we know Allah is pure from all these attributes. His power, like His sovereignty, is unlimited, and there is no likeness to Him.

Third is the negation of a guardian (wali) or supporter against difficulties and failures; the negation of this attribute from the Great and Infinite God is also obvious.

In other words, this verse negates any kind of helper or peer for Allah, whether it be one who is lower (like a son), one who is equal (like a partner), or one who is superior (like a guardian).

The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan narrates from some commentators (whom he does not explicitly name) that this verse is aimed at negating the deviant beliefs of three groups: first, the Christians and Jews who attributed a son to God; second, the Arab polytheists who imagined a partner for Him, and thus said in their morning rituals: "Labbayk, You have no partner, except a partner who is Yours!"; and third, the star-worshippers and Magians, because they believed in a guardian and supporter for God.

2- What is Takbir?

The fact that the Quran here emphatically commands the Prophet to magnify Allah certainly means believing in the greatness of the Lord, not merely saying "Allahu Akbar" with the tongue.

It is also noteworthy that the meaning of believing in the greatness of Allah is not that we consider Him superior and higher in comparison to other beings; rather, such a comparison is fundamentally wrong. We must consider Him loftier than being comparable to anything, as Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام taught us in his short and profound words. We read in a tradition:

Someone said in the presence of the Imam: "Allahu Akbar!" The Imam asked: "Allah is greater than what?" He replied: "Than everything!" The Imam said: "With this statement, you have limited Allah (because you compared Him to other beings and deemed Him superior to them)." The man asked: "Then what should I say?" He said: "Say: Allahu akbaru min an yusaf (Allah is greater than can be described)." [^1]

O You who are beyond imagination, comparison, thought, and fancy, And beyond all that we have seen, written, or read. The gathering is over and life has reached its end, Yet we remain at the very beginning of describing You.

Interestingly, in another tradition narrated from the same Imam عليه السلام, we read: When one of the companions said the meaning is that Allah is greater than everything, the Imam said: "Was there anything else in existence [alongside Him] so that He could be greater than it?" The companion asked the Imam: "Then what should I say?" He said: "Say: Akbaru min an yusaf!" [^2]

3- Answer to a Question

A question arises here: how is the praise (hamd) of Allah in the above verse placed against negative attributes (sifat salbiyyah), while we know that "hamd" is praise for positive attributes (sifat thubutiyyah) like knowledge and power? Attributes such as the negation of a son, partner, and guardian are consistent with glorification (tasbih), not praise.

In response to this question, it can be said: although the domains of positive and negative attributes are distinct—one being suitable for tasbih and the other for hamd—in external reality, these two are inseparable. Negating ignorance from Allah is necessarily accompanied by affirming knowledge, just as affirming knowledge for His Pure Essence is consistent with negating ignorance.

On this basis, there is no obstacle to sometimes focusing on the necessary consequence (lazim) and sometimes on the antecedent (malzum). Just as at the beginning of this Surah, glorification (tasbih) was performed for a positive act: (سُبْحانَ الَّذِي أَسْرى‌ بِعَبْدِهِ لَيْلًا مِنَ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ إِلَى الْمَسْجِدِ الْأَقْصَى‌) "Exalted is He who took His Servant by night from al-Masjid al-Haram to al-Masjid al-Aqsa."

O Lord! Fill our hearts with the light of knowledge and faith so that we may always be humble before Your majesty, believe in Your promises, and submit to Your commands. May we worship none but You and rely on none but You. O Allah! Grant us the success never to step outside the bounds of moderation in our lives and to avoid every kind of excess and negligence.

O Allah! We praise You, we recognize You as One, we magnify You—greater than can be described. Forgive us and make our steps firm in Your path, and grant us victory over the enemies who surround us from within and without.

And connect our victories to the final victory of the uprising of the Promised Mahdi (may our souls be sacrificed for him), and grant us the success to complete this Tafsir in a way that meets Your pleasure and satisfaction.

"End of Surah al-Isra" 3rd of Muharram al-Haram 1402, corresponding to October 31, 1981.
